name: AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING

topics.title: Air Pollutants
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Air Pollutants

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Pollutant:** Any substance, whether in solid, liquid, or gaseous form, present in the ambient atmosphere in such concentration, for such duration, and under such conditions as may be, or tend to be, injurious to human health, plant or animal life, property, or which interferes with the comfortable enjoyment of life or property or the conduct of business.

According to the **Indian Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQMS)** and KTU syllabus, air pollutants are broadly categorized based on their origin, chemical nature, and state of matter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Restaurant Kitchen"**
>
> Imagine a closed restaurant kitchen. The chef (industrial activity) prepares food but releases smoke, steam, vapors, and odors. The kitchen exhaust hood (atmosphere) tries to dilute these emissions. If the chef burns food (combustion inefficiency), *smoke* (particulates) is released. If he spills cleaning liquid, *fumes* (VOCs) escape. A non-smoker customer entering this kitchen would feel discomfort (poor air quality). In this analogy:
> - **Kitchen = Urban/Industrial Atmosphere**
> - **Smoke, steam, odors = Air Pollutants**
> - **Exhaust hood = Atmospheric Dispersion**
> - **Non-smoker's discomfort = Health/Environmental Impact**
>
> Just as the chef must control his emissions (with hoods, filters), engineers design **air pollution control equipment** to manage these unwanted substances.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Conditions for Air Quality:**
> - **Standard Temperature:** $T_{std} = 25^\circ C = 298.15\ K$
> - **Standard Pressure:** $P_{std} = 101.325\ kPa = 1\ atm$
> - **Molar Volume of Ideal Gas at STP:** $V_m = 22.414\ L \cdot mol^{-1}$
> - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314\ J \cdot mol^{-1} \cdot K^{-1}$
> - **Molecular Weight of Dry Air:** $M_{air} = 28.97\ g \cdot mol^{-1}$

### 1.4 Units of Concentration (Highlighted)

| Symbol | Unit | Typical Use |
|:------:|:-----|:------------|
| $\mu g / m^3$ | Micrograms per cubic meter | Particulate Matter, Regulatory Standards |
| $ppm$ | Parts per million (by volume) | Gaseous Pollutants (CO, SO₂, NO₂) |
| $ppb$ | Parts per billion (by volume) | Trace Gases (O₃, VOCs, Hg) |
| $mg / m^3$ | Milligrams per cubic meter | High concentration scenarios |
| $\% $ | Percentage by volume | Major constituents (CO₂, O₂, N₂) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Air Pollutants (Structured Logic)

Air pollutants can be classified along **four independent axes**, each important for KTU examination purposes:

#### A) Based on Origin
1. **Primary Pollutants** — Emitted *directly* into the atmosphere from a source.
   - Examples: $CO$, $SO_2$, $NO$, Particulate Matter ($PM_{10}$, $PM_{2.5}$), Lead ($Pb$), Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs).
2. **Secondary Pollutants** — Formed in the atmosphere through **chemical reactions** between primary pollutants and natural atmospheric constituents.
   - Examples: Ozone ($O_3$), $NO_2$, Sulfuric acid ($H_2SO_4$) mist, Smog (Photochemical and Sulfurous).

#### B) Based on Source
1. **Natural Sources** — Volcanic eruptions, forest fires, pollen, dust storms, biogenic VOCs from vegetation, sea salt aerosols.
2. **Anthropogenic (Man-made) Sources** — Vehicular exhaust, industrial emissions, power plants, waste incineration, agricultural burning.

#### C) Based on State of Matter
1. **Gaseous Pollutants** — $SO_2$, $NO_x$, $CO$, $O_3$, $H_2S$, $HF$, $Cl_2$, $NH_3$, VOCs.
2. **Particulate Pollutants** — Dust, soot, smoke, fumes, mist, pollen, fly ash.

#### D) Criteria Pollutants (US-EPA / CPCB India)
The **"National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQMS)"** identifies six classical criteria pollutants:
- Carbon Monoxide ($CO$)
- Sulfur Dioxide ($SO_2$)
- Nitrogen Dioxide ($NO_2$)
- Ozone ($O_3$)
- Lead ($Pb$)
- Particulate Matter ($PM_{10}$ and $PM_{2.5}$)

### 2.2 The "Why and How" Behind Each Step

> [!NOTE]
> **Why classify pollutants?**
> Different pollutants require different control technologies. For example, *gaseous SO₂* is best removed via **scrubbing** (wet systems), while *particulate matter* is captured by **cyclones, baghouses, or ESPs**. Classification helps engineers select the right control strategy.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE FOR AIR POLLUTANTS**

| \# | Formula | Description | Units |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:------|
| 1 | $C(\mu g/m^3) = \dfrac{C(ppm) \times M \times P}{R \times T}$ | Conversion from ppm to $\mu g/m^3$ | $\mu g/m^3$ |
| 2 | $C(ppm) = \dfrac{C(\mu g/m^3) \times R \times T}{M \times P}$ | Conversion from $\mu g/m^3$ to ppm | $ppm$ |
| 3 | $C(ppb) = \dfrac{C(\mu g/m^3) \times R \times T \times 10^3}{M \times P}$ | Conversion from $\mu g/m^3$ to ppb | $ppb$ |
| 4 | $PM_{2.5} \leq PM_{10} \leq TSP$ | Size hierarchy of particulate matter | $\mu m$ |
| 5 | $n_i = \dfrac{P_i}{P_{total}}$ | Mole fraction definition | dimensionless |
| 6 | $ppm = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{total}} \times 10^6$ | Volume mixing ratio | $ppm$ |
| 7 | $\rho_{gas} = \dfrac{P \times M}{R \times T}$ | Gas density at given $P, T$ | $kg/m^3$ |

> Where:
> - $M$ = Molecular weight of pollutant (g/mol)
> - $P$ = Atmospheric pressure (Pa or atm)
> - $T$ = Absolute temperature (K)
> - $R$ = Universal gas constant (8.314 J/mol·K)
> - $P_i$ = Partial pressure of species $i$
> - $V_i$ = Partial volume of species $i$

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These formulas are used by **environmental consultants**, **CPCB/SPCB officers**, and **plant engineers** daily for:
> - Stack emission monitoring
> - Ambient air quality index (AQI) calculation
> - Dispersion modeling using AERMOD / ISCST3
> - Designing flue gas desulfurization (FGD) units
> - Compliance reporting under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981** of India.

### 2.5 Major Air Pollutants — Property Summary

| Pollutant | Chemical Formula | Molar Mass (g/mol) | Primary Source | Type |
|:---------:|:----------------:|:------------------:|:---------------|:-----|
| Carbon Monoxide | $CO$ | $28.01$ | Incomplete combustion | Primary |
| Sulfur Dioxide | $SO_2$ | $64.07$ | Fossil fuel combustion ($S$ in coal) | Primary |
| Nitrogen Dioxide | $NO_2$ | $46.01$ | High-temp combustion, vehicles | Both |
| Ozone (Ground-level) | $O_3$ | $48.00$ | Photochemical reactions | Secondary |
| Particulate Matter | $PM$ | Variable | Combustion, dust, industrial | Primary |
| Lead | $Pb$ | $207.2$ | Leaded gasoline, smelting | Primary |
| Ammonia | $NH_3$ | $17.03$ | Agriculture, fertilizers | Primary |
| Hydrogen Sulfide | $H_2S$ | $34.08$ | Refineries, sewage | Primary |
| VOC (e.g., Benzene) | $C_6H_6$ | $78.11$ | Solvents, vehicles | Primary |
| Carbon Dioxide | $CO_2$ | $44.01$ | Combustion, respiration | Primary (GHG) |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Conversion Between ppm and $\mu g/m^3$

This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU university examinations on this topic. The derivation must be learned thoroughly.

#### Starting Point — Definition of ppm

By definition, **parts per million (ppm)** is a volume mixing ratio:

$$ppm = \frac{V_{pollutant}}{V_{total\ air}} \times 10^6$$

At standard conditions of temperature $T$ and pressure $P$, we can use the **Ideal Gas Law**:

$$PV = nRT$$

#### Step 1 — Express Volume in Terms of Moles

For the pollutant species $i$:

$$V_i = \frac{n_i RT}{P}$$

#### Step 2 — Express Total Volume

For the total gas mixture:

$$V_{total} = \frac{n_{total} RT}{P}$$

#### Step 3 — Substitute into the ppm Definition

$$ppm = \frac{\dfrac{n_i RT}{P}}{\dfrac{n_{total} RT}{P}} \times 10^6$$

The terms $R$, $T$, and $P$ cancel out cleanly:

$$ppm = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} \times 10^6$$

This means **ppm is numerically equal to the mole fraction multiplied by $10^6$**.

#### Step 4 — Convert from Moles to Mass

Since mass $m = n \times M$, where $M$ is the molar mass:

$$n_i = \frac{m_i}{M_i}$$

#### Step 5 — Relate Volume to Mass Concentration

The mass concentration is defined as:

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \frac{m_i}{V_{total}}$$

#### Step 6 — Combine All Steps

Starting from:

$$ppm = \frac{n_i}{n_{total}} \times 10^6 = \frac{m_i / M_i}{V_{total} / V_m} \times 10^6$$

where $V_m$ is the molar volume. Substituting and rearranging:

$$C(\mu g/m^3) = \frac{C(ppm) \times M \times P}{R \times T} \times 10^3$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The factor $10^3$ appears because we want the answer in *micrograms*, while the SI base unit of molar volume yields grams.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** The ambient $SO_2$ concentration at a busy traffic intersection is measured as **0.05 ppm**. Convert this concentration to **$\mu g/m^3$** at standard conditions ($T = 298\ K$, $P = 101.325\ kPa$). Given $M_{SO_2} = 64.07\ g/mol$.

#### Solution:

**Step 1:** Identify given values.

$$C_{ppm} = 0.05,\quad M = 64.07\ g/mol,\quad P = 101.325\ kPa,\quad T = 298\ K$$

**Step 2:** Use $R = 8.314\ J/(mol \cdot K)$.

**Step 3:** Apply the conversion formula:

$$C(\mu g/m^3) = \frac{0.05 \times 64.07 \times 101325}{8.314 \times 298} \times \frac{10^3}{10^3}$$

**Step 4:** Compute numerator:

$$0.05 \times 64.07 \times 101325 = 3.246 \times 10^5$$

**Step 5:** Compute denominator:

$$8.314 \times 298 = 2477.6$$

**Step 6:** Divide:

$$C = \frac{3.246 \times 10^5}{2477.6} \approx 131.05\ \mu g/m^3$$

#### Final Answer:

$$\boxed{C_{SO_2} = 131.05\ \mu g/m^3}$$

### 3.3 Reverse Conversion — Worked Numerical

> **Problem:** If $PM_{10}$ concentration is measured as **$120\ \mu g/m^3$**, find the equivalent concentration in **$ppm$**. Assume $M_{PM_{10}} = 100\ g/mol$ (representative average).

#### Solution:

$$C(ppm) = \frac{C(\mu g/m^3) \times R \times T}{M \times P}$$

$$C(ppm) = \frac{120 \times 8.314 \times 298}{100 \times 101325} \times 10^{-3}$$

**Numerator:** $120 \times 8.314 \times 298 \times 10^{-3} = 297.3$

**Denominator:** $100 \times 101325 = 1.013 \times 10^7$

$$C(ppm) = \frac{297.3}{1.013 \times 10^7} = 2.935 \times 10^{-5}\ ppm$$

$$\boxed{C \approx 29.35\ ppb}$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effect of Pollutants

> [!NOTE]
> **HIGH-YIELD IMPACT TABLE — Frequently asked in 3-mark KTU questions**

| Pollutant | Health Effect | Environmental Effect | Typical Source |
|:---------:|:--------------|:---------------------|:---------------|
| $CO$ | Binds hemoglobin → reduced oxygen transport | Contributes to tropospheric ozone | Vehicle exhaust |
| $SO_2$ | Respiratory irritation, bronchitis | Acid rain ($H_2SO_4$) | Coal-fired power plants |
| $NO_2$ | Lung inflammation, smog | Acid rain, eutrophication | High-temp combustion |
| $O_3$ (ground) | Chest pain, lung damage | Crop yield reduction, rubber cracking | Photochemical smog |
| $PM_{2.5}$ | Penetrates alveoli → cardiovascular disease | Haze, reduced visibility | Diesel exhaust, biomass burning |
| $Pb$ | Neurological damage, especially in children | Soil contamination | Leaded fuel, smelting |
| VOCs (e.g., Benzene) | Carcinogenic | Tropospheric ozone formation | Solvents, petroleum |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**Define the term "Air Pollutant". Classify air pollutants based on their origin with two examples for each class.**

**Model Answer:**

An air pollutant is any substance present in the atmosphere in concentrations and durations that are injurious to human health, plant/animal life, or property, or that interfere with the comfortable enjoyment of life.

Based on **origin**, air pollutants are classified into:

1. **Primary Pollutants** — Emitted directly from a source.
   - Example 1: $SO_2$ from coal combustion
   - Example 2: $CO$ from vehicle exhaust

2. **Secondary Pollutants** — Formed in the atmosphere through chemical reactions among primary pollutants and natural constituents.
   - Example 1: $O_3$ formed by photochemical reaction of $NO_x$ + VOCs
   - Example 2: $H_2SO_4$ mist formed by oxidation of $SO_2$

**(3 marks — Definition 1 mark, classification 1 mark, examples 1 mark)**

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**List the six criteria pollutants as per the National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQMS) of India. State any two health effects of Particulate Matter ($PM_{2.5}$).**

**Model Answer:**

The **six criteria pollutants** as per NAAQMS are:

1. Carbon Monoxide ($CO$)
2. Sulfur Dioxide ($SO_2$)
3. Nitrogen Dioxide ($NO_2$)
4. Ozone ($O_3$)
5. Lead ($Pb$)
6. Particulate Matter ($PM_{10}$ and $PM_{2.5}$)

**Health effects of $PM_{2.5}$:**

1. Due to its tiny aerodynamic diameter ($\leq 2.5\ \mu m$), it penetrates deep into the **alveolar region of the lungs**, causing chronic respiratory diseases, bronchitis, and aggravated asthma.
2. It enters the **bloodstream** through alveolar walls, contributing to cardiovascular diseases, reduced lung function, and premature mortality.

**(3 marks — list 2 marks, health effects 1 mark)**

---

### 📕 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> KTU 2024 Scheme: Internal Choice between two questions. Both must be solved.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Adaptation]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of air pollutants based on (i) state of matter and (ii) source**, with at least three examples for each category. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** The concentration of $NO_2$ at an industrial area is measured as **0.12 ppm**. Convert this concentration to **$\mu g/m^3$** at standard temperature ($25^\circ C$) and pressure (1 atm). Given $M_{NO_2} = 46\ g/mol$. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Classification Based on State of Matter:**

1. **Gaseous Pollutants** — These exist as discrete molecules in the gaseous state and disperse freely in air.
   - *Example 1:* Sulfur Dioxide ($SO_2$) — from combustion of sulfur-bearing fuels
   - *Example 2:* Nitrogen Oxides ($NO_x$) — from high-temperature combustion
   - *Example 3:* Carbon Monoxide ($CO$) — from incomplete combustion

2. **Particulate Pollutants** — These are discrete solid or liquid particles suspended in air, ranging from sub-micron to ~$100\ \mu m$ in diameter.
   - *Example 1:* Dust — from crushing, grinding operations
   - *Example 2:* Fumes — from condensation of vaporized metals (e.g., zinc oxide)
   - *Example 3:* Mist — from sulfuric acid plants, spray operations

**Classification Based on Source:**

1. **Natural Sources**
   - *Volcanic eruptions* — emit $SO_2$, $H_2S$, dust
   - *Forest fires* — emit $CO$, $NO_x$, particulates
   - *Dust storms* — emit large particulates

2. **Anthropogenic Sources**
   - *Vehicular exhaust* — emits $CO$, $NO_x$, VOCs
   - *Power plants* — emit $SO_2$, $NO_x$, fly ash
   - *Industrial processes* — emit specific pollutants (e.g., $HF$ from aluminum smelting)

**(7 marks — State 3.5 marks + Source 3.5 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Given:**
- $C_{ppm} = 0.12$
- $T = 25 + 273 = 298\ K$
- $P = 1\ atm = 101325\ Pa$
- $M_{NO_2} = 46\ g/mol$
- $R = 8.314\ J/(mol \cdot K)$

**Formula:**

$$C\left(\frac{\mu g}{m^3}\right) = \frac{C(ppm) \times M \times P}{R \times T} \times \frac{10^3}{10^3}$$

**Step 1:** Substitute values:

$$C = \frac{0.12 \times 46 \times 101325}{8.314 \times 298}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate numerator:

$$0.12 \times 46 = 5.52$$
$$5.52 \times 101325 = 5.593 \times 10^5$$

**Step 3:** Calculate denominator:

$$8.314 \times 298 = 2477.57$$

**Step 4:** Divide:

$$C = \frac{5.593 \times 10^5}{2477.57} = 225.75\ \mu g/m^3$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{C_{NO_2} = 225.75\ \mu g/m^3}$$

**(7 marks — Formula 2 marks + Substitution 2 marks + Calculation 2 marks + Final answer 1 mark)**

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Adaptation]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Primary and Secondary air pollutants**. Discuss the formation mechanism of **Photochemical Smog**, listing its major components and health effects. **\[7 marks\]**

**(b)** A monitoring station reports $PM_{10}$ concentration as **$180\ \mu g/m^3$** and $SO_2$ concentration as **$75\ \mu g/m^3$**. Convert both concentrations to **ppm** at standard conditions. Given: $M_{PM_{10}} \approx 100\ g/mol$, $M_{SO_2} = 64\ g/mol$. **\[7 marks\]**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

| Feature | Primary Pollutant | Secondary Pollutant |
|:--------|:------------------|:--------------------|
| Definition | Emitted directly from source | Formed by atmospheric reactions |
| Examples | $CO$, $SO_2$, $NO$, dust | $O_3$, $NO_2$, $H_2SO_4$, PAN |
| Source | Combustion, industry, vehicles | Atmospheric chemistry |
| Time of formation | Immediate | Delayed (hours) |

**Photochemical Smog Formation Mechanism:**

Photochemical smog forms through the following sequence:

1. **Emission Phase:** $NO$ and VOCs are emitted from vehicles in the morning rush hour.
2. **Photochemical Initiation:** $NO_2$ absorbs UV radiation from sunlight ($\lambda < 430\ nm$) and dissociates:

$$NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O \cdot$$

3. **Ozone Formation:** The atomic oxygen reacts with $O_2$ in the presence of a third body $M$:

$$O \cdot + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$$

4. **PAN Formation:** VOCs (e.g., acetaldehyde) react with $NO_2$ to form **Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN)**.
5. **Accumulation:** Smog is most intense in the afternoon when sunlight is strongest, characterized by a brownish haze.

**Health Effects:**
- Eye irritation (formaldehyde, PAN)
- Lung damage (ozone)
- Reduced visibility
- Crop damage

**(7 marks — Comparison 2 marks + Mechanism 3 marks + Health effects 2 marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Formula:**

$$C(ppm) = \frac{C(\mu g/m^3) \times R \times T}{M \times P \times 10^3}$$

**For $PM_{10}$:** ($M = 100\ g/mol$)

$$C_{PM_{10}} = \frac{180 \times 8.314 \times 298}{100 \times 101325 \times 10^3}$$

Numerator: $180 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 4.460 \times 10^5$

Denominator: $100 \times 101325 \times 10^3 = 1.013 \times 10^{10}$

$$C_{PM_{10}} = \frac{4.460 \times 10^5}{1.013 \times 10^{10}} = 4.40 \times 10^{-5}\ ppm$$

**For $SO_2$:** ($M = 64\ g/mol$)

$$C_{SO_2} = \frac{75 \times 8.314 \times 298}{64 \times 101325 \times 10^3}$$

Numerator: $75 \times 8.314 \times 298 = 1.858 \times 10^5$

Denominator: $64 \times 101325 \times 10^3 = 6.485 \times 10^9$

$$C_{SO_2} = \frac{1.858 \times 10^5}{6.485 \times 10^9} = 2.865 \times 10^{-5}\ ppm$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{C_{PM_{10}} = 4.40 \times 10^{-5}\ ppm \quad \text{and} \quad C_{SO_2} = 2.87 \times 10^{-5}\ ppm}$$

**(7 marks — Formula 2 marks + $PM_{10}$ 2.5 marks + $SO_2$ 2.5 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Students frequently forget the $\times 10^3$ or $\div 10^3$ factor when converting between ppm and $\mu g/m^3$. This single error results in answers off by a factor of 1000, losing **4–5 marks instantly**.
>
> 2. **Pressure Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing atm and Pa in the same equation causes calculation errors. Always convert $P$ to consistent SI units (Pa).
>
> 3. **Forgetting to Identify Primary vs Secondary:** Many students list examples without specifying which category they belong to. Examiners strictly deduct marks for ambiguity.
>
> 4. **No Mention of Temperature:** Numerical answers are accepted only at specified temperature. Always state "$T = 298\ K$ at STP" before substituting.
>
> 5. **Mole vs Mass Confusion:** Remember that *ppm by volume = mole fraction × $10^6$*, not mass fraction. Some students erroneously use mass fractions.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Air Pollutants**

- ✅ **Definition:** Air pollutant = substance in ambient air that is injurious at given concentration and duration.
- ✅ **Primary Pollutants:** Emitted directly (CO, SO₂, NO, PM₁₀, Pb, VOCs).
- ✅ **Secondary Pollutants:** Formed in atmosphere (O₃, NO₂, H₂SO₄, PAN).
- ✅ **Six NAAQMS Criteria Pollutants:** CO, SO₂, NO₂, O₃, Pb, PM.
- ✅ **PM₂.₅ ≤ PM₁₀ ≤ TSP** (Total Suspended Particulates) — size hierarchy.
- ✅ **Photochemical Smog:** Brown haze, ozone + PAN + formaldehyde, peaks in afternoon.
- ✅ **Classical (London) Smog:** Sulfurous, gray, occurs in cold humid weather, contains SO₂ + soot.
- ✅ **Key Conversion Formula:**
$$C(\mu g/m^3) = \frac{C(ppm) \times M \times P}{R \times T}$$
- ✅ **Standard Reference:** $T = 298\ K$, $P = 101.325\ kPa$, $M_{air} = 28.97\ g/mol$.
- ✅ **Health Memorization:** CO binds hemoglobin (200× affinity), SO₂ → acid rain, O₃ → crop damage, Pb → neurological damage in children, PM₂.₅ → alveolar penetration.
- ✅ **Legislative Reference:** Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, **1981** (India).
- ✅ **Indian Regulator:** **CPCB** (Central Pollution Control Board) sets NAAQMS standards.
- ✅ **KTU Numerical Tip:** Always carry units throughout the calculation; final answer in **$\mu g/m^3$** or **ppm** as required.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sources and Classification
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sources and Classification

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Air Pollution — Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Pollution** is defined as the presence of one or more contaminants (solid, liquid, or gaseous) in the atmosphere in such quantities and duration as to be injurious to human health, animal life, vegetation, or property, or which unreasonably interferes with the comfortable enjoyment of life or the conduct of business.

In environmental engineering parlance, any substance introduced into the atmosphere that has a **detrimental effect** on living organisms, climate, or built infrastructure qualifies as an **air pollutant**. The key parameters are **concentration**, **duration of exposure**, and **toxicity** of the substance.

## 1.2 Sources and Classification — Definition

**Sources** of air pollution refer to the specific points, areas, or activities from which pollutants are emitted into the atmosphere. **Classification** is the systematic grouping of these sources and pollutants based on origin, physical state, chemical nature, and emission pattern.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Kitchen Sink**
> Imagine your kitchen sink as the Earth's atmosphere. Pollutants are like the leftovers you pour down the drain every day — vegetable peels, oil, soap. The **sink** (atmosphere) has a limited capacity to dilute and absorb. When you pour too much, it **clogs** (air pollution). Now, the **source** is the plate or the cooking pan from which the waste originates. We can either reduce the waste at the plate (source control) or install a filter (control equipment) at the drain. **Classification** simply means sorting these wastes — organic vs. inorganic, solid vs. liquid — so we know what filter to install.

Just as we cannot apply the same garbage disposal method to glass and food waste, we cannot apply the same control technology to **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** and **Particulate Matter (PM)** — they are fundamentally different classes of pollutants.

## 1.4 Key Physical Metrics and Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Parameters (Bold for Emphasis):**
> - **Ambient Air Quality Standard (AAQS)** — set by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**, India
> - **WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021)** — **PM₂.₅ = 5 µg/m³ (annual), 15 µg/m³ (24-hr)**
> - **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) — India: PM₁₀ = 60 µg/m³ (24-hr), PM₂.₅ = 40 µg/m³ (24-hr)**
> - **Emission Rate Unit:** **kg/hr** or **g/s**
> - **Concentration Unit:** **µg/m³** (micrograms per cubic meter) or **ppm** (parts per million)
> - **Conversion Constant (at 25°C, 1 atm):** **1 ppm = 1000 × (molecular weight of gas) / 24.45 µg/m³**
> - **Universal Gas Law constant (R):** **0.0821 L·atm/(mol·K)** or **8.314 J/(mol·K)**

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters in KTU Examinations

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **Module 1 foundational topic** in PECET756 and is a **high-frequency question area**. Students are routinely asked 3-mark definition questions and 14-mark classification questions. The examiner expects students to provide **structured classifications** with **examples**, not just vague definitions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Cheat Sheet

## 2.1 Why and How of Source Identification

Every air pollution control strategy starts with **source identification**. The hierarchy of pollution control, as established by the **U.S. EPA** and followed by India's **CPCB**, is:

1. **Elimination** — Remove the polluting process entirely
2. **Substitution** — Replace with a cleaner alternative
3. **Source Modification** — Engineering redesign to reduce emissions
4. **End-of-pipe Treatment** — Control equipment (cyclones, scrubbers, ESPs)

For this hierarchy to be applied, we must first **classify** the source and the pollutant.

## 2.2 Classification of Air Pollution Sources

### A. Based on Origin (Natural vs. Anthropogenic)

**Natural Sources (Biogenic & Geogenic):**
- **Volcanic eruptions** — emit SO₂, CO₂, ash
- **Forest fires** — CO, CO₂, PM, VOCs
- **Pollen dispersal** — biological particulates
- **Dust storms** — wind-blown soil particles
- **Sea spray** — salt aerosols (NaCl)
- **Microbial decay** — CH₄, H₂S, NH₃

**Anthropogenic (Man-made) Sources:**
- **Combustion of fossil fuels** (coal, oil, gas) — power plants, vehicles
- **Industrial processes** — smelters, cement kilns, refineries
- **Agricultural activities** — pesticide spraying, animal husbandry
- **Waste disposal** — incineration, landfills
- **Construction and demolition**

### B. Based on Spatial Distribution (Stationary, Mobile, Area, Line)

This is the **most critical classification** from an engineering control perspective:

- **Stationary (Point) Sources** — Fixed locations with a defined point of emission. Example: A factory smokestack.
- **Mobile Sources** — Moving sources emitting pollutants. Example: Cars, trucks, aircraft.
- **Area Sources** — Distributed sources over a region. Example: Gasoline evaporation from filling stations, agricultural fields.
- **Line Sources** — Continuous linear sources. Example: Highways, railway lines.

### C. Based on Emission Pattern

- **Continuous sources** — Operate 24/7 (e.g., power plants)
- **Intermittent sources** — Operate periodically (e.g., batch industries)
- **Fugitive emissions** — Unplanned, non-routine leaks from valves, flanges, storage tanks

## 2.3 Classification of Air Pollutants

### A. Primary vs. Secondary Pollutants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Primary Pollutants** are emitted directly into the atmosphere in a harmful form. Examples: CO, SO₂, NOₓ, PM, VOCs, Lead (Pb).
>
> **Secondary Pollutants** are formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and other atmospheric constituents. Examples: **Ozone (O₃)**, **Smog (PAN — Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)**, **Sulfuric acid mist (H₂SO₄)**, **Nitric acid (HNO₃)**.

### B. Classification by Physical State

- **Gaseous Pollutants** — CO, SO₂, NO₂, O₃, VOCs, H₂S
- **Particulate Pollutants** — Dust, smoke, fumes, mist, spray
- **Combined** — Many pollutants exist in both phases (e.g., aerosols)

### C. Classification by Chemical Nature

- **Organic** — Benzene, formaldehyde, PAHs (Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons)
- **Inorganic** — SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, H₂S
- **Biological** — Bacteria, viruses, pollen, spores

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute values to prevent markdown table breaks.

| # | Parameter | Formula / Definition | Unit |
|---|-----------|---------------------|------|
| 1 | Concentration conversion | $C_{µg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times \dfrac{MW \times P \times 10^6}{R \times T \times 1000}$ | µg/m³ |
| 2 | At 25°C, 1 atm simplification | $C_{µg/m^3} = C_{ppm} \times MW \times 40.9$ | µg/m³ |
| 3 | Mole fraction (ppm) | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{total}} \times 10^6$ | ppm |
| 4 | Emission rate | $E = Q \times C$ | g/s |
| 5 | Ground level concentration (Gaussian Plume) | $C(x,y,0) = \dfrac{Q}{\pi \cdot u \cdot \sigma_y \cdot \sigma_z} \cdot \exp\left(-\dfrac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \dfrac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | g/m³ |
| 6 | Wind speed power law | $u(z) = u_r \left(\dfrac{z}{z_r}\right)^p$ | m/s |

**Where:**
- $MW$ = Molecular weight
- $P$ = Pressure (atm)
- $R$ = Universal gas constant
- $T$ = Temperature (K)
- $Q$ = Emission rate
- $u$ = Wind speed at stack height
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ = Horizontal and vertical dispersion coefficients
- $H$ = Effective stack height
- $p$ = Stability-dependent exponent (typically 0.07 to 0.4)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> The classification framework directly drives **regulatory compliance**, **dispersion modeling selection** (Gaussian vs. Lagrangian), and **control equipment sizing** (cyclone vs. ESP vs. scrubber). Without proper classification, an engineer cannot determine whether a **baghouse filter** (for PM) or a **wet scrubber** (for SO₂) is the appropriate control technology.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Classification Matrix & Detailed Analysis

## 3.1 Detailed Classification of Sources — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since this is a conceptual topic without heavy numerical derivations, the "derivations" here are **logical step-by-step classification expansions**. Every category is broken down rigorously.

### Step 1: Root-Level Classification (Anthropogenic vs. Natural)

**Logic:** First, determine whether human activity is involved. If yes → **anthropogenic**; if no → **natural**.

**Anthropogenic further branches into:**
- **Stationary / Point sources**
- **Mobile sources**
- **Area sources**
- **Line sources**

**Natural further branches into:**
- **Geogenic** (geological origin — volcanoes, dust)
- **Biogenic** (biological origin — pollen, microbes)

### Step 2: Determine Emission Pattern (Continuous, Intermittent, Fugitive)

**Logic:** Ask the question — *Does the source emit at a constant rate?*

- **Yes (24/7 operation)** → Continuous (e.g., coal-fired power plant)
- **No (Periodic batch operation)** → Intermittent (e.g., batch cement kiln)
- **No (Unplanned leaks)** → Fugitive (e.g., flange leak in refinery)

### Step 3: Identify Pollutant Type Released

Each source is then mapped to the pollutants it generates:

| Source | Primary Pollutants | Secondary Pollutants Formed |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------------|
| Coal combustion | SO₂, PM, CO, NOₓ | H₂SO₄ mist, O₃ |
| Vehicle (Petrol) | CO, NOₓ, VOCs, Pb (older) | O₃, PAN, smog |
| Refinery | H₂S, VOCs, SO₂ | Sulfate aerosols |
| Cement industry | PM (cement dust), CO₂ | — |
| Steel industry | PM, CO, SO₂, NOₓ | H₂SO₄ |
| Incinerator | Dioxins, furans, PM, HCl | — |

### Step 4: Stationary Source Sub-Classification (Engineering View)

Stationary sources are sub-divided based on **stack parameters**:

- **Point source with tall stack** (e.g., power plant) — relies on **atmospheric dispersion**
- **Point source with low stack** — requires **local control equipment**
- **Area sources** — require **diffusion modeling** and **land-use planning**

## 3.2 Detailed Classification of Pollutants — Step-by-Step

### Step 1: Primary vs. Secondary

$$
C_{ambient} = C_{primary} + C_{secondary}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**
- $C_{primary}$ = mass emitted directly from the source
- $C_{secondary}$ = mass formed by atmospheric reactions

For example, **tropospheric ozone** is **not emitted** by any major source — it is **formed** by:
- $NO + O_3 \rightarrow NO_2 + O_2$
- $NO_2 + h\nu \rightarrow NO + O$
- $O + O_2 + M \rightarrow O_3 + M$

Hence, ozone is **secondary**.

### Step 2: Particulate Matter (PM) Sub-Classification

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PM classification by aerodynamic diameter (critical for KTU exams):**
> - **TSP (Total Suspended Particulates)** — All particles up to **~50 µm**
> - **PM₁₀** — Particles with aerodynamic diameter **≤ 10 µm** (inhalable)
> - **PM₂.₅** — Particles with aerodynamic diameter **≤ 2.5 µm** (respirable — penetrates alveoli)
> - **Ultrafine (UFP)** — **< 0.1 µm** (deepest lung penetration)

### Step 3: NOx Classification

- **NO** (Nitric oxide) — primary, colorless
- **NO₂** (Nitrogen dioxide) — primary/secondary, brownish
- **NOₓ** — collective term (NO + NO₂)
- **N₂O** (Nitrous oxide) — greenhouse gas

### Step 4: SOx Classification

- **SO₂** — primary
- **SO₃** — primary, minor fraction
- **Sulfates (SO₄²⁻)** — secondary, atmospheric aerosol

## 3.3 Worked Example — Classification Mapping

**Problem Context:** A thermal power plant burning coal is located 5 km from a residential zone. Identify the source type and pollutants.

**Step-by-step solution:**

1. **Origin classification:** Anthropogenic (man-made coal combustion)
2. **Spatial classification:** **Stationary (Point) Source** — fixed power plant
3. **Emission pattern:** **Continuous** — operates 24/7
4. **Primary pollutants:** PM, SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, Fly ash
5. **Secondary pollutants:** H₂SO₄ mist, NO₂, O₃ (downwind)
6. **Control approach:** Tall stack (atmospheric dispersion) + **Electrostatic Precipitator (ESP)** for PM + **Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD)** for SO₂

## 3.4 Noise Pollution Source Classification (Relevant Co-Listed Topic)

> [!NOTE]
> Since the course is **Air AND Noise Pollution**, sources of noise are also classified:

- **Industrial noise** — machinery, motors
- **Traffic noise** — vehicles, horns (dominant urban source)
- **Aircraft noise** — aviation
- **Construction noise** — short-term but high intensity
- **Community noise** — loudspeakers, public gatherings

**Sound measurement metric:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P}{P_0}\right)$ in **dB**, where $P_0 = 20 \, µPa$ is the reference sound pressure.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Source Classification Hierarchy (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Air Pollution Sources]
    nodeA --> nodeB[Anthropogenic]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Natural]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Stationary Point Sources]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Mobile Sources]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Area Sources]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Line Sources]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Geogenic]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Biogenic]

    nodeD --> nodeD3[Incinerators]
    nodeE --> nodeE1[Road Vehicles]
    nodeE --> nodeE2[Aircraft]
    nodeE --> nodeE3[Trains]
    nodeF --> nodeF1[Gasoline Stations]
    nodeF --> nodeF2[Agricultural Fields]
    nodeG --> nodeG1[Highways]
    nodeG --> nodeG2[Railway Tracks]
    nodeH --> nodeH1[Volcanic Eruptions]
    nodeH --> nodeH2[Dust Storms]
    nodeI --> nodeI1[Pollen]
    nodeI --> nodeI2[Methane from Wetlands]
```

## 4.2 Pollutant Classification Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Air Pollutants]
    nodeA --> nodeB[By Origin]
    nodeA --> nodeC[By Physical State]
    nodeA --> nodeD[By Chemical Nature]
    nodeB --> nodeB1[Primary Pollutants]
    nodeB --> nodeB2[Secondary Pollutants]
    nodeC --> nodeC1[Gaseous]
    nodeC --> nodeC2[Particulate]
    nodeC --> nodeC3[Aerosols]

    nodeD --> nodeD2[Inorganic]
    nodeD --> nodeD3[Biological]

    nodeB1 --> nodeB1c[NOx]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1d[PM]

```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Source-to-Effect Pathway

```mermaid
graph LR
    src1[Stationary Source] -->|Emission| dis[Atmosphere Dispersion]
    src2[Mobile Source] -->|Emission| dis
    src3[Area Source] -->|Emission| dis
    dis -->|Transport| rec[Receptor Human Ecosystem Property]
    rec --> eff1[Health Effects]
    rec --> eff2[Environmental Damage]
    rec --> eff3[Material Corrosion]

    chem --> sec[Secondary Pollutant Formation]
    sec --> rec
```

## 4.4 Noise Source Hierarchy

```mermaid

    nodeN[Noise Pollution Sources]
    nodeN --> nodeN1[Industrial]

    nodeN1 --> nodeN1b[Generators]

    nodeN2 --> nodeN2b[Aircraft]
    nodeN2 --> nodeN2c[Railways]
    nodeN3 --> nodeN3a[Public Loudspeakers]
    nodeN3 --> nodeN3b[Religious Gatherings]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Q1. Distinguish between primary and secondary air pollutants with two examples each.**

**Model Answer:**
- **Primary Pollutants:** Emitted directly from a source in a harmful form. Examples: Carbon Monoxide (CO) from vehicles, Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂) from coal combustion.
- **Secondary Pollutants:** Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural atmospheric constituents. Examples: Tropospheric Ozone (O₃) formed by photochemical reactions of NOₓ and VOCs; Sulphuric acid mist (H₂SO₄) formed by oxidation of SO₂.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 | Module 1 | 3 Marks]**

**Q2. Classify air pollution sources based on their spatial distribution.**

**Model Answer:**
Based on spatial distribution, air pollution sources are classified as:
1. **Stationary (Point) Sources** — Fixed-location emitters (e.g., factory stack, power plant chimney)
2. **Mobile Sources** — Moving emitters (e.g., cars, aircraft)
3. **Area Sources** — Distributed emitters over a region (e.g., agricultural fields, gasoline stations)
4. **Line Sources** — Continuous linear emitters (e.g., highways, railway corridors)

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 1 | 14 Marks]**

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Classify air pollutants in detail based on their origin and chemical nature, with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the difference between natural and anthropogenic sources of air pollution with at least six examples each. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Classification Based on Origin and Chemical Nature:**

**By Origin:**
- **Primary Pollutants** — emitted directly (CO, SO₂, NOₓ, PM, VOCs, Lead)
- **Secondary Pollutants** — formed by atmospheric reactions (O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄ mist, HNO₃)

**By Chemical Nature:**

| Category | Examples | Source |
|----------|----------|--------|
| Organic | Benzene, Formaldehyde, PAHs | Vehicles, refineries |
| Inorganic | SO₂, NOₓ, CO, CO₂, H₂S | Combustion, industry |
| Biological | Bacteria, pollen, spores | Biogenic |

**By Physical State:**

- **Gaseous:** CO, SO₂, NO₂, O₃, H₂S
- **Particulate:** Dust, smoke, fumes, mist
- **Aerosols:** Combined phase

**(b) Natural vs. Anthropogenic Sources:**

**Natural Sources:**
1. Volcanic eruptions — SO₂, CO₂, ash, PM
2. Forest fires — CO, CO₂, PM
3. Dust storms — wind-blown soil particulates
4. Sea spray — NaCl aerosols
5. Pollen dispersal — biological particulates
6. Microbial decay in wetlands — CH₄, H₂S

**Anthropogenic Sources:**
1. Coal-fired power plants — SO₂, NOₓ, PM, CO₂
2. Motor vehicles — CO, NOₓ, VOCs, lead
3. Refineries — H₂S, VOCs, hydrocarbons
4. Cement industry — cement dust (PM), CO₂
5. Waste incinerators — dioxins, furans, HCl
6. Agricultural burning — CO, PM, CO₂

---

### Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With neat sketches, explain the classification of particulate matter based on aerodynamic diameter and their health implications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the various types of stationary and mobile sources of air pollution with real-world examples. **[7 Marks]**

### Model Solution for Question B

**(a) PM Classification by Aerodynamic Diameter:**

| Category | Diameter (µm) | Penetration Depth in Body |
|----------|--------------|----------------------------|
| TSP | ≤ 50 | Nose, throat (filtered by nasal hair) |
| PM₁₀ (Thoracic) | ≤ 10 | Trachea, bronchi |
| PM₂.₅ (Respirable) | ≤ 2.5 | Alveoli (gas exchange region) |
| Ultrafine (UFP) | < 0.1 | Bloodstream, systemic |

**Health Implications:**
- **TSP** — Rhinitis, irritation
- **PM₁₀** — Asthma, bronchitis
- **PM₂.₅** — COPD, lung cancer, cardiovascular mortality
- **UFP** — Systemic inflammation, neurotoxicity

**Standard Values:**
- WHO 2021: **PM₂.₅ = 5 µg/m³ (annual)**
- CPCB NAAQS India: **PM₂.₅ = 40 µg/m³ (24-hr)**

**(b) Stationary and Mobile Sources:**

**Stationary (Point) Sources** — Emitted from a fixed location:
- Thermal power plants
- Smelters and refineries
- Cement kilns
- Incinerators
- Industrial boilers

**Mobile Sources** — Emitted from moving platforms:
- Road vehicles (cars, trucks, buses)
- Aircraft
- Ships and boats
- Trains (diesel locomotives)
- Non-road mobile machinery (construction equipment)

**Control Difference:** Stationary sources are addressed using tall stacks, ESPs, scrubbers, and cyclones. Mobile sources require catalytic converters, fuel reformulation, and electric vehicle transition.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Do NOT** list pollutants without classifying them — examiners deduct marks for unstructured answers.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse** primary/secondary with natural/anthropogenic — these are **two independent classification axes**.
> 3. **Always give examples** — a classification without examples gets only 50% marks.
> 4. **Use headings and tables** — long prose answers lose marks even if content is correct.
> 5. For **PM questions**, remember that **PM₂.₅ is a subset of PM₁₀**, NOT the other way around.
> 6. **Units matter** — µg/m³ vs. ppm. Examiners deduct for wrong units in numerical problems.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> - **Definition:** Air pollution = harmful substances in atmosphere causing damage to health, environment, or property
> - **Two Major Source Categories:** Natural (volcanoes, dust, pollen) vs. Anthropogenic (combustion, industry, vehicles)
> - **Four Spatial Source Types:** Point (Stationary), Mobile, Area, Line
> - **Primary Pollutants:** CO, SO₂, NOₓ, PM, VOCs, Pb — emitted directly
> - **Secondary Pollutants:** O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, HNO₃ — formed in atmosphere
> - **PM Hierarchy:** TSP > PM₁₀ > PM₂.₅ > UFP (each is a subset of the larger)
> - **PM₂.₅ NAAQS (India):** 40 µg/m³ (24-hr) | WHO: 5 µg/m³ (annual)
> - **Conversion at 25°C, 1 atm:** 1 ppm = MW × 40.9 µg/m³
> - **Three Stationary Source Sub-Types:** Tall stack, low stack, area
> - **Three Emission Patterns:** Continuous, Intermittent, Fugitive
> - **Noise Major Sources:** Traffic (dominant urban), industrial, aircraft, construction, community
> - **Sound Pressure Level Formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(P/P_0)$ dB, with $P_0 = 20 \, µPa$
> - **Ozone is NEVER primary** — always secondary (photochemical)
> - **Lead** is a primary pollutant with no significant natural source in urban areas
> - **PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)** is a key photochemical smog component
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Combustion Processes and Pollutant Emission
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Combustion Processes and Pollutant Emission # MODULE 1: COMBUSTION PROCESSES AND POLLUTANT EMISSION ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Combustion** is a rapid exothermic chemical reaction between a **fuel** (hydrocarbon-based substance) and an **oxidizer** (typically atmospheric oxygen, O₂) that releases heat, light, and produces combustion products (CO₂, H₂O, N₂) along with various air pollutants. In the context of **Air Pollution Control Engineering**, combustion is the *single largest anthropogenic source* of primary air pollutants such as **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**, **Nitrogen Oxides (NOₓ)**, **Sulfur Dioxide (SO₂)**, **Particulate Matter (PM)**, and **Unburnt Hydrocarbons (UHC/VOC)**. > [!NOTE] > **Stoichiometric Combustion**: The idealized combustion process in which *exactly* the theoretical amount of oxygen is supplied to completely burn the fuel, with no oxygen left over and no fuel remaining unburnt. In reality, this is virtually impossible — every real flame operates in *sub-stoichiometric* (fuel-rich) or *excess-air* (fuel-lean) conditions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition) Think of combustion like **lighting a campfire in a closed tent**: - If you have **too little air** (oxygen), the wood smolders — black smoke pours out, soot covers the tent, and unburnt carbon particles fill the air. This is **fuel-rich combustion**, producing **CO, soot, and hydrocarbons**. - If you have **just the right amount of air**, the fire burns cleanly with a bright yellow-orange flame — high heat, minimal smoke. This is **stoichiometric combustion**. - If you **over-blow the fire** (too much air), the flame becomes short and bluish, heat is wasted (cooled by excess N₂), and although smoke is reduced, the flame temperature drops. This is **excess-air combustion** — used in practice to *suppress* smoke but it creates **thermal NOₓ** due to high N₂ + O₂ dissociation at peak flame temperature. > [!TIP] > **Engineering trade-off**: More excess air ⇒ Less CO & soot BUT more NOₓ. Less excess air ⇒ Less NOₓ BUT more CO & soot. The art of combustion design is finding the **optimal excess air window** (typically 5–25 % for most industrial burners). ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants > [!NOTE] > - **Molecular weight of air** = **28.97 kg/kmol** (≈ **29 kg/kmol** for engineering use) > - **Mass ratio of air to oxygen** = **4.773 : 1** (since air is 21 % O₂ and 79 % N₂ by volume; 23.2 % O₂ and 76.8 % N₂ by mass) > - **1 kmol of any ideal gas at STP** = **22.414 m³** (at 0 °C, 101.325 kPa) or **24.04 m³** at 25 °C > - **Lower Heating Value (LHV)** of methane ≈ **50 MJ/kg**; gasoline ≈ **44 MJ/kg**; coal ≈ **24–32 MJ/kg** > - **Adiabatic Flame Temperature** for stoichiometric methane-air flame ≈ **2223 K** (≈ 1950 °C) > - **Stoichiometric A/F (mass basis) for gasoline** = **14.7 : 1** --- ## 2. Global Combustion Picture (Why It Matters) > [!IMPORTANT] > According to the **WHO 2024 Air Quality Report** and **IPCC AR6** findings, **> 85 %** of ambient particulate matter (PM₂.₅) and **> 70 %** of NOₓ emissions in urban Indian cities originate from combustion sources — vehicular exhaust, thermal power plants, industrial boilers, and biomass burning. Hence the *first principle* of air pollution control is **understanding and modifying the combustion process itself** before applying end-of-pipe treatments (scrubbers, ESPs, catalytic converters). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Stoichiometry of Combustion The combustion of a generic hydrocarbon fuel **CₓHᵧSᵤNᵥOᵥ** (e.g., methane CH₄, octane C₈H₁₈) with oxygen proceeds as: $$ \begin{aligned} C_x H_y S_u N_v O_w \; + \; \left(x + \frac{y}{4} + u - \frac{w}{2}\right) O_2 \; \longrightarrow \; x\,CO_2 \; + \; \frac{y}{2}\,H_2O \; + \; u\,SO_2 \; + \; \frac{v}{2}\,N_2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step-by-step logic of the coefficient of O₂: - **Each C atom** → combines with 1 O atom (i.e., ½ O₂ molecule) to form CO₂ - **Each pair of H atoms** → combines with ½ O atom (i.e., ¼ O₂ molecule) to form H₂O - **Each S atom** → combines with 1 O atom to form SO₂ - **Any O already in the fuel** (w) → reduces external O₂ demand So the **stoichiometric oxygen required per mole of fuel** is: $$ \begin{aligned} n_{O_2,\,stoich} = x + \frac{y}{4} + u - \frac{w}{2} \end{aligned} $$ ### 2.2 Stoichiometric Air Requirement Since air contains only **21 % O₂ by volume (and 79 % N₂)**: $$ \begin{aligned} n_{air,\,stoich} = 4.773 \times n_{O_2,\,stoich} \quad \text{(in kmol air per kmol fuel)} \end{aligned} $$ The factor **4.773** arises because for every 1 kmol of O₂, we must supply 1/0.21 = **4.762 kmol of air** (the 0.21 is volume fraction of O₂ in dry air). The slightly different value **4.773** uses the mass-based ratio of air to oxygen (≈ 4.31 / 0.904 = 4.773 for engineering approximation). ### 2.3 Equivalence Ratio & Excess Air Two dimensionless numbers describe departure from stoichiometry: $$ \begin{aligned} \phi &= \frac{(F/A)_{actual}}{(F/A)_{stoich}} \quad \text{(Equivalence Ratio)} \\[4pt] \lambda &= \frac{1}{\phi} = \frac{(A/F)_{actual}}{(A/F)_{stoich}} \quad \text{(Relative Air-Fuel Ratio)} \\[6pt] \%\,\text{Excess Air} &= \frac{A_{actual} - A_{stoich}}{A_{stoich}} \times 100 = (\lambda - 1) \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ - **φ < 1** (λ > 1, excess air) → Lean mixture (more air than needed) - **φ = 1** (λ = 1) → Stoichiometric - **φ > 1** (λ < 1) → Rich mixture (fuel in excess) ### 2.4 Air-Fuel Ratio (A/F) on Mass Basis For converting molar A/F to mass A/F (often used in IC engines and boiler practice): $$ \begin{aligned} (AF)_{mass} = \frac{m_{air}}{m_{fuel}} = (AF)_{mol} \times \frac{M_{air}}{M_{fuel}} = 4.773 \times \frac{28.97}{M_{fuel}} \times n_{O_2,\,stoich} \end{aligned} $$ For **methane (CH₄)**: M_fuel = 16 kg/kmol, n_O₂ = 1 $(AF)_{mass} = 4.773 \times 28.97 / 16 \times 1 ≈ 17.16 : 1$ (stoichiometric) For **octane (C₈H₁₈)**: M_fuel = 114 kg/kmol, n_O₂ = 12.5 $(AF)_{mass} ≈ 15.13 : 1$ (stoichiometric) ### 2.5 Combustion Efficiency $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{comb} = \frac{\text{Heat released by actual combustion}}{\text{Heat that would be released by complete combustion}} \times 100\,\% \end{aligned} $$ Alternative form using flue gas analysis: $$ \begin{aligned} \eta_{comb} = 100 - \left( \frac{L \times (t_g - t_a)}{\text{GCV of fuel}} \right) \,\% \end{aligned} $$ where **L** = mass of dry flue gas per kg of fuel, **t_g** = flue gas temperature, **t_a** = ambient temperature. ### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD FORMULAS — KTU 2024 EXAM PRIORITY TABLE** | # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Stoichiometric O₂ | $n_{O_2} = x + \frac{y}{4} + u - \frac{w}{2}$ | kmol O₂ / kmol fuel | | 2 | Stoichiometric Air (mol) | $n_{air} = 4.773 \cdot n_{O_2}$ | kmol air / kmol fuel | | 3 | Mass of Air per kg Fuel | $m_{air} = 4.773 \cdot n_{O_2} \cdot \frac{28.97}{M_{fuel}}$ | kg air / kg fuel | | 4 | Excess Air | $\%EA = \frac{A_{act} - A_{stoich}}{A_{stoich}} \times 100$ | dimensionless % | | 5 | Equivalence Ratio | $\phi = (F/A)_{act} / (F/A)_{stoich}$ | dimensionless | | 6 | Combustion Efficiency | $\eta_{comb} = 1 - \frac{L \cdot C_{pg} \cdot (t_g - t_a)}{GCV}$ | dimensionless | | 7 | Adiabatic Flame Temp | $T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \frac{\sum n_i (h_f^\circ)_i}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}}$ | Kelvin | | 8 | Orsat O₂ method for EA | $\%EA = \frac{O_2 - \frac{CO}{2}}{0.264 \cdot N_2 - (O_2 - \frac{CO}{2})} \times 100$ | % | | 9 | CO formation index | $\text{CO} \propto \frac{1}{\phi^2}$ (approx) | ppm dry | | 10 | Thermal NOₓ (Zeldovich) | $\frac{d[NO]}{dt} = k_f [O][N_2] - k_r [NO][N]$ | mol/m³·s | > [!NOTE] > The Orsat formula in row 8 uses a simplified version: where O₂ and CO are from Orsat analysis (% by volume on dry basis) and N₂ is determined by difference. Different textbooks give slight variations; always specify the version used in the exam. ### 2.7 Classification of Pollutants from Combustion > [!NOTE] > **Six Major Pollutants from Combustion Sources:** > 1. **CO** — Carbon Monoxide (incomplete combustion) > 2. **NOₓ** — Nitrogen Oxides (NO + NO₂) (thermal, fuel, prompt mechanisms) > 3. **SOₓ** — Sulfur Oxides (SO₂ + SO₃) (sulfur in fuel) > 4. **PM** — Particulate Matter (soot, ash, condensed metals) > 5. **VOC/HC** — Volatile Organic Compounds / Hydrocarbons > 6. **CO₂** — Carbon Dioxide (greenhouse gas, complete combustion) ### 2.8 Why This Matters in Real Engineering - **Power plant boilers**: A/F tuning saves millions of rupees in fuel cost AND reduces pollution penalties (e.g., India's **Coal Cess** and **CEPI** Carbon Emission Performance Index). - **Automotive catalytic converters**: Use *lambda sensor* (λ = 1.000 ± 0.005 control) to keep the engine right at stoichiometry so the three-way catalyst can simultaneously destroy CO, HC, and NOₓ. - **Industrial furnaces**: Run on the *minimum excess air* (e.g., 3–5 %) to maximize efficiency, but only if the fuel is well-mixed and atomized. - **Biomass cookstoves**: Operate at low efficiency (10–20 %) with massive CO and PM emissions — the *WHO* estimates household air pollution causes **3.2 million premature deaths/year**. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Solved Example 1: Stoichiometric Air for Methane Combustion (Full Derivation) > **Problem**: A household natural gas burner uses pure methane (CH₄). Calculate: > (a) the stoichiometric mass of air required per kg of CH₄ > (b) the stoichiometric mass of air required per kg of CH₄ if the supply is at 30 % excess air > (c) the volumetric flow rate of air required (Nm³/hr) for 5 kg/hr of CH₄ at 25 % excess air ### Step 1 — Write the balanced stoichiometric reaction $$ \begin{aligned} CH_4 + 2\,O_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O \quad \text{(stoichiometric)} \end{aligned} $$ The reaction is balanced: 1 C, 4 H, 4 O on each side. ### Step 2 — Identify moles - 1 kmol CH₄ requires **2 kmol O₂** - Molar mass of CH₄ = 12 + 4(1) = **16 kg/kmol** - Molar mass of O₂ = **32 kg/kmol** ### Step 3 — Mass of O₂ per kg of CH₄ $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Mass O}_2 = \frac{2 \;\text{kmol O}_2 \times 32 \;\text{kg/kmol}}{1 \;\text{kmol CH}_4 \times 16 \;\text{kg/kmol CH}_4} = \frac{64}{16} = 4.0 \;\text{kg O}_2 / \text{kg CH}_4 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Mass of air (stoichiometric) Since air is 23.2 % O₂ by mass: $$ \begin{aligned} m_{air,\,stoich} = \frac{m_{O_2}}{0.232} = \frac{4.0}{0.232} = 17.24 \;\text{kg air / kg CH}_4 \end{aligned} $$ (Equivalently, using the molar route: $n_{air} = 2 \times 4.773 = 9.546$ kmol air / kmol CH₄; $m_{air} = 9.546 \times 28.97 / 1 = 276.6$ kg air / kmol CH₄ = 17.29 kg/kg — small rounding difference.) ### Step 5 — Air required at 30 % excess air $$ \begin{aligned} m_{air,\,actual} = m_{air,\,stoich} \times (1 + 0.30) = 17.24 \times 1.30 = 22.41 \;\text{kg air / kg CH}_4 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Volumetric flow rate (Part c) At 25 % excess air, $m_{air} = 17.24 \times 1.25 = 21.55$ kg air / kg CH₄ For **5 kg/hr** CH₄: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{m}_{air} = 5 \times 21.55 = 107.75 \;\text{kg/hr} \end{aligned} $$ Using **ideal gas law at STP (0 °C, 101.325 kPa)** — molar volume = 22.414 m³/kmol: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{V}_{air} = \frac{\dot{m}_{air}}{M_{air}} \times 22.414 = \frac{107.75}{28.97} \times 22.414 = 3.72 \times 22.414 = 83.4 \;\text{Nm}^3/\text{hr} \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > **"Nm³"** stands for **Normal Cubic Meter** — volume of gas measured at *Normal* conditions (0 °C, 1 atm). This is the standard way to express gas flow rates in combustion engineering. **Final Answer Summary**: - (a) **17.24 kg air / kg CH₄** (stoichiometric) - (b) **22.41 kg air / kg CH₄** (at 30 % EA) - (c) **83.4 Nm³/hr** (at 25 % EA, 5 kg/hr CH₄) --- ## 3.2 Solved Example 2: Excess Air from Orsat Flue Gas Analysis > **Problem**: A furnace burns fuel oil with the following dry flue gas Orsat analysis: CO₂ = 12 %, O₂ = 4 %, CO = 1 %, N₂ = 83 % (by volume, dry basis). Calculate: > (a) % excess air supplied > (b) Air-Fuel ratio (mass basis) if fuel oil has formula CH₁.₈ ### Step 1 — Verify the flue gas adds to 100 % 12 + 4 + 1 + 83 = 100 % ✓ ### Step 2 — Apply the excess-air formula from dry flue gas The general nitrogen-balance method: Let the molar A/F ratio on the *supplied* basis be $n_{air}/n_{fuel}$. Nitrogen in flue gas = $0.79 \times n_{air,\,supplied}$ (per kmol fuel). But the dry flue gas is normalized: $n_{N_2} / (n_{CO_2} + n_{O_2} + n_{CO} + n_{N_2}) = 0.83$. A simpler form for **hydrocarbon fuels (no fuel-bound nitrogen)**: $$ \begin{aligned} \%EA = \frac{O_2\,\% - 0.5 \cdot CO\,\%}{0.264 \cdot N_2\,\% - (O_2\,\% - 0.5 \cdot CO\,\%)} \times 100 \end{aligned} $$ > **Derivation of 0.264 factor**: Stoichiometric combustion of hydrocarbons gives dry flue gas with $\approx 79\,\%\,N_2$ when $21\,\%\,O_2$ was in the air. The 0.264 comes from: $(21/79) \times (0.5) \approx 0.133$ for half the O₂ going to N₂... (different sources give slightly different coefficients — most common version: 0.264). ### Step 3 — Plug in values $$ \begin{aligned} \%EA = \frac{4 - 0.5 \times 1}{0.264 \times 83 - (4 - 0.5)} \times 100 = \frac{3.5}{21.91 - 3.5} \times 100 = \frac{3.5}{18.41} \times 100 = 19.0\,\% \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Calculate stoichiometric O₂ for CH₁.₈ $$ \begin{aligned} n_{O_2,\,stoich} = 1 + \frac{1.8}{4} - 0 = 1.45 \;\text{kmol O}_2 / \text{kmol fuel} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Mass A/F ratio Molar mass of CH₁.₈ = 12 + 1.8 = 13.8 kg/kmol Stoichiometric mass of air: $$ \begin{aligned} m_{air,\,stoich} = \frac{4.773 \times 1.45 \times 28.97}{13.8} = \frac{200.4}{13.8} = 14.52 \;\text{kg/kg} \end{aligned} $$ Actual A/F (with 19 % EA): $$ \begin{aligned} (AF)_{actual} = 14.52 \times 1.19 = 17.28 \;\text{kg air / kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer**: - (a) **Excess air ≈ 19 %** - (b) **A/F ratio = 17.28 kg/kg** --- ## 3.3 Solved Example 3: Adiabatic Flame Temperature (Theoretical Maximum) > **Problem**: Calculate the adiabatic flame temperature of stoichiometric methane-air combustion at 25 °C and 1 atm, assuming complete combustion and constant specific heats. ### Step 1 — Reaction (from Section 3.1) $$ \begin{aligned} CH_4 + 2\,O_2 + 2 \times 3.76\,N_2 \longrightarrow CO_2 + 2\,H_2O + 7.52\,N_2 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 2 — Energy balance (adiabatic, no work) $$ \begin{aligned} \sum n_i (h_f^\circ)_{reactants} = \sum n_i (h_f^\circ)_{products} + \sum n_i C_{p,i}(T_{ad} - T_{ref}) \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Standard enthalpies of formation (at 298 K) | Species | $h_f^\circ$ (kJ/kmol) | |---|---| | CH₄ (g) | −74 850 | | O₂ | 0 | | N₂ | 0 | | CO₂ | −393 520 | | H₂O (g) | −241 820 | ### Step 4 — Heat released per kmol CH₄ $$ \begin{aligned} Q = [(−393 520) + 2(−241 820)] - [(−74 850) + 0 + 0] = −877 160 + 74 850 = −802 310 \;\text{kJ/kmol CH}_4 \end{aligned} $$ (Magnitude: **Lower Heating Value of CH₄ ≈ 802 310 kJ/kmol = 50.14 MJ/kg** ✓ matches literature value) ### Step 5 — Product species mole fractions (per kmol CH₄) - CO₂: 1 kmol, $C_p \approx 44$ kJ/(kmol·K) - H₂O: 2 kmol, $C_p \approx 36$ kJ/(kmol·K) - N₂: 7.52 kmol, $C_p \approx 29$ kJ/(kmol·K) $$ \begin{aligned} \sum n_i C_{p,i} = 1(44) + 2(36) + 7.52(29) = 44 + 72 + 218.1 = 334.1 \;\text{kJ/K} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Solve for T_ad $$ \begin{aligned} T_{ad} = T_{ref} + \frac{Q}{\sum n_i C_{p,i}} = 298 + \frac{802 310}{334.1} = 298 + 2401 = 2699 \;\text{K} \end{aligned} $$ > [!TIP] > **Note**: Using *temperature-dependent* $C_p$ values (which increase with T) gives a *lower* $T_{ad}$ of approximately **2223 K**. The constant-$C_p$ assumption always over-predicts flame temperature by ~20 %. Both numbers are acceptable in KTU exams; specify your assumption. **Final Answer**: $T_{ad} \approx 2700$ K (constant $C_p$) or **2223 K** (variable $C_p$). # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Combustion → Pollutants This diagram maps the **inputs, transformation processes, outputs, and pollutant formation pathways** in a generic combustion system. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Fuel Storage

Atmospheric or Forced or Induced Draft] --> D[Air Preheater

Pilot Flame or Spark] --> E E --> G[High-Temperature Reaction Zone
1500 to 2500 K] G --> H[Flue Gas Stream
N2, CO2, H2O, O2] G --> I[Pollutant Formation Zone] I --> J[CO Formation
Fuel Rich Zones, Quenching] I --> K[Thermal NOx
Zeldovich Mechanism above 1800 K] I --> L[Fuel NOx

Boiler Tubes or HRSG] P --> Q[Air Pollution Control Device
ESP, Scrubber, Baghouse, SCR] J --> Q K --> Q L --> Q M --> Q N --> Q O --> Q Q --> R[Cleaned Flue Gas to Stack] Q --> S[Byproducts
Ash, Sludge, Spent Catalyst] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram: Effect of Air-Fuel Ratio on Pollutant Emissions This schematic shows the **classical trade-off curve** that every combustion engineer must understand. ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Equivalence Ratio phi] --> B[phi less than 1
Lean or Excess Air] A --> C[phi equals 1
Stoichiometric] A --> D[phi greater than 1
Rich or Fuel Excess] B --> E[Low CO

High HC
