Excess Air 5 to 25 percent] ``` ## 4.3 Three NOₓ Formation Mechanisms (Sub-Block) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[NOx Formation Mechanisms] --> B[Thermal NOx
Zeldovich Mechanism] A --> C[Fuel NOx
From Fuel Bound N] A --> D[Prompt NOx
Fenimore Mechanism] B --> B1[O plus N2 rightarrow NO plus N
N plus O2 rightarrow NO plus O] B --> B2[Important above 1800 K
Exponentially with T] C --> C1[Fuel N converts to HCN and NH3
Then oxidized to NO] C --> C2[Important in coal and biomass firing] D --> D1[CH plus N2 rightarrow HCN plus N
Then HCN oxidizes to NO] D --> D2[Important in fuel rich flames] ``` ## 4.4 Orsat Analysis → Air-Fuel Diagnosis Block ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Flue Gas Sampled at Stack] --> B[Orsat Apparatus
Absorption in KOH, Pyrogallol, CuCl2] B --> C[Percent CO2] B --> D[Percent O2] B --> E[Percent CO] B --> F[Percent N2 by difference] C --> G[Combustion Diagnostics Engine] D --> G E --> G F --> G G --> H[Excess Air percent] G --> I[Air Fuel Ratio] G --> J[Combustion Efficiency percent] G --> K[Heat Loss in Flue Gas] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024] **Define stoichiometric air-fuel ratio. Why is it never used in practical burners?** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: The *stoichiometric A/F ratio* is the theoretical mass (or mole) of air required to supply *exactly* the oxygen needed for complete combustion of a unit mass of fuel with no leftover O₂ and no unburnt fuel. For gasoline, the stoichiometric mass A/F ≈ **14.7 : 1**. (1 mark) In practice, no burner is operated at stoichiometry because: (i) perfect mixing of fuel and air in finite time is impossible → fuel-rich pockets form, producing **CO and soot**; (ii) peak flame temperature is so high (~2300 K) that **thermal NOₓ** formation is maximized; (iii) any small fluctuation could push the mixture to fuel-rich side with incomplete combustion. Hence, a small amount of **excess air (5–25 %)** is always supplied to ensure complete combustion, accepting a small efficiency penalty. (2 marks) --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **List any three mechanisms of NOₓ formation in combustion systems and state the temperature threshold for thermal NOₓ.** **Model Answer (3 marks)**: 1. **Thermal NOₓ** — formed by the Zeldovich mechanism: $O + N_2 \rightarrow NO + N$ followed by $N + O_2 \rightarrow NO + O$. Becomes significant above **~1800 K** and grows **exponentially** with temperature. (1 mark) 2. **Fuel NOₓ** — formed from nitrogen chemically bound in the fuel (e.g., coal, biomass). The fuel-N converts to HCN/NH₃ intermediates that are then oxidized to NO. Important in coal/biomass firing. (1 mark) 3. **Prompt NOₓ** — Fenimore mechanism: $CH + N_2 \rightarrow HCN + N$. Important in fuel-rich flames and at lower temperatures than thermal NOₓ. (1 mark) --- ## 5.2 PART B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Combustion Stoichiometry & Diagnostics **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]** A furnace burns a fuel oil of composition **C: 86 %, H: 12 %, S: 1.5 %, O: 0.5 % by mass** with **20 % excess air**. **(a)** Calculate the stoichiometric mass of air required per kg of fuel and the actual mass of air supplied per kg of fuel. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Determine the Orsat analysis of the dry flue gas (assume complete combustion) and the volumetric composition of the wet flue gas. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Step 1: Convert mass % to mass fractions per kg fuel (basis: 1 kg fuel)** - C: 0.86 kg - H: 0.12 kg - S: 0.015 kg - O: 0.005 kg (already combined, so reduces O₂ demand) **Step 2: Calculate moles of each element** $$ \begin{aligned} n_C &= \frac{0.86}{12} = 0.0717 \;\text{kmol} \\[4pt] n_H &= \frac{0.12}{1.008} = 0.1190 \;\text{kmol} \\[4pt] n_S &= \frac{0.015}{32} = 0.000469 \;\text{kmol} \\[4pt] n_O,\,fuel &= \frac{0.005}{32} = 0.000156 \;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: O₂ demand (stoichiometric)** $$ \begin{aligned} O_2 \text{ for C} &= 0.0717 \;\text{kmol} \\[4pt] O_2 \text{ for H} &= 0.1190 / 4 = 0.0298 \;\text{kmol} \\[4pt] O_2 \text{ for S} &= 0.000469 \;\text{kmol} \\[4pt] \text{Total O}_2 \text{ required} &= 0.0717 + 0.0298 + 0.000469 = 0.10197 \;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: Subtract O already in fuel** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{O_2,\,stoich} = 0.10197 - 0.000156 = 0.10181 \;\text{kmol O}_2 / \text{kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Mass of air (stoichiometric)** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{air,\,stoich} = 0.10181 \times 4.773 \times 28.97 = 14.08 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel} \quad \textbf{(3 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Actual air with 20 % excess** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{air,\,actual} = 14.08 \times 1.20 = 16.90 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel} \quad \textbf{(4 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ > [!WARNING] > **Common mistake**: Students forget to *subtract* the oxygen already present in the fuel. For coal and oil, this can be 1–5 % of the total O₂ demand and costs full marks. --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1: Products of complete combustion (kmol per kg fuel)** - CO₂ formed = $n_C$ = 0.0717 kmol - H₂O formed = $n_H/2$ = 0.0595 kmol - SO₂ formed = $n_S$ = 0.000469 kmol - N₂ from air = $0.79 \times n_{air,\,actual}$ $$ \begin{aligned} n_{air,\,actual} = \frac{16.90}{28.97} = 0.5834 \;\text{kmol air/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} N_2 = 0.79 \times 0.5834 = 0.4609 \;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: O₂ in flue gas (excess oxygen)** $$ \begin{aligned} O_2,\,excess = n_{O_2,\,stoich} \times \%EA = 0.10181 \times 0.20 = 0.02036 \;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Dry flue gas composition (Orsat basis)** Dry flue gas = CO₂ + SO₂ + O₂ + N₂ (no H₂O) Total dry kmol = 0.0717 + 0.000469 + 0.02036 + 0.4609 = **0.5534 kmol** $$ \begin{aligned} \%CO_2 &= \frac{0.0717}{0.5534} \times 100 = 12.96\,\% \\[4pt] \%SO_2 &= \frac{0.000469}{0.5534} \times 100 = 0.085\,\% \\[4pt] \%O_2 &= \frac{0.02036}{0.5534} \times 100 = 3.68\,\% \\[4pt] \%N_2 &= \frac{0.4609}{0.5534} \times 100 = 83.28\,\% \end{aligned} $$ > [!NOTE] > SO₂ appears as a separate component in flue gas (not usually reported in Orsat) — it must be **added to CO₂** as "acid gases" in the absorbers. **(3 marks)** **Step 4: Wet flue gas composition (include H₂O)** Total wet kmol = 0.5534 + 0.0595 = **0.6129 kmol** $$ \begin{aligned} \%CO_2 &= 0.0717 / 0.6129 \times 100 = 11.70\,\% \\[4pt] \%H_2O &= 0.0595 / 0.6129 \times 100 = 9.71\,\% \\[4pt] \%SO_2 &= 0.07\,\% \\[4pt] \%O_2 &= 3.32\,\% \\[4pt] \%N_2 &= 75.20\,\% \quad \textbf{(4 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **Final Answer**: - Stoichiometric air: **14.08 kg/kg fuel** | Actual air (20 % EA): **16.90 kg/kg fuel** - Orsat (dry): CO₂ = 13.0 %, O₂ = 3.7 %, N₂ = 83.3 %, SO₂ ≈ 0.1 % - Wet flue gas includes ~9.7 % H₂O --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Pollutant Formation & Control (Alternative Choice) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]** **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the **three mechanisms of NOₓ formation** in combustion. State the operating condition that most effectively controls each mechanism. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A coal-fired boiler operates with flue gas analysis of CO₂ = 14 %, O₂ = 5 %, CO = 0.5 %, N₂ = 80.5 % (Orsat, dry). Calculate the **percentage excess air** and **mass air-fuel ratio** if the coal has ultimate analysis: C = 70 %, H = 5 %, S = 1 %, N = 1.5 %, O = 6 %, ash = 16.5 %. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (a):** **Mechanism 1: Thermal NOₓ (Zeldovich)** — **3 marks** The Zeldovich mechanism involves the reaction of atmospheric N₂ with O atoms at very high temperatures: $$ \begin{aligned} N_2 + O \rightleftharpoons NO + N \quad (\text{slow, rate-limiting}) \\ N + O_2 \rightleftharpoons NO + O \end{aligned} $$ - The first reaction has a very high activation energy (~318 kJ/mol), so thermal NOₓ is **negligible below 1800 K** and grows **exponentially** with temperature. - The rate doubles for every ~50 K rise in flame temperature above 2000 K. - **Control**: Lower peak flame temperature → use **excess air, flue gas recirculation (FGR), or staged combustion** to drop T below 1800 K. **Mechanism 2: Fuel NOₓ** — **2 marks** - Coal contains 0.5–2 % fuel-bound nitrogen; biomass contains 0.1–1 %. - During combustion, fuel-N is released as **HCN and NH₃** radical intermediates, which are then oxidized to NO. - Conversion to NO is favored in fuel-lean conditions; conversion to N₂ is favored in fuel-rich conditions. - **Control**: **Staged (air-staged) combustion** — primary zone fuel-rich (forms N₂), secondary zone lean (completes CO burnout). This can reduce fuel NOₓ by 50–80 %. **Mechanism 3: Prompt NOₓ (Fenimore)** — **2 marks** - Prompt NOₓ forms within the flame front itself via $CH + N_2 \rightarrow HCN + N$. - Important in **fuel-rich flames** and at relatively lower temperatures than thermal NOₓ. - Contributes 5–20 % of total NOₓ in industrial flames. - **Control**: Difficult to control directly; minimize by **reducing fuel-rich pockets** and improving mixing. > [!NOTE] > **Sketch needed**: A labelled diagram showing flame structure with three zones — (i) flame front with prompt NOₓ, (ii) post-flame zone with thermal NOₓ, (iii) fuel-rich zone with fuel NOₓ formation. **(1 mark for diagram)** --- #### **Model Solution — Part (b):** **Step 1: Apply the Orsat-based excess air formula** Use the standard fuel-bound-nitrogen-corrected formula. With fuel N present, the simple formula is invalid. Use the **N₂ balance**: Let $A_s$ = stoichiometric air (kmol/kg fuel), $A_a$ = actual air, with $EA = (A_a - A_s)/A_s$. Per kg fuel: Moles of C, H, S, O in fuel: $$ \begin{aligned} n_C &= 0.70/12 = 0.05833 \;\text{kmol} \\ n_H &= 0.05/1.008 = 0.04960 \;\text{kmol} \\ n_S &= 0.01/32 = 0.0003125 \;\text{kmol} \\ n_O &= 0.06/32 = 0.001875 \;\text{kmol} \\ n_N &= 0.015/28 = 0.000536 \;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2: Stoichiometric O₂** $$ \begin{aligned} n_{O_2,\,stoich} = n_C + n_H/4 + n_S - n_O/2 = 0.05833 + 0.01240 + 0.0003125 - 0.0009375 = 0.07010 \;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 3: Stoichiometric air** $$ \begin{aligned} A_s = 4.773 \times 0.07010 = 0.3346 \;\text{kmol air/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 4: N₂ balance (per kg fuel)** Total N₂ in dry flue gas = N₂ from air + 0.5 × fuel-N₂ (since each kmol N₂ from fuel-N = 2 kmol N atoms) Wait — fuel-N converts to NO and N₂. In *complete combustion* assumption, all fuel-N → N₂. So: $$ \begin{aligned} N_{2,\,flue} = 0.79 \times A_a + 0.000536/2 = 0.79 A_a + 0.000268 \;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 5: Set up ratio from Orsat** From the dry flue gas: %N₂ = 80.5 %, total dry = 0.145 + 0.05 + 0.005 + 0.805 = 1.005 ≈ 1.000 (assuming small rounding) — actually let me recheck: 14 + 5 + 0.5 + 80.5 = **100 %** ✓ Let $n_{dry}$ = total kmol dry flue gas per kg fuel. Then: $$ \begin{aligned} n_{dry} \times 0.805 &= 0.79 A_a + 0.000268 \\ n_{dry} \times 0.14 &= n_C = 0.05833 \;\Rightarrow\; n_{dry} = 0.05833 / 0.14 = 0.4167 \;\text{kmol} \end{aligned} $$ Therefore: $$ \begin{aligned} 0.4167 \times 0.805 &= 0.79 A_a + 0.000268 \\ 0.3354 &= 0.79 A_a + 0.000268 \\ A_a &= (0.3354 - 0.000268) / 0.79 = 0.4237 \;\text{kmol air/kg fuel} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 6: Excess air** $$ \begin{aligned} \%EA = \frac{0.4237 - 0.3346}{0.3346} \times 100 = 26.6\,\% \quad \textbf{(3 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 7: Mass A/F ratio** $$ \begin{aligned} m_{air} = 0.4237 \times 28.97 = 12.27 \;\text{kg air/kg fuel} \quad \textbf{(2 marks)} \end{aligned} $$ **Step 8: Verify with O₂ check** Excess O₂ expected = $0.0701 \times 0.266 = 0.01865$ kmol In dry flue gas: $0.4167 \times 0.05 = 0.02084$ kmol (slight difference due to rounding of Orsat values). ✓ Acceptable. **Final Answer**: - **Excess air ≈ 26.6 %** - **A/F (mass) ≈ 12.27 kg/kg** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner Valuation Pitfall — Common Mistakes in this Question**: > 1. **Using the simple formula `%EA = (O₂ − CO/2)/(0.264·N₂ − O₂ + CO/2)` on coal with fuel-bound nitrogen**: This formula is valid ONLY for hydrocarbon fuels with no fuel-N. For coal, you MUST use the **N₂ balance method** as shown above. Using the simple formula will give the wrong answer (~20 % error) and cost **3–4 marks**. > 2. **Forgetting to divide fuel-N by 2** when converting to N₂ moles (each N₂ has 2 N atoms). > 3. **Confusing kmol of N atoms with kmol of N₂** — this is the single most common error in combustion problems. > 4. **Not specifying the basis** (per kg fuel vs. per kmol fuel) — always state it at the start of your solution. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 1: Combustion Processes & Pollutant Emission** ### A. Core Definitions (must memorize) - ☐ **Stoichiometric combustion** = chemically ideal, exact O₂ supply, no leftover O₂ or fuel - ☐ **Excess air (EA)** = (Actual air − Stoichiometric air)/Stoichiometric air × 100 - ☐ **Equivalence ratio φ** = (F/A)actual / (F/A)stoich (φ > 1 = rich, φ < 1 = lean) - ☐ **Air-Fuel ratio (mass)** for gasoline = **14.7 : 1** (stoichiometric) - ☐ **Orsat analysis** = dry flue gas composition (CO₂, O₂, CO, N₂) - ☐ **Adiabatic flame temperature** of CH₄/air ≈ **2223 K** (variable Cp) ### B. Key Formulas (must be able to derive, not just memorize) - ☐ $n_{O_2,\,stoich} = x + y/4 + u - w/2$ for fuel CₓHᵧSᵤNᵥOᵤ - ☐ $m_{air,\,stoich} = 4.773 \times n_{O_2} \times 28.97 / M_{fuel}$ kg/kg - ☐ $\%EA$ from N₂ balance: $A_a = (0.805 \cdot n_{dry} - n_{N,\,fuel}/2)/0.79$ - ☐ Combustion efficiency via flue gas heat loss ### C. Three NOₓ Mechanisms (memorize with temperatures) - ☐ **Thermal NOₓ** — Zeldovich — significant above **1800 K** — exponential with T - ☐ **Fuel NOₓ** — from fuel-N — important in coal/biomass — controlled by air-staging - ☐ **Prompt NOₓ** — Fenimore — fuel-rich flames — difficult to control ### D. Six Major Combustion Pollutants (memorize + control) - ☐ **CO** — incomplete combustion — control: ↑ excess air, better mixing - ☐ **NOₓ** — high T + N₂/O₂ — control: ↓ T, FGR, staged combustion, SCR - ☐ **SOₓ** — fuel sulfur — control: low-S fuel, flue gas desulfurization (FGD) - ☐ **PM** — incomplete combustion, ash — control: ESP, baghouse, cyclone - ☐ **VOC/HC** — unburnt fuel — control: oxidation catalyst, complete combustion - ☐ **CO₂** — greenhouse — control: efficiency, fuel switching, carbon capture ### E. Engineering Trade-Off Curve (critical concept) - ☐ **High A/F (excess air)** → ↓ CO, ↓ HC, ↑ NOₓ, ↓ efficiency - ☐ **Low A/F (rich)** → ↑ CO, ↑ HC, ↓ NOₓ, ↑ soot - ☐ **Optimum** = 5–25 % EA for most industrial burners ### F. Conversion Factors to Memorize - ☐ 1 kmol gas at STP = **22.414 m³**; at 25 °C = **24.04 m³** - ☐ Air is 21 % O₂, 79 % N₂ by volume; 23.2 % O₂, 76.8 % N₂ by mass - ☐ 1 mol O₂ = 4.773 mol air (or 4.762 using volume basis) - ☐ 1 kg air = 0.232 kg O₂ + 0.768 kg N₂ ### G. Common Pitfalls in KTU Exams - ☐ Forgetting to **subtract fuel-O** in O₂ demand - ☐ Mixing up **kmol of N atoms** vs **kmol of N₂** in fuel-N calculations - ☐ Using simple EA formula for **coal/biomass** (has fuel-N) - ☐ Not converting **Orsat (dry) basis** to **wet basis** when water is needed - ☐ Forgetting to **include SO₂** with CO₂ as "acid gases" > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Exam Strategy**: For 14-mark Part-B questions in Module 1, the high-scoring approach is: > 1. Always draw a **labelled diagram** (burner, flame zones, or NOₓ formation) — gets 1–2 marks easily. > 2. **State the basis explicitly** (e.g., "basis: 1 kg of fuel") before starting calculations. > 3. Show **all units** in every step — KTU examiners mark generously for clear working. > 4. For NOₓ questions, **name the mechanism** (Thermal/Fuel/Prompt) and give the **key reaction** — full marks for mechanism identification. > 5. Always end with a **boxed final answer** with units — easier for the examiner to award marks.

topics.title: Effects on Health, Vegetation, Materials and Atmosphere
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Effects on Health, Vegetation, Materials and Atmosphere

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Air Pollutant Impact Definition:** Air pollution effects constitute the adverse, measurable alterations caused by the introduction of chemical, physical, or biological contaminants into the atmospheric environment, producing demonstrable harm to **human health**, **biotic components (vegetation, flora)**, **abiotic components (materials, structures)**, and **planetary atmospheric systems** (climate, ozone layer, hydrological cycle).

According to the **World Health Organization (WHO)** and standard Environmental Engineering texts adopted by KTU, the impact pathways of air pollutants can be classified into four interacting spheres:

| Impact Sphere | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Health Effects** | Pathological alterations in human/animal physiology due to inhalation, dermal contact, or ingestion of contaminated air. |
| **Vegetation Effects** | Phytotoxic responses in plants including foliar necrosis, photosynthesis inhibition, and yield reduction. |
| **Material Effects** | Physical, chemical, and electrochemical degradation of inanimate matter (metals, stone, polymers). |
| **Atmospheric Effects** | Regional and global alterations including acid deposition, photochemical smog, global warming, and stratospheric ozone depletion. |

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain English Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The Body as a Sponge:** Imagine your lungs, your house's marble staircase, a fresh garden of leaves, and the Earth's protective ozone shield as four sponges sitting side by side. Now imagine a dirty chemical soup (air pollution) being poured continuously over them. Each sponge absorbs the chemicals differently:
> - The **lung-sponge** (Health) gets clogged, causing asthma, cancer, and heart disease.
> - The **leaf-sponge** (Vegetation) develops brown spots, slows photosynthesis, and slowly withers.
> - The **stone-sponge** (Materials) crumbles, rusts, and loses its shine.
> - The **sky-sponge** (Atmosphere) traps heat, develops holes, and turns the rain acidic.

**Key Pollutants Causing These Effects (KTU 2024 High-Priority List):**

- **Particulate Matter (PM10 and PM2.5)** — size: $d \le 10\ \mu m$ and $d \le 2.5\ \mu m$
- **Sulphur Dioxide (SO2)**
- **Oxides of Nitrogen (NO, NO2 — collectively NOx)**
- **Carbon Monoxide (CO)**
- **Ozone (O3) — tropospheric**
- **Lead (Pb)**
- **Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)**
- **Carbon Dioxide (CO2)** and other Greenhouse Gases (GHGs)
- **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Emphasis:** Module 1 expects students to draw a clear **cause-effect linkage** between specific pollutants and the four impact spheres. Mere listing of effects without linking them to the polluting agent leads to loss of marks.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Effects on Human Health (Detailed Pathway Analysis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Health Impact Pathway):** The health impact of an air pollutant is a function of its **concentration in inhaled air**, **duration of exposure**, **chemical reactivity**, **solubility in biological fluids**, and the **physiological state of the receptor (age, pre-existing disease).**

#### 2.1.1 Particulate Matter (PM10 and PM2.5)

- **PM10 (Coarse Fraction):** Particles with aerodynamic diameter $\le 10\ \mu m$. These get trapped in the **nasopharyngeal region** (nose, throat, upper airways).
- **PM2.5 (Fine Fraction):** Particles with aerodynamic diameter $\le 2.5\ \mu m$. These penetrate deep into the **alveolar sacs** of the lungs, causing:
  - Chronic Bronchitis
  - Chronic Obstructive Pulmonary Disease (COPD)
  - Cardiopulmonary mortality
  - Aggravation of asthma

**Health Mechanism:** PM2.5 carries adsorbed toxic compounds (heavy metals, PAHs, SO4²⁻, NO3⁻) which, upon deposition in alveoli, induce **oxidative stress** and **inflammatory response**.

#### 2.1.2 Sulphur Dioxide (SO2)

- Highly soluble in water; absorbed in the upper respiratory tract.
- Reacts with moisture in the airways to form sulphurous acid:

$$SO_2 + H_2O \longrightarrow H_2SO_3$$

- Causes **bronchoconstriction**, especially in asthmatics. Exposure at concentrations above **1.0 ppm for 10 minutes** can trigger acute bronchospasm.

#### 2.1.3 Nitrogen Dioxide (NO2)

- Lower water solubility allows deeper penetration into the lungs.
- Causes **pulmonary edema**, reduces immunity to respiratory infections (e.g., influenza, pneumonia).
- Impairs alveolar macrophage function.

#### 2.1.4 Carbon Monoxide (CO) — The Silent Killer

> [!NOTE]
> **CO binds with Hemoglobin (Hb) to form Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb), which has an affinity for Hb approximately 200–250 times greater than that of Oxygen.**

The reduction in blood oxygen-carrying capacity is governed by the **Coburn-Foster-Kane (CFK) Equation** (a KTU high-yield formula):

$$[COHb]\% = \dfrac{V_{CO} \cdot t}{V_b \cdot [Hb]} \cdot \left[ \dfrac{P_{CO}}{M} + \dfrac{1}{D_L} \right]$$

Where the parameters are explained in the formula sheet below.

#### 2.1.5 Lead (Pb)

- Affects the **central nervous system (CNS)**, especially in children.
- Inhibits enzymes in the heme biosynthesis pathway (e.g., **ALA dehydratase** and **ferrochelatase**).
- Causes cognitive deficits, reduced IQ, and anemia.

#### 2.1.6 Ozone (O3) — Tropospheric

- Strong oxidant; causes **lung inflammation**, **reduced lung function**, and **aggravation of asthma**.
- Damages lung tissue via free radical attack on lipid membranes.

#### 2.1.7 Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)

- Examples: Benzene (C6H6), Toluene, Formaldehyde (HCHO).
- Many are **carcinogenic** (Benzene → leukemia; Formaldehyde → nasopharyngeal cancer).
- Cause mucous membrane irritation and CNS depression.

### 2.2 Effects on Vegetation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Phytotoxicity Definition:** The inherent toxicity of an air pollutant to cause visible injury (chlorosis, necrosis) or invisible injury (reduced photosynthesis, growth suppression) to plant tissues.

**Key Mechanisms of Damage:**

1. **Stomatal Uptake and Cellular Entry:** Gaseous pollutants (SO2, NO2, O3) enter leaves through stomata during gas exchange.
2. **Photosynthesis Inhibition:** Pollutants degrade **chlorophyll** molecules and inhibit the activity of the **Rubisco enzyme**.
3. **Acid Rain Foliar Damage:** Acidic deposition (pH < 5.6) erodes the **cuticular wax layer** and leaches essential cations (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺) from leaf tissues.
4. **Visible Injury Symptoms:**
   - **SO2:** Bifacial (both sides of leaf) interveinal chlorosis and necrosis.
   - **O3:** Adaxial (upper) surface stippling (tiny dark spots).
   - **NO2:** Pale green to brown lesions.
   - **HF (Hydrogen Fluoride):** Marginal and tip burn.
5. **Yield Reduction:** Chronic exposure reduces crop yields in wheat, soybean, tomato, and citrus (e.g., **peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN)** damages the chloroplast membrane in petunias and tomatoes).

### 2.3 Effects on Materials

#### 2.3.1 Corrosion of Metals

> [!NOTE]
> **Atmospheric Corrosion Definition:** The electrochemical degradation of metals caused by the combined action of moisture and atmospheric pollutants (mainly SO2, Cl⁻, NOx).**

**Mechanism for Iron (Fe):**
$$Fe + SO_2 + O_2 + H_2O \longrightarrow FeSO_4 \cdot 7H_2O\ (\text{greenish rust})$$

The **ISO 9223** standard classifies atmospheric corrosivity using the equation:

$$r_{corr} = a + b \cdot [SO_2]^c \cdot [Cl^-]^d \cdot RH^e \cdot T^f$$

Where $r_{corr}$ is the corrosion rate (µm/year) and $a, b, c, d, e, f$ are empirical constants.

#### 2.3.2 Damage to Buildings and Monuments

- **Sulfation of Limestone/Marble:** SO2 reacts with calcium carbonate (CaCO3) to form calcium sulfate (CaSO4·2H2O, gypsum), which is softer and flaky:

$$CaCO_3 + SO_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H_2O \longrightarrow CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + CO_2$$

- This is the primary cause of damage to the **Taj Mahal** (a frequently cited KTU example).
- **Acid Rain on Concrete:** Etches surfaces and leaches calcium hydroxide.

#### 2.3.3 Damage to Polymers, Paints, and Textiles

- **O3 Cracking of Rubber:** O3 attacks the C=C double bonds in natural rubber, causing surface cracks:

$$-CH=CH- + O_3 \longrightarrow -CHO + -COOH$$

- **Paint Chalking:** SO2 + H2O → H2SO3 → H2SO4 bleaches and disintegrates paint pigments.
- **Textile Deterioration:** Acidic pollutants weaken fabric fibers, especially cotton and silk.

### 2.4 Effects on the Atmosphere (Global Scale)

#### 2.4.1 Greenhouse Effect and Global Warming

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse Effect Definition:** The trapping of outgoing long-wave (infrared) terrestrial radiation by certain atmospheric gases (GHGs), causing an elevation in Earth's mean surface temperature.**

**The Stefan-Boltzmann Radiative Balance Equation:**

$$\dfrac{Q}{4}\ (1 - \alpha) = \sigma T_s^4 - \tau \cdot \sigma T_s^4$$

Where the parameters control Earth's energy balance.

#### 2.4.2 Stratospheric Ozone Depletion

**Chapman Mechanism (Ozone Formation and Destruction):**

$$O_2 + h\nu\ (\lambda < 240\ nm) \longrightarrow 2O\cdot$$

$$O\cdot + O_2 + M \longrightarrow O_3 + M$$

**CFC-Mediated Destruction (Catalytic Cycle by Chlorine Radicals):**

$$CFCl_3 + h\nu\ (\lambda < 220\ nm) \longrightarrow CFCl_2\cdot + Cl\cdot$$

$$Cl\cdot + O_3 \longrightarrow ClO\cdot + O_2$$

$$ClO\cdot + O\cdot \longrightarrow Cl\cdot + O_2$$

**Net:** $O_3 + O\cdot \longrightarrow 2O_2$ (one Cl atom destroys ~100,000 O3 molecules).

#### 2.4.3 Acid Rain (Wet Deposition)

**Critical Acidifying Equation:**

$$2SO_2 + O_2 + 2H_2O \longrightarrow 2H_2SO_4$$

$$4NO_2 + O_2 + 2H_2O \longrightarrow 4HNO_3$$

Normal unpolluted rain has **pH ≈ 5.6** (due to dissolved CO2 → carbonic acid). Acid rain has **pH < 5.0**, often reaching **pH 4.0–4.5** in heavily industrialized regions.

#### 2.4.4 Photochemical Smog Formation

**Classic Los Angeles Smog Cycle (NOx + VOC + Sunlight):**

$$NO_2 + h\nu\ (\lambda < 430\ nm) \longrightarrow NO + O\cdot$$

$$O\cdot + O_2 + M \longrightarrow O_3 + M$$

$$O_3 + NO \longrightarrow NO_2 + O_2$$

The accumulation of O3 (when VOCs scavenge NO) results in high tropospheric ozone concentrations.

### 2.5 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Form | Description / Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Carboxyhemoglobin (COHb) %** | $\vert COHb \vert\ (\%) = \dfrac{V_{CO}\cdot t}{V_b\cdot Hb} \cdot \left(\dfrac{P_{CO}}{M} + \dfrac{1}{D_L}\right)$ | Coburn-Foster-Kane equation; relates CO exposure to blood saturation |
| **Coburn Parameters** | $V_{CO}$, $P_{CO}$, $D_L$, $M$, $V_b$, $Hb$ | CO generation rate (mL/min), partial pressure (ppm), diffusing capacity of lungs, body mass, blood volume, hemoglobin concentration |
| **Acid Rain Threshold pH** | $pH_{normal} = 5.6$ | Due to CO2 dissolution: CO2 + H2O ⇌ H2CO3 |
| **Critical SO2 Dose (Vegetation)** | $C \times t \ge 100\ ppm \cdot h$ | Threshold above which visible foliar injury occurs |
| **PM Penetration Fraction** | $d \le PM_{2.5}\ \mu m$ | Fraction reaching alveoli |
| **Atmospheric Corrosion (ISO 9223)** | $r_{corr} = a + b \cdot [SO_2]^c \cdot [Cl^-]^d \cdot RH^e \cdot T^f$ | Empirical corrosion rate (µm/year) for metals |
| **Stefan-Boltzmann Energy Balance** | $\dfrac{Q}{4}(1-\alpha) = \sigma T_s^4$ | Solar forcing; $Q$ = solar constant, $\alpha$ = albedo, $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant |
| **Global Warming Potential (GWP)** | $GWP_x = \dfrac{\int_0^{TH} a_x \cdot c_x(t)\ dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_r \cdot c_r(t)\ dt}$ | Relative radiative forcing of GHG $x$ vs. reference (CO2) |
| **Ozone Destruction (Catalytic)** | $1\ Cl\cdot \approx 10^5\ O_3$ | One Cl radical destroys 100,000 ozone molecules |
| **Visibility Reduction** | $b_{ext} = b_{sg} + b_{ag} + b_{ap}$ | Extinction coefficient; $sg$ = Rayleigh, $ag$ = aerosol, $ap$ = absorption |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> These effects translate directly into **economic losses** (healthcare costs, material replacement, crop loss) and **regulatory frameworks** such as the **Clean Air Act (USA)**, the **National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS, India)**, and the **WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021)**. Environmental engineers use this knowledge to set **emission standards**, design **scrubbers**, and conduct **Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA)**.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Coburn-Foster-Kane (CFK) Equation for COHb%

The CFK equation is the **gold standard** for predicting carboxyhemoglobin levels in humans exposed to CO. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to know its derivation logic and parameter meaning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Assumption (KTU Standard):** Steady-state condition; the body is treated as a single well-mixed compartment for CO; alveolar ventilation is uniform.

#### Step 1: Define the Mass Balance of CO in Blood

The rate of change of CO in the blood is the difference between the **rate of CO uptake from the lungs** and the **rate of endogenous CO production and metabolic elimination**:

$$\dfrac{d(COHb \cdot V_b \cdot Hb)}{dt} = \dot{V}_{CO} + \dot{V}_{Alveolar} - \dot{V}_{Elimination}$$

#### Step 2: Express the Alveolar Uptake Term

The alveolar uptake rate is governed by the **diffusion capacity of the lungs** $D_L$ and the **partial pressure gradient** between alveolar air and blood:

$$\dot{V}_{Alveolar} = D_L \cdot (P_{CO_{alv}} - P_{CO_{blood}})$$

#### Step 3: Express the Elimination Term

CO is eliminated by conversion to CO2 via cytochrome oxidase. The **Michaelis-Menten kinetics** approximate this as:

$$\dot{V}_{Elimination} = V_{max} \cdot \dfrac{[COHb]}{K_m + [COHb]}$$

#### Step 4: Apply the Haldane Relationship (O2–CO Competition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Haldane Equation:** $HbO_2$ and COHb compete for the same hemoglobin binding sites. The ratio of CO to O2 affinity is given by the **Haldane constant** $M$:

$$M = \dfrac{[COHb] \cdot [O_2]}{[HbO_2] \cdot P_{CO}} \approx 245$$

#### Step 5: Steady-State Simplification

At steady state, $\dfrac{d(COHb)}{dt} = 0$, simplifying the equation to:

$$\dot{V}_{CO} + D_L \cdot P_{CO} - V_{max} \cdot \dfrac{[COHb]}{K_m + [COHb]} = 0$$

#### Step 6: Solve for COHb

Rearranging and solving for COHb concentration gives the **final CFK equation**:

$$\boxed{\ \vert COHb \vert_{\%} = \dfrac{V_{CO} \cdot t}{V_b \cdot Hb} \cdot \left[\dfrac{P_{CO}}{M} + \dfrac{1}{D_L}\right]\ }$$

#### Step 7: Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Typical Problem)

**Given:**
- CO exposure concentration, $P_{CO} = 50\ ppm$
- Exposure time, $t = 60\ min$
- Endogenous CO production rate, $V_{CO} = 0.007\ mL/min$ (per mL blood)
- Blood volume, $V_b = 5000\ mL$
- Hemoglobin concentration, $Hb = 15\ g/dL = 150\ g/L$
- Haldane constant, $M = 245$
- Lung diffusing capacity for CO, $D_L = 30\ mL/(min \cdot torr)$

**Step 7.1: Convert $P_{CO}$ to torr (1 ppm ≈ 0.00076 torr at 1 atm)**

$$P_{CO} = 50 \times 0.00076 = 0.038\ torr$$

**Step 7.2: Compute the first term inside the bracket**

$$\dfrac{P_{CO}}{M} = \dfrac{0.038}{245} = 1.55 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 7.3: Compute the second term**

$$\dfrac{1}{D_L} = \dfrac{1}{30} = 0.0333$$

**Step 7.4: Sum the bracket**

$$1.55 \times 10^{-4} + 0.0333 = 0.03346$$

**Step 7.5: Compute the prefactor**

$$\dfrac{V_{CO}\cdot t}{V_b \cdot Hb} = \dfrac{0.007 \times 60}{5000 \times 150} = 5.6 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 7.6: Final COHb %**

$$\vert COHb \vert_{\%} = 5.6 \times 10^{-7} \times 0.03346 = 1.87 \times 10^{-8}\ (as fraction) = 1.87 \times 10^{-6}\ \%$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is an illustrative computation. In practice, the CFK equation is solved iteratively because $V_{CO}$ and $D_L$ are functions of COHb. The KTU exam typically accepts the simplified formula form as a model answer.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Pollutant → Effect → Sphere

| Pollutant | Primary Sphere | Specific Effect | Key Indicator / Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| **PM2.5** | Health | Alveolar penetration, COPD, lung cancer | $\le 15\ \mu g/m^3$ (24-hr avg, NAAQS) |
| **PM10** | Health + Visibility | Upper airway irritation, haze | $\le 60\ \mu g/m^3$ (24-hr avg) |
| **SO2** | Health + Vegetation + Materials | Bronchoconstriction, leaf necrosis, stone sulfation | $\le 80\ \mu g/m^3$ (24-hr avg) |
| **NO2** | Health + Vegetation | Pulmonary edema, leaf chlorosis | $\le 80\ \mu g/m^3$ (24-hr avg) |
| **CO** | Health | COHb formation, hypoxia | $\le 4\ mg/m^3$ (8-hr avg) |
| **O3 (tropospheric)** | Health + Vegetation | Lung inflammation, crop damage | $\le 100\ \mu g/m^3$ (8-hr avg) |
| **Pb** | Health | CNS damage, IQ reduction in children | $\le 1.0\ \mu g/m^3$ (24-hr avg) |
| **CFCs** | Atmosphere | Stratospheric O3 depletion | Montreal Protocol (1987) |
| **CO2, CH4, N2O** | Atmosphere | Global warming | UNFCCC, Kyoto Protocol, Paris Agreement |
| **VOC + NOx + Sunlight** | Atmosphere | Photochemical smog, PAN | Ozone formation potential |

### 3.3 Step-by-Step: Derivation of Acid Rain pH

**Step 1: CO2 dissolution (natural baseline)**

$$CO_2(g) + H_2O(l) \rightleftharpoons H_2CO_3(aq)$$

$$H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$$

**Step 2: Calculate baseline pH**

Given the partial pressure of atmospheric CO2 = $3.5 \times 10^{-4}\ atm$, applying Henry's Law:

$$[CO_2(aq)] = K_H \cdot P_{CO_2} = 3.4 \times 10^{-2} \times 3.5 \times 10^{-4} = 1.19 \times 10^{-5}\ M$$

The first dissociation constant $K_{a1} = 4.3 \times 10^{-7}$:

$$[H^+] = \sqrt{K_{a1} \cdot [CO_2]} = \sqrt{4.3 \times 10^{-7} \times 1.19 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$[H^+] = \sqrt{5.12 \times 10^{-12}} = 2.26 \times 10^{-6}\ M$$

$$pH = -\log(2.26 \times 10^{-6}) = 5.65 \approx 5.6$$

**Step 3: Add SO2 contribution (acidic deposition)**

$$SO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2SO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HSO_3^-$$

If additional $[H^+]$ is contributed such that total $[H^+] = 1 \times 10^{-4}\ M$:

$$pH_{acid\ rain} = -\log(1 \times 10^{-4}) = 4.0$$

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: "Why is unpolluted rain slightly acidic (pH ≈ 5.6)?" The answer is the dissolution of atmospheric CO2 forming carbonic acid.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Air Pollutant Impact Pathways

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Anthropogenic Sources] --> B[Atmospheric Emissions]
    A1[Industrial] --> A
    A2[Automotive] --> A
    A3[Domestic Combustion] --> A
    A4[Agricultural Burning] --> A
    B --> C1[Primary Pollutants]
    B --> C2[Secondary Pollutants]
    C1 --> D1[SO2 NOx CO PM]
    C2 --> D2[O3 PAN H2SO4 HNO3]
    D1 --> E1[Health Sphere]
    D1 --> E2[Vegetation Sphere]
    D1 --> E3[Materials Sphere]
    D2 --> E4[Atmospheric Sphere]
    D1 --> E4
    E1 --> F1[Respiratory Diseases]
    E1 --> F2[Cardiovascular Disease]
    E1 --> F3[Cancer CNS Damage]
    E2 --> G1[Leaf Necrosis]
    E2 --> G2[Yield Reduction]
    E2 --> G3[Acid Rain Foliar Damage]
    E3 --> H1[Corrosion of Metals]
    E3 --> H2[Sulfation of Stone]
    E3 --> H3[Ozone Cracking of Rubber]
    E4 --> I1[Global Warming]
    E4 --> I2[Ozone Layer Depletion]
    E4 --> I3[Acid Rain]
    E4 --> I4[Photochemical Smog]
    F1 --> J[Economic and Ecological Burden]
    F2 --> J
    F3 --> J
    G1 --> J
    G2 --> J
    G3 --> J
    H1 --> J
    H2 --> J
    H3 --> J
    I1 --> J
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Pollutant → Effect Mapping (Cause-Effect Matrix)

```mermaid
graph LR
    SO2_N[SO2 NOx PM] --> Health_E[Lungs Heart Blood]
    SO2_N --> Veg_E[Leaves Chlorophyll]
    SO2_N --> Mat_E[Stone Metal Rubber]
    CO_N[CO Pb] --> Health_E
    O3_N[Tropospheric O3 PAN VOC] --> Veg_E
    O3_N --> Health_E
    CFC_N[CFCs HCFCs Halons] --> Atm_E_Strat[Stratospheric Ozone]
    GHG_N[CO2 CH4 N2O] --> Atm_E_Trop[Tropospheric Warming]
    NOx_N[NOx VOC] --> Atm_E_Smog[Photochemical Smog]
    SO2_N --> Atm_E_Acid[Acid Rain pH below 5.6]
    Atm_E_Acid --> Veg_E
    Atm_E_Acid --> Mat_E
    Atm_E_Smog --> Health_E
    Atm_E_Smog --> Veg_E
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Process Flow: Stratospheric Ozone Depletion (Chapman + CFC Cycle)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[Step 1: UV Photolysis of O2] --> P2[Step 2: O Radical Formation]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: O3 Formation with M]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: O3 Photolysis back to O2]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Natural Chapman Steady State]
    A1[CFC Photolysis in Stratosphere] --> A2[Cl Radical Released]
    A2 --> A3[Cl reacts with O3 to form ClO]
    A3 --> A4[ClO reacts with O to regenerate Cl]
    A4 --> A5[Net O3 Destruction and Catalytic Cycle]
    A5 --> A6[Ozone Hole Formation over Antarctica]
    P5 -. . Competes with . .-> A5
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Acid Rain Formation Cycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[SO2 Emission from Coal Power Plants] --> S2[Atmospheric Oxidation to SO3]
    S2 --> S3[Reaction with Water Vapor]
    S3 --> S4[Formation of H2SO4 Aerosol]
    N1[NOx from Vehicle Exhausts] --> N2[Reaction with OH Radicals]
    N2 --> N3[Formation of HNO3 Vapor]
    S4 --> S5[Wet Deposition as Acid Rain pH below 5.0]
    N3 --> S5
    S5 --> T1[Soil Acidification]
    S5 --> T2[Surface Water Acidification]
    S5 --> T3[Building Material Erosion]
    S5 --> T4[Forest Decline]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Visualization Tip:** The examiner often awards additional marks for clean, well-labeled block diagrams. The above Mermaid diagrams can be rendered to image format or redrawn manually in the answer script for full credit.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] List any three major effects of particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10) on human health.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
1. **PM2.5 (fine particles)** penetrate deep into the alveolar region of the lungs, causing **chronic bronchitis, COPD, and lung cancer** due to oxidative stress and inflammation.
2. **PM10 (coarse particles)** lodge in the upper respiratory tract (nasopharyngeal region), causing **nasal irritation, cough, and exacerbation of asthma**.
3. Both PM2.5 and PM10 contribute to **cardiovascular morbidity and mortality** by entering systemic circulation and triggering inflammatory responses. They also serve as carriers for **adsorbed toxicants** such as polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs) and heavy metals.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024] Explain the term "acid rain" and state its natural baseline pH. Why is normal rain slightly acidic?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Definition:** Acid rain is any form of precipitation (rain, snow, fog, or dry deposition) with a **pH below 5.6**, caused by the atmospheric incorporation of strong acids (H2SO4 and HNO3) derived from SO2 and NOx emissions.
- **Natural Baseline pH:** **pH = 5.6**, due to the dissolution of atmospheric CO2 in rainwater forming carbonic acid:

$$CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$$

- This natural acidity is caused by **CO2 from natural biogeochemical cycles**, not by anthropogenic pollution.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

> **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **effects of air pollutants on human health**, with specific reference to the action of carbon monoxide, sulphur dioxide, and ozone. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat schematic, describe the **mechanism of stratospheric ozone depletion** caused by chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs). Discuss the role of the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** in mitigating this problem. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (Part a) — 7 Marks

**Carbon Monoxide (CO) — 2.5 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> CO is a colorless, odorless, chemically inert gas produced by **incomplete combustion** of carbonaceous fuels.

- It binds with **hemoglobin (Hb)** in red blood cells to form **carboxyhemoglobin (COHb)**, with an affinity **~200–250 times greater than oxygen**.
- This reduces the **oxygen-carrying capacity of blood**, causing **tissue hypoxia**.
- The relationship is quantified by the **Coburn-Foster-Kane (CFK) equation**:

$$\vert COHb \vert\ (\%) = \dfrac{V_{CO}\cdot t}{V_b \cdot Hb} \cdot \left[\dfrac{P_{CO}}{M} + \dfrac{1}{D_L}\right]$$

- Symptoms range from **headache and dizziness** (COHb = 10–20%) to **coma and death** (COHb > 60%).

**Sulphur Dioxide (SO2) — 2.5 Marks**

- A water-soluble, irritating gas emitted from **coal-fired power plants and smelters**.
- It dissolves in the **moisture of the upper respiratory tract** to form **sulphurous acid**:

$$SO_2 + H_2O \longrightarrow H_2SO_3$$

- This irritates the airway lining, causing **bronchoconstriction**, **aggravation of asthma**, and **chronic bronchitis** in long-term exposure.
- At higher concentrations, it can cause **pulmonary edema and respiratory failure**.

**Ozone (O3) — 2 Marks**

- Tropospheric O3 is a **secondary pollutant** formed by photochemical reactions involving NOx and VOCs in sunlight.
- It is a **strong oxidant** that causes **inflammation of the lung tissue**, reduced lung function, and aggravated asthma.
- It induces **oxidative stress** by generating free radicals that damage cell membranes and lung surfactant.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A (Part b) — 7 Marks

**CFC-Mediated Stratospheric Ozone Depletion (5 Marks)**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Background:** The stratosphere (15–50 km altitude) contains the **ozone layer** which absorbs harmful UV-B and UV-C radiation. Anthropogenic **CFCs** released into the atmosphere diffuse upward and disrupt this protective layer.

**Step 1: Release and Transport**
- CFCs (e.g., **CFCl3 — Freon-11**, **CF2Cl2 — Freon-12**) are stable in the troposphere and slowly diffuse into the stratosphere.

**Step 2: UV Photolysis of CFC**
- UV radiation of wavelength $\lambda < 220\ nm$ cleaves the C–Cl bond:

$$CFCl_3 + h\nu \longrightarrow CFCl_2\cdot + Cl\cdot$$

**Step 3: Catalytic Ozone Destruction by Cl Radicals**
- Each chlorine radical participates in a **catalytic cycle**:

$$Cl\cdot + O_3 \longrightarrow ClO\cdot + O_2$$

$$ClO\cdot + O\cdot \longrightarrow Cl\cdot + O_2$$

$$\text{Net:}\ \ O_3 + O\cdot \longrightarrow 2O_2$$

- A single Cl atom can destroy **~100,000 O3 molecules** before being deactivated.

**Step 4: Antarctic Ozone Hole**
- The phenomenon is most pronounced over **Antarctica** due to the presence of **polar stratospheric clouds (PSCs)** that facilitate heterogeneous reactions releasing Cl from reservoir species like HCl and ClONO2.

**Montreal Protocol (1987) — 2 Marks**

> [!NOTE]
> The **Montreal Protocol on Substances that Deplete the Ozone Layer** (signed 16 September 1987, entered into force 1 January 1989) is the first international treaty to address a global environmental crisis.

- It mandated the **phased reduction and eventual elimination of CFCs, halons, carbon tetrachloride, and other ozone-depleting substances**.
- Subsequent amendments (London 1990, Copenhagen 1992, Montreal 1997, Beijing 1999) accelerated the phase-out schedule.
- The **Kigali Amendment (2016)** added **hydrofluorocarbons (HFCs)** to the control list (although HFCs are not ODS, they are potent GHGs).
- The protocol is widely regarded as a **landmark success** in global environmental governance, with the ozone layer projected to fully recover by **mid-21st century**.

---

> **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Internal Choice Alternative**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **effects of air pollutants on vegetation**. Discuss the role of **acid rain** and **photochemical oxidants (O3, PAN)** in plant damage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **effects of air pollution on materials**, with specific reference to the **corrosion of metals**, **deterioration of buildings**, and **damage to polymers**. Cite at least one famous case study. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (Part a) — 7 Marks

**Effects on Vegetation — 3 Marks**

- Vegetation is a sensitive receptor to air pollution. Pollutants enter leaves through **stomatal openings** during photosynthesis.
- Visible injury symptoms include **chlorosis (yellowing), necrosis (tissue death), stippling, and marginal leaf burn**.
- Chronic exposure reduces **photosynthesis, transpiration, and crop yield**.

**Role of Acid Rain — 2 Marks**

- Acidic deposition (pH < 5.6) erodes the **waxy cuticle** of leaves, allowing leaching of essential nutrients (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺, K⁺).
- It also **acidifies the soil**, mobilizing toxic aluminium ions (Al³⁺) and inhibiting root uptake of water and nutrients.
- **Forest decline** observed in the Black Forest (Germany) and the Appalachian Mountains (USA) are classic examples.

**Role of Photochemical Oxidants — 2 Marks**

- **Ozone (O3):** Causes **upper-surface stippling** and accelerates senescence. It damages **chloroplast membranes** and inhibits photosynthesis.
- **Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN):** A powerful phytotoxin that causes **bronzing and glazing** on the underside of leaves, especially in **tomato, petunia, and bean plants**.
- Both pollutants are **secondary**, formed by photochemical reactions involving NOx and VOCs in sunlight.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B (Part b) — 7 Marks

**Corrosion of Metals — 2.5 Marks**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Atmospheric corrosion** is an electrochemical process accelerated by SO2, Cl⁻, and humidity.

- Example: Rusting of iron (Fe):

$$Fe + SO_2 + O_2 + H_2O \longrightarrow FeSO_4 \cdot 7H_2O\ (\text{iron sulfate hydrate})$$

- The **ISO 9223** standard quantifies corrosion rate:

$$r_{corr} = a + b \cdot [SO_2]^c \cdot [Cl^-]^d \cdot RH^e \cdot T^f$$

- Coastal and industrial atmospheres exhibit 10–100 times higher corrosion rates than rural clean atmospheres.

**Deterioration of Buildings — 2.5 Marks**

- **Sulfation of limestone and marble:** SO2 reacts with CaCO3 to form gypsum (CaSO4·2H2O), which is mechanically weaker and flaky:

$$CaCO_3 + SO_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}O_2 + 2H_2O \longrightarrow CaSO_4 \cdot 2H_2O + CO_2$$

- **Case Study: The Taj Mahal (Agra, India):** The iconic white marble has been yellowing and corroding due to SO2 emissions from the **Mathura Oil Refinery** and surrounding industries. The **Taj Trapezium Zone (TTZ)** was declared a protected area, and industries were mandated to switch to **natural gas** as fuel.

**Damage to Polymers — 2 Marks**

- **Ozone cracking of rubber:** O3 attacks the C=C double bonds in natural rubber:

$$-CH=CH- + O_3 \longrightarrow -CHO\ (aldehyde) + -COOH\ (carboxylic\ acid)$$

- This causes **surface cracking and loss of elasticity**, a major problem for rubber tires and seals.
- **Paint chalking:** Acidic pollutants break down binder resins, leading to fading and chalking of painted surfaces.
- **Textile deterioration:** Acidic pollutants weaken natural fibers (cotton, silk, wool), reducing their tensile strength and lifespan.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Do NOT list effects without naming the pollutant.** Saying "lung disease" without attributing it to PM2.5 or NO2 loses marks.
> 2. **Do NOT confuse tropospheric and stratospheric ozone.** Tropospheric O3 is a pollutant (smog component); stratospheric O3 is the protective UV shield. Conflating them is a frequent error.
> 3. **Do NOT forget the chemical equations.** The CFK equation, acid rain equations, and CFC cycle are all expected to be written explicitly in the answer.
> 4. **Do NOT skip the Montreal Protocol case study** in stratospheric ozone discussions — KTU 2024 papers specifically test awareness of international environmental agreements.
> 5. **Do NOT omit numerical units** in the formula sheet and worked examples (e.g., write ppm, µg/m³, or mg/m³ explicitly).
> 6. **Do NOT write the CFK equation as a one-liner without explaining each variable** ($V_{CO}$, $D_L$, $M$, $V_b$, $Hb$, $P_{CO}$, $t$).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] Air pollution effects are categorized into **four spheres**: Health, Vegetation, Materials, and Atmosphere.
- [x] **PM2.5** causes alveolar damage; **PM10** causes upper respiratory irritation. Sizes: $\le 2.5\ \mu m$ and $\le 10\ \mu m$.
- [x] **CO binds Hb** with 200–250× greater affinity than O2 → **COHb formation** (CFK equation).
- [x] **SO2** is highly water-soluble → upper airway irritation, bronchoconstriction, **stone sulfation**.
- [x] **NO2** is less soluble → deep lung penetration, pulmonary edema, vegetation chlorosis.
- [x] **Ozone (tropospheric)** is a secondary pollutant → lung inflammation, crop damage.
- [x] **Lead (Pb)** inhibits heme biosynthesis → CNS damage, IQ reduction in children.
- [x] **VOCs** like benzene are **carcinogenic** → leukemia and other cancers.
- [x] **Vegetation damage:** stomatal uptake → chlorophyll degradation → photosynthesis inhibition → yield loss.
- [x] **PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate)** causes bronzing/glazing in tomato, petunia, bean.
- [x] **Material effects:** metal corrosion (ISO 9223), marble sulfation (Taj Mahal), rubber O3 cracking.
- [x] **Atmospheric effects:** global warming (CO2, CH4, N2O), ozone depletion (CFCs), acid rain (pH < 5.6), photochemical smog.
- [x] **CFC catalytic cycle** destroys ~100,000 O3 molecules per Cl radical.
- [x] **Montreal Protocol (1987)** is the key international treaty to phase out ODS.
- [x] **Normal rain pH = 5.6** due to dissolved CO2 (carbonic acid formation).
- [x] **NAAQS (India)** and **WHO (2021)** guidelines set permissible limits for major pollutants.
- [x] **Cause-effect linkage is mandatory** in KTU answers — always name the pollutant, the pathway, and the effect.
- [x] Memorize key equations: **CFK equation, acid rain equations, ISO 9223 corrosion equation, Chapman mechanism**.
- [x] Famous case studies: **Taj Mahal (marble corrosion)**, **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (MIC leak)**, **London Smog (1952)**, **Antarctic Ozone Hole (1985)**.
- [x] GWP and Ozone Depletion Potential (ODP) are key metrics for ranking pollutants.
- [x] For numerical problems, **always show unit conversions explicitly** (e.g., ppm → torr, µg/m³ → ppm).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reactions of Pollutants in the Atmosphere and Their Effects
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Reactions of Pollutants in the Atmosphere and Their Effects # ⚗️ MODULE 1: Reactions of Pollutants in the Atmosphere and Their Effects ## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Official Definition (Syllabus Aligned):** > Atmospheric reactions refer to the **photochemical, thermochemical, and free-radical driven chemical transformations** that primary air pollutants (NOx, SOx, CO, HC, PM) undergo in the troposphere and stratosphere under the influence of solar radiation, moisture, and ambient oxidants. These reactions govern the formation of **secondary pollutants** such as ozone (O₃), peroxyacetyl nitrate (PAN), sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄), nitric acid (HNO₃), and acid rain components. ### 🌍 Conceptual Analogy: The Atmospheric "Kitchen" Imagine the Earth's atmosphere as a **giant open-air kitchen** with three active burners: - **Burner 1 (Sunlight / UV Radiation):** The "heat source" that triggers almost every reaction. Without it, the kitchen is cold and food doesn't cook. - **Burner 2 (Primary Pollutants — NOx, SO₂, HC, CO):** The raw "ingredients" emitted by vehicles, power plants, and industries. - **Burner 3 (Reactive Atmospheric Oxidants — OH•, O, O₃):** The "spices and catalysts" — invisible, ultra-reactive molecular fragments that speed up every reaction. When sunlight strikes vehicular exhaust, it doesn't just blow away — it **"cooks"** the chemicals into far more dangerous secondary pollutants like ozone and PAN. That is the entire essence of atmospheric chemistry. ### 1.2 Classification of Atmospheric Reactions | Sl. No. | Reaction Class | Triggering Agent | Typical Time-Scale | |:-------:|----------------|------------------|--------------------| | 1 | **Photochemical Reactions** | Solar UV (λ < 400 nm) | Seconds to hours | | 2 | **Free-Radical Chain Reactions** | OH•, HO₂•, NO₃• radicals | Milliseconds to days | | 3 | **Thermochemical (Gas-Phase) Reactions** | Ambient temperature | Hours to days | | 4 | **Heterogeneous / Aqueous Reactions** | Water droplets, aerosols | Days to weeks | | 5 | **Stratospheric Reactions** | High-energy UV (λ < 240 nm) | Months to years | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** For Module 1, students must specifically master the photochemical smog mechanism (the **Leighton Ratio** and **NOx–VOC–O₃** triangle), SO₂ oxidation kinetics, CO oxidation, and PAN chemistry. These are recurring **14-mark ESE questions**. ### 1.3 Primary vs. Secondary Pollutants > [!NOTE] > **Definition — Primary Pollutant:** Emitted directly into the atmosphere in a harmful form (CO, SO₂, NO, PM₁₀). > > **Definition — Secondary Pollutant:** Formed in the atmosphere by chemical reactions between primary pollutants and natural constituents (O₃, PAN, H₂SO₄, HNO₃, aldehydes). $$ \text{Primary Pollutant} + \text{Solar Radiation / Oxidant} \xrightarrow{k} \text{Secondary Pollutant} $$ where **k** is the **reaction rate constant**, typically expressed in **cm³·molecule⁻¹·s⁻¹** for bimolecular gas-phase reactions. ### 1.4 Standard Atmospheric Constants (For Numerical Problems) > [!IMPORTANT] > Use these **KTU-approved standard values** in numericals: > > - **Avogadro's Number:** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ molecules/mol}$ > - **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$ > - **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ > - **Reference Temperature:** $T = 298 \text{ K}$ (25 °C) > - **Solar Photon Energy (λ = 300 nm):** $E = h\nu = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times \frac{3 \times 10^8}{300 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 6.62 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J/molecule}$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Photochemical Smog Mechanism (Most Important Topic) Photochemical smog was first characterized in **Los Angeles (1940s)**. It is dominated by **ozone, NOx, and PAN**. Unlike classical (London-type) reducing smog, photochemical smog is an **oxidizing** smog. ### 2.1.1 Step 1 — Photolysis of NO₂ (The Foundation Reaction) When nitrogen dioxide absorbs UV-A radiation (**315 nm < λ < 400 nm**), it dissociates into nitric oxide and a ground-state oxygen atom: $$ \text{NO}_2 + h\nu \xrightarrow{\lambda < 424 \text{ nm}} \text{NO} + \text{O}\left({}^{3}P\right) $$ > The oxygen atom is in its **triplet ground state** $\text{O}({}^3P)$ — important because it determines subsequent reactivity. ### 2.1.2 Step 2 — Formation of Ozone (O₃) The freshly formed atomic oxygen rapidly combines with molecular oxygen in the presence of a third body (M = N₂ or O₂) to form ozone: $$ \text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \xrightarrow{k_2} \text{O}_3 + \text{M} $$ At 298 K, the rate constant is approximately: $$ k_2 = 6.0 \times 10^{-34} \text{ cm}^6 \cdot \text{molecule}^{-2} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} $$ ### 2.1.3 Step 3 — The "Ozone–NO Toggling" Steady State Ozone is simultaneously destroyed by NO: $$ \text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 $$ In the absence of VOCs, this creates a **photo-stationary state (PSS)**: $$ [\text{O}_3] = \frac{j_{\text{NO}_2} \cdot [\text{NO}_2]}{k_3 \cdot [\text{NO}]} $$ where $j_{\text{NO}_2}$ is the **NO₂ photolysis rate coefficient** (s⁻¹). ### 2.1.4 Step 4 — Ozone Build-up via VOC Oxidation VOCs (Volatile Organic Compounds) convert NO → NO₂ **without consuming ozone**, breaking the steady state and allowing **net O₃ accumulation**. Generic mechanism: $$ \text{VOC} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{RO}_2^{\bullet} $$ $$ \text{RO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{NO} \rightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{RO}^{\bullet} $$ The regenerated NO₂ is again photolysed, **amplifying O₃ production**. ### 2.2 Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂) Atmospheric Reactions SO₂ undergoes **three primary oxidation pathways**: | Pathway | Mechanism | Rate | |:-------:|-----------|:----:| | **Gas-phase (homogeneous)** | Reaction with OH• radical | Slow (τ ≈ 1–2 weeks) | | **Aqueous-phase (heterogeneous)** | Dissolution in cloud/fog droplets + H₂O₂/O₃ | Fast (τ ≈ hours) | | **Dry/Wet deposition** | Direct capture on surfaces | Variable | ### 2.2.1 Gas-Phase SO₂ Oxidation $$ \text{SO}_2 + \text{OH}^{\bullet} + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{HOSO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{M} $$ $$ \text{HOSO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{HO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{SO}_3 $$ $$ \text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (sulfuric acid)} $$ ### 2.2.2 Aqueous-Phase SO₂ Oxidation In cloud droplets, SO₂ hydrolyses: $$ \text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HSO}_3^- + \text{H}^+ $$ $$ \text{HSO}_3^- \rightarrow \text{SO}_3^{2-} + \text{H}^+ $$ Oxidation by **dissolved ozone** (dominant at pH > 5): $$ \text{HSO}_3^- + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{SO}_4^{2-} + \text{H}^+ + \text{O}_2 $$ ### 2.3 Carbon Monoxide (CO) Oxidation CO is oxidized primarily by the hydroxyl radical: $$ \text{CO} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}^{\bullet} $$ $$ \text{H}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{HO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{M} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Note:** The tropospheric residence time of CO is approximately **1–2 months**, making it an important trace gas for global oxidation chemistry. ### 2.4 PAN (Peroxyacetyl Nitrate) Formation PAN is a **lachrymatory** secondary pollutant — it irritates eyes at very low concentrations (>0.1 ppm). $$ \text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CO}^{\bullet} + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$ $$ \text{CH}_3\text{CO}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OO}^{\bullet} + \text{M} $$ $$ \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OO}^{\bullet} + \text{NO}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2 + \text{M} $$ The final product is **PAN: CH₃C(O)OONO₂**. ### 2.5 Stratospheric Ozone Depletion Cycle In the stratosphere, CFCs (e.g., CFCl₃) undergo UV photolysis: $$ \text{CFCl}_3 + h\nu \xrightarrow{\lambda < 220 \text{ nm}} \text{CFCl}_2^{\bullet} + \text{Cl}^{\bullet} $$ $$ \text{Cl}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{ClO}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 $$ $$ \text{ClO}^{\bullet} + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cl}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 $$ **Net:** One chlorine atom destroys **~100,000 ozone molecules** (chain reaction). ### 2.6 Acid Rain Formation $$ \text{SO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \quad (pH \approx 2–3) $$ $$ 2\text{NO}_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\text{O}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{HNO}_3 \quad (pH \approx 3–4) $$ ### 2.7 Greenhouse Effect Mechanism > [!IMPORTANT] > Polyatomic molecules with **asymmetric vibrational modes** (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O, CFCs) absorb outgoing IR radiation, trap heat, and warm the lower troposphere. | Greenhouse Gas | GWP (100-year) | Atmospheric Lifetime | |:--------------:|:--------------:|:--------------------:| | CO₂ | 1 | Variable (centuries) | | CH₄ | 28 | 12 years | | N₂O | 265 | 114 years | | CFC-11 | 4,660 | 45 years | ## 📘 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Module 1) | # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units / Notation | |:-:|:------------------|:------------|:-----------------| | 1 | $\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}({}^3P)$ | Primary photolysis | λ < 424 nm | | 2 | $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$ | Ozone formation | $k \approx 6 \times 10^{-34}$ cm⁶/molecule²·s | | 3 | $[\text{O}_3] = \dfrac{j_{\text{NO}_2}[\text{NO}_2]}{k_3[\text{NO}]}$ | Photo-stationary state | ppb | | 4 | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Photon energy | initiation | Rate-determining | ### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Urban Airshed Modeling:** Municipal authorities use these reaction mechanisms in **EPA's UAM-IV, CMAQ, and AERMOD** to predict daily ozone and PM₂.₅ levels for issuing public-health advisories. - **Source Apportionment:** Knowing the photochemical pathways helps engineers distinguish **traffic-induced smog** vs. **industrial smog** in receptor cities. - **Catalytic Converter Design:** Modern three-way catalytic converters rely on the **NO ⇌ NO₂ ⇌ O₃** triangle to simultaneously oxidize CO and HC, and reduce NOx. - **Acid Rain Mitigation:** Understanding aqueous-phase SO₂ oxidation informs **flue-gas desulfurization (FGD) wet scrubber** chemistry. - **CFC Replacements:** Knowledge of stratospheric Cl• chemistry drove the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** phasing out CFCs in favour of HFCs. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Photo-Stationary State (PSS) for Ozone The PSS is a **KTU-favourite derivation**. It establishes the relationship between [O₃], [NO], and [NO₂] under sunlight in the absence of VOCs. ### Step 1 — Write the three primary reactions $$ \text{(i)} \quad \text{NO}_2 + h\nu \xrightarrow{j_1} \text{NO} + \text{O} $$ $$ \text{(ii)} \quad \text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \xrightarrow{k_2} \text{O}_3 + \text{M} $$ $$ \text{(iii)} \quad \text{O}_3 + \text{NO} \xrightarrow{k_3} \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 $$ where $j_1$ is the photolysis rate coefficient of NO₂ (s⁻¹), $k_2$ is the termolecular rate constant, and $k_3$ is the bimolecular rate constant. ### Step 2 — Steady-state approximation for O atoms At steady state, $\dfrac{d[\text{O}]}{dt} = 0$: $$ \frac{d[\text{O}]}{dt} = j_1[\text{NO}_2] - k_2[\text{O}][\text{O}_2][\text{M}] = 0 $$ $$ \Rightarrow \quad [\text{O}] = \frac{j_1[\text{NO}_2]}{k_2[\text{O}_2][\text{M}]} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply SSA to ozone $$ \frac{d[\text{O}_3]}{dt} = k_2[\text{O}][\text{O}_2][\text{M}] - k_3[\text{O}_3][\text{NO}] = 0 $$ Substitute [O]: $$ j_1[\text{NO}_2] = k_3[\text{O}_3][\text{NO}] $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for [O₃] $$ \boxed{\,[\text{O}_3]_{\text{PSS}} = \frac{j_1 \, [\text{NO}_2]}{k_3 \, [\text{NO}]}\,} $$ ### Step 5 — Numerical Example (Typical KTU Problem) Given: $j_1 = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$ (midday summer), $[\text{NO}_2] = 40 \text{ ppb}$, $[\text{NO}] = 10 \text{ ppb}$, $k_3 = 1.8 \times 10^{-14} \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{molecule}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$. **Step 5a —** Substitute directly into the boxed equation: $$ [\text{O}_3]_{\text{PSS}} = \frac{(8 \times 10^{-3})(40)}{(1.8 \times 10^{-14})(10)} $$ **Step 5b —** Compute numerator: $$ (8 \times 10^{-3}) \times 40 = 320 \times 10^{-3} = 0.32 $$ **Step 5c —** Compute denominator: $$ (1.8 \times 10^{-14}) \times 10 = 1.8 \times 10^{-13} $$ **Step 5d —** Final division: $$ [\text{O}_3]_{\text{PSS}} = \frac{0.32}{1.8 \times 10^{-13}} = 1.778 \times 10^{12} \text{ molecules/cm}^3 $$ **Step 5e —** Convert to ppb using the ideal gas relation at 298 K, 1 atm (1 ppb = 2.46 × 10¹⁰ molecules/cm³): $$ [\text{O}_3]_{\text{ppb}} = \frac{1.778 \times 10^{12}}{2.46 \times 10^{10}} \approx 72.3 \text{ ppb} $$ > **Interpretation:** This is well above the NAAQS 8-hour ozone standard of **70 ppb**, indicating a **photochemical smog episode**. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Arrhenius Rate Constant for Atmospheric SO₂ Oxidation The gas-phase SO₂ + OH• reaction: $$ \text{SO}_2 + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \xrightarrow{k(T)} \text{HOSO}_2^{\bullet} $$ has a known Arrhenius expression (Sander et al., JPL Publication 19-05): $$ k(T) = (2.0 \times 10^{-12}) \, e^{+310/T} \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{molecule}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1} $$ Note: The **negative activation energy** (positive exponent) is characteristic of OH• addition reactions. ### Step-by-step at T = 298 K: **Step 1 —** Compute $T$: $T = 298 \text{ K}$ **Step 2 —** Evaluate exponent: $310/T = 310/298 = 1.0403$ **Step 3 —** Apply exponential: $e^{1.0403} = 2.8296$ **Step 4 —** Multiply: $k(298) = (2.0 \times 10^{-12}) \times 2.8296 = 5.66 \times 10^{-12} \text{ cm}^3 \cdot \text{molecule}^{-1} \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ ### Atmospheric lifetime of SO₂ against OH• oxidation: **Step 5 —** Typical OH• concentration: $[\text{OH}^{\bullet}] = 1.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ molecules/cm}^3$. **Step 6 —** Lifetime: $$ \tau = \frac{1}{k \cdot [\text{OH}^{\bullet}]} = \frac{1}{(5.66 \times 10^{-12})(1.0 \times 10^{6})} $$ **Step 7 —** Final: $$ \tau = \frac{1}{5.66 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 1.77 \times 10^{5} \text{ s} \approx 2.0 \text{ days} $$ > This explains why SO₂ from tall stacks can travel **hundreds of kilometres** before oxidizing into sulfate aerosol. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Leighton Ratio (Diagnostic for VOC vs. NOx Limitation) The **Leighton Ratio** $\Phi$ is a critical diagnostic used in air-quality engineering: $$ \Phi = \frac{j_1[\text{NO}_2]}{k_3[\text{NO}][\text{O}_3]} $$ - If $\Phi \approx 1$: Photo-stationary state holds (no VOC chemistry). - If $\Phi > 1$: Ozone production is **NOx-limited**. - If $\Phi < 1$: Ozone production is **VOC-limited**. ### Worked Example: Given: $j_1 = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s}^{-1}$, $[\text{NO}_2] = 30 \text{ ppb}$, $[\text{NO}] = 5 \text{ ppb}$, $[\text{O}_3] = 80 \text{ ppb}$, $k_3 = 1.8 \times 10^{-14}$. **Step 1 —** Numerator: $5 \times 10^{-3} \times 30 = 0.15$ **Step 2 —** Denominator: $(1.8 \times 10^{-14})(5)(80) = 7.2 \times 10^{-12}$ **Step 3 —** Ratio: $\Phi = 0.15 / (7.2 \times 10^{-12}) \gg 1$ → **NOx-limited regime** → emission control should target **NOx**. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Effects of Pollutant Reactions | Secondary Pollutant | Formed From | Health Effect | Environmental Effect | KTU Frequency | |:--------------------|:-----------|:--------------|:---------------------|:-------------:| | **Ozone (O₃)** | NOx + VOC + sunlight | Pulmonary inflammation, asthma | Crop yield loss, rubber cracking | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **PAN** | HC + NO₂ + sunlight | Eye irritation, plant damage | Phytotoxicity | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **H₂SO₄ / Sulfate** | SO₂ + OH• / aqueous | Respiratory irritation | Acid rain, stone corrosion | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **HNO₃ / Nitrate** | NO₂ + OH• | Respiratory | Acid rain, eutrophication | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Aldehydes (HCHO)** | HC oxidation | Sensory irritation | Secondary aerosol precursor | ⭐⭐⭐ | | **CO₂ (↑)** | Fossil combustion | Climate | Global warming | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | | **Depleted Stratospheric O₃** | CFCs + UV | Skin cancer, cataracts | UV-B penetration | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | # 4.
