KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 × 3 Marks = 6 Marks) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023]** Distinguish between **classical (London-type) smog** and **photochemical (Los Angeles-type) smog**. Mention four key differences. **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Parameter | Classical Smog (London-Type) | Photochemical Smog (LA-Type) | |:----------|:----------------------------|:------------------------------| | **Chemical Nature** | Reducing | Oxidizing | | **Trigger** | SO₂ + soot + fog | NOx + VOC + sunlight | | **Time of Occurrence** | Early morning (winter) | Midday to afternoon (summer) | | **Key Secondary Pollutant** | Sulfuric acid mist | Ozone, PAN | | **Color** | Dark/yellowish-black | Brownish haze | | **Primary Fuel** | Coal | Petroleum/gasoline | | **Health Impact** | Bronchitis, lung damage | Eye irritation, asthma | | **City Example** | London (1952) | Los Angeles, Delhi, Mexico City | --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** Define **PAN** and write the sequence of reactions involved in its formation. **Model Answer (3 marks):** > **Peroxyacetyl Nitrate (PAN)** is a secondary organic pollutant formed by the photochemical oxidation of hydrocarbons (especially acetaldehyde) in the presence of NO₂. It is a potent **eye irritant** and phytotoxicant. **Formation Mechanism:** **Step 1:** OH• abstracts H from acetaldehyde: $\text{CH}_3\text{CHO} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CO}^{\bullet} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ **Step 2:** Acetyl radical adds O₂: $\text{CH}_3\text{CO}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OO}^{\bullet} + \text{M}$ **Step 3:** Peroxyacetyl radical combines with NO₂: $\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OO}^{\bullet} + \text{NO}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O)OONO}_2 \text{ (PAN)} + \text{M}$ --- ## 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Choice-Based, 14 Marks Each) > **KTU ESE Pattern (Module 1 Internal Choice):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each question has two sub-parts: **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]** **(a)** Explain in detail the **photochemical smog formation mechanism**, clearly stating the role of NO₂ photolysis, ozone formation, and the photo-stationary state. Derive the expression for the steady-state ozone concentration in a clean (VOC-free) atmosphere. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Discuss the **atmospheric reactions of SO₂** leading to acid rain. Compare the gas-phase and aqueous-phase oxidation pathways in terms of reaction rates and environmental significance. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Photochemical Smog Mechanism** > [!NOTE] > **Photolysis of NO₂ (1 mark):** > When solar UV radiation (315–400 nm) strikes NO₂, the molecule absorbs a photon and dissociates: > > $\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}({}^3P)$ > > The energy requirement: $E = hc/\lambda$. At λ = 400 nm, $E \approx 4.97 \times 10^{-19}$ J ≈ 3.1 eV — well within UV-A energy. > **Ozone Formation (1 mark):** > The atomic oxygen rapidly combines with O₂: > > $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$ > > M is a third-body collision partner (N₂ or O₂) that absorbs excess vibrational energy. > **Photo-Stationary State Derivation (3 marks):** > Setting up SSA for [O] and [O₃], and solving, we derived: > > $[\text{O}_3]_{\text{PSS}} = \dfrac{j_1[\text{NO}_2]}{k_3[\text{NO}]}$ > > In clean air, [O₃] is typically 30–50 ppb, but in VOC-rich atmospheres, the ratio is broken and [O₃] can exceed 200 ppb. > **Role of VOC and Ozone Build-up (1 mark):** > VOCs convert NO → NO₂ via RO₂• radicals, bypassing the ozone-destroying step. This leads to **net ozone accumulation** — the hallmark of photochemical smog. > **Effects (1 mark):** > Ozone damages lung tissue, PAN irritates eyes, NO₂ causes bronchitis. Crops (e.g., tobacco, tomato) suffer yield loss. Materials (rubber, paint) deteriorate. --- **Part (b) — SO₂ Atmospheric Reactions and Acid Rain** > **Step 1 — Gas-Phase Oxidation (3 marks):** > The hydroxyl radical pathway dominates in clear air: > > $\text{SO}_2 + \text{OH}^{\bullet} + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{HOSO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{M}$ > $\text{HOSO}_2^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{SO}_3 + \text{HO}_2^{\bullet}$ > $\text{SO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ > > **Lifetime ≈ 1–2 weeks** under typical atmospheric OH• concentrations (~10⁶ molecules/cm³). > **Step 2 — Aqueous-Phase Oxidation (3 marks):** > Inside cloud and fog droplets: > > $\text{SO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightleftharpoons \text{HSO}_3^- + \text{H}^+$ > $\text{HSO}_3^- + \text{O}_3(aq) \rightarrow \text{SO}_4^{2-} + \text{H}^+ + \text{O}_2$ > > **Lifetime ≈ hours to 1 day** — much faster than gas-phase. > **Step 3 — Comparison and Significance (1 mark):** > Aqueous oxidation dominates globally because clouds cover ~60% of the Earth's surface. Acid rain is therefore more strongly correlated with **regional cloud cover and humidity** than with local SO₂ emission rates alone. --- ### **Question B — Alternative Choice (KTU Pattern)** **(a)** Describe the **chlorine radical-catalysed destruction of stratospheric ozone**. Discuss how CFCs released at the ground reach the stratosphere and explain the role of Polar Stratospheric Clouds (PSCs). **(7 marks)** **(b)** With suitable reactions, explain the formation of **acid rain** from SO₂ and NOx emissions. Discuss its environmental consequences. **(7 marks)** #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Stratospheric Ozone Depletion (7 marks)** > **CFC Release and Transport (2 marks):** > CFCl₃ and CF₂Cl₂ are inert in the troposphere (lifetime 50–100 years). They are transported upward by **Brewer–Dobson circulation** over 2–5 years and reach the stratosphere (15–35 km). > **UV Photolysis (2 marks):** > At λ < 220 nm: > > $\text{CFCl}_3 + h\nu \rightarrow \text{CFCl}_2^{\bullet} + \text{Cl}^{\bullet}$ > **Catalytic Cycle (2 marks):** > > $\text{Cl}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{ClO}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2$ > $\text{ClO}^{\bullet} + \text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cl}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2$ > > **Net:** $\text{O}_3 + \text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{O}_2$ > > Each Cl• destroys ~10⁵ ozone molecules before being deactivated. > **Polar Stratospheric Clouds (1 mark):** > During Antarctic winter, PSCs at 15–25 km adsorb HCl and ClONO₂, releasing molecular Cl₂. In spring sunlight, Cl₂ → 2Cl•, triggering the massive ozone hole observed every September–October over Antarctica. --- **Part (b) — Acid Rain (7 marks)** > **From SO₂ (3 marks):** > $\text{SO}_2 + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{HOSO}_2^{\bullet} \rightarrow \cdots \rightarrow \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ > $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{H}_3\text{O}^+ + \text{HSO}_4^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}$ (pH ≈ 2–3) > > **From NO₂ (2 marks):** > $\text{NO}_2 + \text{OH}^{\bullet} + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{HNO}_3 + \text{M}$ (daytime) > $\text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{NO}_3 + \text{O}_2$; $\text{NO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{HNO}_3$ (nighttime) > > **Environmental Consequences (2 marks):** > - Lowering lake pH → fish mortality (e.g., Scandinavian lakes) > - Soil nutrient leaching (Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺) > - Corrosion of marble monuments (Taj Mahal, Parthenon) > - Forest dieback (Black Forest, Germany) --- ## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Module 1 Atmosphere-Reaction Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting the third-body "M" in ozone formation.** Many students write $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{O}_3$. This is **wrong** — a third body is mandatory to absorb excess energy. Lose **0.5 mark** immediately. > > 2. **Confusing photochemical vs. classical smog triggers.** Photochemical = NOx + VOC + **sunlight**; classical = SO₂ + soot + **fog**. Examiners look for the word "photo-stationary state" specifically — including it gives a bonus impression. > > 3. **Omitting units in numerical PSS problems.** Always show $[\text{O}_3]$ in **ppb** or **µg/m³**. Without units, **2 marks** may be deducted. > > 4. **Writing CFC destruction without the "Cl• regeneration" step.** You must show that Cl• is regenerated in the catalytic cycle. Writing only $\text{Cl}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{ClO}^{\bullet} + \text{O}_2$ is incomplete — full marks require the second step. > > 5. **Using incorrect molar masses in SO₂ → H₂SO₄ stoichiometry.** SO₂ = 64 g/mol, H₂SO₄ = 98 g/mol. Mixing these up causes numerical errors in acid rain mass-balance questions. > > 6. **Spelling "photochemical" as "photo-chemical" or "photo chemical".** This is a flagged spell-check item on KTU answer sheets — keep it as a single word. --- ## 🔁 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Module 1 Revision Bulletins — Keep This Sticky-Noted:** - ✅ **Atmospheric chemistry** transforms primary pollutants into **secondary pollutants** under solar radiation. - ✅ **Photochemical smog** = oxidizing smog = NOx + VOC + UV → **O₃ + PAN + aldehydes**. - ✅ **Classical smog** = reducing smog = SO₂ + soot + fog → sulfuric acid mist. - ✅ The **photo-stationary state equation** is: $[\text{O}_3] = \dfrac{j_1[\text{NO}_2]}{k_3[\text{NO}]}$. - ✅ **Ozone formation** requires a third-body M: $\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \rightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$. - ✅ **PAN formation pathway:** CH₃CHO → CH₃CO• → CH₃C(O)OO• → PAN (with NO₂). - ✅ **SO₂ oxidation:** gas-phase is **slow** (~weeks), aqueous-phase is **fast** (~hours) — clouds dominate global sulfate production. - ✅ **CO oxidation:** $\text{CO} + \text{OH}^{\bullet} \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + \text{H}^{\bullet}$ is the dominant sink. - ✅ **Stratospheric O₃ depletion:** Cl• catalytic cycle destroys ~10⁵ O₃ per Cl; CFCs are the major Cl source; PSCs amplify Antarctic ozone hole. - ✅ **Acid rain** = H₂SO₄ + HNO₃; pH 2–5; damages ecosystems, monuments, and forests. - ✅ **Leighton Ratio** $\Phi$ diagnoses whether O₃ formation is **NOx-limited** ($\Phi > 1$) or **VOC-limited** ($\Phi < 1$) — critical for designing control strategies. - ✅ **Greenhouse gases** (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, CFCs) absorb outgoing IR radiation and warm the troposphere. - ✅ **Photolysis energy** at λ = 400 nm ≈ 3.1 eV; at λ = 220 nm ≈ 5.6 eV — high-energy UV is required to break O₃, O₂, and CFC bonds. - ✅ **Residence times to remember:** CO ≈ 1–2 months; SO₂ ≈ 1–2 weeks; NO₂ ≈ 1–3 days; O₃ (tropospheric) ≈ 1–3 months. - ✅ **Order of reactivity of OH•-driven reactions:** Isobutene > Propene > Toluene > n-Butane > CH₄ > CO.

topics.title: Smoke, Smog and Ozone Layer Disturbance
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Smoke, Smog and Ozone Layer Disturbance

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Smoke — The KTU Syllabus Definition

**Smoke** is defined as the visible suspension of carbonaceous (soot) and non-carbonaceous solid and liquid particulates in a gaseous effluent resulting from the incomplete combustion of organic (carbon-based) fuels. In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, smoke is treated as a **primary air pollutant** and is quantified by the **Ringelmann Scale (0–5)** and the **Smoke Density / Smoke Number (Bacharach scale)**.

The composition of smoke can be expressed as a heterogeneous aerosol:

$$\text{Smoke} = \underbrace{C(s)}_{\text{Soot}} + \text{Tar Vapours} + \text{Ash} + \underbrace{SO_2, NO_x}_{\text{Gaseous co-pollutants}} + \text{H}_2\text{O (vapour)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Smoke is **NOT** a single chemical species — it is a **two-phase system** (solid + gas). The dark colour arises from elemental carbon (soot) formed when hydrocarbons undergo pyrolysis in oxygen-deficient zones of a flame.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Candle Jar
Imagine a candle burning inside a glass jar. The yellow luminous flame you see is essentially **glowing soot particles** heated to incandescence. If you close the lid, oxygen drops, the flame sputters, and thick black smoke puffs out — that black puff is "industrial smoke" in miniature. The same physics happens in a diesel engine or a coal-fired boiler, except the stakes are urban air quality and human health.

### Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Ringelmann Number:** dimensionless scale **0 (white) → 5 (black)**, where 0 = 0% opacity, 5 = 100% opacity.
- **Smoke Shade / Smoke Density:** measured in **Bacharach units** or **% opacity** using a **Smoke Opacimeter (Ringelmann Chart)**.
- **Particulate Emission Standard (Indian CPCB):** **≤ 150 mg/Nm³** for particulate matter from industrial stacks (corrected to 12% CO₂ basis).
- **Standard Reference Value:** **Particulate Matter (PM)}<2.5\ \mu\text{m}$ = 0** $\mu\text{g/m}^3$** (WHO ideal).

> [!NOTE]
> **Smoke vs. Dust vs. Fume (KTU Frequently Asked Distinction):**
> - **Smoke** → carbonaceous, from **combustion**.
> - **Dust** → inorganic, from **mechanical operations** (crushing, grinding).
> - **Fume** → metallic oxide, from **condensation of metal vapours** (welding).

---

## 1.2 Smog — The KTU Syllabus Definition

**Smog** is a portmanteau of **smoke + fog**, originally coined by Dr. Henry Antoine Des Voeux in **1905 (London)** to describe the dense, dark, sulphur-laden haze that frequently engulfed Victorian-era London. The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates a clear two-type classification.

### The Two Faces of Smog

| Property | Classical (London / Reducing) Smog | Photochemical (Los Angeles / Oxidising) Smog |
|---|---|---|
| **Synonym** | Sulphurous smog, Winter smog, Reducing smog | Oxidising smog, Summer smog, LA-type smog |
| **Season** | Winter (Dec–Feb) | Summer (May–Aug) |
| **Fuel Source** | Coal + Sulphur | Automobiles + NOx + VOCs |
| **Key Pollutant** | SO₂ + Soot + Particulates | O₃ + PAN + NO₂ + VOCs |
| **Chemistry** | Reducing (excess fuel, deficient O₂) | Oxidising (excess O₂, photolysis) |
| **Colour** | Dark grey / black | Yellowish-brown |
| **Health Effect** | Bronchitis, aggravated asthma | Eye irritation, lung damage, plant injury |
| **Visibility** | Very low (<100 m) | Moderate (1–2 km), brown haze |

> [!NOTE]
> **Kerala-Specific KTU Note:** Kochi and the Indo-Gangetic plain experience **mixed-smog** events during the northeast monsoon (Oct–Dec) when continental biomass-burning aerosols (Stubble burning) overlay local vehicular emissions — a hybrid that examiners love to test.

### Conceptual Analogy — The Two Smogs
- **London Smog is like a woollen blanket over a fireplace** — it traps the warm, dirty combustion gases close to the ground, choking everything beneath.
- **LA Smog is like a sun-cooked salad of chemicals** — sunlight is the chef that cooks the raw exhaust into a new toxic product (ozone). You can literally feel the photochemistry by walking into a hazy summer afternoon.

---

## 1.3 Ozone Layer Disturbance — The KTU Syllabus Definition

The **stratospheric ozone layer** resides between **15 km and 35 km altitude** (concentration peak at **~25 km**, in the lower stratosphere) and contains the highest natural concentration of **O₃ (~300–350 Dobson Units, DU)**. Its disturbance refers to the **anthropogenic thinning** of this protective shield, primarily by **Chlorofluorocarbons (CFCs)**, **Halons**, **Carbon tetrachloride (CCl₄)**, and **HCFCs**.

**Ozone Layer Disturbance = Catalytic destruction of stratospheric O₃ by anthropogenic halogen radicals (Cl•, Br•) and dynamic transport phenomena (Polar Vortex).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Must-Know Landmark:** The **Antarctic Ozone Hole** was first reported by **Farman, Gardiner & Shanklin (1985, British Antarctic Survey)** over **Halley Bay**. It is **NOT** a true "hole" (where O₃ = 0) — rather, O₃ concentrations drop below **~220 DU** (the threshold defined by the WMO).

### Conceptual Analogy — The Sunscreen Erosion
Think of the ozone layer as the Earth's **invisible sunscreen (SPF 1000+)**. CFCs are like an unauthorized solvent sprayed on that sunscreen — every single chlorine atom released can destroy ~**100,000** ozone molecules before being deactivated. This is why even a few parts per billion of CFCs cause planet-scale damage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Smoke — Mechanism of Formation

Smoke forms through a **three-stage kinetic pathway** in the combustion chamber:

### Step-by-Step Logic of Soot Formation (KTU High-Yield)

1. **Pyrolysis (Thermal Decomposition):** In fuel-rich, oxygen-starved zones (T > 1000 K), the parent hydrocarbon cracks into smaller radicals and unsaturated species.
2. **Nucleation (Particle Inception):** Polycyclic Aromatic Hydrocarbons (PAHs), notably **pyrene (C₁₆H₁₀)** and **coronene (C₂₄H₁₂)**, stack via van der Waals forces to form the first **~1–2 nm soot nuclei**.
3. **Coagulation + Surface Growth:** Nuclei collide, fuse (coagulate), and accrete gas-phase hydrocarbons on their surface, growing to **0.05–1 μm** primary particles.
4. **Agglomeration:** Primary spheres chain into **fractal aggregates (50–500 nm)**, which is what an electron microscope actually images as "soot."

> **The 'Why' behind each step:** Pyrolysis is the *necessary supplier* of radicals; PAH condensation is the *mandatory precursor*; surface growth (HACA — Hydrogen Abstraction C₂H₂ Addition) explains *why soot is so carbon-rich*.

### Key Quantities of Smoke

- **Smoke Density (Bacharach):** Reflectance of a filter paper through which a fixed volume of flue gas is drawn. Reported as **0–9 scale** (9 = darkest).
- **Opacity (%):** The fraction of incident light **absorbed or scattered** by the smoke plume.

$$\text{Opacity (\%)} = \left(1 - \frac{I_{\text{transmitted}}}{I_{\text{incident}}}\right) \times 100$$

- **Ringelmann Number Equivalence:** Ringelmann **1 = 20% opacity**, Ringelmann **2 = 40%**, **3 = 60%**, **4 = 80%**, **5 = 100%**.

---

## 2.2 Smog — Mechanistic Pathways

### 2.2.1 Classical (London) Smog — The Reducing Pathway

**Net Reaction (simplified):**

$$\text{S (in coal)} + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{SO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{catalytic oxidation, } \text{H}_2\text{O, soot}} \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ (acid mist)}$$

The soot provides the catalytic surface; fog droplets dissolve SO₂ forming sulphurous acid (H₂SO₃), which is then catalytically oxidised to sulphuric acid (H₂SO₄) by dissolved metal ions (Fe³⁺, Mn²⁺). The acid mist is the actual respiratory irritant.

### 2.2.2 Photochemical (LA) Smog — The Oxidising Pathway

This is the **most-tested** smog mechanism in KTU papers. It proceeds in **five cardinal steps**:

1. **Photolysis of NO₂ (the gatekeeper reaction):**
$$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \ (\lambda \leq 430 \text{ nm}) \longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad (1)$$
2. **Ozone formation (O + O₂ + M):**
$$\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \longrightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M} \quad (2)$$
3. **NO + O₃ titration (re-establishes Leighton ratio):**
$$\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \quad (3)$$
4. **VOC radical propagation (OH• chain):**
$$\text{OH} + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{R}^\bullet + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{RO}_2^\bullet$$
$$\text{RO}_2^\bullet + \text{NO} \longrightarrow \text{RO}^\bullet + \text{NO}_2 \quad (4)$$
5. **PAN formation (peroxyacetyl nitrate — eye irritant):**
$$\text{CH}_3\text{CO}\dot{\text{O}}_2 + \text{NO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{CO}\dot{\text{O}}_2\text{NO}_2 \ \ (\text{PAN})$$

> **The 'Why':** Step (1) and (2) explain *why sunlight is mandatory*; Step (3) shows the **photo-stationary state (Leighton ratio)** that would *limit* O₃ if VOCs were absent. Step (4) is the **VOC-fed cycle** that converts NO → NO₂ *without* destroying O₃, allowing O₃ to accumulate. **PAN** is the molecular fingerprint of photochemical smog.

### 2.2.3 Leighton Ratio (KTU Favourite)

$$ \Phi = \frac{[\text{NO}]}{[\text{NO}_2]} = \frac{k_3}{k_1 \cdot J} \approx 0.55 \ \text{at midday summer sunlight} $$

where $J$ is the NO₂ photolysis rate constant. When VOCs are added, NO is consumed (Step 4) without consuming O₃, so $\Phi$ drops and O₃ rises — **this is the diagnostic mathematical signature of photochemical smog**.

---

## 2.3 Ozone Layer — Destruction Catalytic Cycles

### 2.3.1 Chapman Cycle (Natural Background)

The **Chapman Cycle (1930)** describes natural O₃ production and destruction:

$$\text{O}_2 + h\nu \ (\lambda \leq 240\ \text{nm}) \longrightarrow 2\text{O}$$
$$\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \longrightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$$
$$\text{O}_3 + h\nu \ (\lambda \leq 320\ \text{nm}) \longrightarrow \text{O}_2 + \text{O}$$
$$\text{O} + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow 2\text{O}_2}$$

### 2.3.2 The Molina–Rowland CFC–Ozone Cycle (1974 — Nobel 1995)

**Step A — Photolysis of CFC-12 in stratosphere:**
$$\text{CF}_2\text{Cl}_2 + h\nu \ (\lambda \leq 220\ \text{nm}) \longrightarrow \text{CF}_2\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{Cl}^\bullet$$

**Step B — Catalytic O₃ destruction (cycles ~10⁵ times per Cl atom):**
$$\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \quad (i)$$
$$\text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \quad (ii)$$

**Net:** $\text{O}_3 + \text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{O}_2$  ; the **Cl• is regenerated** — the definition of a *catalyst*.

> **The 'Why' of stratospheric residence:** CFCs are **ODP = 1.0 reference**, **GWP up to 10,900** (CFC-12), and have **stratospheric lifetimes of 50–100 years** because they contain no H-atoms to be scrubbed by tropospheric OH•.

### 2.3.3 Heterogeneous PSCs (Polar Stratospheric Clouds) — Antarctic Ozone Hole

The **Ozone Hole** is a **heterogeneous chemistry** phenomenon, NOT a homogeneous one:

- During the **polar night**, temperatures inside the **Polar Vortex** drop below **−78 °C**.
- **Type I PSC** (HNO₃·3H₂O) and **Type II PSC** (H₂O ice) form at 15–25 km.
- On their surfaces:
$$\text{ClONO}_2 + \text{HCl} \xrightarrow{\text{PSC surface}} \text{Cl}_2 + \text{HNO}_3$$
- At **polar sunrise**, sunlight liberates Cl₂ → 2 Cl•, triggering the **explosive** depletion of O₃ in early Antarctic spring (Sept–Oct).

### 2.3.4 NOx and HOx Cycles (Bonus)

- **NOx cycle (Crutzen, 1970):** NO + O₃ → NO₂ + O₂ ; NO₂ + O → NO + O₂
- **HOx cycle:** OH + O₃ → HO₂ + O₂ ; HO₂ + O → OH + O₂

---

## KTU Formula Sheet (Quick Revision Table)

| # | Formula / Quantity | Mathematical Form | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Opacity | $\left(1 - I_t / I_0\right) \times 100$ | % | Smoke measurement |
| 2 | Ringelmann ↔ Opacity | $R = \text{Opacity}/20$ | dimensionless | Smoke chart |
| 3 | Leighton Ratio | $\Phi = [\text{NO}]/[\text{NO}_2] = k_3/(k_1 J)$ | — | Photochemical smog |
| 4 | Pseudo-first-order Cl cycle | $d[\text{O}_3]/dt = -2 k [\text{Cl}] [\text{O}_3]$ | ppb s⁻¹ | Stratospheric O₃ loss |
| 5 | Dobson Unit | $1\ \text{DU} = 2.69 \times 10^{16}\ \text{molec. cm}^{-2}$ | DU | Total column O₃ |
| 6 | ODP definition | $\text{ODP}_x = \dfrac{\Delta[\text{O}_3]_x}{\Delta[\text{O}_3}]_{\text{CFC-11}}$ | — | CFC potential |
| 7 | GWP (100-yr) | $\text{GWP} = \int_0^{100} a_x \cdot [x(t)]/[x(0)] \cdot dt$ | — | Climate impact |
| 8 | Bacharach scale | 0–9 (reflectance inverse) | — | Smoke density |
| 9 | Global mean column O₃ | ≈ 300 DU (pre-1980) → 290 DU (2024) | DU | Background reference |
| 10 | Pollutant Standard Index | $\text{PSI} = \dfrac{(C - C_{\text{low}})}{(C_{\text{high}} - C_{\text{low}})} \times (I_{\text{high}} - I_{\text{low}}) + I_{\text{low}}$ | — | Air quality index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Use:** The Ringelmann chart is still used by CPCB inspectors in India for on-the-spot smoke compliance. The Leighton ratio is the cornerstone of EPA's **Ozone Isopleth Plotting (EKMA box model)** used to design VOC control strategies. ODP/GWP values are the *legal basis* of the **Kigali Amendment (2016)** to the Montreal Protocol.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Leighton Photo-Stationary State (PSS)

**Goal:** Show that without VOCs, the O₃ concentration is **capped** by a fixed ratio to [NOx].

### Given Reactions
$$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \xrightarrow{J} \text{NO} + \text{O} \quad (1)$$
$$\text{O} + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \xrightarrow{k_2} \text{O}_3 + \text{M} \quad (2)$$
$$\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \xrightarrow{k_3} \text{NO}_2 + \text{O}_2 \quad (3)$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

Step 1: Apply the **quasi-steady-state approximation (QSSA)** to the [O] radical (lifetime ~10 ms).

$$\frac{d[\text{O}]}{dt} = J[\text{NO}_2] - k_2[\text{O}][\text{O}_2][\text{M}] = 0$$

$$ \Rightarrow \quad [\text{O}] = \frac{J[\text{NO}_2]}{k_2[\text{O}_2][\text{M}]} $$

This is the rate of O atom supply — equal to the rate of O₃ formation in step (2).

Step 2: The net rate of O₃ production is governed by the difference between the *formation rate* (from step 2) and the *destruction rate* (from step 3).

$$\frac{d[\text{O}_3]}{dt} = k_2[\text{O}][\text{O}_2][\text{M}] - k_3[\text{NO}][\text{O}_3]$$

Step 3: At **photo-stationary state (PSS)**, $d[\text{O}_3]/dt = 0$. Substituting [O] from Step 1:

$$J[\text{NO}_2] = k_3[\text{NO}][\text{O}_3]$$

Step 4: Solve for the **Leighton ratio**:

$$ \frac{[\text{NO}]}{[\text{NO}_2]} = \frac{J}{k_3[\text{O}_3]} $$

Step 5: Therefore, at PSS the O₃ concentration is:

$$ [\text{O}_3]_{\text{PSS}} = \frac{J[\text{NO}_2]}{k_3[\text{NO}]} = \frac{J \cdot [\text{NO}_x]}{k_3 [\text{NO}]([\text{NO}] + [\text{NO}_2])} $$

For a fixed total NOx (= [NO] + [NO₂]), [O₃] is a strong inverse function of [NO]. **This is the math behind why reducing NO emissions alone does not always reduce O₃** — the *VOC/NOx ratio* dictates the regime.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical significance:** If the VOC/NOx ratio is HIGH (VOC-limited regime), VOC control reduces O₃. If LOW (NOx-limited regime), NOx control reduces O₃. This is the basis of the **EPA Ozone Isopleth (EKMA)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Steady-State O₃ Concentration With VOC

Add the VOC radical channel:

$$\text{RO}_2^\bullet + \text{NO} \xrightarrow{k_4} \text{RO}^\bullet + \text{NO}_2$$

The NO → NO₂ conversion now has **two parallel pathways**: by O₃ (Step 3) and by RO₂ (Step 4). New PSS balance:

$$J[\text{NO}_2] = k_3[\text{NO}][\text{O}_3] + k_4[\text{RO}_2^\bullet][\text{NO}]$$

Solving for [O₃]:

$$ [\text{O}_3] = \frac{J[\text{NO}_2] - k_4[\text{RO}_2^\bullet][\text{NO}]}{k_3[\text{NO}]} $$

Because the second term in the numerator is **positive and subtractive on the O₃ sink side**, *more* NO is converted to NO₂ *without* consuming O₃, hence [O₃] **rises**. The rise is linear in [RO₂•] (and thus in VOC concentration).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Catalytic Chain Length of Cl•

**Goal:** Quantify how many O₃ molecules a single Cl• can destroy during its stratospheric residence.

### Lifetime of Cl• in stratosphere

Main loss is via reaction with CH₄ and HCl formation:

$$\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{CH}_4 \xrightarrow{k_a} \text{HCl} + \text{CH}_3^\bullet$$

with $k_a \approx 1.0 \times 10^{-13}\ \text{cm}^3\,\text{molec}^{-1}\,\text{s}^{-1}$ at 25 km (T ≈ 220 K).

### Derivation Steps

Step 1: Pseudo-first-order loss rate of Cl•:

$$\tau_{\text{Cl}}^{-1} = k_a [\text{CH}_4] = (1.0 \times 10^{-13})(1.5 \times 10^{13}) \approx 1.5 \times 10^{0}\ \text{s}^{-1}$$

$$\Rightarrow \tau_{\text{Cl}} \approx 0.67\ \text{s per Cl–O}_3\ \text{collision}$$

Step 2: Rate of O₃ destruction by Cl• (per Cl atom):

$$R_{\text{destroy}} = k_i[\text{Cl}^\bullet][\text{O}_3] = (2.9 \times 10^{-11})(5 \times 10^{12}) \approx 145\ \text{cm}^{-3}\,\text{s}^{-1}$$

Step 3: Number of O₃ molecules destroyed during the residence time $\tau_{\text{res}}$ of the Cl radical before it is sequestered as HCl:

$$ N_{\text{O}_3} = k_i [\text{O}_3] \cdot \tau_{\text{res}} $$

If $\tau_{\text{res}} \approx 10^4\ \text{s}$ (typical), then:

$$ N_{\text{O}_3} = (2.9 \times 10^{-11})(5 \times 10^{12})(10^4) \approx 1.45 \times 10^{6} $$

Step 4: **Chain length** (number of O₃ destroyed per Cl• released):

$$ \boxed{\text{Chain Length} = \frac{k_i [\text{O}_3]}{k_a [\text{CH}_4]} \approx \frac{2.9 \times 10^{-11} \cdot 5 \times 10^{12}}{1.0 \times 10^{-13} \cdot 1.5 \times 10^{13}} \approx 96{,}667 } $$

This gives the textbook KTU value of **~10⁵** ozone molecules destroyed per Cl• — the *quantitative justification* for the severity of stratospheric chlorine loading.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Property | Classical Smog | Photochemical Smog | Stratospheric O₃ Loss |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Dominant species** | SO₂, soot, H₂SO₄ | O₃, PAN, NO₂ | ClO, ClONO₂ |
| **Photochemistry role** | None | Sunlight = reagent | UV = Cl• source |
| **Catalyst** | Soot, metal ions | NO, NO₂ | Cl•, Br•, OH•, NO |
| **Net reaction** | S → H₂SO₄ | VOC + NOx + hv → O₃ + PAN | O₃ → O₂ (catalysed) |
| **Control strategy** | Desulphurisation, tall stacks | VOC & NOx reduction | CFC phase-out (Montreal) |
| **Time of day peak** | Night / early morning | Afternoon (2–4 pm) | Spring (Sept–Oct for SH) |
| **Temperature** | Cold (0–10 °C) | Hot, sunny (>25 °C) | Very cold (PSC) |
| **Air parcel** | Stagnant, humid | Convective, dry | Polar Vortex |
| **Discovered / Coined** | 1905 (Des Voeux, London) | 1940s–50s (Haagen-Smit, LA) | 1985 (Farman, Antarctic) |
| **Nobel connection** | — | — | 1995 (Crutzen, Molina, Rowland) |
| **Indian city most affected** | Delhi (winter), Kanpur | Delhi (summer), Mumbai | Tropical band (weakly) |
| **Key KTU keyword** | "**Reducing**" | "**Oxidising**" | "**Heterogeneous / PSCs**" |
| **First reported event** | 1952 Great London Smog (~12,000 deaths) | 1943 LA "gas-eye" episode | 1985 Antarctic "Hole" |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Soot Formation Pathway (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Fuel Hydrocarbon] --> B[Pyrolysis at T greater than 1000 K]
    B --> C[Acetylene and PAH Precursors]
    C --> D[Nucleation: 1 to 2 nm Soot Nuclei]
    D --> E[Surface Growth via HACA Mechanism]
    E --> F[Coagulation: 50 to 1000 nm Spherules]
    F --> G[Agglomeration: Fractal Chain Aggregates]
    G --> H[Visible Smoke Plume]
    H --> I[Ringelmann Number measured by Opacimeter]
    I --> J{Opacity compliance?}
    J -->|Yes| K[Emission passes CPCB norm]
    J -->|No| L[Baghouse / ESP / Scrubber retrofit]
```

## 4.2 Photochemical Smog Formation Cycle (Mermaid Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[NO2 emitted from vehicles] --> B[Solar UV less than 430 nm]
    B --> C[NO plus O radical]
    C --> D[O plus O2 plus M]
    D --> E[O3 formed]
    E --> F[O3 plus NO titration]
    F --> A
    G[VOCs from petrol evaporation] --> H[OH radical attack]
    H --> I[RO2 peroxy radical]
    I --> J[RO2 plus NO reaction]
    J --> K[NO2 regenerated without O3 loss]
    K --> E
    E --> L[O3 accumulates]
    I --> M[CH3CO3 plus NO2]
    M --> N[PAN peroxyacetyl nitrate]
    N --> O[Eye irritant and plant toxicant]
```

## 4.3 Stratospheric Ozone Depletion Chain (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CFC 12 released at ground level] --> B[Tropospheric transport 5 to 15 years]
    B --> C[Stratospheric injection at 15 to 50 km]
    C --> D[UV photolysis at lambda less than 220 nm]
    D --> E[Cl radical released]
    E --> F[Cl plus O3]
    F --> G[ClO plus O2]
    G --> H[ClO plus O atom]
    H --> E
    E --> I[Cl radical regenerated chain of 10 to the 5]
    I --> J[Net O3 plus O becomes 2 O2]
    J --> K[Ozone column thinned measured in DU]
    K --> L[Antarctic Ozone Hole below 220 DU]
    M[Polar Stratospheric Clouds form below minus 78 C] --> N[Heterogeneous activation]
    N --> O[ClONO2 plus HCl on PSC surface]
    O --> P[Cl2 released at polar sunrise]
    P --> E
```

## 4.4 Comparative Smog Architecture (Mermaid Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CS[Classical Smog System]
        C1[Coal combustion] --> C2[SO2 and soot emission]
        C2 --> C3[Fog water and metal catalysis]
        C3 --> C4[H2SO4 acid mist]
        C4 --> C5[Bronchitis and visibility loss]
    end
    subgraph PS[Photochemical Smog System]
        P1[Vehicle exhaust] --> P2[NOx and VOC release]
        P2 --> P3[Sunlight photolysis]
        P3 --> P4[O3 and PAN build up]
        P4 --> P5[Eye irritation and crop damage]
    end
    subgraph OS[Ozone Layer Disturbance]
        O1[CFC emission] --> O2[Stratosphere photolysis]
        O2 --> O3[Cl radical cycle]
        O3 --> O4[O3 destruction 10 to the 5 per Cl]
        O4 --> O5[UV B increase at surface]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading tip for KTU diagrams:** Always start from the *emission source* (left), follow the *transformation pathway* (middle), and end at the *receptor impact* (right). Examiners reward schematic clarity in the 14-mark questions.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PECET756]**
**Define smoke. With a neat sketch, explain the Ringelmann scale for the measurement of smoke density.**

**Model Answer:**

**Smoke:** Smoke is a visible colloidal suspension of solid carbon particles (soot), tar vapours, ash, and associated gases (SO₂, NOₓ) produced by the **incomplete combustion of carbonaceous fuels**.

**Ringelmann Scale:** It is a visual **comparative opacity chart** ranging from **0 to 5**, where:
- Ringelmann **0** = pure white card (0% opacity).
- Ringelmann **1** = 20% black grid (20% opacity).
- Ringelmann **2** = 40% opacity.
- Ringelmann **3** = 60% opacity.
- Ringelmann **4** = 80% opacity.
- Ringelmann **5** = pure black (100% opacity).

An observer compares the **grey shade of the smoke plume against the chart held at a fixed distance**, normally with the sun behind the observer. CPCB regulations in India permit industrial stack emissions up to **Ringelmann 1** for new plants.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, PECET756]**
**Distinguish between classical (London-type) smog and photochemical (Los Angeles-type) smog. List any four points.**

**Model Answer (4 distinguishing points):**

| # | Classical Smog | Photochemical Smog |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Occurs in **winter**, cold, humid conditions | Occurs in **summer**, hot, dry, sunny conditions |
| 2 | Formed by **SO₂ + soot + fog** | Formed by **NOₓ + VOC + sunlight** |
| 3 | **Reducing** in nature; no photochemistry | **Oxidising** in nature; requires photolysis |
| 4 | Key pollutant is **H₂SO₄ acid mist**; affects respiratory system | Key pollutants are **O₃ and PAN**; cause eye irritation and crop damage |
| 5 | First reported in **London (1952)** | First reported in **Los Angeles (1943)** |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Long-Answer Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** [7 marks] With balanced chemical equations, explain the **photochemical smog formation mechanism**. Discuss the role of **NOₓ, VOCs, and solar radiation** in the production of **ozone** and **PAN**.

**(b)** [7 marks] What is the **Leighton ratio**? Derive the **photo-stationary-state equation** for ozone concentration and show mathematically how the addition of VOCs leads to ozone accumulation.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Photochemical smog is a secondary, oxidising air pollution phenomenon caused by the **photochemically driven reaction of NOₓ and VOCs** in the presence of **sunlight (λ < 430 nm)**, producing a mixture of oxidants such as **O₃, PAN, HNO₃**, and aldehydes.

**2. Primary reactions (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — NO₂ photolysis (initiation):**
$$\text{NO}_2 + h\nu \ (\lambda \le 430\ \text{nm}) \longrightarrow \text{NO} + \text{O}^\bullet$$

**Step 2 — Ozone formation:**
$$\text{O}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 + \text{M} \longrightarrow \text{O}_3 + \text{M}$$

**Step 3 — VOC attack by OH•:**
$$\text{OH}^\bullet + \text{RH} \longrightarrow \text{R}^\bullet + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$
$$\text{R}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 \longrightarrow \text{RO}_2^\bullet$$
$$\text{RO}_2^\bullet + \text{NO} \longrightarrow \text{RO}^\bullet + \text{NO}_2$$

**Step 4 — PAN formation:**
$$\text{CH}_3\text{COO}_2^\bullet + \text{NO}_2 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{COO}_2\text{NO}_2\ (\text{PAN})$$

**3. Role of each factor (3 marks):**
- **NOₓ:** Source of O₃ via photolysis; reacts with RO₂• to regenerate NO₂, sustaining the cycle.
- **VOCs:** Provide RO₂• radicals that convert NO to NO₂ *without* consuming O₃, breaking the Leighton limit.
- **Solar radiation:** Provides the energy to photolyse NO₂ and to drive the radical chain; intensity governs reaction rate (peaks 12:00–16:00).

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**1. Leighton Ratio definition (1 mark):**
$$ \Phi = \frac{[\text{NO}]}{[\text{NO}_2]} $$

**2. QSSA on [O] (1 mark):**
$$ [\text{O}] = \frac{J[\text{NO}_2]}{k_2[\text{O}_2][\text{M}]} $$

**3. PSS on O₃ (2 marks):**
$$ J[\text{NO}_2] = k_3[\text{NO}][\text{O}_3] $$
$$ \Rightarrow \quad [\text{O}_3]_{\text{PSS}} = \frac{J[\text{NO}_2]}{k_3[\text{NO}]} $$

**4. With VOC addition (2 marks):**
$$ J[\text{NO}_2] = k_3[\text{NO}][\text{O}_3] + k_4[\text{RO}_2^\bullet][\text{NO}] $$
$$ [\text{O}_3] = \frac{J[\text{NO}_2] - k_4[\text{RO}_2^\bullet][\text{NO}]}{k_3[\text{NO}]} $$

The extra term $k_4[\text{RO}_2^\bullet][\text{NO}]$ in the numerator is **subtractive from the O₃ sink side**, hence [O₃] **rises** linearly with [RO₂•] ∝ [VOC].

**5. Conclusion (1 mark):**
The Leighton ratio drops as VOCs increase, mathematically proving that **VOC control** in a VOC-limited regime is the key to reducing tropospheric ozone.

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — **CHOICE 2** (Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Module 1]**

**(a)** [7 marks] What is meant by **depletion of the ozone layer**? With reactions, describe the **Molina–Rowland mechanism** for the catalytic destruction of stratospheric ozone by **CFCs**. Why is the chain length so high?

**(b)** [7 marks] Explain the formation of the **Antarctic Ozone Hole** with reference to **Polar Stratospheric Clouds (PSCs)** and the role of the **Polar Vortex**. Mention the **Montreal Protocol (1987)** and **Kigali Amendment (2016)** in this context.

---

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 marks

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Depletion of the ozone layer refers to the **anthropogenic reduction in the stratospheric ozone column** (normally ~300 DU) primarily by **halogen radicals (Cl•, Br•)** released from stable, man-made chemicals like **CFCs, Halons, and CCl₄** that are photolysed by UV radiation.

**2. CFC-12 photolysis (1 mark):**
$$ \text{CF}_2\text{Cl}_2 + h\nu \ (\lambda \le 220\ \text{nm}) \longrightarrow \text{CF}_2\text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{Cl}^\bullet $$

**3. Catalytic Cl cycle (2 marks):**
$$ \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_3 \longrightarrow \text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 $$
$$ \text{ClO}^\bullet + \text{O} \longrightarrow \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{O}_2 $$
$$ \boxed{\text{Net:}\ \text{O}_3 + \text{O} \longrightarrow 2\text{O}_2} $$

**4. Chain length explanation (2 marks):**
A single Cl• destroys ~10⁵ O₃ molecules because the **regeneration step (ClO + O → Cl + O₂)** is fast and the **Cl sink (Cl + CH₄ → HCl)** is slow in the dry stratosphere. The Cl• is **resuspended** as Cl₂ on PSCs, allowing perpetual cycling. The chain length $\approx k_i[\text{O}_3]/k_a[\text{CH}_4] \approx 10^5$.

**5. Deactivation reservoir (1 mark):**
$$ \text{Cl}^\bullet + \text{CH}_4 \longrightarrow \text{HCl} + \text{CH}_3^\bullet $$
HCl is the temporary reservoir species; it is reactivated heterogeneously on PSCs.

---

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 marks

**1. Polar Vortex (1 mark):** A strong circumpolar wind system that isolates Antarctic air in winter, allowing temperatures to plummet below **−78 °C** in the lower stratosphere.

**2. PSC formation (1.5 marks):** At very low temperatures, **Type I (HNO₃·3H₂O)** and **Type II (H₂O ice)** PSCs form at 15–25 km. They provide enormous surface area for heterogeneous reactions.

**3. Heterogeneous chemistry (1.5 marks):**
$$ \text{ClONO}_2 + \text{HCl} \xrightarrow{\text{PSC surface}} \text{Cl}_2\ (\text{g}) + \text{HNO}_3\ (\text{particulate}) $$
$$ \text{HOCl} + \text{HCl} \xrightarrow{\text{PSC}} \text{Cl}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O} $$

**4. Spring photolysis and O₃ hole (1.5 marks):** At **polar sunrise (Sept–Oct)**, Cl₂ is photolysed to 2 Cl•, triggering massive O₃ destruction — column drops below **220 DU**, defining the "ozone hole."

**5. Montreal + Kigali (1.5 marks):**
- **Montreal Protocol (1987):** First international treaty to phase out CFCs; ratified by 198 countries (the only UN treaty with universal ratification).
- **Kigali Amendment (2016):** Extends phase-out to **HFCs** (high GWP refrigerants) under the same framework to mitigate **climate change** (avoids ~0.5 °C warming by 2100).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Spots:**
> 1. Writing **smog = smoke** or **smog = fog** (it is *smoke + fog*; partial mark only).
> 2. Confusing **Leighton ratio** direction (it's NO/NO₂, NOT NO₂/NO).
> 3. Forgetting that **Ozone Hole is NOT a true hole** — it's a region of *thinned* O₃ below 220 DU.
> 4. Missing the **photolysis wavelength** in NO₂ reaction (must mention **λ ≤ 430 nm**).
> 5. Not citing **Crutzen, Molina, Rowland (Nobel 1995)** for stratospheric ozone depletion — KTU examiners reward names.
> 6. Treating **smoke** as a gas — it is an **aerosol (solid + liquid dispersed in gas)**.
> 7. Confusing **ODP (Ozone Depletion Potential)** with **GWP (Global Warming Potential)**.
> 8. Forgetting to mention **PAN** as the eye-irritant in photochemical smog.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)

- ✅ **Smoke** = Carbonaceous aerosol from incomplete combustion; quantified by **Ringelmann (0–5)** and **Bacharach (0–9)** scales.
- ✅ **Smog** has **two** types: **Classical (reducing, SO₂)** vs **Photochemical (oxidising, O₃)** — both must be contrasted.
- ✅ The **photo-stationary state (Leighton ratio)** is the *mathematical heart* of photochemical smog theory.
- ✅ **PAN (peroxyacetyl nitrate)** is the molecular signature of photochemical smog and the cause of eye irritation.
- ✅ **Ozone layer** is in the **stratosphere (15–35 km)**, peak at **~25 km**; background ≈ **300 Dobson Units**.
- ✅ **Ozone Hole** is **NOT** a hole — it is a region where O₃ < **220 DU** over Antarctica in **Sept–Oct**.
- ✅ **CFC–O₃ cycle** is **catalytic** (Cl• regenerated), chain length ~**10⁵** per Cl• released.
- ✅ **PSCs (Polar Stratospheric Clouds)** are the **heterogeneous reactors** that unlock the Antarctic O₃ destruction.
- ✅ **Montreal Protocol (1987)** phased out CFCs; **Kigali Amendment (2016)** added HFCs.
- ✅ **Crutzen, Molina, Rowland** — **Nobel Prize in Chemistry 1995** — must be cited in any stratospheric chemistry answer.
- ✅ **Chapman Cycle (1930)** = natural O₃ background; **Molina–Rowland (1974)** = anthropogenic destruction.
- ✅ **ODP** reference = **CFC-11 (CCl₃F) = 1.0**; **CFC-12 ODP = 1.0**; **HCFC-22 ODP = 0.055**; **HFC-134a ODP = 0**.
- ✅ **WHO PM₂.₅ guideline = 15 µg/m³ (24-hr)**; **Indian NAAQS PM₂.₅ = 60 µg/m³ (24-hr)**.
- ✅ **Photochemical smog peaks in summer afternoons**; **classical smog peaks in winter mornings**.
- ✅ **India's most smog-affected metros**: Delhi (both types, seasonally), Mumbai (photochemical), Kolkata (mixed).

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "SOAP" for Smog Types:**
> **S**moke + f**O**g = **A**cidic (London) → **P**ollutants SO₂/H₂SO₄.
> **S**unlit + NOₓ + VO**C**s = **O**xidising (LA) → **P**AN + O₃.

---
