topics.title: Greenhouse Effect
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Greenhouse Effect

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greenhouse Effect (KTU Syllabus Definition):**
> The Greenhouse Effect is a natural radiative transfer phenomenon in which short-wave solar radiation (insolation) emitted by the Sun passes relatively unimpeded through the Earth's atmosphere, is absorbed by the Earth's surface, and is re-emitted as long-wave (infrared) terrestrial radiation. This outgoing long-wave radiation (OLR) is partially absorbed and re-emitted by trace atmospheric constituents known as **Greenhouse Gases (GHGs)**, thereby trapping a portion of the thermal energy within the lower troposphere and warming the Earth's surface to a mean temperature of approximately **288 K (15 °C)**.

Without this naturally occurring effect, the Earth's effective radiating temperature, computed purely from the Stefan–Boltzmann equilibrium, would be about **255 K (–18 °C)**, rendering the planet uninhabitable for most contemporary life forms.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Blanket on a Bed

Imagine sleeping under a **thin cotton blanket** on a cold winter night:
- Your body generates heat (analogous to **Earth's re-emitted IR radiation**).
- The blanket does not generate any heat of its own, yet you feel warm because it **traps** the heat you release (analogous to **GHGs absorbing OLR**).
- If you add a second blanket (analogous to **increased atmospheric GHG concentration**), you get uncomfortably hot — this is **global warming** in climatic analogy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic Tip for KTU Viva:** Just as glass walls of a greenhouse let sunlight in but trap the warm air inside, GHGs let visible light through but absorb outgoing infrared radiation — hence the name "Greenhouse Effect."

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar constant (at TOA) | $S_0$ | **1361** | $W/m^2$ |
| Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ | $W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}$ |
| Earth's albedo (planetary) | $\alpha$ | **0.30** | dimensionless |
| Mean surface temperature | $T_s$ | 288 | K |
| Effective radiating temperature | $T_e$ | 255 | K |
| Greenhouse warming increment | $\Delta T$ | **+33** | K |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Step-by-Step Physical Mechanism

1. **Solar Short-Wave Input:** The Sun emits radiation predominantly in the 0.2–3.0 µm band (visible + UV). Approximately **342 W/m²** of average insolation reaches the top of the atmosphere (TOA), distributed over a sphere (factor of 4 reduction from disk area).
2. **Atmospheric Transmission & Reflection:** Clouds, aerosols, and the surface reflect about **30 %** ($\alpha = 0.30$) back to space. The remaining **~240 W/m²** is absorbed by the surface and atmosphere.
3. **Surface Absorption & Re-emission:** The warmed Earth’s surface emits radiation per the Stefan–Boltzmann law, peaking in the infrared band **8–14 µm** (the atmospheric "window" for clear-sky OLR).
4. **GHG Absorption Bands:** Polyatomic GHGs (CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, H₂O, O₃, CFCs) possess vibrational-rotational modes that couple with specific IR wavelengths, absorbing outgoing IR.
5. **Counter-Radiation (Back-Radiation):** The excited GHGs re-emit IR isotropically — half goes back to Earth, half escapes to space. The downward flux is called **atmospheric counter-radiation** ($L^{\downarrow}$).
6. **Net Radiative Equilibrium:** Energy in = Energy out. The surface temperature adjusts upward until the *enhanced* outgoing long-wave radiation balances incoming short-wave.
7. **Anthropogenic Enhancement:** Combustion of fossil fuels, deforestation, and industrial processes raise the mixing ratios of long-lived GHGs, intensifying step 4 and 5 — driving **global warming**.

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these five equations — they cover ~90 % of numerical problems in Module 1.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\textbf{(1) Incoming Solar Flux at TOA (disk-averaged)} \\
&\qquad F_{in} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{4} \;=\; \frac{1361}{4} \;=\; 340.25 \;\; W/m^2 \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(2) Absorbed Solar Flux (after albedo)} \\
&\qquad F_{abs} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{4}\,(1 - \alpha) \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(3) Effective Radiating Temperature (no atmosphere)} \\
&\qquad T_e \;=\; \left[\frac{S_0}{4\sigma(1-\alpha)}\right]^{1/4} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(4) Stefan–Boltzmann Outgoing IR Flux} \\
&\qquad F_{out} \;=\; \varepsilon\,\sigma\,T_s^{4} \\[6pt]
&\textbf{(5) Radiative Forcing (IPCC AR6 definition)} \\
&\qquad \Delta F \;=\; 5.35\,\ln\!\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) \;\; \text{for CO}_2 \;\; [W/m^2] \\[4pt]
&\textbf{(6) Global Warming Potential (GWP)} \\
&\qquad GWP_x \;=\; \frac{\int_0^{TH} a_x\,C_x(t)\,dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2}\,C_{CO_2}(t)\,dt}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $S_0$ | Total Solar Irradiance (TSI) | $W/m^2$ |
| $\alpha$ | Bond albedo (Earth) | dimensionless (≈ 0.30) |
| $\sigma$ | Stefan–Boltzmann constant | $W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}$ |
| $\varepsilon$ | Emissivity of surface | dimensionless |
| $T_s$ | Surface absolute temperature | K |
| $C$ | Current CO₂ concentration | ppm |
| $C_0$ | Reference CO₂ concentration | ppm |
| $GWP_x$ | GWP of gas $x$ over time horizon $TH$ | dimensionless |
| $a_x$ | Radiative efficiency of gas $x$ | $W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot ppb^{-1}$ |

### 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The quantification of the greenhouse effect underpins:
- **Climate-conscious HVAC design** — selecting refrigerants with low GWP (e.g., R-32 vs R-410A).
- **Carbon capture & storage (CCS)** sizing — mass balance of $\Delta F$ to allowable emission caps.
- **Building energy codes** — U-factor and SHGC calculations implicitly counter greenhouse trapping in urban canopies.
- **Emissions trading** — pricing of CO₂-equivalent credits ($\text{CO}_2\text{e}$).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation — Earth's Effective Radiating Temperature $T_e$

This is a **very high-yield derivation** for KTU 14-mark problems.

**Step 1: Solar flux intercepted by Earth's disk**
The Sun emits $S_0$ perpendicular to a unit area. Earth intercepts this flux over its **cross-sectional disk** $\pi R^{2}$, but re-radiates from its **full spherical surface** $4\pi R^{2}$.

$$
\text{Flux intercepted per unit Earth area} \;=\; \frac{S_0 \cdot \pi R^{2}}{4\pi R^{2}} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{4}
$$

**Step 2: Apply planetary albedo correction**
A fraction $\alpha$ of the incoming radiation is reflected back to space without being absorbed.

$$
F_{abs} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{4}\,(1 - \alpha)
$$

**Step 3: Equate absorbed flux to outgoing blackbody flux (energy balance)**

$$
\frac{S_0}{4}\,(1 - \alpha) \;=\; \sigma\,T_e^{4}
$$

**Step 4: Solve for $T_e$**

$$
T_e \;=\; \left[\frac{S_0}{4\sigma(1-\alpha)}\right]^{1/4}
$$

**Step 5: Numerical evaluation**

$$
T_e \;=\; \left[\frac{1361}{4 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (1 - 0.30)}\right]^{1/4}
$$

$$
T_e \;=\; \left[\frac{1361}{1.5876 \times 10^{-7}}\right]^{1/4} \;=\; \left[8.572 \times 10^{9}\right]^{1/4}
$$

$$
\boxed{T_e \;\approx\; 254.97 \;\; K \;\; (\approx -18.03^\circ C)}
$$

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Principal GHGs

| Gas | Pre-industrial (ppb) | 2023 Level (ppb) | Lifetime (yrs) | GWP-100 | Major Source |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂** | 280 000 | 421 000 | 300–1000 | **1** (ref) | Fossil combustion, deforestation |
| **CH₄** | 700 | 1 920 | 12 | **27–30** | Livestock, landfills, oil-gas |
| **N₂O** | 270 | 336 | 109 | **273** | Fertilizers, industry |
| **HFC-134a** | 0 | 90 | 14 | **1 430** | Refrigerants, ACs |
| **SF₆** | 0 | 11 | 3 200 | **25 200** | Electrical switchgear |
| **CFC-12** | 0 | 0.5 | 100 | **10 900** | (Phased out — Montreal Protocol) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Favourite:** "Why is SF₆ only 11 ppt yet so climatically dangerous?" — Because its **GWP-100 is 25 200**, meaning 1 tonne of SF₆ traps as much heat as **25 200 tonnes of CO₂** over a century.

### 3.3 Worked Example — Radiative Forcing from CO₂ Doubling

**Problem (KTU Pattern):** Pre-industrial CO₂ = 280 ppm; current = 420 ppm. Compute the radiative forcing $\Delta F$.

**Step 1:** Plug into the logarithmic forcing relation:

$$
\Delta F \;=\; 5.35\,\ln\!\left(\frac{420}{280}\right)
$$

**Step 2:** Compute ratio:

$$
\frac{420}{280} \;=\; 1.50
$$

**Step 3:** Natural logarithm:

$$
\ln(1.50) \;=\; 0.4055
$$

**Step 4:** Multiply:

$$
\Delta F \;=\; 5.35 \times 0.4055 \;\approx\; 2.17 \;\; W/m^2
$$

**Step 5:** Climate sensitivity interpretation. Using equilibrium climate sensitivity $ECS = 0.8 \, K$ per $W/m^2$ forcing:

$$
\Delta T_{eq} \;=\; ECS \times \Delta F \;=\; 0.8 \times 2.17 \;\approx\; 1.74 \;\; K
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conclusion:** A mere **50 % rise** in CO₂ from the industrial baseline forces an additional **2.17 W/m²** of trapped heat — equivalent to running **~2 billion extra 100 W bulbs continuously over every square metre of Earth**. This is why CO₂ is the primary driver of observed global warming.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Flow — Atmospheric Radiative Energy Budget

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    SUN[Sun T 5778 K] -->|Shortwave 342 W/m2| TOA[Top of Atmosphere]
    TOA -->|Reflect 30 percent| SPACE1[Reflected to Space]
    TOA -->|Transmit 70 percent| ATM[Atmospheric Column]
    ATM -->|Absorb 23 percent| ATMABS[Atmospheric SW Abs]
    ATM -->|Transmit 47 percent| SRF[Earth Surface]
    SRF -->|Absorb 47 percent| SRFABS[Surface SW Abs]
    SRFABS -->|Re-emit IR sigma T4| OLROUT[Outgoing Longwave 396 W/m2]
    SRFABS -->|Latent Heat 80| LAT[Evapotranspiration]
    SRFABS -->|Sensible Heat 17| SENS[Convection]
    OLROUT -->|GHG absorb 90 percent| GHG[Greenhouse Gases CO2 CH4 H2O]
    GHG -->|Back-radiation 333 W/m2| CR[Counter Radiation to Surface]
    GHG -->|Escape 63 W/m2| SPACE2[To Space through window]
    CR -->|Reinforce T surface| SRFABS
```

### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Carbon Cycle & Climate Feedback

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EMIT[Anthropogenic Sources]
        FF[Fossil Fuel Combustion 9 GtC per yr]
        DEF[Deforestation 1.5 GtC per yr]
        IND[Industrial Processes 1 GtC per yr]
    end

    subgraph RES[Atmospheric Reservoirs]
        ATM_CO2[Atmospheric CO2 421 ppm]
        CH4[Atmospheric CH4 1920 ppb]
        N2O[Atmospheric N2O 336 ppb]
    end

    subgraph SINK[Natural Sinks]
        OCN[Ocean Uptake 26 percent]
        BIO[Land Biosphere 28 percent]
        SIL[Silicate Weathering]
    end

    subgraph FEED[Climate Feedback Loops]
        POS1[Ice Albedo Positive]
        POS2[Water Vapour Positive]
        NEG1[Carbon Fertilisation Negative]
    end

    FF --> ATM_CO2
    DEF --> ATM_CO2
    IND --> ATM_CO2
    ATM_CO2 --> OCN
    ATM_CO2 --> BIO
    OCN --> SIL
    BIO --> SIL
    ATM_CO2 --> POS1
    ATM_CO2 --> POS2
    ATM_CO2 --> NEG1
    POS1 --> ATM_CO2
    POS2 --> ATM_CO2
    NEG1 --> ATM_CO2
```

### 4.3 Spectral Absorption Bands of Major GHGs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph VIS[Visible 0.4 to 0.7 micrometre]
        O3V[O3 minor absorption]
    end
    subgraph NIR[Near IR 0.7 to 3 micrometre]
        H2O_N[H2O bands]
        CO2_N[CO2 1.6 and 2.0 micrometre]
    end
    subgraph MIR[Mid IR 3 to 8 micrometre]
        CH4_M[CH4 3.3 and 7.7 micrometre]
        N2O_M[N2O 4.5 and 7.8 micrometre]
    end
    subgraph THERM[Thermal IR 8 to 14 micrometre Window]
        WIN[Atmospheric Window 8 to 12 micrometre]
    end
    subgraph FIR[Far IR 14 to 100 micrometre]
        CO2_F[CO2 15 micrometre strong band]
        H2O_F[H2O continuum]
        O3_F[O3 9.6 micrometre]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Tip:** The **15 µm CO₂ band** is the dominant GHG absorption feature for outgoing terrestrial radiation — any KTU question on "most influential greenhouse gas" must reference this band.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Greenhouse Effect." List any four major greenhouse gases with their typical atmospheric lifetimes.*

**Model Answer:**

The Greenhouse Effect is the natural atmospheric process by which trace gases (greenhouse gases) absorb and re-emit the long-wave infrared radiation emitted by the Earth's surface, thereby warming the lower atmosphere and the surface itself.

Four major GHGs and lifetimes:

| Gas | Atmospheric Lifetime |
|---|---|
| CO₂ | 300–1000 years (variable across reservoirs) |
| CH₄ | ~12 years |
| N₂O | ~109 years |
| SF₆ | ~3 200 years |

**[3 Marks: Definition 1, List 2]**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*What is meant by Global Warming Potential (GWP)? Why is methane's GWP higher than CO₂'s despite a shorter atmospheric lifetime?*

**Model Answer:**

GWP is an index measuring the **time-integrated radiative forcing** of a unit mass of a given GHG relative to the same mass of CO₂ over a chosen time horizon (commonly 100 years).

$$
GWP_x \;=\; \frac{\int_0^{TH} a_x\,C_x(t)\,dt}{\int_0^{TH} a_{CO_2}\,C_{CO_2}(t)\,dt}
$$

Methane's GWP (≈ 28) exceeds CO₂'s (1) because:
- CH₄ has a **per-molecule radiative efficiency** ~28× that of CO₂.
- Although it has a shorter lifetime (~12 yrs), its decay is **slow on the 100-yr horizon**, so a large fraction remains trapped.
- CO₂, despite very long lifetime, has weaker per-molecule absorption cross-section.

**[3 Marks]**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Model)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) With neat sketches, explain the physical mechanism of the Greenhouse Effect. Discuss the role of major GHGs in altering Earth's energy balance. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Mechanism — Stepwise description**

1. **Solar input:** The Sun delivers $S_0 \approx 1361 \, W/m^2$ at TOA; averaged over the Earth sphere, this yields $\sim 342 \, W/m^2$.
2. **Albedo reflection:** Clouds, aerosols, and bright surfaces reflect 30 % back to space ($\alpha = 0.30$).
3. **Surface absorption:** The remaining $\sim 240 \, W/m^2$ heats the ground.
4. **IR emission:** Earth radiates back as blackbody IR at $T_s \approx 288 \, K$, peaking near 10 µm.
5. **GHG absorption:** CO₂ (15 µm), H₂O (6.3 µm and continuum), CH₄ (7.7 µm), N₂O (7.8 µm), O₃ (9.6 µm) absorb this IR.
6. **Counter-radiation:** Re-emitted IR is split — half returns to surface (downward flux ~ 333 W/m²), half escapes upward (~ 63 W/m² through the 8–12 µm window).

**(ii) Role of major GHGs (Table form for marks)**

| GHG | Absorption Band (µm) | Relative Contribution |
|---|---|---|
| H₂O vapour | 6.3 + continuum | 60 % |
| CO₂ | 15.0 | 20 % |
| O₃ | 9.6 | 7 % |
| CH₄ + N₂O | 7.7 / 7.8 | 5 % combined |
| CFCs | 8–12 | trace but high GWP |

**(iii) Sketch** — refer to the Mermaid flowchart in Section 4.1. [In pen-and-paper KTU exams, draw a labelled cross-section of atmosphere showing incoming SW, surface emission, and counter-radiation.]

**(b) Derive the expression for Earth's effective radiating temperature $T_e$ in the absence of any atmosphere. Evaluate numerically using $S_0 = 1361 \, W/m^2$, $\alpha = 0.30$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}$. Comment on the discrepancy with the observed surface temperature. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Flux intercepted per unit Earth area:**

The Sun's parallel rays fall on a disk of area $\pi R^2$, but the Earth re-radiates from its entire spherical surface $4\pi R^2$. Hence the average intercepted flux per unit area:

$$
F_{in} \;=\; \frac{S_0 \cdot \pi R^{2}}{4\pi R^{2}} \;=\; \frac{S_0}{4} \;=\; \frac{1361}{4} \;=\; 340.25 \;\; W/m^2
$$

**Step 2 — Subtract reflected portion using Bond albedo $\alpha = 0.30$:**

$$
F_{abs} \;=\; (1 - \alpha)\,\frac{S_0}{4} \;=\; 0.70 \times 340.25 \;=\; 238.18 \;\; W/m^2
$$

**Step 3 — Radiative equilibrium with blackbody emission:**

$$
F_{abs} \;=\; \sigma\,T_e^{4}
$$

$$
T_e^{4} \;=\; \frac{F_{abs}}{\sigma} \;=\; \frac{238.18}{5.67 \times 10^{-8}} \;=\; 4.201 \times 10^{9} \;\; K^{4}
$$

**Step 4 — Take the fourth root:**

$$
T_e \;=\; \left(4.201 \times 10^{9}\right)^{1/4} \;\approx\; 254.9 \;\; K
$$

**Step 5 — Comment on discrepancy:**

The observed mean surface temperature is $T_s \approx 288 \, K$. The difference:

$$
\Delta T \;=\; T_s - T_e \;=\; 288 - 255 \;=\; 33 \;\; K
$$

This 33 K **enhancement** cannot be explained without the atmosphere. It is the magnitude of the **natural greenhouse warming** maintained by water vapour and CO₂. Anthropogenic GHGs add a further $\sim 1.2 \, K$ on top.

**[14 Marks: a = 7, b = 7]**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Explain the concept of Radiative Forcing. The concentration of CO₂ has risen from a pre-industrial value of 280 ppm to 420 ppm. Estimate the additional radiative forcing using the relation $\Delta F = 5.35 \ln(C/C_0)$. Discuss its implications on global mean temperature assuming a climate sensitivity of 0.8 K per W/m². (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Radiative Forcing Definition:**

> Radiative forcing (RF) is the net change in the energy balance of the Earth system due to an imposed perturbation, expressed in $W/m^2$ at the tropopause after allowing stratospheric temperatures to readjust.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\Delta F \;=\; 5.35\,\ln\!\left(\frac{420}{280}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute ratio and logarithm:**

$$
\frac{420}{280} \;=\; 1.500 \quad;\quad \ln(1.500) \;=\; 0.4055
$$

**Step 3 — Multiply:**

$$
\Delta F \;=\; 5.35 \times 0.4055 \;\approx\; 2.17 \;\; W/m^2
$$

**Step 4 — Translate to temperature:**

$$
\Delta T_{eq} \;=\; ECS \times \Delta F \;=\; 0.8 \times 2.17 \;\approx\; 1.74 \;\; K
$$

**Step 5 — Implications:**
- A 50 % rise in CO₂ produces ~2.17 W/m² extra forcing — analogous to ~2.2 trillion 100-W bulbs over the entire Earth continuously.
- This corresponds to a committed warming of ~1.7 K, consistent with observed warming of ~1.2 K since 1880.
- With ocean thermal inertia, full equilibrium may take centuries.

**(b) Discuss the Global Warming Potential (GWP) of the following gases and rank them from most to least potent over a 100-year horizon: CO₂, CH₄, N₂O, SF₆. What engineering controls can mitigate their emissions? (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**GWP-100 values (IPCC AR6):**

| Gas | GWP-100 |
|---|---|
| CO₂ | 1 |
| CH₄ | 27.9 |
| N₂O | 273 |
| SF₆ | 25 200 |

**Ranking (most → least potent):**
**SF₆ (25 200) > N₂O (273) > CH₄ (27.9) > CO₂ (1)**

**Engineering controls:**

| Gas | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|
| **CO₂** | Carbon Capture & Storage (CCS), renewable energy, afforestation, fuel switching |
| **CH₄** | Landfill gas capture, anaerobic digester covers, reduced livestock enteric fermentation (feed additives), pipeline leak detection (optical/IR imaging) |
| **N₂O** | Catalytic converters in nitric/adipic acid plants, precision agriculture, nitrification inhibitors |
| **SF₆** | Substitute with **g³** (CO₂/N₂/CF₄ mix) or **vacuum interrupters** in switchgear; recycling & leak-tight sealing |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 4** when converting disk-area insolation to global average — this is the single most common mark-loser in $T_e$ derivations.
> 2. **Confusing GWP and GHG concentration** — students often state "CH₄ has GWP 27, so 27 ppb of CH₄ equals 27 ppm of CO₂" — incorrect; GWP applies on a **per-mass basis**, not per-volume.
> 3. **Mixing up units** — $T_e$ is in Kelvin, not Celsius. Always convert.
> 4. **Omitting stratospheric adjustment** when describing radiative forcing — RF is measured at tropopause after stratospheric re-equilibration, not at TOA.
> 5. **Ignoring water vapour feedback** — students often forget that warming causes more evaporation → more H₂O → positive feedback amplifying initial forcing by a factor of ~2–3.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Greenhouse Effect (Module 1)**

- **Core definition:** Short-wave in, long-wave trapped by GHGs → surface warming of +33 K above bare-rock equilibrium.
- **Effective temperature formula:** $T_e = [S_0(1-\alpha)/(4\sigma)]^{1/4} \Rightarrow T_e \approx 255 \, K$.
- **Observed surface temp:** $T_s \approx 288 \, K$ → greenhouse enhancement $\Delta T \approx 33 \, K$.
- **Six principal GHGs:** H₂O, CO₂, O₃, CH₄, N₂O, CFCs/SF₆.
- **Dominant CO₂ absorption band:** **15 µm** (bending mode of O=C=O).
- **Atmospheric window:** 8–12 µm — clean-air IR escape route.
- **GWP-100 ranking (descending):** SF₆ ≫ N₂O ≫ CH₄ ≫ CO₂.
- **Forcing equation for CO₂:** $\Delta F = 5.35\,\ln(C/C_0) \, W/m^2$.
- **Climate sensitivity range:** $ECS = 0.8 \pm 0.2 \, K$ per $W/m^2$ (IPCC AR6 best estimate).
- **Anthropogenic CO₂ doubling (~280 → 560 ppm) forcing:** $\Delta F \approx 3.7 \, W/m^2$.
- **Positive feedbacks to remember:** Ice–albedo, water-vapour, permafrost CH₄ release.
- **Negative feedback to remember:** Carbon fertilisation, Lapse-rate (partial).
- **Mitigation hierarchy:** Avoid → Reduce → Substitute → Capture (CCS).
- **Numerical constants to commit:** $S_0 = 1361$, $\alpha = 0.30$, $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$.
- **Last 3 KTU board-exam favourite ask:** "Why is Venus hotter than Mercury despite being farther from the Sun?" — Answer: Runaway greenhouse effect from a 96.5 % CO₂ atmosphere with surface pressure ~92 bar.
- **Mnemonic for GHG molecules:** **"CO₂ Main O₃ Natural Choice"** → **C**O₂, **M**ethane, **O**₃, **N**₂O, **C**FCs.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Sampling and Pollution Measurement Methods, Principles and Instruments
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air Sampling and Pollution Measurement Methods, Principles and Instruments

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Air Sampling - The Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 2)**  
> *Air Sampling* is the **systematic, scientific process of capturing a representative portion of ambient or source air** using calibrated physical and chemical methods, in order to determine the **type, concentration, and physical/chemical characteristics** of air pollutants present, thereby enabling quantitative assessment of air quality against prescribed regulatory standards.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, air sampling is broadly classified into **three functional paradigms**:

| Sampling Type | Purpose | Duration |
|--------------|--------|----------|
| **Grab Sampling** | Instantaneous "snapshot" of pollutant concentration | Seconds to minutes |
| **Continuous Sampling** | Real-time, uninterrupted monitoring | Hours to years |
| **Integrated Sampling** | Average concentration over a defined period | 1 hour to 24 hours |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Why is Air Sampling Critical?**  
> Without representative samples, *no analytical instrument—no matter how expensive—can yield meaningful data*. Sampling is the **foundation** of air quality measurement. Errors introduced at this stage **propagate and amplify** through all downstream chemical and instrumental analysis.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy - "The Tea-Tasting Problem"

Imagine you want to know whether a **large cauldron of tea** is sweet enough, but you cannot taste the entire cauldron. So you take a **small spoonful** at the right place, at the right time, using the right utensil. The sweetness of that spoonful *represents* the whole cauldron—**only if your sampling was representative**.

In air pollution engineering:
- **The cauldron** = the actual atmosphere / stack emissions (the *population*).
- **The spoon** = the sampling probe and collection device (the *instrument*).
- **The taste** = the analytical result (the *measurement*).

If you take the spoon when the tea is being stirred (worst case) or only skim the surface (poor technique), your measurement will be **misleading**. Similarly, in air sampling:
- A sample drawn **near the source** ≠ atmospheric concentration.
- A sample taken at **inlet velocity ≠ stack velocity** gives wrong particulate concentration.
- A sample held too long in a rubber bag loses reactive gases like SO₂ or NO₂.

> [!TIP]  
> **The Three Golden Rules of Air Sampling (for KTU exam answers):**  
> 1. **Isokinetic Sampling** — for particulates in stacks (sample velocity = gas stream velocity).  
> 2. **No Contamination** — sample lines, filters, and absorbents must be inert.  
> 3. **No Loss of Analyte** — reactive/soluble gases must be stabilized immediately (e.g., absorbing in a suitable reagent).

## 1.3 Pollution Measurement - Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Definition - Pollution Measurement:**  
> Pollution measurement is the **quantitative determination of the concentration, mass flow rate, or emission rate** of specific air pollutants using standardized physical, chemical, electrochemical, or spectroscopic principles, calibrated against traceable reference standards (e.g., NIST Primary Gas Mixtures).

Two broad measurement philosophies exist:

1. **Direct-Reading Instruments** — give instantaneous readings (e.g., chemiluminescent NOₓ analyzer, FID, NDIR).
2. **Laboratory (Off-line) Methods** — sample collected, transported, then analyzed (e.g., gravimetric PM analysis, GC-MS, HPLC).

---

## 1.4 Classification of Air Pollutants Relevant to Measurement

For KTU 2024 Module 2, the pollutants of interest and their **standard measurement techniques** are:

| Pollutant Class | Examples | Typical Measurement Principle | KTU Standard Reference |
|-----------------|----------|-------------------------------|----------------------|
| **Particulate Matter** | TSP, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅ | Gravimetric (filtration + weighing) | IS 5182 Part IV; CPCB |
| **Sulfur Compounds** | SO₂, H₂S | West-Gaeke (pararosaniline) | IS 5182 Part II |
| **Oxides of Nitrogen** | NO, NO₂, NOₓ | Griess-Saltzman / Chemiluminescence | IS 5182 Part VI |
| **Carbon Monoxide** | CO | NDIR / Electrochemical sensor | IS 5182 Part X |
| **Ozone** | O₃ | UV Photometry / Chemiluminescence | IS 5182 Part IX |
| **Hydrocarbons** | THC, NMHC | Flame Ionization Detector (FID) | IS 5182 Part XI |
| **Lead & Heavy Metals** | Pb, Cd, Ni | AAS / ICP-MS after digestion | IS 5182 Part XXII |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** Always cite the relevant **IS Code / CPCB Method Number** alongside the principle when answering measurement questions. This shows **examiner-friendly rigor**.

---

## 1.5 The Measurement Chain - Overview Diagram (Conceptual)

```
┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐    ┌──────────────┐
│   SOURCE /   │ -> │   SAMPLING   │ -> │  TRANSPORT / │ -> │   ANALYSIS   │
│   AMBIENT    │    │   TRAIN      │    │  STORAGE     │    │   & QUANT.   │
│   AIR        │    │ (Probe+Filter│    │ (Bags/Tubes) │    │ (Instruments)│
└──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘    └──────────────┘
      │                     │                   │                    │
   Sampling          Isokinetic          No Adsorption        Calibration
   Location          Condition           / No Reaction         Standards
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Insight:** Every link in this chain introduces an **uncertainty budget**. A KTU-quality answer must identify where in the chain a particular error is introduced (e.g., probe-induced turbulence, filter adsorption losses, transport losses, instrument drift).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principles of Air Sampling - Theoretical Foundation

The act of drawing an air sample into a measurement device is governed by **fluid mechanics, thermodynamics, and mass transfer principles**. KTU Module 2 emphasizes these four foundational principles:

### A. Isokinetic Sampling Principle (For Particulates)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Isokinetic Sampling Definition:**  
> Sampling in which the **velocity of the gas entering the sampling nozzle equals the velocity of the gas stream at the sampling point**. This ensures that the **size distribution and concentration** of particulates entering the nozzle equals that of the undisturbed stream.

**Why is this critical?**  
Particles have inertia. If the gas entering the nozzle accelerates (sub-isokinetic, nozzle velocity > stream velocity), **finer particles** tend to follow gas streamlines and may **avoid** the nozzle, while **larger particles** (with greater inertia) enter anyway → **over-sampling of coarse particles**.

Conversely, in super-isokinetic sampling (nozzle velocity < stream velocity), fine particles enter preferentially → **under-sampling of coarse particles**.

### B. The Isokinetic Ratio Equation

$$
\text{Isokinetic Ratio} = \frac{V_n}{V_s} = 1.0
$$

Where:  
- $V_n$ = velocity at the nozzle inlet (m/s)  
- $V_s$ = velocity of gas stream at sampling point (m/s)  

The **actual sample flow rate** required at the nozzle for isokinetic conditions:

$$
Q_n = A_n \cdot V_s = A_n \cdot V_n \quad (\text{at isokinetic condition})
$$

Where: $A_n$ = cross-sectional area of the nozzle (m²).

The **true sample volume** at stack conditions:

$$
Q_s = A_s \cdot V_s \quad (\text{total stack flow rate})
$$

### C. Conversion to Standard Conditions

Since flue gas densities change with temperature and pressure, all flow rates must be referenced to **standard dry conditions** (typically 25 °C, 101.325 kPa):

$$
Q_{\text{std,dry}} = Q_m \cdot \frac{P_{\text{std}}}{P_m} \cdot \frac{T_m}{T_{\text{std}}} \cdot \frac{(1 - \text{Moisture Fraction})}
$$

Where:  
- $Q_m$ = measured volumetric flow rate (m³/min)  
- $P_m, T_m$ = measured pressure (kPa) and temperature (K)  
- $P_{\text{std}} = 101.325$ kPa, $T_{\text{std}} = 298.15$ K  
- Moisture Fraction = fractional water vapor content

### D. Particulate Concentration (Dry Basis)

$$
C_{\text{particulate}} = \frac{M_{\text{final}} - M_{\text{initial}}}{Q_{\text{std,dry}} \cdot t} \quad \left[\frac{\text{mg}}{\text{Nm}^3}\right]
$$

Where:  
- $M_{\text{final}} - M_{\text{initial}}$ = mass of particulates collected on filter (mg)  
- $t$ = sampling duration (minutes)  
- $Q_{\text{std,dry}}$ = dry standard volumetric flow rate (Nm³/min)

---

## 2.2 Particulate Sampling Train - Component Analysis

A **standard EPA-style particulate sampling train** (also referenced in KTU Module 2) consists of:

| Component | Function | KTU Key Concept |
|-----------|----------|-----------------|
| **Pitot Tube** | Measures gas stream velocity | Used for $V_s$ calculation |
| **Sampling Nozzle** | Sized to achieve isokinetic flow | Diameter chosen based on $V_s$ |
| **Probe (Liner)** | Conveys sample, often heated | Maintains temperature above dew point |
| **Filter Holder** | Houses glass-fiber / quartz filter | Collection medium for particulates |
| **Condenser / Dryer** | Removes moisture | Enables dry-basis reporting |
| **Gas Meter** | Measures sample volume | Calibrated against standard |
| **Pump** | Provides suction | Maintains isokinetic flow via variable speed |

> [!TIP]  
> **Exam Memory Trick — "P-N-P-F-C-G-P"** — **P**itot, **N**ozzle, **P**robe, **F**ilter, **C**ondenser, **G**as-meter, **P**ump. Read in flow direction (air enters from probe side, exits via pump).

---

## 2.3 Gaseous Sampling - Principles

For **gaseous pollutants**, the sample must either be:
1. **Analyzed in real-time** (continuous analyzer), OR
2. **Collected in a suitable medium** that traps and stabilizes the analyte.

The KTU syllabus emphasizes the following collection techniques:

### A. Absorption in Liquid (Impingers / Bubblers)

> [!DEFINITION]  
> **Absorption Sampling:** The air sample is bubbled through a liquid reagent that chemically reacts with the target gas, fixing it as a stable dissolved species. Later, the solution is analyzed colorimetrically or by ion chromatography.

**KTU Example — West-Gaeke Method for SO₂:**
- Absorbing reagent: **Sodium Tetrachloromercurate (TCM)**
- Reaction: SO₂ + [HgCl₄]²⁻ → [HgCl₂(SO₃)]²⁻ (dichlorosulfitomercurate)
- Stable for several hours, prevents oxidation to SO₄²⁻
- Later analysis: **Pararosaniline dye** → magenta color → measured at 560 nm

**KTU Example — Griess-Saltzman Method for NO₂:**
- Absorbing reagent: **Modified Griess-Saltzman reagent** (sulfanilic acid + NEDA)
- Reaction: NO₂ → diazonium salt → pink azo dye
- Absorbance measured at 540 nm

### B. Adsorption on Solid Sorbent

For non-reactive or volatile organics, solid sorbents like **activated charcoal, Tenax, silica gel, or molecular sieves** are used. Sample gas passes through a tube packed with sorbent; analytes are trapped, then **thermally desorbed** for GC/GC-MS analysis.

### C. Whole-Air Sampling (Canisters / Tedlar Bags)

For non-reactive gases (CO, CO₂, CH₄, NMOGs), **passivated stainless steel canisters** or **Tedlar® bags** (PVF — polyvinyl fluoride) are used. Sampling is grab or time-integrated; subsequent analysis by GC-FID, GC-MS, or NDIR.

### D. Cryogenic Trapping

Sample gas passes through a cooled loop (liquid N₂ at −196 °C); volatile organics condense and are trapped. Useful for trace-level VOC analysis.

---

## 2.4 Instrument Principles — Categorical Mastery

KTU Module 2 requires understanding of the following major analytical principles:

| Principle | Physics/Chem Basis | Target Pollutants | Key Equation/Concept |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Gravimetric** | Mass measurement on filter | PM (TSP, PM₁₀, PM₂.₅) | $C = \Delta m / V$ |
| **Chemiluminescence** | Light emitted from excited-state products | NO, NO₂, O₃ | $\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \to \text{NO}_2^* + \text{O}_2$; $\text{NO}_2^* \to \text{NO}_2 + h\nu$ |
| **NDIR (Non-Dispersive IR)** | IR absorption by molecular vibrational modes | CO, CO₂, SO₂, NO, hydrocarbons | Beer-Lambert Law: $I = I_0 e^{-\alpha C L}$ |
| **UV Photometry** | UV absorption at specific wavelength | O₃ (254 nm), SO₂ (285 nm), NO₂ | Same Beer-Lambert form |
| **Flame Ionization (FID)** | Ionization of HC in H₂ flame | THC, NMHC | Ion current ∝ C-atoms |
| **Photoionization (PID)** | UV lamp ionizes VOCs at 10.6 eV | BTEX, VOCs | Current ∝ concentration |
| **Electrochemical Sensor** | Redox reaction at electrode surface | CO, H₂S, NO₂, SO₂, O₃ | Nernst Equation |
| **AAS / ICP** | Atomic absorption / emission spectroscopy | Pb, Cd, Ni, Hg, Cr, As | $A = k \cdot C$ |
| **Gas Chromatography** | Differential partitioning between mobile & stationary phase | Speciated VOCs, PAHs | Retention time = qualitative; peak area = quantitative |

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet — High-Yield Table

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Below is your one-stop revision formula sheet for KTU Module 2 examinations. Memorize the units and the limiting conditions.**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $C = \dfrac{\Delta m}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | Particulate concentration (gravimetric) | $\mu g/m^3$ or $\text{mg/Nm}^3$ |
| 2 | $V_n = V_s$ | Isokinetic sampling condition | m/s |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{std,dry}} = Q_m \cdot \dfrac{P_{\text{std}}}{P_m} \cdot \dfrac{T_m}{T_{\text{std}}} \cdot (1 - X_w)$ | Dry standard volumetric flow | Nm³/min |
| 4 | $V_{\text{nozzle}} = \dfrac{Q_m}{A_n \cdot 60}$ | Sample volumetric flow at nozzle | m³/s |
| 5 | $\eta = \dfrac{N_o - N}{N_o} \times 100$ | Collection efficiency of sampler | % |
| 6 | $A = -\log_{10}(I/I_0) = \varepsilon C L$ | Beer-Lambert Law (absorbance) | dimensionless |
| 7 | $C = \dfrac{A}{\varepsilon L}$ | Concentration from absorbance | mol/L or ppm |
| 8 | $R_f = \dfrac{d_s}{d_f}$ | Retention factor in chromatography | dimensionless |
| 9 | $Q_{\text{stack}} = A_{\text{stack}} \cdot V_s$ | Total stack gas volumetric flow | m³/s |
| 10 | $E = C \cdot Q_{\text{stack}}$ | Emission rate (mass per time) | g/s or kg/h |
| 11 | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot \dfrac{V_s^2}{2}$ | Velocity pressure from Pitot | Pa |
| 12 | $V_s = C_p \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho}}$ | Stack velocity from Pitot | m/s |

> [!NOTE]  
> **Symbols used:**  
> $C$ = concentration; $\Delta m$ = mass change; $V$ = volume; $V_n, V_s$ = nozzle, stream velocities; $Q$ = volumetric flow; $P, T$ = pressure, temperature; $X_w$ = moisture fraction; $A$ = absorbance; $\varepsilon$ = molar absorptivity; $L$ = path length; $C_p$ = Pitot coefficient (~0.84 for standard Pitot); $\rho$ = gas density.

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

These measurement methods underpin:

- **Emission Inventory Compilation** for CPCB / State Pollution Control Boards.
- **Compliance Testing** of industries against consent-decree emission limits.
- **Epidemiological Studies** correlating ambient PM₂.₅ / O₃ / NO₂ with mortality and morbidity.
- **Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS)** for online regulatory reporting.
- **Stack Testing Certification** for newly installed control equipment (ESP, Baghouse, Scrubber).
- **Industrial Hygiene** for occupational exposure assessments (CAA, OSHA reference values).

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Isokinetic Sampling Flow Rate Calculation

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Problem Framework:** Given a stack diameter, gas velocity, temperature, and moisture content, derive the **sampling nozzle diameter** required for isokinetic sampling at a chosen sampling flow rate.

### Step 1 — Establish the Isokinetic Condition

At isokinetic sampling, the velocity at the nozzle inlet $V_n$ must equal the gas stream velocity at the sampling point $V_s$:

$$
V_n = V_s
$$

### Step 2 — Express the Nozzle Velocity in Terms of Flow and Area

The volumetric flow rate at the nozzle $Q_n$ (measured at meter conditions) is related to nozzle velocity $V_n$ via the nozzle cross-sectional area $A_n$:

$$
Q_n = A_n \cdot V_n = A_n \cdot V_s
$$

Solving for the nozzle diameter $D_n$:

$$
D_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 A_n}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 Q_n}{\pi \cdot V_s}}
$$

### Step 3 — Correct the Stack Velocity to the Sampling Point Conditions

The stream velocity at the sampling point $V_s$ is calculated using the Pitot tube reading and corrected for temperature and pressure (relative to flue gas conditions at the sampling location):

$$
V_s = C_p \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot \Delta P_s}{\rho_s}}
$$

Where: $\Delta P_s$ = velocity pressure at the sampling point (Pa); $\rho_s$ = flue gas density at sampling conditions (kg/m³); $C_p$ = Pitot coefficient (≈ 0.84 for S-type, 0.99 for standard).

### Step 4 — Calculate Flue Gas Density

Using ideal gas law corrected for moisture:

$$
\rho_s = \rho_{\text{dry,air}} \cdot (1 - X_w) + \rho_{\text{water vapor}} \cdot X_w
$$

Or more compactly using molecular weight of wet flue gas $M_w$:

$$
\rho_s = \dfrac{P_s \cdot M_w}{R \cdot T_s}
$$

Where: $M_w$ = molecular weight (kg/kmol); $R = 8.314$ kJ/(kmol·K); $T_s$ = absolute temperature at sampling point (K); $P_s$ = absolute pressure (kPa).

### Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**  
- Stack diameter $D = 1.5$ m; Area $A = \pi (0.75)^2 = 1.767$ m²  
- Stack gas temperature $T_s = 423$ K (150 °C)  
- Stack pressure $P_s = 101.325$ kPa  
- Moisture fraction $X_w = 0.10$ (10% by volume)  
- Molecular weight of wet flue gas $M_w = 28.97 (1 - 0.10) + 18.02 (0.10) = 27.87$ kg/kmol  
- Velocity pressure $\Delta P_s = 25$ Pa; Pitot coefficient $C_p = 0.84$  
- Desired sample flow $Q_n = 0.020$ Nm³/min at meter conditions  

**Step 5a — Compute flue gas density:**

$$
\rho_s = \dfrac{101.325 \times 27.87}{8.314 \times 423} = \dfrac{2824.1}{3516.8} = 0.803 \text{ kg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5b — Compute stack velocity:**

$$
V_s = 0.84 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 25}{0.803}} = 0.84 \times \sqrt{62.27} = 0.84 \times 7.891 = 6.629 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5c — Compute total stack flow:**

$$
Q_{\text{stack}} = A \cdot V_s = 1.767 \times 6.629 = 11.71 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 5d — Convert sample flow to actual m³/s at stack conditions:**

The sample flow $Q_n = 0.020$ Nm³/min = $3.33 \times 10^{-4}$ Nm³/s.

**Step 5e — Compute nozzle diameter for isokinetic sampling:**

$$
D_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times Q_n}{\pi \cdot V_s}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 3.33 \times 10^{-4}}{\pi \times 6.629}}
$$

$$
D_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{1.333 \times 10^{-3}}{20.823}} = \sqrt{6.40 \times 10^{-5}} = 8.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 8.0 \text{ mm}
$$

**Result:** The sampling nozzle diameter should be **8.0 mm** for isokinetic sampling.

> [!TIP]  
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always quote the **nozzle diameter in mm** (as it's an engineering practical number) and note the **assumed $C_p$ value** (typically 0.84 for S-type Pitot per IS 11255).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Particulate Emission Concentration (mg/Nm³)

### Step 1 — Filter Mass Gain

After sampling, the filter is conditioned (24 h at 20 °C, 50% RH per IS 5182 Part IV) and weighed:

$$
\Delta m = m_{\text{after}} - m_{\text{before}} \quad [\text{mg}]
$$

### Step 2 — Sample Volume at Standard Dry Conditions

$$
V_{\text{sample,std,dry}} = Q_{\text{meter}} \cdot t \cdot \dfrac{P_m}{P_{\text{std}}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{std}}}{T_m} \cdot (1 - X_w)
$$

### Step 3 — Concentration Calculation

$$
C_{\text{PM}} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{V_{\text{sample,std,dry}}} \quad \left[\dfrac{\text{mg}}{\text{Nm}^3}\right]
$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Worked Example

**Given:**  
- Filter pre-weight = 142.5638 mg; post-weight = 144.1872 mg  
- Sample time $t = 60$ min  
- Meter reading $Q_m = 1.2$ m³/h (dry gas meter at $T_m = 298$ K, $P_m = 100$ kPa)  
- Moisture fraction $X_w = 0.02$ (trap removed moisture)

**Step 4a — Mass gain:**

$$
\Delta m = 144.1872 - 142.5638 = 1.6234 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 4b — Sample volume at meter conditions:**

$$
V_{\text{meter}} = 1.2 \times (60/60) = 1.2 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 4c — Convert to standard dry conditions:**

$$
V_{\text{std,dry}} = 1.2 \times \dfrac{100}{101.325} \times \dfrac{298.15}{298.15} \times (1 - 0.02) = 1.2 \times 0.9870 \times 1 \times 0.98
$$

$$
V_{\text{std,dry}} = 1.2 \times 0.9673 = 1.161 \text{ Nm}^3
$$

**Step 4d — Particulate concentration:**

$$
C_{\text{PM}} = \dfrac{1.6234}{1.161} = 1.398 \text{ mg/Nm}^3
$$

**Result:** $C_{\text{PM}} \approx 1.40$ mg/Nm³ (well below CPCB standard of 150 mg/Nm³ for stack PM in many industries).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Chemiluminescent NOₓ Analyzer Response

### Step 1 — Reaction Sequence

In a chemiluminescent NOₓ analyzer, NO reacts with ozone to form excited-state NO₂:

$$
\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \to \text{NO}_2^{*} + \text{O}_2
$$

The excited NO₂* relaxes by photon emission:

$$
\text{NO}_2^{*} \to \text{NO}_2 + h\nu \quad (\lambda \approx 600 \text{ to } 875 \text{ nm})
$$

### Step 2 — Photon Count → Concentration

The emitted light intensity $I$ is proportional to NO concentration:

$$
I = K \cdot [\text{NO}]
$$

Where $K$ is an instrument-specific calibration constant. After PMT (photomultiplier tube) detection, the analyzer outputs concentration directly.

### Step 3 — Total NOₓ Measurement

To measure **total NOₓ**, the sample first passes through a **molybdenum converter** at ~325 °C, which reduces NO₂ to NO:

$$
\text{NO}_2 \xrightarrow{\text{Mo, 325 °C}} \text{NO} + \frac{1}{2} \text{O}_2
$$

Then the sum [NO + converted-NO₂] is measured. Subtracting the original [NO] yields [NO₂]:

$$
[\text{NO}_2] = [\text{NO}_x]_{\text{total}} - [\text{NO}]
$$

### Step 4 — Calibration Relation

Using a known NO standard (e.g., 50 ppb NIST-traceable NO in N₂):

$$
[\text{NO}]_{\text{unknown}} = [\text{NO}]_{\text{std}} \cdot \dfrac{I_{\text{unknown}} - I_{\text{zero}}}{I_{\text{std}} - I_{\text{zero}}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Selection of Sampling Method

The choice of sampling technique depends on **pollutant nature, concentration level, and information required**. The following decision matrix summarizes KTU-recommended selection:

| Pollutant / Goal | Best Method | Why This Method |
|------------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **TSP (24 h avg)** | High-Volume Sampler (Hi-Vol) with glass-fiber filter | Standard CPCB method; gravimetric |
| **PM₁₀** | Respirable Dust Sampler (RDS) with cyclone + filter | 10 µm cut-point via cyclone |
| **PM₂.₅** | Impactor-based sampler (e.g., Andersen) or Beta Attenuation Monitor | 2.5 µm cut-point; mass or continuous |
| **SO₂ (24 h avg)** | West-Gaeke with TCM impinger | Stable, sensitive (10 µg/m³ LOD) |
| **NO₂ (1 h avg)** | Griess-Saltzman absorber | Colorimetric, simple |
| **Continuous SO₂** | UV Fluorescence SO₂ analyzer | Real-time, ppb sensitivity |
| **Continuous NO/NO₂** | Chemiluminescence | Selective, fast response |
| **Continuous CO** | NDIR | Stable, no interference |
| **Continuous O₃** | UV Photometry | 254 nm Hg lamp line |
| **VOC speciation** | Canister + GC-MS | Whole-air, comprehensive |
| **Lead in PM₁₀** | Hi-Vol filter + acid digestion + AAS | Standard EPA/CPCB method |
| **Stack PM isokinetic** | EPA Method 5 train | US-EPA reference; IS 11255 |

---

## 3.5 High-Volume Sampler (Hi-Vol) — Component Analysis

The Hi-Vol sampler is a workhorse for ambient PM (TSP). Operating principle:

1. **Blower** draws ambient air through a filter at 1.0 to 1.7 m³/min.
2. Filter (8″ × 10″ glass-fiber) collects **Total Suspended Particulates (TSP)**.
3. Sampling duration: 24 hours (typical).
4. Pre/post weighing under controlled RH/temperature yields mass.

**Cut-point characteristic:** Hi-Vol has a 50% cut-point of ~25–50 µm aerodynamic diameter (depends on shelter design and wind). It does NOT separate PM₁₀ or PM₂.₅ — for that, use size-selective inlets.

> [!NOTE]  
> **KTU Frequently Asked:** "Differentiate between Hi-Vol and Respirable Dust Sampler (RDS)."  
> **Answer (key points):** Hi-Vol → TSP, 1.0–1.7 m³/min, no size cut. RDS → PM₁₀, ~1.0 m³/min, uses a **2.5 m³/hr cyclone** as size-selective inlet.

---

## 3.6 Impinger Design (Standard Reference: Greenberg-Smith / AGH-1)

**Impingers** are glass vessels with a fritted nozzle that breaks sample gas into fine bubbles. Used for collecting gases (SO₂, NO₂, NH₃, HF, Cl₂).

**Selection criteria for impinger:**

- **Type:** Standard midget impinger (30 mL) for IS methods; larger (500 mL) for HCl.
- **Frit porosity:** Coarse (40–60 µm) for soluble gases; fine (10–15 µm) for slow-reacting gases.
- **Sampling rate:** 0.5 to 2.0 L/min typically.
- **Collection efficiency target:** ≥ 95% for the analyte.

**Collection efficiency for an impinger:**

$$
\eta = 1 - \exp(-K \cdot Q_{\text{liq}} / G)
$$

Where: $K$ = overall mass-transfer coefficient; $Q_{\text{liq}}$ = liquid flow rate; $G$ = gas flow rate. (Often simplified: efficiency depends on contact time, gas/liquid ratio.)

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**  
> **Define isokinetic sampling. Why is it necessary for particulate matter measurement in stacks?**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Definition (1.5 marks):**  
Isokinetic sampling is the process of drawing a gas sample from a flowing stream in such a way that the velocity of the gas entering the sampling nozzle is exactly equal to the velocity of the gas stream at the point of sampling.

**Necessity (1.5 marks):**  
It is necessary for accurate measurement of particulate matter because particles possess inertia. If sampling is non-isokinetic:
- In **sub-isokinetic** condition (nozzle velocity > stream velocity), large particles deviate from streamlines and are preferentially collected → overestimation of mass and coarse fraction.
- In **super-isokinetic** condition (nozzle velocity < stream velocity), fine particles are preferentially drawn in → underestimation of mass.

Hence, isokinetic sampling preserves the true size distribution and concentration of particulates.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**  
> **Explain the working principle of a Chemiluminescent NOₓ analyzer with a neat sketch.**

#### Model Answer (3 marks):
**Principle (2 marks):**  
NO reacts with excess ozone in a reaction chamber to form excited-state NO₂:

$$
\text{NO} + \text{O}_3 \to \text{NO}_2^{*} + \text{O}_2
$$

The excited NO₂* emits light (λ ≈ 600–875 nm) upon relaxation:

$$
\text{NO}_2^{*} \to \text{NO}_2 + h\nu
$$

Light intensity is proportional to NO concentration and detected by a photomultiplier tube (PMT). For total NOₓ, the sample is passed through a molybdenum converter (325 °C) which reduces NO₂ to NO; the difference yields [NO₂].

**Sketch description (1 mark):**  
Diagram includes: sample inlet → Mo converter (bypass) → O₃ chamber → optical filter → PMT → signal processor → display.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions with Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**  
> **(a)** Explain the components and working of the **EPA Method 5 Particulate Sampling Train** with a neat diagram. Discuss the importance of **isokinetic sampling** in detail. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** Describe the **West-Gaeke Method** for the measurement of ambient SO₂. Include the chemistry, sampling procedure, and analysis. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) (7 marks)

**Components of EPA Method 5 Train (3 marks):**

1. **Pitot Tube Assembly:** Type-S or standard Pitot; measures velocity pressure (ΔP) → stream velocity ($V_s$).
2. **Sampling Nozzle:** Sized such that nozzle velocity $V_n = V_s$. Typically 6–13 mm diameter; stainless steel or glass.
3. **Heated Probe Liner:** Maintains sample temperature above dew point (typically 120 °C) to prevent moisture condensation and losses of soluble gases.
4. **Filter Holder:** Contains glass-fiber filter (e.g., Whatman GFA) for particulate collection.
5. **Condenser and Impingers:** First impinger collects condensate; second contains silica gel for final moisture removal; protects the gas meter.
6. **Dry Gas Meter:** Measures volumetric flow at meter temperature and pressure.
7. **Vacuum Pump and Rotameter:** Pulls sample through train; maintains flow.
8. **Barometer and Thermometers:** Provide ambient and meter temperature/pressure for STP conversion.

**Working Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Conduct preliminary traverse to determine $V_s$ profile.
2. Select nozzle diameter based on desired flow rate and $V_s$.
3. Heat probe to 120 °C; assemble train; leak-check.
4. Begin sampling at calculated flow; record ΔP, meter readings, temperatures.
5. After run, recover filter, probe rinse, impinger contents → gravimetric + wet chemistry.
6. Calculate $C_{\text{PM}}$ in mg/Nm³ (dry).

**Importance of Isokinetic Sampling (2 marks):**

- Preserves particle size distribution and concentration.
- Avoids inertial bias at nozzle inlet.
- Required by EPA Method 5, IS 11255, and CPCB protocols.
- Critical for compliance testing of ESPs, fabric filters, wet scrubbers.

#### Model Answer — (b) (7 marks)

**Chemistry of West-Gaeke Method (3 marks):**

**Step 1 — Absorption:** Ambient air is drawn at ~0.5–2.0 L/min through an **impinger containing 0.04 M Sodium Tetrachloromercurate (TCM)** solution. SO₂ dissolves and forms a stable dichlorosulfitomercurate complex:

$$
\text{SO}_2 + [\text{HgCl}_4]^{2-} + \text{H}_2\text{O} \to [\text{HgCl}_2(\text{SO}_3)]^{2-} + 2\text{HCl}
$$

This complex is stable for several hours, preventing oxidation to sulfate.

**Step 2 — Color Development:** In the laboratory, the complex is reacted with:
- **Formaldehyde (HCHO)** 
- **Pararosaniline dye (PRA)** — bleached, acidified form

A red-purple **pararosaniline methyl sulfonic acid** dye is formed, with absorbance maximum at **560 nm**.

**Step 3 — Quantification:** Absorbance is measured using a UV-Vis spectrophotometer. Concentration is determined from a calibration curve (Beer's Law):

$$
A = \varepsilon \cdot b \cdot C
$$

**Sampling Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Use **midget impingers** (30 mL TCM).
2. Sample for 24 h at 0.5–2.0 L/min (typically 1.0 L/min).
3. Field blank: identical impinger not exposed.
4. After sampling, store at < 5 °C; analyze within 30 days.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$
[\text{SO}_2] = \dfrac{(A_s - A_b) \cdot V_t \cdot 1000}{V_{\text{sample}} \cdot \varepsilon_{\text{std}}}
$$

Where $A_s$ = sample absorbance, $A_b$ = blank absorbance, $V_t$ = total volume of absorbing solution, $V_{\text{sample}}$ = sampled air volume at standard conditions.

**Result expressed in µg/m³.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**  
> **(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle and components of a **Continuous Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Station (CAAQMS)**. List the parameters monitored and one analytical principle for each. **(7 marks)**  
> **(b)** A Hi-Volume Sampler operates for **24 hours** with the following data:  
> Pre-filter weight = 142.5638 g; Post-filter weight = 144.1872 g  
> Average flow rate = 1.20 m³/min; Barometric pressure = 750 mmHg; Ambient temperature = 30 °C  
> Moisture fraction removed by trap = 2%  
> **Calculate:** (i) Total suspended particulate (TSP) concentration in µg/m³; (ii) Compare with NAAQS standard. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — (a) (7 marks)

**CAAQMS — Functional Architecture (4 marks):**

A CAAQMS is a stationary, integrated monitoring station that continuously measures multiple criteria pollutants in ambient air. Components:

1. **Sampling Manifold and Inlet:** Heated glass manifold at ~10 m height (above breathing zone); minimizes particle losses and provides common inlet.
2. **Particulate Analyzers:**
   - **PM₁₀** and **PM₂.₅** measured by **Beta Attenuation Monitor (BAM)** or **TEOM** (Tapered Element Oscillating Microbalance). Both work by collecting particulates on a filter tape and measuring mass via beta-ray absorption or oscillating frequency.
3. **Gaseous Analyzers:**
   - **SO₂:** UV Fluorescence (UV absorption at 215 nm → fluorescence at ~330 nm)
   - **NO/NO₂/NOₓ:** Chemiluminescence (NO + O₃ → NO₂* → light)
   - **CO:** Non-Dispersive Infrared (NDIR) spectroscopy
   - **O₃:** UV Photometry (absorption at 254 nm, Hg lamp)
   - **BTEX/VOCs:** PID + GC or online GC-FID
4. **Meteorological Sensors:** Wind speed, wind direction, ambient T, RH, solar radiation, rainfall.
5. **Calibration System:** Zero air generator, dynamic gas calibrator (for span checks, performed bi-weekly or monthly).
6. **Data Acquisition System (DAQ):** Logs 1-min, 8-h, 24-h averages; transmits to CPCB server via GPRS/satellite.

**Parameters and Principles (3 marks):**

| Pollutant | Analytical Principle |
|-----------|---------------------|
| PM₂.₅, PM₁₀ | Beta Attenuation (or TEOM) |
| SO₂ | UV Fluorescence |
| NO₂ | Chemiluminescence (after Mo converter) |
| O₃ | UV Photometry |
| CO | NDIR |
| BTEX | PID + GC |
| NH₃ | Chemiluminescence (with converter) |

**Working:** Sample air enters manifold → distributed to parallel analyzers → each analyzer produces voltage/current signal → DAQ converts to ppb/µg/m³ → telemetry.

---

#### Model Answer — (b) (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Pre-weight = 142.5638 g; Post-weight = 144.1872 g  
- Sampling time = 24 h = 1440 min  
- Average flow = 1.20 m³/min  
- Barometric pressure = 750 mmHg = 99.97 kPa (1 mmHg = 0.13332 kPa)  
- Ambient temperature = 30 °C = 303.15 K  
- Moisture fraction $X_w = 0.02$  

**Step (i) — Mass of Particulate Collected:**

$$
\Delta m = 144.1872 - 142.5638 = 1.6234 \text{ g} = 1623.4 \text{ mg} = 1.6234 \times 10^6 \text{ µg}
$$

**Step (ii) — Sample Volume at Meter Conditions:**

$$
V_m = 1.20 \times 1440 = 1728 \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step (iii) — Convert to Standard Dry Conditions:**

$$
V_{\text{std,dry}} = V_m \cdot \dfrac{P_m}{P_{\text{std}}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{\text{std}}}{T_m} \cdot (1 - X_w)
$$

$$
V_{\text{std,dry}} = 1728 \times \dfrac{99.97}{101.325} \times \dfrac{298.15}{303.15} \times (1 - 0.02)
$$

$$
V_{\text{std,dry}} = 1728 \times 0.9867 \times 0.9835 \times 0.98
$$

$$
V_{\text{std,dry}} = 1728 \times 0.9512 = 1643.7 \text{ Nm}^3
$$

**Step (iv) — TSP Concentration:**

$$
C_{\text{TSP}} = \dfrac{\Delta m}{V_{\text{std,dry}}} = \dfrac{1.6234 \times 10^6 \text{ µg}}{1643.7 \text{ Nm}^3}
$$

$$
\boxed{C_{\text{TSP}} \approx 987.6 \text{ µg/m}^3 \approx 988 \text{ µg/m}^3}
$$

**Step (v) — Comparison with NAAQS (CPCB):**

The Indian NAAQS for **TSP (24 h)** is **not directly defined** under NAAQS-2009 (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅ are defined). However, the older SPM (Suspended Particulate Matter) standard was **500 µg/m³ (24-h, Industrial)** and **200 µg/m³ (Residential)**.

For PM₁₀ (24-h): **100 µg/m³**; for PM₂.₅ (24-h): **60 µg/m³**.

If we assume the Hi-Vol collected TSP, the value 988 µg/m³ **exceeds both industrial and residential SPM standards**, indicating the area is heavily polluted and corrective action is required.

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**  
> 1. **Forgetting the unit conversions:** Students often directly divide g/m³ without converting to standard dry conditions → mark loss.  
> 2. **Ignoring moisture correction:** Trap removed 2% moisture; forgetting (1−X_w) overestimates volume → underestimates concentration.  
> 3. **Pressure unit mismatch:** 750 mmHg ≠ 750 kPa. Always convert: $P_{\text{kPa}} = 0.13332 \times P_{\text{mmHg}}$.  
> 4. **Wrong standard reference:** Don't quote obsolete TSP standards. Use current PM₁₀ (100 µg/m³) and PM₂.₅ (60 µg/m³) NAAQS-2009.  
> 5. **Confusing Hi-Vol (TSP) with PM₁₀ sampler (RDS):** Hi-Vol measures TSP, not PM₁₀ — direct comparison to PM₁₀ NAAQS is not valid without size cut.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]  
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Final Week before KTU Exam)**

### A. Must-Know Definitions
- ✅ Air sampling, grab/continuous/integrated sampling
- ✅ Isokinetic sampling, stack velocity, nozzle velocity
- ✅ Gravimetric analysis, Beer-Lambert Law
- ✅ West-Gaeke method, Griess-Saltzman method
- ✅ Chemiluminescence, NDIR, UV Photometry, FID, PID
- ✅ Hi-Vol, RDS (PM₁₀), BAM/TEOM (continuous PM)
- ✅ CAAQMS, CEMS

### B. Must-Know Formulae (Memorize with units)
- ✅ $C = \Delta m / V$ (gravimetric)
- ✅ $V_n = V_s$ (isokinetic)
- ✅ $V_{\text{std,dry}}$ conversion (P, T, X_w corrections)
- ✅ $A = \varepsilon C L$ (Beer-Lambert)
- ✅ $V_s = C_p \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ (Pitot)
- ✅ $Q = A \cdot V$ (volumetric flow)
- ✅ $E = C \cdot Q$ (emission rate)

### C. Must-Know Indian Standards / Methods
- ✅ IS 5182 (ambient air quality methods)
- ✅ IS 11255 (stack monitoring)
- ✅ CPCB NAAQS 2009 — PM₁₀ (100 µg/m³), PM₂.₅ (60 µg/m³), SO₂ (80 µg/m³), NO₂ (80 µg/m₃), O₃ (180 µg/m³), CO (4 mg/m³ 8-h)
- ✅ EPA Method 5, Method 17 (in-stack filtration)
- ✅ West-Gaeke — IS 5182 Part II

### D. Common Trap Topics in KTU Module 2
- 🔴 Difference between **isokinetic** and **isoaxial** sampling.
- 🔴 Difference between **TSP**, **PM₁₀**, and **PM₂.₅** measurements.
- 🔴 Why **moisture removal** is essential before the gas meter.
- 🔴 Why **filter conditioning** at 20 °C / 50% RH for 24 h is mandatory.
- 🔴 Difference between **West-Gaeke** (SO₂) and **Griess-Saltzman** (NO₂) absorption chemistries.
- 🔴 Role of **Mo converter** in NOₓ chemiluminescent analyzers.
- 🔴 Calibration hierarchy: **Primary Standard → Working Standard → Field Instrument**.

### E. Diagram-Drawing Mastery Checklist
- ✅ EPA Method 5 train — labeled in flow direction
- ✅ West-Gaeke impinger train — with blank & sample paths
- ✅ Chemiluminescent analyzer — with Mo converter bypass
- ✅ CAAQMS — with all analyzers & DAQ
- ✅ Hi-Vol vs RDS — with size-selective inlets

### F. Numerical Problem Pattern Recognition
- "Calculate TSP concentration from Hi-Vol data" → **3-step solution** (mass gain → STP dry volume → divide).
- "Determine nozzle diameter for isokinetic sampling" → **$V_n = V_s$, $A_n = Q/V$, $D_n = \sqrt{4A/\pi}$**.
- "Find PM emission rate from a stack" → **$E = C \cdot Q_{\text{stack}}$** with standard conversion.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Final KTU Mantra for Module 2:**  
> *"Sample right, sample representative, analyze precise — that's the air pollution engineer's mantra."*  
> For every measurement, **state the principle, name the standard (IS/EPA), show the calculation with units, and compare against NAAQS/emission limit**.
