topics.title: Ambient Air Quality and Emission Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Ambient Air Quality and Emission Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Ambient Air Quality (AAQ)

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ambient Air** refers to the outside air of the troposphere to which the general public has access. **Ambient Air Quality (AAQ)** represents the measured concentration of specific pollutants in this surrounding atmosphere, evaluated against prescribed regulatory limits to determine its suitability for human health, vegetation, and ecosystem sustainability.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of ambient air like the water in a public swimming pool. The pool management sets quality standards (chlorine level, pH) to ensure it is safe for swimmers. Similarly, governments set ambient air quality standards (like limits for PM2.5, SO₂, NO₂) to ensure the air everyone breathes in their neighborhood is safe. Just as the pool water is tested periodically, ambient air is monitored through air quality stations in cities.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Metric**: Ambient air quality is universally expressed in **micrograms per cubic meter (μg/m³)** for particulate matter and for gases (often also in **parts per million (ppm)** or **parts per billion (ppb)** by volume).

### 1.2 Emission Standards

**Formal Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Emission Standards** are legally enforceable limits on the quantity, concentration, or physical characteristics of pollutants that can be discharged into the atmosphere from specific stationary (industrial stacks) or mobile (vehicles) sources over a defined time period.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a kitchen exhaust fan. Emission standards are like a rule that says "the smoke coming out of your exhaust must not exceed X grams of grease per hour." For industries, instead of grease, it's sulfur dioxide, particulate matter, or dioxins. It controls the pollution *at the source* (the chimney or tailpipe), while ambient air quality controls the *final concentration* in the surrounding environment.

### 1.3 National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) - India

The **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)**, under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**, has notified NAAQS. The latest revision (2009) established a two-tier system:

- **Industrial, Residential, Rural & Other Areas**
- **Ecologically Sensitive Areas** (notified by the Central Government)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Pollutants (Criteria Pollutants) under NAAQS India (2009):**
> 1. Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅)
> 2. Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)
> 3. Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)
> 4. Ammonia (NH₃)
> 5. Carbon Monoxide (CO)
> 6. Lead (Pb)
> 7. Benzene (C₆H₆)
> 8. Benzo(a)Pyrene (BaP) – particulate phase
> 9. Arsenic (As)
> 10. Nickel (Ni)
> 11. Ozone (O₃)

### 1.4 Need for Standards

1. **Health Protection**: To prevent respiratory, cardiovascular, and carcinogenic effects.
2. **Vegetation and Material Protection**: Acid rain damage, corrosion of monuments (Taj Mahal case).
3. **Legal Enforcement**: Provides a yardstick for prosecution under environmental law.
4. **Planning Tool**: Used in land-use planning, industrial siting, and Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA).
5. **International Compliance**: Aligns with **WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2005, updated 2021)**.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Framework of AAQ

The ambient air quality is established based on a chain of scientific evidence:

**Step 1: Identification of Pollutants**
- Selection based on toxicity, persistence, prevalence, and measurability.
- Result: 12 **criteria pollutants** globally (US EPA lists 6 core: CO, Pb, NO₂, O₃, PM, SO₂).

**Step 2: Health-Based Threshold Determination**
- Dose-response relationships from epidemiological studies.
- Reference Concentration (RfC) and Reference Dose (RfD) are derived.
- Application of **safety factors** (10× for human variability, 10× for animal-to-human extrapolation) yields protective levels.

**Step 3: Averaging Time Selection**
Different averaging times are used because pollutant effects vary with exposure duration:
- **1-hour average**: For short-term acute effects (e.g., SO₂, NO₂).
- **8-hour average**: For occupational/exposure-related effects (e.g., CO).
- **24-hour average**: For short-term cumulative exposure (e.g., PM₁₀).
- **Annual average**: For long-term chronic effects (cancer risk, lung function decline).

**Step 4: Standard Notification & Legal Backing**
- Published in the Official Gazette of India.
- Enforceable under the **Air Act, 1981**.

### 2.2 NAAQS India (CPCB, 2009) — Detailed Standards

| Pollutant | Time-Weighted Average | Industrial/Residential/Rural/Other Areas | Ecologically Sensitive Areas |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------------------------|------------------------------|
| **PM₂.₅ (μg/m³)** | Annual | 40 | 40 |
| **PM₂.₅ (μg/m³)** | 24 hours | 60 | 60 |
| **PM₁₀ (μg/m³)** | Annual | 60 | 60 |
| **PM₁₀ (μg/m³)** | 24 hours | 100 | 100 |
| **SO₂ (μg/m³)** | Annual | 50 | 20 |
| **SO₂ (μg/m³)** | 24 hours | 80 | 80 |
| **NO₂ (μg/m³)** | Annual | 40 | 30 |
| **NO₂ (μg/m³)** | 24 hours | 80 | 80 |
| **O₃ (μg/m³)** | 8 hours | 100 | 100 |
| **O₃ (μg/m³)** | 1 hour | 180 | 180 |
| **CO (mg/m³)** | 8 hours | 04 | 04 |
| **CO (mg/m³)** | 1 hour | 10 | 10 |
| **Pb (μg/m³)** | Annual | 0.50 | 0.50 |
| **Pb (μg/m³)** | 24 hours | 1.0 | 1.0 |
| **NH₃ (μg/m³)** | Annual | 100 | 100 |
| **NH₃ (μg/m³)** | 24 hours | 400 | 400 |

### 2.3 Emission Standards — Indian Context

The **Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC)** notifies emission standards for 17 categories of major industries under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** (E(P)Act).

**Important Emission Standards:**

| Industry Sector | Pollutant | Standard |
|----------------|-----------|----------|
| **Thermal Power Plants** (Coal-based) | PM | 30–100 mg/Nm³ (based on capacity) |
| Thermal Power Plants | SO₂ | 100 mg/Nm³ (≤500 MW), varies |
| Thermal Power Plants | NOₓ | 300 mg/Nm³ (new plants) |
| **Cement Plants** | PM | 30 mg/Nm³ |
| **Iron & Steel** | PM | 50 mg/Nm³ (captive power) |
| **Petroleum Refineries** | SO₂ | 400 mg/Nm³ (heater) |
| **Diesel Gensets** | PM/CO/NOₓ/HC | As per CPCB norms (Tier-I to Tier-IV) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Normality Condition**: Emission concentrations are reported at **1% excess O₂** for solid fuels, **3% O₂** for liquid/gaseous fuels, unless otherwise specified. "Nm³" refers to Normal cubic meter at 0°C, 1 atm.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Concentration Conversion (ppm to μg/m³)** | $C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times M \times 10^3}{24.45}$ | Convert gaseous pollutant ppm to μg/m³ |
| 2 | **Concentration Conversion (μg/m³ to ppm)** | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{M \times 10^3}$ | Reverse conversion |
| 3 | **Concentration (ppb to μg/m³)** | $C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{C_{ppb} \times M}{24.45}$ | Trace gas conversions |
| 4 | **STP Molar Volume** | 24.45 L/mol (at 25°C, 1 atm); 22.4 L/mol (at 0°C) | For concentration conversion |
| 5 | **Emission Rate** | $E = C \times Q$ | Mass emission per second (mg/s) |
| 6 | **Effective Stack Height (H)** | $H = h + 1.5 \cdot L$ (CPCB India) | Where L = lesser of height or width of building |
| 7 | **Air Quality Index (Sub-Index)** | $I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | AQI sub-index for pollutant p |
| 8 | **Wind Speed Power Law** | $u = u_r \left( \dfrac{z}{z_r} \right)^p$ | Adjusts stack height dispersion calculations |

Where: M = Molecular weight of gas; C = concentration; Q = volumetric flow rate; p = stability exponent.

### 2.5 Air Quality Index (AQI) – Indian CPCB Method (2014)

The **AQI** transforms complex pollutant concentrations into a single number (0–500) representing overall air quality.

**AQI Categories (CPCB):**

| AQI Range | Category | Health Impact |
|-----------|----------|---------------|
| 0–50 | Good | Minimal impact |
| 51–100 | Satisfactory | Minor breathing discomfort to sensitive people |
| 101–200 | Moderate | Breathing discomfort to people with lung/heart disease |
| 201–300 | Poor | Breathing discomfort to most people on prolonged exposure |
| 301–400 | Very Poor | Respiratory illness on prolonged exposure |
| 401–500 | Severe | Affects healthy people, serious health impacts |

**8 Pollutants considered in AQI:** PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb.
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: ppm to μg/m³ Conversion (Foundation of Air Quality Calculations)

The relationship between parts-per-million (by volume) and mass concentration is fundamental for gaseous pollutants. The derivation below is exhaustive.

**Starting Definition (ppm by volume):**

$$1 \text{ ppm} = \dfrac{1 \text{ part of gas}}{10^6 \text{ parts of air by volume}}$$

**Step 1:** Express 1 ppm as a volume fraction.

$$V_{gas} = \dfrac{V_{air}}{10^6} = \dfrac{1 \text{ m}^3}{10^6} = 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2:** Convert the gas volume to moles using the molar volume at standard conditions.

$$n_{gas} = \dfrac{V_{gas}}{V_m}$$

where $V_m$ = 24.45 L/mol at 25°C, 1 atm (or 22.4 L/mol at 0°C). We convert L to m³: 1 m³ = 1000 L.

$$n_{gas} = \dfrac{10^{-6} \times 1000 \text{ L}}{24.45 \text{ L/mol}} = \dfrac{10^{-3}}{24.45} \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3:** Convert moles to mass using molecular weight M (g/mol).

$$m_{gas} = n_{gas} \times M = \dfrac{10^{-3} \times M}{24.45} \text{ g}$$

**Step 4:** This mass is present in 1 m³ of air. Convert g to μg (1 g = 10⁶ μg).

$$C = \dfrac{m_{gas}}{1 \text{ m}^3} = \dfrac{10^{-3} \times M \times 10^6}{24.45} \text{ μg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times M \times 10^3}{24.45}}$$

**Step 5: Reverse Conversion (μg/m³ to ppm).** Taking the inverse:

$$\boxed{C_{ppm} = \dfrac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{M \times 10^3}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Assumption**: M is in g/mol. The factor 24.45 L/mol corresponds to **25°C and 1 atm**. At 0°C, replace 24.45 with 22.4. Many exam problems specify 25°C.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Problems

#### **Problem 1: ppm to μg/m³ Conversion**

**Question**: The NAAQS for SO₂ (8-hour) in India is 80 μg/m³. Convert this to ppm. M(SO₂) = 64 g/mol.

**Solution**:

Step 1: Identify knowns.

$$C = 80 \text{ μg/m}^3, \quad M = 64 \text{ g/mol}, \quad V_m = 24.45 \text{ L/mol}$$

Step 2: Apply the reverse formula.

$$C_{ppm} = \dfrac{80 \times 24.45}{64 \times 10^3}$$

Step 3: Compute numerator and denominator.

$$C_{ppm} = \dfrac{1956}{64000}$$

$$\boxed{C_{ppm} = 0.0306 \text{ ppm} \approx 30.6 \text{ ppb}}$$

**Interpretation**: 80 μg/m³ ≈ 0.03 ppm SO₂, consistent with WHO 2021 guideline values.

#### **Problem 2: ppb to μg/m³ Conversion**

**Question**: The NAAQS for Ozone (1-hour) is 180 μg/m³. Express this in ppb. M(O₃) = 48 g/mol.

**Solution**:

Step 1: Use ppb conversion formula (since 1 ppb = 10⁻⁹ by volume).

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{C_{ppb} \times M}{24.45}$$

Step 2: Rearrange to solve for $C_{ppb}$.

$$C_{ppb} = \dfrac{C_{\mu g/m^3} \times 24.45}{M}$$

Step 3: Substitute values.

$$C_{ppb} = \dfrac{180 \times 24.45}{48}$$

Step 4: Compute.

$$C_{ppb} = \dfrac{4401}{48}$$

$$\boxed{C_{ppb} \approx 91.7 \text{ ppb}}$$

#### **Problem 3: Emission Rate Calculation**

**Question**: A power plant stack emits 80 mg/Nm³ of PM at a flue gas flow rate of 200,000 Nm³/hr. Calculate the total daily emission in kg/day.

**Solution**:

Step 1: Identify the relationship.

$$E = C \times Q$$

Step 2: Substitute values (ensure units are consistent).

$$E = 80 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 \times 200{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$E = 16{,}000{,}000 \text{ mg/hr} = 16{,}000 \text{ g/hr} = 16 \text{ kg/hr}$$

Step 3: Multiply by 24 hours for daily emission.

$$E_{daily} = 16 \times 24 = 384 \text{ kg/day}$$

$$\boxed{E_{daily} = 384 \text{ kg/day}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight**: This value helps in **Consent to Operate** compliance verification by the State Pollution Control Board (SPCB) and is the basis for **consent fee calculation** as per the Water Cess Act analogue.

#### **Problem 4: AQI Sub-Index Calculation**

**Question**: The 24-hour average PM₂.₅ concentration at a station is 90 μg/m³. Calculate the AQI sub-index using the breakpoints: 0–30 → AQI 0–50; 30–60 → AQI 51–100; 60–90 → AQI 101–200; 90–120 → AQI 201–300.

**Solution**:

Step 1: Identify which breakpoint $C_p$ falls in.

$$C_p = 90 \text{ μg/m}^3 \rightarrow \text{Breakpoint: } BP_{Lo} = 60, \, BP_{Hi} = 90, \, I_{Lo} = 101, \, I_{Hi} = 200$$

Step 2: Apply the linear interpolation formula.

$$I_p = \dfrac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$$

$$I_p = \dfrac{200 - 101}{90 - 60} (90 - 60) + 101$$

Step 3: Simplify.

$$I_p = \dfrac{99}{30} \times 30 + 101 = 99 + 101$$

$$\boxed{I_p = 200}$$

**Interpretation**: AQI of 200 = "Poor" category, affecting people with lung/heart disease.

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: NAAQS vs. WHO Guidelines

| Pollutant | Averaging Time | India NAAQS | WHO 2021 | Stricter? |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PM₂.₅** | Annual | 40 μg/m³ | 5 μg/m³ | WHO (8×) |
| **PM₂.₅** | 24-hr | 60 μg/m³ | 15 μg/m³ | WHO (4×) |
| **PM₁₀** | Annual | 60 μg/m³ | 15 μg/m³ | WHO (4×) |
| **PM₁₀** | 24-hr | 100 μg/m³ | 45 μg/m³ | WHO (≈2×) |
| **NO₂** | Annual | 40 μg/m³ | 10 μg/m³ | WHO (4×) |
| **NO₂** | 24-hr | 80 μg/m³ | 25 μg/m³ | WHO (≈3×) |
| **SO₂** | 24-hr | 80 μg/m³ | 40 μg/m³ | WHO (2×) |
| **O₃** | 8-hr (peak season) | 100 μg/m³ | 60 μg/m³ | WHO (1.7×) |
| **CO** | 24-hr | 4 mg/m³ | 4 mg/m³ | Equal |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway**: India's NAAQS are **significantly more lenient** than WHO 2021 guidelines. Globally, India is among the countries with the highest PM₂.₅ exposure. Many cities exceed NAAQS itself, indicating a major implementation gap.

### 3.4 Effective Stack Height Derivation (India CPCB)

**Heuristic Formula:**

$$H = h + 1.5 \cdot L$$

where:
- H = effective stack height (m)
- h = actual physical height of the stack (m)
- L = lesser of the height or width of the building (m)

**Reasoning**: Plume must clear turbulent wake of the building to avoid downwash. Turbulent wake extends ~1.5 times the smallest building dimension above it.

**Example**: For a 30 m building with a 50 m stack:
$$L = 30 \text{ m} \quad \Rightarrow \quad H = 50 + 1.5 \times 30 = 95 \text{ m}$$
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Hierarchy of Air Quality Standards (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[International Level] --> A1[WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines 2005 Updated 2021]
    A --> A2[UNECE Geneva Convention on LRTAP]

    B[National Level India] --> B1[Air Act 1981]
    B1 --> B2[CPCB notifies NAAQS 2009]
    B2 --> B3[12 Criteria Pollutants]
    B2 --> B4[Industrial Residential Rural Areas]
    B2 --> B5[Ecologically Sensitive Areas]

    C[State Level] --> C1[SPCB Enforcement]
    C1 --> C2[Consent to Establish]
    C1 --> C3[Consent to Operate]
    C1 --> C4[Continuous Emission Monitoring]

    D[Source Level] --> D1[Stack Emission Standards MoEFCC]
    D1 --> D2[17 Industry Sectors]
    D1 --> D3[Vehicle Emission Standards BS-VI]

    E[Monitoring Network] --> E1[CAAQM Stations]
    E1 --> E2[Manual Stations]
    E1 --> E3[Continuous Stations]
    E1 --> E4[Air Quality Index Dissemination]
```

### 4.2 NAAQS Standard Setting Workflow (CPCB Process)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Health Effect Studies] --> S2[Identify Pollutants of Concern]
    S2 --> S3[Establish Dose Response Relationship]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Safety Factors]
    S4 --> S5[Set Numerical Limit]
    S5 --> S6[Choose Averaging Time]
    S6 --> S7[Stakeholder Consultation]
    S7 --> S8[Notification in Gazette]
    S8 --> S9[Implementation via SPCB]
    S9 --> S10[Periodic Review every 5 years]
    S10 --> S1
```

### 4.3 AQI Computation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input[24 hour Average Pollutant Concentrations] --> Step1[Step 1 Identify Sub Index Pollutants]
    Step1 --> Step2[Step 2 Lookup Breakpoint Concentrations BPlo BPhi]
    Step2 --> Step3[Step 3 Apply Linear Interpolation Formula]
    Step3 --> Step4[Step 4 Compute Sub Indices for all 8 Pollutants]
    Step4 --> Step5[Step 5 Select MAXIMUM Sub Index as Overall AQI]
    Step5 --> Step6[Step 6 Map to Health Category]
    Step6 --> Output[Public Health Advisory]
```

### 4.4 Emission Standard Compliance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Plant[Industrial Plant] --> Stack[Stack Chimney]
    Stack --> Monitor[CEMS Continuous Emission Monitoring System]
    Monitor --> Data[Real time Data to CPCB SPCB Server]
    Data --> Check{Within Standard?}
    Check -- Yes --> Consent[Consent Renewed]
    Check -- No --> Penalty[Legal Action Closure Direction]

    Plant --> Lab[Quarterly Stack Monitoring by NABL Accredited Lab]
    Lab --> Report[Stack Monitoring Report]
    Report --> Audit[SPCB Audit]
    Audit --> Compliance[Compliance Status]
```

### 4.5 Sampling and Measurement Architecture for AAQ

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    AmbAir[Ambient Air] --> Sampler[Air Sampler]
    Sampler --> PM[PM2.5 PM10 Gravimetric Beta Attenuation]
    Sampler --> Gas[Gaseous Pollutants]
    Gas --> SO2[SO2 - West Gaeke Method]
    Gas --> NO2[NO2 - Jacob Hochheiser Modified]
    Gas --> CO[CO - NDIR Analyzer]
    Gas --> O3[O3 - UV Photometric Chemiluminescence]
    Sampler --> Metals[Heavy Metals XRF ICP MS]
    Sampler --> VOC[VOC Canister Sampling GC MS]
```
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Questions

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the following:**
(a) NAAQS
(b) Emission Standards
(c) AQI

**Model Answer:**

**(a) NAAQS (National Ambient Air Quality Standards):**
NAAQS are legally enforceable ambient air quality standards notified by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** under the **Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981**, specifying the maximum permissible concentration of 12 criteria pollutants in ambient air over specified averaging times (1-hr, 8-hr, 24-hr, annual) for industrial/residential and ecologically sensitive areas.

**(b) Emission Standards:**
Emission standards are legally prescribed maximum limits on the **quantity, concentration, or rate** of pollutants that can be discharged into the atmosphere from a specific source (e.g., industrial stack, vehicle tailpipe) over a defined period. In India, they are notified under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** for 17 industry categories.

**(c) AQI (Air Quality Index):**
AQI is a single composite index (0–500 scale) computed from the concentrations of 8 major pollutants (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, SO₂, NO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb) that translates complex air quality data into an easily understandable category (Good → Severe) for public health communication.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Explain the significance of averaging times in ambient air quality standards with one example each for short-term and long-term averaging.**

**Model Answer:**

Averaging time is the period over which pollutant concentration is averaged to compare with the standard. It is significant because:
- **Short exposure to high concentration** of a pollutant (e.g., SO₂ causing bronchoconstriction) requires **short averaging times** (1-hour, 8-hour).
- **Long exposure to lower concentrations** of carcinogens (e.g., Benzene causing leukemia) requires **long averaging times** (annual average) to capture chronic health risks.

**Examples:**
- **Short-term (1-hour):** Ozone (O₃) — 180 μg/m³, as acute exposure causes respiratory irritation.
- **Long-term (Annual):** Lead (Pb) — 0.50 μg/m³, as chronic exposure causes cumulative neurotoxicity, especially in children.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**(a) List the 12 criteria pollutants covered under the NAAQS notified by CPCB in 2009. Explain the difference between "Industrial/Residential/Rural Areas" and "Ecologically Sensitive Areas" with suitable examples. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The 12 criteria pollutants under NAAQS (2009) are:
1. Particulate Matter (PM₁₀ and PM₂.₅)
2. Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)
3. Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)
4. Ammonia (NH₃)
5. Carbon Monoxide (CO)
6. Lead (Pb)
7. Benzene (C₆H₆)
8. Benzo(a)Pyrene (BaP)
9. Arsenic (As)
10. Nickel (Ni)
11. Ozone (O₃)

**Difference between the two area categories:**

| Parameter | Industrial/Residential/Rural Areas | Ecologically Sensitive Areas |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Areas where people live, work, and industries operate | Areas notified by Central Govt. requiring stricter protection (e.g., national parks, biosphere reserves, monuments) |
| **Standard Strictness** | Relatively lenient | Stricter (lower limits) |
| **Examples** | Mumbai suburbs, Kerala rural zones | Taj Trapezium Zone (Agra), Sundarbans, Silent Valley, Western Ghats |
| **Basis** | Mixed land use, some industrial activity acceptable | High biodiversity, endangered species, cultural heritage |
| **Typical SO₂ (24-hr)** | 80 μg/m³ | 80 μg/m³ |
| **Typical SO₂ (Annual)** | 50 μg/m³ | 20 μg/m³ (more stringent) |

**Justification for stricter ESA standards:** Ecologically sensitive areas have ecosystems more vulnerable to pollutant accumulation (e.g., lichens sensitive to SO₂, lichen decline is a bio-indicator). Acid rain, ozone, and PM cause irreversible damage to heritage monuments and biodiversity hotspots.

---

**(b) The concentration of NO₂ at a monitoring station is 0.05 ppm. Calculate its concentration in μg/m³ at 25°C. Given: Molecular weight of NO₂ = 46 g/mol. Comment on its compliance with NAAQS (24-hour) standard of 80 μg/m³. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify given data.**

$$C_{ppm} = 0.05 \text{ ppm}, \quad M = 46 \text{ g/mol}, \quad T = 25°C$$

**Step 2: Apply the conversion formula.**

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times M \times 10^3}{24.45}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values.**

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{0.05 \times 46 \times 10^3}{24.45}$$

**Step 4: Compute.**

$$C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{2300}{24.45}$$

$$\boxed{C_{\mu g/m^3} = 94.07 \text{ μg/m}^3}$$

**Step 5: Compliance check.**

Standard: 80 μg/m³. Measured: 94.07 μg/m³. 

$$94.07 \text{ μg/m}^3 > 80 \text{ μg/m}^3$$

**Result: NON-COMPLIANT.** The NO₂ level exceeds the NAAQS 24-hour standard by ~17.6%, indicating immediate need for pollution mitigation measures (traffic restrictions, industrial emission control).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) With the help of a neat flowchart, explain the methodology adopted by CPCB for setting ambient air quality standards in India. [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

The methodology of NAAQS setting is a science-based, multi-step process:

**Step 1: Identification of Pollutants**
Review of epidemiological and toxicological evidence, emission inventories, and ambient monitoring data to identify pollutants of concern.

**Step 2: Selection of Critical Effects**
Identify the most sensitive adverse health effect (e.g., for SO₂ → bronchoconstriction in asthmatics; for PM₂.₅ → premature mortality).

**Step 3: Dose-Response Assessment**
Establish the relationship between pollutant concentration and the magnitude of the health effect using human clinical, occupational, and animal studies.

**Step 4: Application of Uncertainty/Safety Factors**
Apply factors of 10 for intra-species variation and 10 for inter-species extrapolation, yielding protective Reference Concentrations (RfC).

**Step 5: Selection of Averaging Time**
Match averaging time to the relevant health endpoint (acute vs. chronic).

**Step 6: Stakeholder Consultation and Public Hearing**
Engage industries, NGOs, scientists, and the public.

**Step 7: Notification**
Standards are published in the **Official Gazette of India**, becoming legally binding under the **Air Act, 1981**.

**Step 8: Periodic Review**
CPCB revises standards every 5 years based on new scientific evidence and technological feasibility.

**Flowchart:**

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1 Pollutant Identification] --> A2[Step 2 Select Critical Health Effect]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Dose Response Analysis]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Apply Safety Factors 10 to 100]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Choose Averaging Time]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Stakeholder Consultation]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Gazette Notification]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8 SPCB Implementation]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9 Review Every 5 Years]
    A9 --> A1
```

---

**(b) A thermal power plant emits flue gas at the rate of 250,000 Nm³/hr with an SO₂ concentration of 600 mg/Nm³. The stack must comply with the emission standard of 400 mg/Nm³. [7 Marks]**
**Compute:**
**(i) Actual SO₂ emission rate in kg/hr**
**(ii) Excess SO₂ mass flow that violates the standard in kg/day**
**(iii) Suggest one control technology to comply with the standard.**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Actual SO₂ emission rate:**

$$E_{actual} = C \times Q = 600 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 \times 250{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr}$$

$$E_{actual} = 150{,}000{,}000 \text{ mg/hr} = 150{,}000 \text{ g/hr} = 150 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**(ii) Excess SO₂ (violation):**

$$E_{standard} = 400 \text{ mg/Nm}^3 \times 250{,}000 \text{ Nm}^3/\text{hr} = 100{,}000{,}000 \text{ mg/hr} = 100 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$E_{excess} = 150 - 100 = 50 \text{ kg/hr}$$

$$E_{excess, daily} = 50 \text{ kg/hr} \times 24 \text{ hr/day}$$

$$\boxed{E_{excess, daily} = 1200 \text{ kg/day}}$$

**(iii) Control Technology:**

**Flue Gas Desulfurization (FGD)**, specifically **Wet Limestone Scrubbing** (most common for large thermal power plants in India), can reduce SO₂ by up to **95%**. Other options: Dry Scrubbing using lime/NaOH, or **Circulating Dry Scrubber (CDS)**. The Ministry of Environment mandates FGD installation in all coal-based TPPs by 2026.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Losing Mistakes (KTU Valuation Patterns):**
> 1. **Unit Confusion**: Students often write ppm = μg/m³. Always convert using M and 24.45. Examiners specifically deduct marks if units are inconsistent.
> 2. **Molar Volume Error**: Using 22.4 (at 0°C) instead of 24.45 (at 25°C) when the problem states 25°C — read carefully.
> 3. **Confusing AQI Sub-Index with Final AQI**: The final AQI is the **maximum** sub-index among all pollutants, not the average. Wrong interpretation = lose 3–4 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting to Convert mg → kg**: Emission rate problems often require kg/day or tonnes/day. Missing unit conversion is a frequent deduction.
> 5. **Misidentifying "Industrial/Residential" vs "Ecologically Sensitive Area" standards**: Many students cite the same values. Always distinguish based on land-use category.
> 6. **Writing "L/m³" instead of "Nm³"**: For stack emissions, "N" denotes normal conditions (0°C, 1 atm or 25°C, 1 atm as defined). L/m³ ≠ Nm³.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **AAQ Standards** specify the *concentration* of pollutants in the *surrounding* air (e.g., 60 μg/m³ PM₂.₅ 24-hr India).
- **Emission Standards** specify the *amount* of pollutants discharged *at the source* (e.g., 30 mg/Nm³ PM from a cement stack).
- **NAAQS India (2009)** covers **12 criteria pollutants** and is enforceable under the **Air Act, 1981**.
- **Two-tier standards**: Industrial/Residential (lenient) vs. Ecologically Sensitive Areas (strict).
- **Averaging times** (1-hr, 8-hr, 24-hr, annual) align with the timescale of health effects (acute vs. chronic).
- **AQI** is the **maximum** of 8 pollutant sub-indices (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb).
- **Conversion formula**: $C_{\mu g/m^3} = \dfrac{C_{ppm} \times M \times 10^3}{24.45}$ (at 25°C, 1 atm).
- **Effective stack height** (CPCB): $H = h + 1.5 \cdot L$.
- **WHO 2021 guidelines are 4–8× stricter** than Indian NAAQS for PM₂.₅.
- **Emission standards in India** are notified under **E(P) Act, 1986** for **17 industrial sectors**.
- **CEMS** (Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems) is mandatory for **Red Category** industries and large TPPs.
- **Units to remember**: μg/m³ (ambient), mg/Nm³ (stack), ppm/ppb (volume fractions), kg/day (emission rate).
- **Key Indian Acts**: Air Act 1981, Water Act 1974, E(P) Act 1986, EPA 2020 draft.
- **FGD mandate** for thermal power plants is a high-priority KTU 2024 topic due to ongoing implementation.
- **Air Quality Index (CPCB 2014)** uses 8 pollutants; categories from "Good" (0–50) to "Severe" (401–500).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic for 12 Criteria Pollutants**: "**S**o **N**o **C**omplaints, **P**eople **L**eave **B**ecause **N**o **A**rrs **B**a**P**" → SO₂, NO₂, CO, PM, Lead, Benzene, NH₃, As, Ni, BaP (and PM₁₀/PM₂.₅).
</mm:think><!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Pollution Indices
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air Pollution Indices

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Air Pollution Indices

## 1. Core Technical Definition

An **Air Pollution Index (API)** is a standardized, dimensionless numerical scale used to communicate the quality of ambient air to the public in a simple, easy-to-understand manner. It transforms complex pollutant concentration data from multiple sources into a single value (or color-coded category) that reflects the relative health risk associated with the measured air quality.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus context, three major indices are of primary importance:
1. **Air Quality Index (AQI)** – Used by the U.S. EPA and adopted globally (including India's CPCB).
2. **Pollutant Standards Index (PSI)** – Developed by the U.S. EPA (1976) and used in Singapore, Malaysia, and other regions.
3. **Air Quality Health Index (AQHI)** – A health-risk-based index developed by Health Canada.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Indian National Air Quality Index (IND-AQI), notified by the **Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB)** in **October 2014**, is the most frequently examined index. It monitors **8 pollutants (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, and Pb)** and is divided into **8 AQI bands (0–500)** and **6 health-impact categories**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a thermometer, but instead of measuring temperature, it measures "how polluted the air is." Just as a weather report says "It is 35 °C – Hot," an air pollution index says "The AQI is 287 – Poor air quality." 

Think of it like a **school grading system** for the atmosphere:
- 90–100 = A grade (Excellent air)
- 75–89 = B grade (Acceptable)
- 50–74 = C grade (Caution for sensitive groups)
- Below 50 = Fail (Severe health impact)

The genius of the index is **synthesis**: instead of forcing the public to understand that "PM₂.₅ = 78 µg/m³ AND O₃ = 142 µg/m³ AND NO₂ = 64 µg/m³," the index collapses these into a **single number** that tells you *what to do* (e.g., "Avoid outdoor exercise").

### Standard Metrics in Air Pollution Indices

- **AQI Range:** **0 – 500** (higher = worse)
- **Reporting Frequency:** Hourly (real-time) for most pollutants; 8-hourly rolling averages for CO and O₃.
- **Governing Body (India):** Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) under the Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change (MoEFCC).
- **WHO 2021 Annual Guideline (PM₂.₅):** **5 µg/m³** (for reference only – not used in index calculation).

> [!NOTE]
> An AQI is **NOT a direct measurement** of any single pollutant. It is a *transformed* and *capped* value derived from the *worst-performing* pollutant at a given location and time.

---

## 2. Need and Purpose of Air Pollution Indices

The fundamental reasons for developing pollution indices are:

1. **Public Communication:** Pollutant concentrations are reported in different units (µg/m³, ppm, ppb), making comparison difficult. A unit-less index solves this problem.
2. **Health Risk Translation:** Converts chemical concentrations into health advisory language (Good, Moderate, Unhealthy, etc.).
3. **Regulatory Benchmark:** Provides a single trigger value for issuing health advisories, industrial shutdowns, or vehicular restrictions (e.g., the Graded Response Action Plan – GRAP in Delhi-NCR).
4. **Spatial and Temporal Comparison:** Enables comparison of air quality between cities, states, and even countries on a common scale.
5. **Policy Evaluation:** Tracks the effectiveness of pollution control measures over time.

---

## 3. The Indian National Air Quality Index (IND-AQI)

The CPCB notified the Indian National AQI in **October 2014**, replacing the earlier National Ambient Air Quality Monitoring Programme (NAMP) reporting format. The system is based on **24-hourly average concentrations** (with the exception of O₃ and CO, which use 8-hour averages).

### 3.1 The 8 Pollutants and Their Average Periods

| # | Pollutant | Symbol | Averaging Period |
|---|-----------|--------|------------------|
| 1 | Particulate Matter < 2.5 µm | PM₂.₅ | 24 hours |
| 2 | Particulate Matter < 10 µm | PM₁₀ | 24 hours |
| 3 | Nitrogen Dioxide | NO₂ | 24 hours |
| 4 | Sulphur Dioxide | SO₂ | 24 hours |
| 5 | Carbon Monoxide | CO | 8 hours |
| 6 | Ozone | O₃ | 8 hours |
| 7 | Ammonia | NH₃ | 24 hours |
| 8 | Lead | Pb | 24 hours |

### 3.2 The 8 AQI Bands and 6 Categories

| AQI | Category | Color Code | Health Impact |
|-----|----------|------------|----------------|
| 0–50 | **Good** | Green | Minimal impact |
| 51–100 | **Satisfactory** | Light Green | Minor breathing discomfort to sensitive people |
| 101–200 | **Moderate** | Yellow | Breathing discomfort to people with lung disease, children, older adults |
| 201–300 | **Poor** | Orange | Breathing discomfort to most people on prolonged exposure |
| 301–400 | **Very Poor** | Red | Respiratory illness on prolonged exposure |
| 401–500 | **Severe** | Dark Red / Maroon | Affects healthy people and seriously impacts those with existing diseases |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **maximum of all 8 sub-indices** becomes the reported AQI. The pollutant that produces the maximum sub-index is called the **"Critical Pollutant"** and is reported alongside the AQI.

---

## 4. Pollutant Standards Index (PSI)

The **Pollutant Standards Index (PSI)** was developed by the U.S. EPA in **1976** and is still used in countries like Singapore and Malaysia. It is based on **24-hour average concentrations** of five pollutants:

- **PM₁₀** (Particulate Matter)
- **SO₂** (Sulphur Dioxide)
- **CO** (Carbon Monoxide)
- **O₃** (Ozone)
- **NO₂** (Nitrogen Dioxide)

### 4.1 PSI Categories and Health Advisories

| PSI Value | Descriptor | General Health Effects |
|-----------|------------|------------------------|
| 0–50 | Good | No health effects expected |
| 51–100 | Moderate | Few or no health effects |
| 101–199 | Unhealthy | Slight aggravation of symptoms for sensitive groups |
| 200–299 | Very Unhealthy | Significant aggravation of symptoms; decreased exercise tolerance |
| 300–399 | Hazardous | Premature onset of certain diseases; serious aggravation of symptoms |
| 400–500 | Hazardous | Premature death of ill and elderly; serious health effects in healthy population |

> [!NOTE]
> The PSI is similar in structure to the AQI but uses **fewer pollutants (5 vs 8)** and is based purely on the **U.S. National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS)** of 1987.

---

## 5. Air Quality Health Index (AQHI)

The **Air Quality Health Index (AQHI)** was developed by **Health Canada** in **2005** and represents a paradigm shift from concentration-based to **health-risk-based** reporting.

### 5.1 Key Differences from AQI/PSI

| Feature | AQI / PSI | AQHI |
|---------|-----------|------|
| Basis | Concentration breakpoints tied to standards | Mortality and morbidity risk from epidemiological studies |
| Pollutants | 5–8 | 3 (O₃, NO₂, PM₂.₅) |
| Output | Range 0–500 | Range 1–10+ |
| Weighting | Maximum sub-index | Combined excess risk summation |
| Reporting | Health category | Health risk + advice |

### 5.2 AQHI Scale and Health Messages

| AQHI Value | Health Risk | Health Message |
|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| 1–3 | Low | Enjoy your usual outdoor activities |
| 4–6 | Moderate | Consider reducing strenuous outdoor activity |
| 7–10 | High | Reduce or reschedule strenuous outdoor activities |
| 10+ | Very High | Avoid strenuous outdoor activities |

### 5.3 AQHI Formula

The AQHI is calculated by summing the **excess relative risk** contributions of the three pollutants:

$$AQHI = \frac{100}{10.4} \times \left( e^{0.000871 \times \Delta O_3} - 1 + e^{0.000537 \times \Delta PM_{2.5}} - 1 + e^{0.000871 \times \Delta NO_2} - 1 \right)$$

Where Δ represents the **3-hour moving average concentration** of the pollutant.

---

## 6. Comparative Summary of Major Air Pollution Indices

| Parameter | Indian AQI (CPCB) | U.S. AQI (EPA) | PSI | AQHI |
|-----------|-------------------|----------------|-----|------|
| Year | 2014 | 1999 (revised) | 1976 | 2005 |
| No. of Pollutants | 8 | 5 | 5 | 3 |
| AQI Range | 0–500 | 0–500 | 0–500 | 1–10+ |
| Overall Index | Max of sub-indices | Max of sub-indices | Max of sub-indices | Sum of risks |
| Health Basis | Standard-based | Standard-based | Standard-based | Risk-based |
| Used in | India | USA, many countries | Singapore, Malaysia | Canada |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 1. The Sub-Index Calculation (Linear Interpolation Method)

The cornerstone of AQI computation is the **linear interpolation formula**. This method maps a measured pollutant concentration (which has no inherent "index" value) onto the AQI scale (0–500) using predefined concentration breakpoints.

### 1.1 Conceptual Logic — Step by Step

- **Step 1:** Establish "concentration breakpoints" — these are reference concentration values (e.g., 0, 30, 60, 90... µg/m³ for PM₂.₅) at which the air quality transitions from one health category to another.
- **Step 2:** Assign a corresponding AQI value to each breakpoint (e.g., 0, 50, 100, 200...).
- **Step 3:** When a measured concentration lies *between two breakpoints*, use **linear interpolation** to estimate the AQI sub-index.
- **Step 4:** Repeat for all 8 pollutants → obtain 8 sub-indices.
- **Step 5:** The **overall AQI = MAX of all 8 sub-indices**.

### 1.2 Why Linear Interpolation?

Linear interpolation assumes that within a breakpoint interval, the health risk **changes linearly** with concentration. While not perfectly accurate (health risks often follow non-linear dose-response curves), it provides a **simple, transparent, and reproducible** method suitable for regulatory and public communication purposes.

---

## 2. The Master Formula (Universal Sub-Index Equation)

The same formula is used for all pollutants in all standard-based indices (AQI, PSI). The only thing that changes is the **breakpoint table**.

$$I_p = \left[ \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \right] \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$$

### Variable Definitions

| Symbol | Meaning | Unit |
|--------|---------|------|
| $I_p$ | Sub-Index for pollutant *p* | Dimensionless (0–500) |
| $C_p$ | Measured (truncated) concentration of pollutant *p* | µg/m³ (or mg/m³ for CO) |
| $BP_{Hi}$ | Concentration BreakPoint $\geq$ $C_p$ (Higher limit) | µg/m³ |
| $BP_{Lo}$ | Concentration BreakPoint $\leq$ $C_p$ (Lower limit) | µg/m³ |
| $I_{Hi}$ | AQI value corresponding to $BP_{Hi}$ | Dimensionless |
| $I_{Lo}$ | AQI value corresponding to $BP_{Lo}$ | Dimensionless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Truncation Rule:** The measured concentration $C_p$ is **truncated to the same number of decimal places as the breakpoints** before applying the formula. For example, if PM₂.₅ breakpoints are in whole numbers (0, 30, 60...), then a reading of 35.7 µg/m³ becomes 35.0 µg/m³.

---

## 3. KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (Quick Reference Table)

| # | Formula | Application |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $I_p = \left[ \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \right] \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$ | Sub-index calculation for any pollutant |
| 2 | $AQI = \max(I_1, I_2, I_3, \dots, I_8)$ | Overall AQI = Maximum sub-index |
| 3 | $\text{Critical Pollutant} = \arg\max_{p} (I_p)$ | Pollutant with highest sub-index |
| 4 | $AQHI = \frac{100}{10.4} \left( e^{0.000871 \Delta O_3} + e^{0.000537 \Delta PM_{2.5}} + e^{0.000871 \Delta NO_2} - 3 \right)$ | Health-risk based index (Canada) |
| 5 | $\text{Non-cancer Hazard Index} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{C_i}{RfC_i}$ | Cumulative exposure risk (advanced topic) |

### Boundary Conditions

| Condition | Treatment |
|-----------|-----------|
| $C_p < BP_{Lo, \min}$ | $I_p = 0$ (treated as clean) |
| $C_p > BP_{Hi, \max}$ | $I_p = 500$ (capped at hazardous level) |
| Missing pollutant data | Sub-index for that pollutant is **not computed**; AQI uses available data |
| Negative concentrations | Treated as zero |

---

## 4. Real-World Engineering Utility

1. **Urban Air Quality Management:** Municipal corporations use AQI dashboards (e.g., CPCB's Sameer app) to issue daily health advisories.
2. **Emergency Action Plans:** The Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP) in Delhi-NCR triggers specific measures based on AQI thresholds (e.g., Stage I at AQI > 300, Stage IV at AQI > 500).
3. **Industrial Emission Control:** Continuous Emission Monitoring Systems (CEMS) in industries use index-based reporting to flag non-compliance.
4. **Smart City Infrastructure:** IoT-based sensor networks (e.g., SAFAR, OpenAQ) feed real-time AQI values to public displays and mobile apps.
5. **Public Health Studies:** Epidemiologists correlate AQI trends with hospital admissions for respiratory and cardiovascular diseases.

# Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 1. The Linear Interpolation Formula — Full Derivation

The AQI sub-index formula is derived from the **two-point form of a straight line**.

### Derivation

We are given two known points on the AQI scale:
- **Point 1:** $(BP_{Lo}, I_{Lo})$ — Lower concentration breakpoint and its AQI value.
- **Point 2:** $(BP_{Hi}, I_{Hi})$ — Higher concentration breakpoint and its AQI value.

We want to find the AQI value $I_p$ corresponding to a measured concentration $C_p$ that lies *between* these two points.

The **equation of a straight line** passing through two points $(x_1, y_1)$ and $(x_2, y_2)$ is:

$$y - y_1 = \frac{y_2 - y_1}{x_2 - x_1} \times (x - x_1)$$

Substituting our variables — let $x = C_p$, $y = I_p$, $x_1 = BP_{Lo}$, $y_1 = I_{Lo}$, $x_2 = BP_{Hi}$, $y_2 = I_{Hi}$:

$$I_p - I_{Lo} = \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \times (C_p - BP_{Lo})$$

Rearranging to isolate $I_p$:

$$\boxed{I_p = \left[ \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \right] \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}}$$

This is the **universal sub-index formula** used for all pollutants in the Indian AQI, U.S. AQI, and PSI systems.

---

## 2. Worked-Out Numerical Examples (Indian AQI Breakpoints)

### 2.1 The Indian AQI Breakpoint Table (CPCB, 2014)

| AQI | PM₂.₅ (24h) | PM₁₀ (24h) | NO₂ (24h) | SO₂ (24h) | CO (8h, mg/m³) | O₃ (8h) | NH₃ (24h) | Pb (24h) |
|-----|-------------|------------|-----------|-----------|----------------|---------|-----------|----------|
| 0–50 | 0–30 | 0–50 | 0–40 | 0–40 | 0–1.0 | 0–50 | 0–200 | 0–0.5 |
| 51–100 | 31–60 | 51–100 | 41–80 | 41–80 | 1.1–2.0 | 51–100 | 201–400 | 0.6–1.0 |
| 101–200 | 61–90 | 101–250 | 81–180 | 81–380 | 2.1–10 | 101–168 | 401–800 | 1.1–2.0 |
| 201–300 | 91–120 | 251–350 | 181–280 | 381–800 | 10.1–17 | 169–208 | 801–1200 | 2.1–3.0 |
| 301–400 | 121–250 | 351–430 | 281–400 | 801–1600 | 17.1–34 | 209–748 | 1201–1800 | 3.1–3.5 |
| 401–500 | 250+ | 430+ | 400+ | 1600+ | 34+ | 748+ | 1800+ | 3.5+ |

*(All concentrations in µg/m³ except CO in mg/m³)*

---

### 2.2 Worked Example 1 — Single Pollutant AQI

**Problem:** A monitoring station records the following 24-hour average concentrations:
- PM₂.₅ = 78 µg/m³
- PM₁₀ = 145 µg/m³
- NO₂ = 95 µg/m³

Calculate the AQI and identify the critical pollutant.

#### Step 1: Calculate Sub-Index for PM₂.₅ (C = 78 µg/m³)

From the breakpoint table, 78 µg/m³ falls in the range **61–90** (AQI 101–200).
- $BP_{Lo} = 61$, $I_{Lo} = 101$
- $BP_{Hi} = 90$, $I_{Hi} = 200$
- $C_p = 78$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \left[ \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} \right] \times (78 - 61) + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \left[ \frac{99}{29} \right] \times 17 + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = 3.4138 \times 17 + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = 58.034 + 101 = 159.03 \approx 159$$

#### Step 2: Calculate Sub-Index for PM₁₀ (C = 145 µg/m³)

From the table, 145 µg/m³ falls in the range **101–250** (AQI 101–200).
- $BP_{Lo} = 101$, $I_{Lo} = 101$
- $BP_{Hi} = 250$, $I_{Hi} = 200$
- $C_p = 145$

$$I_{PM_{10}} = \left[ \frac{200 - 101}{250 - 101} \right] \times (145 - 101) + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{10}} = \left[ \frac{99}{149} \right] \times 44 + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{10}} = 0.6644 \times 44 + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{10}} = 29.235 + 101 = 130.23 \approx 130$$

#### Step 3: Calculate Sub-Index for NO₂ (C = 95 µg/m³)

From the table, 95 µg/m³ falls in the range **81–180** (AQI 101–200).
- $BP_{Lo} = 81$, $I_{Lo} = 101$
- $BP_{Hi} = 180$, $I_{Hi} = 200$
- $C_p = 95$

$$I_{NO_2} = \left[ \frac{200 - 101}{180 - 81} \right] \times (95 - 81) + 101$$

$$I_{NO_2} = \left[ \frac{99}{99} \right] \times 14 + 101$$

$$I_{NO_2} = 1.0 \times 14 + 101 = 115$$

#### Step 4: Determine Overall AQI

$$AQI = \max(159, 130, 115) = 159$$

**Critical Pollutant = PM₂.₅** (since 159 is the maximum sub-index)

**AQI Category:** 159 falls in the **101–200** range → **Moderate** (Yellow)

> [!NOTE]
> Notice how PM₂.₅ drives the AQI even though PM₁₀ has a higher absolute concentration. This is because PM₂.₅ has **stricter health-based breakpoints** — it is more toxic per unit mass.

---

### 2.3 Worked Example 2 — Boundary Concentration (Truncation Rule)

**Problem:** A station measures PM₂.₅ = 35.7 µg/m³. Calculate the sub-index.

#### Step 1: Truncate the Concentration

PM₂.₅ breakpoints are in whole numbers → truncate 35.7 to **35.0 µg/m³**.

#### Step 2: Identify Breakpoints

35 µg/m³ falls in **31–60** (AQI 51–100).
- $BP_{Lo} = 31$, $I_{Lo} = 51$
- $BP_{Hi} = 60$, $I_{Hi} = 100$

#### Step 3: Apply Formula

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \left[ \frac{100 - 51}{60 - 31} \right] \times (35 - 31) + 51$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \left[ \frac{49}{29} \right] \times 4 + 51$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = 1.6897 \times 4 + 51 = 6.759 + 51 = 57.76 \approx 58$$

**Category:** 58 → **Satisfactory** (Light Green)

---

### 2.4 Worked Example 3 — Above Maximum Breakpoint (Capping)

**Problem:** SO₂ = 1800 µg/m³ observed. Find sub-index.

Since 1800 ≥ 1600 (the highest breakpoint), the sub-index is **capped at 500**.

$$I_{SO_2} = 500$$

**Category:** 500 → **Severe** (Maroon)

---

## 3. Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Aspect | Indian AQI | U.S. AQI | PSI | AQHI |
|--------|-----------|----------|-----|------|
| Governing Body | CPCB, India | U.S. EPA | U.S. EPA (legacy) | Health Canada |
| Number of Pollutants | 8 | 5 (PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, O₃, CO, SO₂, NO₂) | 5 | 3 |
| AQI Range | 0–500 | 0–500 | 0–500 | 1–10+ |
| Reporting AQI Method | Max of sub-indices | Max of sub-indices | Max of sub-indices | Sum of excess risks |
| Standard for Breakpoints | CPCB NAAQS (2009) | U.S. NAAQS | U.S. NAAQS (1987) | Epidemiological risk functions |
| Carbon Monoxide Unit | mg/m³ (8h) | ppm (8h) | ppm (8h) | Not included |
| Frequency of Update | Real-time + 24h rolling | Real-time + 24h rolling | 24h rolling | Real-time (hourly) |
| Color Coding | 6 categories | 6 categories | 5 categories | 4 categories |
