# KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Define Air Quality Index. List any four pollutants considered in the Indian National AQI (CPCB).**

**Model Answer:**

The **Air Quality Index (AQI)** is a standardized, dimensionless numerical scale (0–500) used to communicate ambient air quality and associated health risks to the general public in a simple, single-value format. It is computed by converting measured pollutant concentrations into sub-indices via linear interpolation, and the overall AQI is reported as the maximum of all sub-indices.

The four pollutants considered in the Indian National AQI (notified by CPCB in 2014) are:

1. **Particulate Matter (PM₂.₅)** – 24-hour average
2. **Particulate Matter (PM₁₀)** – 24-hour average
3. **Nitrogen Dioxide (NO₂)** – 24-hour average
4. **Sulphur Dioxide (SO₂)** – 24-hour average

*(The other four are CO, O₃, NH₃, and Pb.)*

---

### **Question 2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**What is the Pollutant Standards Index (PSI)? How does it differ from the Air Quality Health Index (AQHI)?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Pollutant Standards Index (PSI)** is a concentration-based air quality index developed by the U.S. EPA in 1976. It uses five pollutants (PM₁₀, SO₂, CO, O₃, NO₂) and reports air quality on a scale of 0–500 based on 24-hour average concentrations, with the overall PSI being the maximum of the individual sub-indices.

| Feature | PSI | AQHI |
|---------|-----|------|
| Basis | Concentration-based (standards) | Health risk-based (epidemiology) |
| Number of Pollutants | 5 | 3 (O₃, NO₂, PM₂.₅) |
| Scale | 0–500 | 1–10+ |
| Reporting | Maximum sub-index | Sum of excess mortality/morbidity risk |
| Developed by | U.S. EPA (1976) | Health Canada (2005) |

The key conceptual difference is that **PSI is regulatory-standard-based**, while **AQHI is epidemiological-risk-based** — meaning AQHI directly reflects the *actual health outcome* (deaths/hospitalizations) rather than just the concentration.

---

## Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the methodology for computing the Indian National Air Quality Index (AQI) as per CPCB guidelines. List all eight pollutants and their respective averaging periods. (7 marks)

**(b)** A monitoring station records the following pollutant concentrations:
- PM₂.₅ = 85 µg/m³ (24-hour average)
- PM₁₀ = 220 µg/m³ (24-hour average)
- NO₂ = 150 µg/m³ (24-hour average)

Calculate the overall AQI and identify the critical pollutant. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Part (a)

**Methodology for Indian National AQI Computation (CPCB, 2014):**

**Step 1 — Pollutant Identification:** Eight criteria pollutants are monitored:

| # | Pollutant | Averaging Period |
|---|-----------|------------------|
| 1 | PM₂.₅ | 24 hours |
| 2 | PM₁₀ | 24 hours |
| 3 | NO₂ | 24 hours |
| 4 | SO₂ | 24 hours |
| 5 | CO | 8 hours |
| 6 | O₃ | 8 hours |
| 7 | NH₃ | 24 hours |
| 8 | Pb | 24 hours |

**Step 2 — Truncation:** Each measured concentration is truncated to the number of decimal places of the relevant breakpoint.

**Step 3 — Breakpoint Identification:** The truncated concentration is located within the CPCB breakpoint table, and the corresponding lower ($BP_{Lo}$, $I_{Lo}$) and upper ($BP_{Hi}$, $I_{Hi}$) limits are noted.

**Step 4 — Sub-Index Calculation:** For each pollutant *p*, the sub-index is:

$$I_p = \left[ \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \right] \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$$

**Step 5 — Overall AQI Determination:** The reported AQI is the **maximum** of all eight sub-indices.

**Step 6 — Critical Pollutant Identification:** The pollutant corresponding to the maximum sub-index is reported as the "critical pollutant."

**Step 7 — Categorization:** The AQI value is mapped to one of 8 bands (0–500) and 6 health categories with color coding (Good, Satisfactory, Moderate, Poor, Very Poor, Severe).

---

#### Solution to Part (b)

**Given:** PM₂.₅ = 85 µg/m³, PM₁₀ = 220 µg/m³, NO₂ = 150 µg/m³

##### Sub-Index for PM₂.₅ (C = 85 µg/m³)

From CPCB table, 85 µg/m³ falls in the range **61–90** (AQI 101–200).
- $BP_{Lo} = 61$, $I_{Lo} = 101$
- $BP_{Hi} = 90$, $I_{Hi} = 200$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \left[ \frac{200 - 101}{90 - 61} \right] \times (85 - 61) + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \left[ \frac{99}{29} \right] \times 24 + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = 3.4138 \times 24 + 101 = 81.93 + 101 = 182.93 \approx 183$$

##### Sub-Index for PM₁₀ (C = 220 µg/m³)

From CPCB table, 220 µg/m³ falls in the range **101–250** (AQI 101–200).
- $BP_{Lo} = 101$, $I_{Lo} = 101$
- $BP_{Hi} = 250$, $I_{Hi} = 200$

$$I_{PM_{10}} = \left[ \frac{200 - 101}{250 - 101} \right] \times (220 - 101) + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{10}} = \left[ \frac{99}{149} \right] \times 119 + 101$$

$$I_{PM_{10}} = 0.6644 \times 119 + 101 = 79.07 + 101 = 180.07 \approx 180$$

##### Sub-Index for NO₂ (C = 150 µg/m³)

From CPCB table, 150 µg/m³ falls in the range **81–180** (AQI 101–200).
- $BP_{Lo} = 81$, $I_{Lo} = 101$
- $BP_{Hi} = 180$, $I_{Hi} = 200$

$$I_{NO_2} = \left[ \frac{200 - 101}{180 - 81} \right] \times (150 - 81) + 101$$

$$I_{NO_2} = \left[ \frac{99}{99} \right] \times 69 + 101$$

$$I_{NO_2} = 1.0 \times 69 + 101 = 170$$

##### Overall AQI and Critical Pollutant

$$AQI = \max(183, 180, 170) = 183$$

**Critical Pollutant:** **PM₂.₅** (highest sub-index = 183)

**AQI Category:** 183 falls in **101–200** → **Moderate** (Yellow)

**Health Advisory:** Breathing discomfort possible for people with lung disease, children, and older adults during prolonged outdoor exertion.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a)** Discuss the Air Quality Health Index (AQHI) in detail. Compare its approach with the AQI and PSI. (7 marks)

**(b)** The following 8-hour and 24-hour average concentrations were recorded at a city station:

| Pollutant | Concentration | Averaging Period |
|-----------|--------------|------------------|
| SO₂ | 290 µg/m³ | 24 hours |
| NO₂ | 130 µg/m³ | 24 hours |
| PM₂.₅ | 55 µg/m³ | 24 hours |
| CO | 6.5 mg/m³ | 8 hours |

Determine the AQI using the CPCB sub-index formula. (7 marks)

---

#### Solution to Part (a)

**Air Quality Health Index (AQHI) — Detailed Discussion:**

The **AQHI** is a health-risk-based air quality index developed by **Health Canada** in 2005. Unlike concentration-based indices (AQI, PSI), the AQHI is derived from **epidemiological dose-response relationships** that quantify the *excess risk of mortality and morbidity* attributable to short-term exposure to air pollutants.

**Key Features of AQHI:**

1. **Pollutants:** Three core pollutants — **O₃, NO₂, and PM₂.₅** — chosen based on robust epidemiological evidence linking them to acute health outcomes.
2. **Mathematical Basis:** The index uses the **relative risk (RR) functions** from time-series studies. The excess risk for each pollutant is:
   - $\Delta Risk = e^{\beta \times \Delta C} - 1$
   - Where $\beta$ is the epidemiological coefficient and $\Delta C$ is the 3-hour moving average concentration increment above a reference.
3. **Aggregation:** Unlike AQI/PSI (which take the maximum sub-index), the AQHI **sums the excess risks** of the three pollutants, providing a cumulative health risk estimate.
4. **Scale:** Reported on a scale of **1 to 10+**, with health messages for each band.

**Comparison Table:**

| Aspect | AQI | PSI | AQHI |
|--------|-----|-----|------|
| Basis | Concentration breakpoints | Concentration breakpoints | Mortality/morbidity risk |
| Number of Pollutants | 5–8 | 5 | 3 |
| Scale | 0–500 | 0–500 | 1–10+ |
| Aggregation | Maximum sub-index | Maximum sub-index | Sum of excess risks |
| Health Messaging | Category-based | Category-based | Risk-graded advice |
| Authority | U.S. EPA / CPCB | U.S. EPA | Health Canada |

**Key Conceptual Difference:** AQI and PSI tell you *"how much pollution is in the air"*; AQHI tells you *"what is the health consequence of that pollution."*

---

#### Solution to Part (b)

**Given:** SO₂ = 290 µg/m³ (24h), NO₂ = 130 µg/m³ (24h), PM₂.₅ = 55 µg/m³ (24h), CO = 6.5 mg/m³ (8h)

##### Sub-Index for SO₂ (C = 290 µg/m³)

From CPCB table, 290 µg/m³ falls in the range **81–380** (AQI 101–200).
- $BP_{Lo} = 81$, $I_{Lo} = 101$
- $BP_{Hi} = 380$, $I_{Hi} = 200$

$$I_{SO_2} = \left[ \frac{200 - 101}{380 - 81} \right] \times (290 - 81) + 101$$

$$I_{SO_2} = \left[ \frac{99}{299} \right] \times 209 + 101$$

$$I_{SO_2} = 0.3311 \times 209 + 101 = 69.20 + 101 = 170.20 \approx 170$$

##### Sub-Index for NO₂ (C = 130 µg/m³)

From CPCB table, 130 µg/m³ falls in the range **81–180** (AQI 101–200).
- $BP_{Lo} = 81$, $I_{Lo} = 101$
- $BP_{Hi} = 180$, $I_{Hi} = 200$

$$I_{NO_2} = \left[ \frac{200 - 101}{180 - 81} \right] \times (130 - 81) + 101$$

$$I_{NO_2} = 1.0 \times 49 + 101 = 150$$

##### Sub-Index for PM₂.₅ (C = 55 µg/m³)

From CPCB table, 55 µg/m³ falls in the range **31–60** (AQI 51–100).
- $BP_{Lo} = 31$, $I_{Lo} = 51$
- $BP_{Hi} = 60$, $I_{Hi} = 100$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \left[ \frac{100 - 51}{60 - 31} \right] \times (55 - 31) + 51$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = \left[ \frac{49}{29} \right] \times 24 + 51$$

$$I_{PM_{2.5}} = 1.6897 \times 24 + 51 = 40.55 + 51 = 91.55 \approx 92$$

##### Sub-Index for CO (C = 6.5 mg/m³)

From CPCB table, 6.5 mg/m³ falls in the range **2.1–10** (AQI 101–200).
- $BP_{Lo} = 2.1$, $I_{Lo} = 101$
- $BP_{Hi} = 10$, $I_{Hi} = 200$

$$I_{CO} = \left[ \frac{200 - 101}{10 - 2.1} \right] \times (6.5 - 2.1) + 101$$

$$I_{CO} = \left[ \frac{99}{7.9} \right] \times 4.4 + 101$$

$$I_{CO} = 12.5316 \times 4.4 + 101 = 55.14 + 101 = 156.14 \approx 156$$

##### Overall AQI and Critical Pollutant

$$AQI = \max(170, 150, 92, 156) = 170$$

**Critical Pollutant:** **SO₂** (highest sub-index = 170)

**AQI Category:** 170 falls in **101–200** → **Moderate** (Yellow)

**Health Advisory:** People with respiratory or heart disease, children, and older adults should limit prolonged outdoor exertion.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Truncation Rule:** KTU examiners frequently test this. If a reading is 35.7 µg/m³, you must truncate to 35.0 before applying the formula. Failing to do so will cost **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Confusing the overall AQI rule:** Many students *average* the sub-indices. The correct rule is **MAXIMUM**, not mean. This is a **3-mark deduction** if wrong.
> 3. **Mixing up averaging periods:** CO and O₃ use **8-hour averages**, while all other pollutants use **24-hour averages**. Wrong averaging period = incorrect breakpoints = **2–3 marks lost**.
> 4. **Not identifying the critical pollutant:** Examiners award **1 mark** for explicitly stating which pollutant drove the AQI.
> 5. **Unit errors for CO:** CO breakpoints are in **mg/m³**, not µg/m³. A common mistake is using the wrong unit. (1 ppm CO ≈ 1.145 mg/m³)
> 6. **Reporting decimal AQI:** KTU expects **whole-number AQI values** (rounded). Reporting 159.7 instead of 160 is acceptable, but reporting 159.03 may invite minor deductions for sloppy rounding.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### 🔑 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **AQI Definition:** Single dimensionless number (0–500) that summarizes ambient air quality and associated health risk.
- ✅ **Indian AQI (CPCB 2014):** Uses **8 pollutants** — PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, CO, O₃, NH₃, Pb.
- ✅ **Averaging Periods:** **8 hours** for CO and O₃; **24 hours** for all other six pollutants.
- ✅ **Universal Sub-Index Formula:** $I_p = \left[ \frac{I_{Hi} - I_{Lo}}{BP_{Hi} - BP_{Lo}} \right] \times (C_p - BP_{Lo}) + I_{Lo}$
- ✅ **Overall AQI = MAX** of all sub-indices (NOT average, NOT sum).
- ✅ **Critical Pollutant:** The pollutant whose sub-index equals the overall AQI.
- ✅ **6 AQI Categories in India:** Good (0–50), Satisfactory (51–100), Moderate (101–200), Poor (201–300), Very Poor (301–400), Severe (401–500).
- ✅ **Truncation Rule:** Always truncate measured concentration to the same decimal precision as the breakpoint table.
- ✅ **Capping Rule:** If concentration exceeds the highest breakpoint, sub-index = 500.
- ✅ **PSI (1976):** U.S. EPA legacy index using 5 pollutants (PM₁₀, SO₂, CO, O₃, NO₂) on a 0–500 scale.
- ✅ **AQHI (2005):** Health Canada index based on **epidemiological risk summation** for 3 pollutants (O₃, NO₂, PM₂.₅); scale 1–10+.
- ✅ **Key Difference:** AQI/PSI = concentration-based; AQHI = risk-based (uses mortality/morbidity data).
- ✅ **CO Unit:** mg/m³ in Indian AQI; ppm in U.S. AQI/PSI. (1 ppm = 1.145 mg/m³)
- ✅ **O₃ Scale:** 8-hour average in Indian AQI (0–748 µg/m³ range, AQI 0–500).
- ✅ **Graded Response Action Plan (GRAP):** Delhi-NCR emergency protocol triggered by AQI thresholds (Stage I at AQI > 300, Stage IV at AQI > 500).
- ✅ **Pollutant Health Priority:** PM₂.₅ has the **strictest breakpoints** among all 8 pollutants, making it the most common critical pollutant in Indian cities.

### 🎯 High-Yield Quick Facts for Last-Minute Revision

| Fact | Value |
|------|-------|
| Indian AQI year of launch | **2014** (CPCB notification, October) |
| Total pollutants in IND-AQI | **8** |
| Total pollutants in PSI | **5** |
| Total pollutants in AQHI | **3** |
| AQI upper cap | **500** |
| Highest-breakpoint sub-index | **500 (Severe)** |
| Color for "Satisfactory" | **Light Green** |
| Color for "Severe" | **Maroon / Dark Red** |
| PM₂.₅ most common critical pollutant? | **Yes** (in most Indian cities) |
| 8-hour pollutants | **CO, O₃** |
| 24-hour pollutants | **PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, NO₂, SO₂, NH₃, Pb** |
| AQHI developer | **Health Canada (2005)** |
| PSI developer | **U.S. EPA (1976)** |

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Air Act, Legislation and Regulations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Air Act, Legislation and Regulations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Air Pollution Legislation: The Legal Backbone of Environmental Engineering

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)
**Air Pollution Legislation** refers to the comprehensive framework of national statutes, rules, notifications, and regulatory standards enacted by the Government of India to prevent, control, and abate atmospheric pollution. It establishes the legal authority of statutory bodies (CPCB & SPCB), prescribes emission and ambient standards, defines penalties for non-compliance, and provides mechanisms for enforcement and judicial recourse.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The legislative framework is the *enforcement arm* of environmental engineering. Without statutory backing, technical control measures (modules 1, 3, 4) have no legal compulsion. This module binds the entire curriculum into practice.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **traffic system**. Traffic lights, speed limits, and license rules don't *physically* stop accidents, but they **legally compel** drivers to drive safely and give police the *authority* to penalize violators. Similarly:
- **Acts** = The traffic laws (e.g., "you must not exceed 60 km/h")
- **Rules/Standards** = The actual speed limit number
- **CPCB/SPCB** = The traffic police
- **Consent Orders** = The driving license
- **Penalties** = The challan/fine

Without these laws, industries could legally release unlimited toxic gases, and citizens would have no legal remedy.

### Key Statutory Bodies (Standard Metrics in **bold**)
| Body | Full Form | Jurisdiction | Established By |
|------|-----------|--------------|----------------|
| **CPCB** | Central Pollution Control Board | Whole of India | Water Act, **1974** |
| **SPCB** | State Pollution Control Board | Respective State | Water Act, **1974** |
| **MoEFCC** | Ministry of Environment, Forest and Climate Change | Policy & Apex | Government of India |
| **NGT** | National Green Tribunal | Environmental Adjudication | NGT Act, **2010** |

### The Five Major Indian Environmental Statutes (Hierarchical Pyramid)
The Indian environmental legal framework operates in a **pyramidal hierarchy** (the *Doctrine of Harmonious Construction*):

1. **Constitution of India** (Articles 14, 19(1)(a), 21, 48A, 51A(g)) — Apex Law
2. **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** — Umbrella Act
3. **Water (P&CP) Act, 1974** — First specialized act
4. **Air (P&CP) Act, 1981** — The act for air quality
5. **Rules/Notifications** — Detailed technical standards

> [!NOTE]
> **Polluter Pays Principle** & **Precautionary Principle** are the two foundational doctrines flowing from Articles 21, 48A, and judicial interpretations (Vellore Citizens Forum case, 1996).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Air (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1981

This is the **principal statute** for air pollution control in India, enacted under Article 253 of the Constitution (to give effect to the Stockholm Conference decisions of 1972).

### 2.1.1 Salient Features & Sections

**Section 3 — Constitution of Central & State Boards**
- Empowered the Central Government to constitute the **CPCB** (already existing under Water Act) and the State Governments to constitute **SPCBs**.
- Functions: Advise, coordinate, plan, and execute the national program for air pollution prevention.

**Section 4 — Constitution of State Boards**
- One SPCB for each state, with a **minimum 15 members** including a full-time Chairman.

**Section 19 — Power of State Boards**
- Power to lay down standards for emissions from industrial plants and automobiles.
- Power to inspect, prescribe samples, and enter premises.

**Section 20 — Power of State Boards to Declare "Air Pollution Control Area"**
- The SPCB, with state government approval, can declare any area as an **Air Pollution Control Area (APCA)**, where no industrial plant shall be operated without proper consent.

**Section 21 — Restrictions on Establishment of Industries in APCA**
- No person shall establish/operate any industrial plant in an APCA without prior **Consent** of the SPCB.

**Section 22 — Consent to Establish & Consent to Operate**
- **Consent to Establish (CTE):** Permission to set up the industry.
- **Consent to Operate (CTO):** Permission to actually start operations.
- Validity: Usually **5 years** (varies by state).
- Refusal grounds: Use of polluting technology, likely violation of standards.

**Section 25 — Penalties**
- Imprisonment up to **3 months** and/or fine up to **₹10,000** for non-compliance.
- Subsequent offence: Imprisonment up to **6 months** and/or fine up to **₹5,000/day**.

**Section 31 — Offences by Companies**
- Specifies liability of directors, managers, secretaries, and other officers.

**Section 37 — Cognizance of Offences**
- No court shall take cognizance of any offence except on a complaint by a Board.

**Section 39 — Power of Central Government to Supersede State Boards**
- If a State Board fails to perform its duties, the Central Government can supersede it.

### 2.1.2 The Doctrine of Consent
The **Consent Mechanism** is the *operational heart* of the Air Act.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** "Explain the consent mechanism under the Air Act, 1981." (Repeatedly asked for 7-14 marks)

The consent mechanism has **three stages**:

| Stage | Type | Acronym | Meaning | Issued At |
|-------|------|---------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | **Consent to Establish** | CTE | Permission to construct the plant | Pre-construction |
| 2 | **Consent to Operate** | CTO | Permission to commence production | Pre-operation |
| 3 | **Renewal of CTO** | — | Re-permission after validity period | Every 5 years |

**Operational Logic:**
1. Industry applies to SPCB with **Detailed Project Report (DPR)** including pollution control equipment design.
2. SPCB evaluates pollution load projections against **emission standards**.
3. If satisfactory → **CTE granted** with specific conditions.
4. Industry constructs the plant with mandated APCD (Air Pollution Control Devices).
5. Industry applies for CTO; SPCB inspects the installed APCD.
6. **CTO granted** with monitoring conditions (stack monitoring, AAQ monitoring).
7. **SPCB conducts periodic inspections** to ensure ongoing compliance.
8. Non-compliance → **Show-cause notice → Refusal/Suspension/Cancellation of consent → Prosecution**.

## 2.2 The Water (Prevention and Control of Pollution) Act, 1974

Although primarily for water, this act is the **parent legislation** for environmental regulators in India. It is included in Module 2 because it established the **CPCB and SPCBs** — the bodies that later enforced the Air Act.

### Key Provisions:
- **Section 4:** Constitution of CPCB (Central Pollution Control Board).
- **Section 5:** Composition of CPCB (full-time Chairman + members).
- **Section 17:** Functions of CPCB (coordinate, advise, collect data).
- **Section 25/26:** Constitution and functions of SPCBs.
- **Section 33:** Restrictions on new outlets and discharges (Consent mechanism for water).
- **Section 42A:** Authorised officers and penalty up to **₹1 lakh + 6 years imprisonment** (inserted by 1988 amendment).
- **Section 44:** Penalties for non-compliance of directions.

## 2.3 The Environment (Protection) Act, 1986 — The Umbrella Act

### 2.3.1 Genesis and Significance
Enacted in the aftermath of the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (December 1984)** and **Ozone Depletion concerns**, this is the most comprehensive environmental act. It is called an **"Umbrella Act"** because it covers *all aspects* of environmental protection not specifically addressed by the Air or Water Acts.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** "Why is EPA, 1986 called an Umbrella Act?" — Because it empowers the Central Government to take *all necessary measures* to protect and improve the environment, and to coordinate actions of various regulatory bodies.

### 2.3.2 Key Provisions
- **Section 3:** Power of Central Government to take all necessary measures.
- **Section 5:** Power to give directions in writing to any person, officer, or authority.
- **Section 6:** Power to prescribe **environmental standards** for emissions and effluents.
- **Section 7:** Power to prescribe procedures for handling hazardous substances.
- **Section 8:** Power to prohibit or restrict the location of industries.
- **Section 15:** Penalty — Imprisonment up to **5 years** and/or fine up to **₹1 lakh**; continuing failure → additional **₹5,000/day**.
- **Section 19:** No civil court has jurisdiction to entertain any suit relating to EPA.

### 2.3.3 Rules Framed Under EPA, 1986
Multiple critical rules are notified under Section 6/8 of EPA:

| Rule | Notification Year | Purpose |
|------|------------------|---------|
| **Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules** | **2000** | Ambient noise standards, silent zones |
| **Hazardous Waste (Management, Handling & Transboundary Movement) Rules** | **1989** (amended 2008, 2016) | Management of hazardous waste |
| **Municipal Solid Waste (Management & Handling) Rules** | **2000** (revised **2016**) | Solid waste rules |
| **E-Waste (Management) Rules** | **2011** (revised **2016, 2022**) | E-waste management |
| **Plastic Waste Management Rules** | **2011** (revised **2016, 2022**) | Plastic ban, EPR |
| **Construction & Demolition Waste Rules** | **2016** | C&D waste |
| **Bio-Medical Waste Management Rules** | **2016** | Hospital waste |
| **Wetland (Conservation and Management) Rules** | **2010** (revised **2017**) | Wetland conservation |

## 2.4 The Motor Vehicles Act, 1988

### Critical for Air Pollution Control:
- **Section 20:** Necessity for registration.
- **Section 56:** Necessity for fitness certificate (emission compliance).
- **Section 110:** Power to check emission levels.
- **Central Motor Vehicles Rules (CMVR), 1989:** 
  - **Rule 115:** Emission standards for petrol vehicles (**Bharat Stage VI** since April 2020).
  - **Rule 116:** Emission standards for diesel vehicles.
  - **Rule 116A:** Emission standards for CNG/LPG vehicles.

### Bharat Stage (BS) Emission Standards
India follows **European (EURO) emission standards** with a time lag (BS VI = EURO VI, implemented April 1, 2020).
Key pollutants regulated: **CO, HC, NOx, PM (for diesel)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **BS-VI Norms:** Limit **70%** of NOₓ from diesel cars and **25%** of PM from petrol cars compared to BS-IV. Requires **Sulfur content in fuel < 10 ppm** (down from 50 ppm in BS-IV).

## 2.5 The Factories Act, 1948 (as amended in 1987)

- **Section 6:** Ventilation and temperature control.
- **Section 13:** Ventilation by circulation of fresh air.
- **Section 14:** Precautions against dangerous fumes.
- **Section 41A (1987 amendment):** Compulsory disclosure of manufacturing process, effluents, and emissions by occupier.
- **Section 112:** Penalties.

> [!NOTE]
> The Factories Act deals with **occupational health** (workplace air quality) and works *in conjunction* with the Air Act (ambient air quality).

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Standard / Unit | Application |
|------------------|------------|-----------------|-------------|
| **Emission Standard** | $\text{Concentration} = \frac{\text{Mass of pollutant}}{\text{Volume of flue gas}}$ | $\text{mg/Nm}^3$ | Stack monitoring |
| **Loading Rate (Pollution Load)** | $L = Q \times C$ | kg/day | Industrial discharge assessment |
| **NAAQS averaging time** | $\text{C}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i$ | $\mu g/m^3$ | Ambient air monitoring |
| **Emission rate (mass flow)** | $E = C \times Q$ | g/s | Dispersion modeling input |
| **AERMOD Gaussian Plume** | $C(x,y,z) = \frac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_y \sigma_z} \exp\left(-\frac{y^2}{2\sigma_y^2} - \frac{H^2}{2\sigma_z^2}\right)$ | $\mu g/m^3$ | Regulatory modeling |
| **Fuel Sulfur Penalty** | $\text{SO}_2 = 2 \times S \times F$ (where S = sulfur %, F = fuel burned, kg) | kg/day | Emission inventory |
| **Consent Validity Period** | Fixed | **5 years** (typical) | SPCB compliance |
| **BS-VI PM limit (Diesel)** | Direct regulatory standard | $\text{30 mg/kWh}$ (PC), $\text{5 mg/kWh}$ (LDV) | Vehicle emission testing |
| **Penalty — Air Act** | Direct statutory | Up to **₹10,000 + 3 months** imprisonment | First offence |
| **Penalty — EPA 1986** | Direct statutory | Up to **₹1 lakh + 5 years** imprisonment | First offence |

## 2.7 National Ambient Air Quality Standards (NAAQS) — Central Pollution Control Board, 2009

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Most Repeated KTU 2024 Table — Memorize the standards and averaging periods.**

| Pollutant | Time-Weighted Average | Industrial / Residential / Rural / Ecological |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------------------------------------|
| **PM₂.₅** | Annual / 24 hr | 40 / 60 $\mu g/m^3$ |
| **PM₁₀** | Annual / 24 hr | 60 / 100 $\mu g/m^3$ |
| **SO₂** | 24 hr / Annual | 80 / 50 $\mu g/m^3$ |
| **NO₂** | 24 hr / Annual | 80 / 40 $\mu g/m^3$ |
| **O₃** | 8 hr / 1 hr | 100 / 180 $\mu g/m^3$ |
| **CO** | 8 hr / 1 hr | 2000 / 4000 $\mu g/m^3$ |
| **Pb** | 24 hr / Annual | 1.0 / 0.5 $\mu g/m^3$ |
| **NH₃** | 24 hr / Annual | 400 / 100 $\mu g/m^3$ |
| **Benzene** | Annual | 5 $\mu g/m^3$ |
| **BaP** | Annual | 1 ng/m$^3$ |

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility
The legislative framework is not a stand-alone subject. In **Consultancy & Industry**, it is used for:
- **EIA (Environmental Impact Assessment) Notification, 2006** — Statutory requirement for project clearance.
- **Consent Management Software** — SPCB uses this to grant CTE/CTO.
- **Audit & Compliance** — Industries appoint Environmental Auditors (statutory requirement for **17 categories of highly polluting industries**).
- **Legal Due Diligence** — Banks and financial institutions require environmental clearance before funding.
- **Insurance** — Polluter Pays principle makes environmental insurance mandatory for high-risk industries.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Analysis of Major Acts

> This is a **high-yield comparison matrix** expected in KTU 2024 examination (asked as "Compare Air Act 1981 and Water Act 1974" type questions).

| Parameter | **Water Act, 1974** | **Air Act, 1981** | **EPA, 1986** |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| **Year enacted** | 1974 | 1981 | 1986 |
| **Trigger event** | Stockholm Conference (1972) | International concern + Air pollution rise | Bhopal Tragedy (1984) |
| **Primary focus** | Water pollution | Air pollution | **All environmental media** (Umbrella) |
| **Type of act** | Specific | Specific | **Umbrella / Generic** |
| **Constitutional basis** | Article 252 (originally) | Article 253 | Article 253 |
| **Statutory body created** | CPCB + SPCBs | Strengthened SPCBs | No new body (uses existing CPCB) |
| **Key sections** | S. 25/26, 33 | S. 19, 20, 21, 22 | S. 3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 15 |
| **Consent mechanism** | S. 25/26 | S. 21/22 | Not specific; via rules |
| **Penalty (first offence)** | Up to ₹10,000 + 3 months | Up to ₹10,000 + 3 months | **Up to ₹1 lakh + 5 years** |
| **Adjudicating body** | SPCB/CPCB | SPCB/CPCB | **CPCB (delegated)** |
| **Special feature** | First act; created pollution boards | Power to declare APCA | Power to set standards, close industries, declare Ecologically Sensitive Areas (ESAs) |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: The Statutory Enforcement Workflow

This derivation outlines the *exact procedural flow* from pollution occurrence to legal penalty. Useful for KTU 14-mark questions.

**Step 1: Monitoring & Data Collection**
$$\text{Ambient Concentration } C = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i$$
where $C_i$ is the concentration reading at the $i^{th}$ hour, and $n$ is the total number of observations. The 24-hour average must be below the NAAQS threshold.

**Step 2: Comparison with NAAQS**
$$\text{Violation Index (VI)} = \frac{C_{\text{observed}}}{C_{\text{standard}}}$$
If VI > 1, there is a violation.

**Step 3: Identification of Polluter**
The SPCB identifies the source based on:
- **Point source** (single identifiable stack) — easily traceable via stack monitoring.
- **Area source** (multiple small sources) — requires dispersion modeling.
- **Mobile source** (vehicles) — handled by RTOs under Motor Vehicles Act.

**Step 4: Issuance of Show-Cause Notice**
SPCB issues a **written notice** under Section 31A of the Air Act, providing:
- Specific sections violated.
- Evidence (monitoring data, photographs, lab reports).
- Time period to respond (typically **15-30 days**).

**Step 5: Hearing & Adjudication**
The polluter is given an opportunity to be heard (principle of *audi alteram partem*). The SPCB can:
- **Reject consent** and order closure.
- **Suspend** the consent until compliance.
- **Levy environmental compensation** (added by EPA amendment).

**Step 6: Legal Prosecution**
If the offence continues, a complaint is filed in court under:
- Section 37 of Air Act → Magistrate court.
- Section 19 of EPA → Sessions court (more serious).

**Step 7: Penalty Calculation**
$$\text{Total Fine} = \text{Base Fine} + (\text{Continuing Days} \times \text{Per Day Fine})$$

For EPA, 1986:
$$\text{Fine}_{\text{total}} = 1{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}000 \times D_{\text{continuing}}$$
where $D_{\text{continuing}}$ is the number of days the violation continued.

For Air Act, 1981:
$$\text{Fine}_{\text{total}} = 10{,}000 + 5{,}000 \times D_{\text{continuing}}$$

**Step 8: Alternative Remedy — Appeal to NGT**
Under the **National Green Tribunal Act, 2010**, any person can file an appeal before the NGT within **30 days** of the order. The NGT has powers to:
- Award compensation (up to **₹100 crore**).
- Order restitution.
- Impose damages on the *Polluter Pays* principle.

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Stack Height Calculation as Per CPCB Norms

A classic **14-mark problem** in KTU 2024 (frequently repeated).

**Background:** The CPCB mandates minimum stack height for industrial emissions to ensure adequate dispersion.

**General Formula (simplified):**
$$H = 14 \times Q^{0.3}$
where:
- $H$ = Stack height above ground (m)
- $Q$ = Emission rate of SO₂ (kg/hr)

**Derivation Logic:**
1. The plume rise is proportional to heat emission and stack exit velocity.
2. Effective stack height = Physical stack height + Plume rise.
3. CPCB empirical correlations based on Holland's formula:
$$\Delta H = \frac{1.5 v_s d + 4 \times 10^{-5} Q_H}{u}$$
where:
- $v_s$ = Stack gas exit velocity (m/s)
- $d$ = Stack diameter (m)
- $Q_H$ = Heat emission rate (cal/s)
- $u$ = Wind speed (m/s)

**Sample Numerical Derivation (KTU 2024 style):**
> An industry emits SO₂ at the rate of **$Q = 27$ kg/hr**. Calculate the minimum stack height.

**Solution:**
Given: $Q = 27$ kg/hr

Apply CPCB empirical formula:
$$H = 14 \times Q^{0.3}$$

Step 1: Calculate $Q^{0.3}$
$$Q^{0.3} = 27^{0.3}$$
$$27^{0.3} = (3^3)^{0.3} = 3^{0.9}$$

Step 2: Use logarithm:
$$\log(3^{0.9}) = 0.9 \times \log(3) = 0.9 \times 0.4771 = 0.4294$$
$$\therefore 3^{0.9} = 10^{0.4294} \approx 2.687$$

Step 3: Substitute:
$$H = 14 \times 2.687 = 37.62 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Verification (use alternate formula sometimes seen):**
$$H = 6 \times \left( \frac{Q}{1000} \right)^{0.25}$$
$$H = 6 \times \left( \frac{27}{1000} \right)^{0.25} = 6 \times (0.027)^{0.25}$$
$$(0.027)^{0.25} = 10^{0.25 \times \log(0.027)} = 10^{0.25 \times (-1.5686)} = 10^{-0.3922} \approx 0.405$$
$$H = 6 \times 0.405 = 2.43 \text{ m (not practical)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The first formula $H = 14 Q^{0.3}$ is the **standard CPCB formula** and is the expected answer. The second formula has different empirical constants. Use the first unless otherwise specified.

**Final Answer:** $H \approx 37.62$ m. So, a stack of at least **38 m** is required.

## 3.4 KTU 2024 — Critical Penalties and Authority Summary Table

| Statute | Penalty (First Offence) | Continuing Failure | Adjudicating Authority |
|---------|-------------------------|-------------------|------------------------|
| Air Act, 1981 | Up to 3 months + ₹10,000 | ₹5,000/day + 6 months | Magistrate (1st Class) |
| Water Act, 1974 | Up to 3 months + ₹10,000 | ₹5,000/day | Magistrate (1st Class) |
| EPA, 1986 | Up to 5 years + ₹1 lakh | ₹5,000/day | Sessions Court |
| Hazardous Waste Rules, 2016 | Up to 5 years + ₹1 lakh | ₹5,000/day | Sessions Court |
| Noise Pollution Rules, 2000 | Up to 5 years + ₹1 lakh | Continuous | Magistrate |
| NGT Act, 2010 | Compensation up to ₹100 cr | — | **NGT** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Indian Environmental Legal Framework — Hierarchical Pyramid

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Constitution of India<br>Art 14, 19, 21, 48A, 51A] --> B[Environment Protection Act 1986<br>Umbrella Act]
    B --> C[Air Act 1981]
    B --> D[Water Act 1974]
    B --> E[Motor Vehicles Act 1988]
    B --> F[Factories Act 1948]
    B --> G[Wildlife Protection Act 1972]
    B --> H[Forest Conservation Act 1980]
    C --> I[Air Pollution Rules]
    C --> J[Emission Standards NAAQS]
    D --> K[Water Cess Act 1977]
    E --> L[CMVR 1989<br>BS VI Norms]
    F --> M[Factory Inspectorate]
    B --> N[Noise Rules 2000]
    B --> O[Hazardous Waste Rules 2016]
    B --> P[EIA Notification 2006]
    I --> Q[SPCB Enforcement]
    J --> Q
    K --> Q
    L --> R[RTO Enforcement]
    M --> S[Inspectorate]
    N --> Q
    O --> T[CPCB Delegation]
    P --> U[SEIAA EAC Review]
    Q --> V[CPCB Apex Coordination]
    R --> V
    S --> V
    T --> V
    U --> V
    V --> W[National Green Tribunal<br>Adjudication 2010]
    V --> X[Supreme Court<br>Article 136 142]
```

## 4.2 The Consent Mechanism Workflow — Air Act, 1981

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Industry Identifies Project] --> B[Pre-Application Consultation with SPCB]
    B --> C[Submission of DPR with APCD Design]
    C --> D{SPCB Evaluation}
    D -->|Compliant| E[Grant of CTE]
    D -->|Non-Compliant| F[Refusal with Reasons]
    F --> G[Appeal to Appellate Authority]
    E --> H[Construction of Plant]
    H --> I[Installation of APCD]
    I --> J[Application for CTO]
    J --> K[SPCB Inspection]
    K --> L{Inspection Satisfactory?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Grant of CTO 5 years]
    L -->|No| N[Compliance Directive 30 days]
    N --> K
    M --> O[Continuous Monitoring Stack AAQ]
    O --> P{Compliance Maintained?}
    P -->|Yes| Q[Renewal of CTO]
    P -->|No| R[Show Cause Notice]
    R --> S[Suspension or Cancellation]
    S --> T[Prosecution under Section 37]
    T --> U[Magistrate Court]
    T --> V[NGT Appeal 30 days]
    V --> W[Compensation Order]
```

## 4.3 CPCB & SPCB Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Central Government] --> B[CPCB under EPA 1986]
    A --> C[State Government]
    C --> D[SPCB in each state]
    B --> E[National Level Functions]
    D --> F[State Level Functions]
    E --> E1[Coordinating State Boards]
    E --> E2[Setting National Standards]
    E --> E3[Research and Training]
    E --> E4[Major Industry Inspection]
    E --> E5[National Air Quality Monitoring NAMP]
    F --> F1[Consent to Establish CTE]
    F --> F2[Consent to Operate CTO]
    F --> F3[Local Industry Inspection]
    F --> F4[State Air Monitoring]
    F --> F5[Complaint Redressal]
    E --> G[CPCB and SPCB Coordination]
    F --> G
    G --> H[MoEFCC Apex Ministry]
    B --> H
    D --> H
    H --> I[NGT for Disputes]
    G --> I
```

## 4.4 The Precautionary and Polluter Pays Principles — Application Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pollution Activity] --> B{Harm Proven?}
    B -->|Yes| C[Polluter Pays Principle]
    B -->|Uncertain but Risk Exists| D[Precautionary Principle]
    C --> E[Cost of Damage to Environment]
    C --> F[Cost of Health Damage]
    C --> G[Cost of Restoration]
    E --> H[Total Compensation]
    F --> H
    G --> H
    D --> I[Burden of Proof on Industry]
    D --> J[Shifted to Industry]
    D --> K[Regulatory Preemptive Action]
    I --> L[Industry Must Prove Safety]
    J --> L
    K --> M[No New Projects in Ecologically Sensitive Areas]
    H --> N[NGT Order]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Final Implementation]
```
