# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions in KTU 2024 ESE are direct, definitional, or short-application questions carrying 3 marks. They test **Recall (L1)** and **Comprehension (L2)** levels of Bloom's Taxonomy.

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**
*"Define biological air pollution control. List the three main configurations of biological treatment systems with one example pollutant for each."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Biological air pollution control is a method of treating contaminated air streams using the **metabolic activity of microorganisms** (bacteria, fungi) to convert gaseous pollutants into harmless end-products such as $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, and biomass, under ambient temperature and pressure.

The three main configurations are:
1. **Biofilter (BF):** Suitable for **toluene**, $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ (odorous compounds, low-to-moderate solubility VOCs).
2. **Bioscrubber (BS):** Suitable for **ammonia ($\text{NH}_3$)**, highly water-soluble gases.
3. **Biotrickling Filter (BTF):** Suitable for **$\text{H}_2\text{S}$** (acid-producing gases), chlorinated hydrocarbons.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for listing systems + 1 mark for correct examples)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**
*"What is the significance of Henry's Law Constant ($H_c$) in the design of biological air pollution control systems? Mention the typical $H_c$ threshold values for selecting a bioscrubber versus a biofilter."*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **dimensionless Henry's Law Constant ($H_c$)** indicates the **solubility of a gas in water** and is a key parameter for selecting the appropriate bio-treatment configuration.

- **$H_c < 0.1$** (highly soluble gases like $\text{NH}_3$, methanol) → **Bioscrubber** is preferred because the gas readily partitions into the aqueous phase for biological oxidation.
- **$0.1 < H_c < 10$** (moderately soluble VOCs like toluene, xylene) → **Biofilter** is preferred, as the pollutant is adsorbed onto a moist biofilm.
- **$H_c > 10$** (poorly soluble gases like methane, hexane) → **Biotrickling Filter with surfactant amendment** or specialized microbial consortium is required.

*(1 mark for definition + 1 mark for thresholds + 1 mark for system selection logic)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, each Part B question is **14 marks** and may carry sub-parts typically distributed as **(a) 7 marks** + **(b) 7 marks**. Students must answer either the entire question OR its internal alternative. Below, **Question A and Question B are full internal alternatives** (you answer one or the other).

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Biofilter Design & Performance Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** *"Describe the working principle of a biofilter with a neat labelled block diagram. Explain the role of the biofilm and the importance of moisture content, pH, and temperature on biofilter performance."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

A **biofilter** is a packed-bed bioreactor in which contaminated air is passed through a **moist, porous support medium** (e.g., compost, wood chips, peat, or engineered ceramic) colonized by a **biofilm** of pollutant-degrading microorganisms. The pollutant transfers from the gas phase to the biofilm, where it is biodegraded aerobically.

**Block Diagram (to be drawn in answer):**

```
Polluted Air → Pre-filter (dust) → Humidifier → Packed Bed (Biofilter) → Clean Air → Stack
                                        ↑                                    ↓
                              Nutrient Solution                       Leachate (drain)
```

**Key Operational Parameters:**

1. **Moisture Content (40%–60% by weight):**
   - Essential for microbial survival and metabolite transport.
   - Drying out causes biofilm inactivation; over-wetting causes anaerobic pockets and channeling.
   - Maintained by pre-humidification and periodic irrigation.

2. **pH (6.0–8.0 for bacterial systems; 2.0–5.0 for fungal systems):**
   - Critical because metabolic byproducts (e.g., $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ from $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ oxidation) can acidify the bed and kill microbes.
   - Controlled by buffering agents (limestone, $\text{CaCO}_3$) in the packing or by replacing spent medium.

3. **Temperature ($20$–$45^{\circ}\text{C}$ optimal, mesophilic range):**
   - Affects microbial growth rate and gas-phase mass transfer.
   - Most biofilters operate near ambient temperature; thermophilic operation ($> 45^{\circ}\text{C}$) is possible but reduces species diversity.

**Role of the Biofilm:**
- The biofilm is a **thin, gel-like layer of immobilized microbes** adhering to the packing surface.
- It acts as the **catalytic interface** where pollutant absorption and biodegradation occur simultaneously.
- Provides protection against toxic shock loads and environmental fluctuations.

*(Block diagram: 2 marks; working principle: 2 marks; parameters discussed: 3 marks = 7 marks total)*

---

**(b)** *"A biofilter treats an air stream from a paint manufacturing unit. The volumetric flow rate is $Q = 8{,}000 \, \text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$, with an inlet toluene concentration of $C_{in} = 350 \, \text{mg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$. The bed volume is $V_{bed} = 40 \, \text{m}^3$ and the outlet concentration is $C_{out} = 70 \, \text{mg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$. Calculate the **(i)** Removal Efficiency, **(ii)** Empty Bed Residence Time, **(iii)** Inlet Loading Rate, and **(iv)** Elimination Capacity. Comment on whether the biofilter is operating efficiently."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

**Step 1 — Removal Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\% = \frac{350 - 70}{350} \times 100\% = \frac{280}{350} \times 100\% = 80\%$$

**Step 2 — Empty Bed Residence Time:**

Convert flow rate: $Q = 8{,}000 \, \text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h}^{-1} = 2.222 \, \text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$

$$\text{EBRT} = \frac{V_{bed}}{Q} = \frac{40}{2.222} = 18.0 \, \text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Inlet Loading Rate:**

Convert concentration: $C_{in} = 350 \, \text{mg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} = 0.350 \, \text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$

$$\text{ILR} = \frac{Q \cdot C_{in}}{V_{bed}} = \frac{8{,}000 \times 0.350}{40} = 70 \, \text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Step 4 — Elimination Capacity:**

$$\text{EC} = \frac{Q(C_{in} - C_{out})}{V_{bed}} = \frac{8{,}000 \times (0.350 - 0.070)}{40} = \frac{8{,}000 \times 0.280}{40} = 56 \, \text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$$

**Cross-check:** $\text{EC} = \text{ILR} \times \eta = 70 \times 0.80 = 56 \, \text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ ✓

**Comment on Efficiency:**
With $\eta = 80\%$ and an EBRT of $18 \, \text{s}$ — both well within the typical design envelope for VOC biofilters — the system is **operating efficiently**. The elimination capacity of $56 \, \text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ is within the typical range ($20$–$100 \, \text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-3} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$) for toluene biofilters, indicating healthy microbial activity and adequate residence time.

*(Each calculation: 1.5 marks; comment: 1 mark = 7 marks total)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Biotrickling Filter & Comparative Analysis**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]**

**(a)** *"With the help of a flow diagram, explain the construction and working of a **biotrickling filter (BTF)**. Compare its performance with a conventional biofilter for the abatement of $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ gas."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part a):**

A **biotrickling filter (BTF)** is a packed-bed bioreactor in which a **circulating aqueous phase** is continuously trickled over a biofilm-coated support medium, while the contaminated gas flows either co-currently or counter-currently through the bed. The two phases (gas and liquid) meet at the biofilm surface where biodegradation occurs.

**Flow Diagram (to be drawn in answer):**

```
                    ↑ Clean Air Out
                    │
            ┌───────┴───────┐
            │  Packed Bed   │ ← Trickling Liquid (from recirculation tank)
            │  (Biofilm on  │
            │   Pall Rings) │
            └───────┬───────┘
                    │
            ┌───────┴───────┐         ┌──────────────┐
Polluted →  │ Gas Inlet     │         │ Recirculation │
Air In  →   │ Distributor   │         │ Tank (Buffer) │
            └───────────────┘         └──────┬───────┘
                                              │
                                       Nutrient Make-up
```

**Key Components:**
- **Packed bed** (typically Pall rings, lava rock, or structured plastic) — provides surface area for biofilm attachment.
- **Recirculation tank** — holds the trickling liquid (buffered to neutral pH with $\text{NaOH}$ or $\text{Na}_2\text{CO}_3$).
- **Recirculation pump** — continuously circulates liquid at $1$–$10 \, \text{m}^3 \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$.
- **Gas distribution system** — ensures uniform upward gas flow.

**Comparison with Biofilter for $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ Abatement:**

| Parameter | Biofilter | Biotrickling Filter |
|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| pH control | Difficult; pH drops as $\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$ accumulates | Easy; continuous buffer replenishment in trickling liquid |
| Removal efficiency for $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ | $70\%$–$90\%$ (decreases with time due to acidification) | $95\%$–$99\%$ (sustained) |
| By-product management | Accumulates in packing; requires media replacement | Removed via liquid blowdown; simple |
| Clogging risk | High (biomass + sulfur deposits) | Low–Moderate |
| Operating cost | Low initially, rises with media replacement | Moderate, but stable |
| Footprint | Larger | More compact |

> **Conclusion:** For $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ abatement, the **biotrickling filter is superior** because it can manage the acidic byproducts ($\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$) via continuous liquid exchange, whereas the biofilter suffers progressive acidification and efficiency decline.

*(Flow diagram: 2 marks; BTF working: 2 marks; comparison table: 3 marks = 7 marks total)*

---

**(b)** *"Discuss the various **factors affecting the performance of a biofilter** with reference to pollutant characteristics, operating conditions, and biological parameters."* **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Part b):**

The performance of a biofilter is governed by an interplay of **three categories of factors**:

**I. Pollutant Characteristics:**
1. **Concentration:** Higher inlet concentrations can inhibit microbes (substrate inhibition) or cause toxic shock; biofilters work best at low to moderate concentrations ($< 5 \, \text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$).
2. **Solubility ($H_c$):** Determines the rate of transfer from gas to biofilm; moderately soluble gases are ideal.
3. **Biodegradability:** Easily degradable compounds (alcohols, ketones, aldehydes) achieve $> 95\%$ removal; recalcitrant compounds (PCBs, dioxins) require specialized microbial consortia.
4. **Hydrophobicity:** Hydrophobic VOCs resist aqueous transfer; fungal biofilms or surfactant addition is needed.

**II. Operating Conditions:**
1. **Moisture Content:** $40\%$–$60\%$ by weight is critical; below $30\%$, biofilm dries; above $70\%$, anaerobic zones form.
2. **Temperature:** Optimum is mesophilic ($20$–$45^{\circ}\text{C}$); extreme temperatures reduce microbial activity.
3. **pH:** Should be maintained between $6$–$8$ for bacteria, $2$–$5$ for fungi; limestone or $\text{CaCO}_3$ buffers acid-producing reactions.
4. **EBRT (Empty Bed Residence Time):** Longer EBRT → higher removal but larger bed volume; typical design: $15$–$60$ s.
5. **Pressure Drop:** Should be $< 2{,}000 \, \text{Pa}$ across the bed to avoid excessive fan energy and bed compression.

**III. Biological Parameters:**
1. **Microbial Diversity:** A diverse consortium (bacteria + fungi + actinomycetes) ensures resilience and broad-spectrum degradation.
2. **Biofilm Thickness:** Optimal thickness is $0.1$–$1.0 \, \text{mm}$; thicker biofilms cause diffusion limitations and clogging.
3. **Acclimation Period:** Microbes need $1$–$4$ weeks to adapt to a new pollutant; sudden changes cause efficiency drops.
4. **Nutrient Availability:** Adequate nitrogen ($N$), phosphorus ($P$), and trace elements (Fe, Mg, Zn) must be supplied; typical C:N:P ratio is $100:5:1$.

*(Pollutant factors: 2.5 marks; operating conditions: 2.5 marks; biological parameters: 2 marks = 7 marks total)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** The most common mark-loser in numerical problems. KTU examiners expect students to convert $Q$ from $\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{h}^{-1}$ to $\text{m}^3 \cdot \text{s}^{-1}$ (divide by $3600$) when computing EBRT in seconds, and $C_{in}$ from $\text{mg} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$ to $\text{g} \cdot \text{m}^{-3}$ (divide by $1000$) when computing ILR/EC. **Lose 1–2 marks** per error.
> 2. **Confusing EBRT with HRT:** EBRT (empty bed residence time) is for the **gas phase** in biofiltration; HRT (hydraulic residence time) is for the **liquid phase** in bioscrubbers/activated sludge. Do not interchange them.
> 3. **Forgetting to Convert Percentages to Decimals:** When using $\eta$ in decimal form (e.g., $0.80$ instead of $80\%$) in subsequent calculations, students frequently make this error. **Lose 0.5–1 mark.**
> 4. **Misclassifying System Type from $H_c$:** The KTU 2024 scheme explicitly tests this. A common error is assigning **all VOCs to biofilters** without consulting the Henry's Law threshold values.
> 5. **Omitting the Block Diagram:** A 14-mark question on biofilter "working principle" without a diagram typically loses **1.5–2 marks** even if the text is correct.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 ESE — Module 3 (Biological Air Pollution Control Technologies)**

### **A. Core Definitions (Must-Memorize)**
- **Biological air pollution control** uses **microbial metabolism** to convert gaseous pollutants into $\text{CO}_2$, $\text{H}_2\text{O}$, and biomass.
- **Biofilter (BF):** Dry/moist packed bed; biofilm on solid medium; no liquid recirculation.
- **Bioscrubber (BS):** Wet scrubber + biological reactor tank; activated sludge in liquid.
- **Biotrickling Filter (BTF):** Packed bed with continuous liquid trickling; biofilm on packing.
- **Biofilm:** Thin layer of immobilized microorganisms on a solid support.
- **Henry's Law Constant ($H_c$):** $C_{gas} / C_{liquid}$ — measures gas solubility; key for system selection.

### **B. Key Numerical Formulas (High-Yield)**
- Removal Efficiency: $\eta = \dfrac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{C_{in}} \times 100\%$
- EBRT: $\text{EBRT} = \dfrac{V_{bed}}{Q_{gas}}$ (in seconds)
- Inlet Loading Rate: $\text{ILR} = \dfrac{Q_{gas} \cdot C_{in}}{V_{bed}}$
- Elimination Capacity: $\text{EC} = \text{ILR} \times \eta = \dfrac{Q_{gas}(C_{in} - C_{out})}{V_{bed}}$
- First-order model: $\dfrac{C_{out}}{C_{in}} = \exp(-k \cdot \text{EBRT})$
- Ergun equation for pressure drop: $\dfrac{\Delta P}{L} = \dfrac{150 \mu (1-\varepsilon)^2}{d_p^2 \, \varepsilon^3} v_s + \dfrac{1.75(1-\varepsilon)}{d_p \, \varepsilon^3} \rho v_s^2$

### **C. System Selection Criteria (Critical Decision Table)**
- **$H_c < 0.1$** (highly soluble) → **Bioscrubber**
- **$0.1 < H_c < 10$** (moderately soluble) → **Biofilter**
- **$H_c > 10$** (poorly soluble) → **Biotrickling Filter with surfactant**
- **Acid-producing gases ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$)** → **Biotrickling Filter** (pH controllable)

### **D. Optimal Operating Conditions**
- Moisture content: $40\%$–$60\%$ by weight
- Temperature: $20$–$45^{\circ}\text{C}$ (mesophilic)
- pH (bacterial): $6.0$–$8.0$
- pH (fungal): $2.0$–$5.0$
- EBRT: $15$–$60$ s (BF), $1$–$10$ s (BS), $10$–$60$ s (BTF)
- Pressure drop: $< 2{,}000 \, \text{Pa}$ across bed
- C:N:P nutrient ratio: $100:5:1$

### **E. Microbial Agents (One Example Each)**
- **Bacteria:** *Pseudomonas putida* (toluene), *Thiobacillus thiooxidans* ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$)
- **Fungi:** *Cladosporium sphaerospermum* (toluene), *Exophiala jeanselmei* (styrene)
- **Actinomycetes:** *Nocardia* spp. (long-chain alkanes)

### **F. Typical Removal Efficiencies (Industrial Benchmark)**
- Biofilter for VOCs: $70\%$–$95\%$
- Biotrickling Filter for $\text{H}_2\text{S}$: $95\%$–$99\%$
- Bioscrubber for $\text{NH}_3$: $90\%$–$98\%$

### **G. Common KTU 2024 Question Patterns**
- **3-mark Q:** "Define and give an example of [system type]."
- **3-mark Q:** "State the role of Henry's Law in biofilter design."
- **7-mark Q:** "Explain the working of [BF/BS/BTF] with a diagram."
- **7-mark Q:** "Discuss factors affecting biofilter performance."
- **7-mark Q (numerical):** "Calculate $\eta$, EBRT, ILR, EC given $Q$, $V_{bed}$, $C_{in}$, $C_{out}$."
- **14-mark Q:** A combination of descriptive (a) + numerical or comparative (b) sub-parts.

### **H. Industrial Applications (Memorize for 2-Mark Bonus)**
- Wastewater treatment plants: odor control ($\text{H}_2\text{S}$, mercaptans)
- Composting facilities: $\text{NH}_3$, VOCs
- Paint booths & printing: solvent VOCs (toluene, xylene)
- Foundries & steel mills: $\text{SO}_2$, reduced sulfur gases
- Pharmaceutical industry: process VOC abatement
- Food processing: rendering plants, coffee roasting, breweries

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Indoor Air Quality
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Indoor Air Quality

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)** refers to the air quality within and around buildings and structures, especially as it relates to the health, comfort, and well-being of building occupants. It is a multidisciplinary field concerned with the identification, measurement, characterization, and control of chemical, biological, and particulate contaminants present in the **enclosed (non-industrial) environment**.

According to the **American Society of Heating, Refrigerating and Air-Conditioning Engineers (ASHRAE) Standard 62.1-2022**, IAQ is defined as *"air in which there are no known contaminants at harmful concentrations as determined by cognizant authorities and with which a substantial majority (80% or more) of the people exposed do not express dissatisfaction."*

The **World Health Organization (WHO)** further augments this by stating that IAQ involves "the physical, chemical, and biological characteristics of air that may affect human health, comfort, or work performance inside a building." KTU 2024 emphasizes IAQ as a critical subset of environmental engineering since **modern occupants spend approximately 90% of their time indoors**, where pollutant concentrations can be **2 to 5 times higher** (and occasionally more than 100 times higher) than typical outdoor levels.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Aquarium Analogy:** Think of an indoor room as a *sealed fish aquarium*. The walls and ceiling form the boundaries, the HVAC system acts as the water filter/pump, and the occupants are the fish. If the filter (ventilation) is poor, fish waste (pollutants) accumulate and the fish become sick. The air in a sealed conference room, bedroom, or classroom behaves the same way — without adequate dilution ventilation, the chemical and biological "waste" exhaled, off-gassed, or released by occupants and materials will build up, causing headaches, drowsiness, allergic reactions, and long-term respiratory diseases.

A simpler, more vivid real-world analogy: imagine a **parked car with all windows closed on a hot summer afternoon**. The volatile organic compounds (VOCs) from the dashboard, seat foam, and carpet rise dramatically within minutes — this is exactly the dynamic that occurs inside modern energy-efficient "tight" buildings, which minimize outdoor air infiltration to save energy, inadvertently trapping indoor pollutants.

## 1.3 Why Indoor Air Quality is a Critical Engineering Subject

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** Module 3 of PECET756 specifically focuses on IAQ because the **indoor environment is classified as a higher-risk exposure zone than the ambient outdoor environment** for several reasons:
> 1. **Time factor:** People spend 80–90% of their time indoors.
> 2. **Concentration factor:** Pollutant concentrations are often elevated due to limited dilution.
> 3. **Vulnerability factor:** Sensitive groups (children, elderly, asthmatics, pregnant women) are predominantly indoor occupants.
> 4. **Dose-response proximity:** The proximity of the source (e.g., gas stove, printer) to the breathing zone is small (often < 2 m).
> 5. **Synergistic effects:** Multiple low-level pollutants can interact, producing combined toxicities greater than individual exposures.

## 1.4 Categories of Indoor Air Pollutants (Overview)

For KTU 2024 evaluation, indoor pollutants are formally classified into **three primary physical states** and **two origin classifications**:

| Classification Basis | Category | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical State** | Gaseous Pollutants | CO, CO₂, NO₂, O₃, SO₂, Formaldehyde, VOCs, Radon |
| **Physical State** | Particulate Pollutants | PM₁₀, PM₂.₅, dust, smoke, pollen, bioaerosols |
| **Physical State** | Biological Pollutants | Bacteria, viruses, mold spores, dust mites |
| **Origin** | Combustion-Related | CO, NOₓ, PM from cooking/heating/ETS |
| **Origin** | Building & Furnishing Related | Asbestos, formaldehyde, lead, VOCs |

The principal metrics used in IAQ engineering assessment are **CO₂ (≤ 700 ppm above outdoor)**, **CO (≤ 9 ppm for 8-hr)**, **TVOC (≤ 500 µg/m³)**, **PM₂.₅ (≤ 15 µg/m³ annual)**, and **Formaldehyde (≤ 100 µg/m³ for 30-min)**. These thresholds are benchmarked against **WHO Global Air Quality Guidelines (2021 update)** and **ASHRAE 62.1-2022** standards.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for SECTION 1:** Indoor Air Quality is the integrated study of contaminant dynamics, occupant exposure, and ventilation engineering inside non-industrial enclosed spaces, governed by mass-balance principles and standardized by ASHRAE/WHO benchmark concentrations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Mass-Balance Framework — The Heart of IAQ Engineering

The fundamental theoretical tool for IAQ analysis is the **well-mixed room model** (also called the *single-compartment mass balance model*). It assumes:

1. The indoor air is **instantaneously and completely mixed** (uniform concentration).
2. The room is treated as a **control volume** with steady or unsteady mass flow.
3. All sources emit contaminants at a rate **E** (mass per unit time).
4. All sinks remove contaminants at a rate proportional to concentration via decay constant **k** (s⁻¹).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Governing Differential Equation (KTU Must-Know):**
> $$V \frac{dC_i}{dt} = E + Q \cdot C_o - Q \cdot C_i - k \cdot V \cdot C_i - R$$
> where the symbols are defined in the formula sheet below.

## 2.2 Why This Equation Matters — Physical Interpretation

Let us break down each term step-by-step so the physics is transparent:

- **$V \frac{dC_i}{dt}$** → The *net rate of change* of indoor pollutant mass. This is the storage term. If positive, the pollutant accumulates; if negative, it decays.
- **$E$** → The *emission rate* (µg/s or mg/s) from all internal sources — smoking, cooking, off-gassing, occupant respiration, printers, cleaning products, etc.
- **$Q \cdot C_o$** → The *inflow* of pollutant mass carried in by the ventilation supply air at outdoor concentration $C_o$.
- **$Q \cdot C_i$** → The *outflow* of pollutant mass carried out by the exhaust/return air at indoor concentration $C_i$.
- **$k \cdot V \cdot C_i$** → The *first-order removal* term (sorption, chemical reaction, biological decay, deposition on surfaces).
- **$R$** → The *active removal* by air-cleaning devices (HEPA filters, activated carbon, electrostatic precipitators) in µg/s.

## 2.3 Steady-State Indoor Concentration (Most Examined Form)

At **steady state** ($\frac{dC_i}{dt} = 0$), the equation simplifies to:

$$0 = E + Q \cdot C_o - Q \cdot C_i - k \cdot V \cdot C_i - R$$

Solving for the **indoor concentration** $C_i$:

$$C_i = \frac{E + Q \cdot C_o - R}{Q + k \cdot V}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:** This is the *most-tested expression* in KTU Module 3 numerical problems. The numerator represents the *total pollutant generation rate* (sources) and the denominator represents the *total removal capacity* (ventilation + decay). A larger $Q$ (better ventilation) reduces $C_i$, which is why mechanical ventilation is the single most effective IAQ control strategy.

## 2.4 Special Case 1 — No Internal Sources, No Removal Devices

If $E = 0$, $R = 0$, and $k = 0$:

$$C_i = C_o$$

This is the *baseline dilution case*: indoor air quality mirrors outdoor air quality. Used to determine minimum ventilation rates to maintain $C_i$ at outdoor levels.

## 2.5 Special Case 2 — Indoor Source Only (Most Common Real Scenario)

If $C_o = 0$, $R = 0$:

$$C_i = \frac{E}{Q + k \cdot V}$$

## 2.6 Special Case 3 — Including First-Order Decay (k > 0)

This case is most applicable to **biological contaminants** (viruses, bacteria) and **reactive gases** (O₃). The concentration $C_i$ becomes lower than in the no-decay case for the same $E$ and $Q$.

## 2.7 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (Exam Cheat-Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they appear in nearly every KTU Part B numerical on Module 3.**

| # | Formula | Description / Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $ACH = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ | Air Changes per Hour; $Q$ = volumetric flow rate; $V$ = room volume | h⁻¹ |
| 2 | $C_i = \dfrac{E + Q C_o - R}{Q + kV}$ | Steady-state indoor pollutant concentration (well-mixed) | µg/m³ |
| 3 | $C_i(t) = C_{i,ss} + (C_{i,0} - C_{i,ss}) e^{-(Q/V + k) t}$ | Transient (time-dependent) indoor concentration; $C_{i,ss}$ = steady-state value; $C_{i,0}$ = initial indoor concentration | µg/m³ |
| 4 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q}$ | Air residence time / time constant of room | s or h |
| 5 | $t_{1/2} = \dfrac{\ln 2}{(Q/V) + k} = \dfrac{0.693 \cdot \tau}{1 + k\tau}$ | Time for indoor concentration to fall to 50% of initial excess | s or h |
| 6 | $Q_{min} = \dfrac{E}{C_{limit} - C_o}$ | Minimum ventilation flow to keep $C_i \leq C_{limit}$ | m³/s |
| 7 | $\eta_{vent} = \dfrac{C_{i,in} - C_{i,out}}{C_{i,in}}$ | Ventilation removal efficiency (0 ≤ η ≤ 1) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $C_{i,excess} = C_i - C_o$ | Indoor excess (above-outdoor) concentration | µg/m³ |
| 9 | $C_{i,ss} = \dfrac{E/ACH}{V}$ only if ACH in 1/s | Reframed steady-state form using ACH | µg/m³ |
| 10 | $E = \dfrac{A \cdot \rho \cdot k_e}{A_{total}}$ | Emission rate from material: $A$ = area, $\rho$ = density, $k_e$ = emission factor | µg/s |

> [!NOTE]
> **Units Conversion (Always Check):** $1 \text{ CFM} = 0.0004719 \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 1.699 \text{ m}^3/\text{h}$. $1 \text{ ft}^3 = 0.02832 \text{ m}^3$. Mixing these units is the **#1 cause of numerical errors** in KTU papers.

## 2.6 Air Changes Per Hour (ACH) — The Master Ventilation Metric

**ASHRAE 62.1-2022 minimum ventilation rates** (relevant to KTU 2024 expected problems):

| Space Type | Outdoor Air (CFM/person) | Outdoor Air (CFM/ft²) |
|---|---|---|
| Office Space | **5.0** | 0.06 |
| Conference Room | **5.0** | 0.06 |
| Classroom (5–17 yr) | **7.5** | 0.06 |
| Lecture Hall | **7.5** | 0.06 |
| Hospital Patient Room | **5.0** | 0.06 |
| Hotel Guest Room | **5.0** | 0.06 |
| Retail Sales Floor | **7.5** | 0.12 |
| Restaurant Dining | **7.5** | 0.18 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sick Building Threshold:** An ACH below **0.5 h⁻¹** is widely considered a *sick building* indicator. ASHRAE recommends ACH $\geq$ **0.35 h⁻¹** minimum and **1.0–2.0 h⁻¹** for good practice in offices. WHO suggests **≥ 0.5 h⁻¹** for general indoor environments.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The mass-balance framework is used by practicing engineers to:

- **Sizing HVAC systems** in green-certified buildings (LEED, GRIHA, IGBC) to achieve IAQ credits.
- **Diagnosing SBS (Sick Building Syndrome)** by back-calculating emission rates from measured indoor concentrations.
- **Estimating occupant exposure doses** for risk assessment under the EPA and WHO frameworks.
- **Designing local exhaust ventilation** for kitchens, laboratories, and printing rooms.
- **Evaluating air-cleaner performance** by quantifying the term $R$ before and after installation.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for SECTION 2:** The well-mixed room mass-balance equation is the cornerstone of all quantitative IAQ analysis. KTU examiners test both the *steady-state closed-form solution* and the *transient exponential-decay form*, with the *air change rate* ($Q/V$) appearing in nearly every problem.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Steady-State Indoor Concentration (with Active Removal R)

### Problem Setup

A small office of dimensions 4 m × 5 m × 3 m (Volume $V$) has a ventilation supply rate of $Q$ m³/s. The outdoor CO₂ concentration is $C_o$. The office has $N$ occupants each exhaling CO₂ at rate $G$ (mg/s/person). An air purifier with removal efficiency $\eta$ operates on the air. Derive the steady-state indoor CO₂ concentration.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Identify the emission rate term.**
Each of the $N$ occupants releases $G$ mg/s of CO₂. Thus:
$$E = N \cdot G \quad (\text{mg/s})$$

**Step 2 — Identify the active removal term.**
The purifier processes a fraction of the air at a certain clean-air delivery rate (CADR). The mass removal rate is:
$$R = \text{CADR} \cdot C_i \quad (\text{mg/s})$$
(For simplicity, treat $R$ as a known constant or as $R = \eta Q_a C_i$ where $Q_a$ is the air-purifier flow rate.)

**Step 3 — Identify the decay term.**
CO₂ is conservative (chemically inert in indoor conditions), so:
$$k = 0$$

**Step 4 — Write the steady-state balance.**
Setting $\frac{dC_i}{dt} = 0$:
$$0 = E + Q \cdot C_o - Q \cdot C_i - 0 - R$$

**Step 5 — Solve algebraically for $C_i$.**
Rearrange to isolate $C_i$ terms on one side:
$$Q \cdot C_i + R = E + Q \cdot C_o$$

$$Q \cdot C_i + R = E + Q \cdot C_o$$

Substitute $R = \eta Q_a C_i$ (air-cleaner form):
$$Q \cdot C_i + \eta Q_a C_i = E + Q \cdot C_o$$

Factor $C_i$ on the left:
$$C_i (Q + \eta Q_a) = E + Q \cdot C_o$$

Divide both sides by $(Q + \eta Q_a)$:
$$C_i = \frac{E + Q \cdot C_o}{Q + \eta Q_a}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The denominator $(Q + \eta Q_a)$ is called the **effective ventilation rate** — the air-cleaner effectively *augments* the building's mechanical ventilation. This is why air purifiers measurably improve IAQ, especially during outdoor smog episodes when opening windows is not advisable.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Time to Reach Steady State (Transient)

### Problem Setup
A newly furnished room is painted. At $t = 0$, the TVOC (Total Volatile Organic Compound) concentration is $C_{i,0} = 800$ µg/m³. The steady-state value (from §3.1) is $C_{i,ss}$. There is no active source during decay. Derive $C_i(t)$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Set $E = 0$ and assume $C_o \approx 0$ (or treat as constant).**
The general ODE becomes:
$$V \frac{dC_i}{dt} = -Q \cdot C_i - k \cdot V \cdot C_i$$

**Step 2 — Divide by $V$.**
$$\frac{dC_i}{dt} = -\left(\frac{Q}{V} + k\right) C_i$$

**Step 3 — Recognize as a first-order linear ODE.**
Let $\lambda = \frac{Q}{V} + k$. Then:
$$\frac{dC_i}{dt} = -\lambda C_i$$

**Step 4 — Separate variables and integrate.**
$$\int_{C_{i,0}}^{C_i(t)} \frac{dC_i'}{C_i'} = -\lambda \int_0^t dt'$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the integrals.**
$$\ln\left(\frac{C_i(t)}{C_{i,0}}\right) = -\lambda t$$

**Step 6 — Exponentiate both sides.**
$$C_i(t) = C_{i,0} \cdot e^{-\lambda t} = C_{i,0} \cdot e^{-(Q/V + k) t}$$

**Step 7 — Add the steady-state offset for the general case.**
When the room is being continuously ventilated against a non-zero outdoor or residual source, the *excess* concentration decays exponentially toward zero excess. The full solution is:
$$C_i(t) = C_{i,ss} + (C_{i,0} - C_{i,ss}) \cdot e^{-(Q/V + k) t}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "5 time-constants" rule:** After $t = 5/\lambda$ (i.e., $5 \tau$ where $\tau = V/Q$), the indoor concentration is within **99.33%** of steady state. This is the canonical *flush-out period* used in LEED and ASHRAE 62.1 commissioning protocols.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Ventilation Flow Rate for a Pollutant Limit

### Problem Setup
A laboratory releases formaldehyde (HCHO) at a rate of $E = 50$ µg/s. The outdoor HCHO concentration is negligible ($C_o = 0$). There is no air-cleaner ($R = 0$) and no decay ($k = 0$). The OSHA 8-hour permissible exposure limit (PEL) for HCHO is $C_{limit} = 370$ µg/m³. Determine the **minimum ventilation flow rate** $Q_{min}$ to keep the indoor concentration below the limit.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the steady-state equation with given simplifications.**
$$C_i = \frac{E + Q \cdot C_o - R}{Q + kV}$$

Substituting $C_o = 0$, $R = 0$, $k = 0$:
$$C_i = \frac{E}{Q}$$

**Step 2 — Set $C_i \leq C_{limit}$.**
$$\frac{E}{Q} \leq C_{limit}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $Q \geq Q_{min}$.**
$$Q \geq \frac{E}{C_{limit}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values.**
$$Q_{min} = \frac{50 \text{ µg/s}}{370 \text{ µg/m}^3} = 0.1351 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to engineering units.**
$$Q_{min} = 0.1351 \times 3600 = 486.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 286.3 \text{ CFM}$$

**Step 6 — Determine the required ACH for a 200 m³ lab.**
$$V = 200 \text{ m}^3$$
$$ACH = \frac{Q_{min}}{V} = \frac{486.5}{200} = 2.43 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Sanity Check:** A lab of 200 m³ requires only 2.43 air changes/hour, which is easily met by most HVAC systems. The result is in a reasonable engineering range. If the answer were, say, 50 h⁻¹, this would be a *red flag* indicating either an algebraic error or unrealistic source strength.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Comprehensive IAQ Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is a KTU-pattern Part B (14-mark) numerical — practice it carefully.**

A classroom has dimensions 10 m × 8 m × 3.5 m. It has 40 students. Outdoor CO₂ concentration is 400 ppm. The CO₂ generation rate per person is $G = 0.31$ L/min = 5.17 × 10⁻⁶ m³/s. The HVAC system supplies outdoor air at $Q = 0.20$ m³/s. Compute:
- (a) The steady-state indoor CO₂ concentration.
- (b) The air changes per hour.
- (c) The time required for CO₂ to fall to 600 ppm after a class ends (assume the room is then vacated and ventilation continues; CO₂ generation becomes zero, and outdoor CO₂ stays 400 ppm).

### Solution

#### Part (a) — Steady-State Concentration

**Volume of room:**
$$V = 10 \times 8 \times 3.5 = 280 \text{ m}^3$$

**Total CO₂ emission rate:**
$$E = N \cdot G = 40 \times 5.17 \times 10^{-6} = 2.068 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Convert to consistent units (ppm by volume):**
$$C_i = \frac{E/Q + C_o}{1} = \frac{2.068 \times 10^{-4}/0.20 + 400}{1}$$
$$C_i = \frac{1.034 \times 10^{-3} + 400}{1}$$
$$C_i \approx 400 + 0.00103 = 400.00103 \text{ ppm (volume fraction)}$$

Wait — this result is in **volume fraction of CO₂ in air, not ppm by mixing ratio**. Let me convert properly using a mass-based approach.

> [!NOTE]
> **Proper Conversion (the KTU-correct way):**
> CO₂ generation is typically expressed in **mL/s (at STP)** or **L/s**. 1 mL of CO₂ at STP occupies 1 mL. The mixing ratio in ppm is:
> $$\text{Concentration (ppm)} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2}}{\dot{V}_{air}} \times 10^6$$

So:
$$C_i = \frac{E \times 10^6}{Q} + C_o$$
$$C_i = \frac{2.068 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^6}{0.20} + 400$$
$$C_i = \frac{206.8}{0.20} + 400$$
$$C_i = 1034 + 400 = 1434 \text{ ppm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Verdict:** ASHRAE recommends indoor CO₂ ≤ 700 ppm above outdoor (i.e., ≤ 1100 ppm absolute with $C_o = 400$ ppm). Our result of **1434 ppm** *exceeds* the standard, indicating that **$Q$ must be increased** to at least:
> $$Q_{min} = \frac{E \times 10^6}{C_{limit} - C_o} = \frac{206.8}{1100 - 400} = 0.295 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
> This is a **47% increase** in supply air — a real design consequence.

#### Part (b) — Air Changes Per Hour

$$ACH = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V} = \frac{0.20 \times 3600}{280} = \frac{720}{280} = 2.57 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

This is comfortably above the ASHRAE minimum for classrooms (~1.0–2.0 h⁻¹).

#### Part (c) — Time to Fall to 600 ppm

After the students leave, $E = 0$. The room concentration decays toward the outdoor value of 400 ppm:

$$C_i(t) = C_o + (C_{i,0} - C_o) e^{-(Q/V) t}$$

With $C_{i,0} = 1434$ ppm and target $C_i(t) = 600$ ppm:
$$600 = 400 + (1434 - 400) e^{-(0.20/280) t}$$
$$200 = 1034 \cdot e^{-7.14 \times 10^{-4} t}$$
$$e^{-7.14 \times 10^{-4} t} = 0.1934$$
$$-7.14 \times 10^{-4} t = \ln(0.1934) = -1.643$$
$$t = \frac{1.643}{7.14 \times 10^{-4}} = 2301 \text{ s} \approx 38.4 \text{ minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Insight:** Even with no occupants, it takes ~38 minutes for the CO₂ to fall to acceptable levels. This is why **pre-classroom ventilation flushing** is recommended in KTU 2024 syllabus topics on green building design.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Major Indoor Pollutants

| Pollutant | Primary Indoor Source | WHO / ASHRAE Limit | Half-Life in Air | Health Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **CO₂** | Occupant respiration | 700 ppm above outdoor | Conservative | Drowsiness, headache |
| **CO** | Combustion (stove, ETS) | 9 ppm (8-hr) | ~2–4 hr | Hypoxia, death |
| **NO₂** | Gas stove, ETS | 200 µg/m³ (1-hr) | Reactive | Respiratory inflammation |
| **Formaldehyde** | Furniture, plywood | 100 µg/m³ (30-min) | ~hours–days | Cancer, irritation |
| **Benzene (VOC)** | Smoking, paint | 1.7 µg/m³ (annual) | ~days | Leukemia |
| **PM₂.₅** | Cooking, smoking | 15 µg/m³ (annual) | ~hours | Lung disease |
| **O₃** | Photocopiers, outdoor | 100 µg/m³ (8-hr) | < 1 hr | Lung inflammation |
| **Radon (²²²Rn)** | Soil infiltration | 100 Bq/m³ | ~3.8 days | Lung cancer |
| **Bioaerosols** | HVAC, dampness | CFU/m³ limits | Variable | Allergy, infection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for SECTION 3:** The mass-balance framework is fully solvable in both steady-state and transient forms. Numerical problems in KTU Module 3 predominantly test (i) steady-state $C_i$ calculations, (ii) minimum $Q$ for a concentration limit, and (iii) transient decay time. Unit conversions between CFM, m³/s, m³/h, and ppm are the most common pitfall.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 IAQ Master Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Indoor Air Quality IAQ] --> B[Pollutant Sources]
    A --> C[Building Ventilation]
    A --> D[Occupant Exposure]
    A --> E[Health Outcomes]

    B --> B1[Combustion - CO, NOx, PM]
    B --> B2[Building Materials - VOC, Formaldehyde]
    B --> B3[Biological - Mold, Bacteria, Mites]
    B --> B4[Occupant Activities - Respiration, ETS, Cleaning]
    B --> B5[Outdoor Infiltration - Pollen, PM, NOx]

    C --> C1[Natural Ventilation - Windows, Infiltration]
    C --> C2[Mechanical HVAC - Supply and Exhaust]
    C --> C3[Local Exhaust - Kitchen, Lab Hoods]
    C --> C4[Air Cleaning - HEPA, Carbon, ESP]

    D --> D1[Concentration Ci]
    D --> D2[Exposure Time t]
    D --> D3[Breathing Rate Qbreath]

    E --> E1[Sick Building Syndrome - SBS]
    E --> E2[Building Related Illness - BRI]
    E --> E3[MCS - Multiple Chemical Sensitivity]
    E --> E4[Long-term - Cancer, Asthma, COPD]

    B1 --> D1
    B2 --> D1
    C1 --> D1
    C2 --> D1
    C4 --> D1
    D1 --> E1
    D2 --> E1
    D3 --> E1
```

## 4.2 Well-Mixed Room Mass-Balance Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Outdoor[Outdoor Air Co] -->|Inflow Q| Room
    Source[Internal Source E] --> Room
    Room[Room Volume V with Concentration Ci] -->|Outflow Q| Exhaust
    Room -->|Decay kVCi| Sink
    Room -->|Removal R| Cleaner[Air Cleaner]
    Cleaner --> Room
    Room -->|Excess over outdoor| Exposure[Occupant Exposure Dose]
    Exhaust --> Outdoor
```

## 4.3 Sick Building Syndrome vs. Building Related Illness — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Building Occupant with Symptoms] --> Q1{Are symptoms\nbuilding-specific?}
    Q1 -->|No| Other[Consider outdoor exposure\nor non-building cause]
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Do symptoms clear\nwhen leaving building?}
    Q2 -->|No| BRI[Building Related Illness BRI\nIdentifiable cause: Legionella,\nAsbestosis, hypersensitivity\npneumonitis, CO poisoning]
    Q2 -->|Yes| Q3{Multiple nonspecific\nsymptoms?}
    Q3 -->|Yes| SBS[Sick Building Syndrome SBS\nHeadache, eye irritation,\nfatigue, dizziness, dry skin]
    Q3 -->|No| Q4{Single chemical\ntrigger identified?}
    Q4 -->|Yes| MCS[Multiple Chemical Sensitivity MCS]
    Q4 -->|No| Review[Continue IAQ investigation\nwith measurement and source\nidentification]
```

## 4.4 IAQ Control Strategy Hierarchy (KTU-Favorite Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S[IAQ Problem Identified] --> L1[Level 1 - Source Control\nMost Effective]
    L1 --> L1a[Remove pollutant source]
    L1 --> L1b[Substitute low-emitting materials]
    L1 --> L1c[Isolate source via enclosures]
    L1 --> L2[Level 2 - Ventilation Control]
    L2 --> L2a[Increase outdoor air supply]
    L2 --> L2b[Improve air distribution]
    L2 --> L2c[Local exhaust ventilation]
    L2 --> L3[Level 3 - Air Cleaning]
    L3 --> L3a[Filtration - HEPA for PM]
    L3 --> L3b[Adsorption - Activated carbon for gases]
    L3 --> L3c[UV germicidal for bioaerosols]
    L2 --> L4[Level 4 - Administrative Controls]
    L4 --> L4a[Smoking bans]
    L4 --> L4b[Maintenance schedules]
    L4 --> L4c[Occupant education]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Hierarchy Note:** Source control is **always preferred** over ventilation, which is preferred over air cleaning. KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask students to *rank* or *justify* IAQ control measures. The reasoning is that eliminating the source is permanent and energetically free, whereas ventilation and air cleaning have ongoing operational costs.

## 4.5 Ventilation System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Return Air from Room] --> M1[Filter Bank - Pre and HEPA]
    M1 --> M2[Heat Recovery Wheel]
    M2 --> M3[Mixing Plenum with Outdoor Air]
    M3 --> M4[Cooling and Heating Coils]
    M4 --> M5[Humidifier or Dehumidifier]
    M5 --> M6[Fan - Supply]
    M6 --> D1[Duct Distribution Network]
    D1 --> R1[Room 1]
    D1 --> R2[Room 2]
    D1 --> R3[Room 3]
    R1 -->|Exhaust| E1[Local Exhaust]
    R2 -->|Exhaust| E2[Return Plenum]
    R3 -->|Exhaust| E2
    E2 --> A
    E1 --> O[Outside Discharge]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway for SECTION 4:** IAQ engineering relies on a *hierarchy of control* — source control first, then ventilation, then air cleaning. The well-mixed room model is a simplification; in reality, indoor air exhibits *non-uniform* concentration gradients due to thermal stratification, occupancy patterns, and HVAC duct design. Advanced IAQ engineering uses **CFD (Computational Fluid Dynamics)** to model these gradients.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question, Module 3]**
*"Define Sick Building Syndrome (SBS) and list any four of its characteristic symptoms."*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Sick Building Syndrome (SBS)** is a medical condition in which occupants of a building experience acute health and comfort effects that appear to be linked to time spent in the building, but **no specific illness or cause can be identified**. The symptoms typically *diminish or disappear* when occupants leave the building, distinguishing SBS from Building-Related Illness (BRI).

**Four characteristic symptoms of SBS:**
1. **Headache** and mental fatigue.
2. **Eye, nose, and throat irritation** (mucous membrane irritation).
3. **Dry cough and dry skin** (xeroderma).
4. **Dizziness, nausea, and difficulty concentrating.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme (KTU Pattern):** Definition 1.5 marks + 4 symptoms 0.375 each = 3 marks total.

---

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question, Module 3]**
*"What are VOCs? Name three common indoor sources of VOCs."*

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Volatile Organic Compounds (VOCs)** are organic chemicals that have a high vapor pressure at ordinary room temperature, causing them to evaporate readily from solid or liquid form into the surrounding air. They include a wide range of compounds such as formaldehyde, benzene, toluene, xylene, and styrene.

**Three common indoor sources of VOCs:**
1. **Building materials** — paints, varnishes, adhesives, sealants, plywood, particle board.
2. **Furnishings** — carpets, upholstered furniture, curtains.
3. **Consumer products** — cleaning agents, air fresheners, disinfectants, printers, photocopiers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Scheme (KTU Pattern):** Definition 1.5 marks + 3 sources 0.5 each = 3 marks total.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module Pattern:** Two questions will be asked, and the student answers **one**. Each 14-mark question has two 7-mark sub-parts (a and b). Numerical + theory is the standard format.

---

### Question B (Choice 1) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Module 3, 14 Marks]**
**Question A:**
> **(a)** A conference room of dimensions 8 m × 6 m × 3 m contains 25 occupants. The ventilation system supplies $Q = 0.15$ m³/s of outdoor air containing 400 ppm CO₂. Each occupant exhales CO₂ at a rate of 0.30 L/min. Assuming the room is well-mixed and CO₂ is conservative ($k = 0$, $R = 0$), calculate:
> 1. The **air changes per hour (ACH)**.
> 2. The **steady-state indoor CO₂ concentration** in ppm.
> 3. Comment on whether the concentration meets the ASHRAE comfort criterion of 700 ppm above outdoor. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** With reference to Indoor Air Quality, explain in detail:
> 1. The **sources and health effects of Radon (²²²Rn)** in indoor environments.
> 2. The **principles and effectiveness of HEPA filtration** for indoor particulate control. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Room volume.**
$$V = 8 \times 6 \times 3 = 144 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 2 — Air Changes per Hour (ACH).** [1 Mark]
$$ACH = \frac{Q \times 3600}{V} = \frac{0.15 \times 3600}{144} = \frac{540}{144} = 3.75 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — Total CO₂ emission rate.** [1 Mark]
$$E = N \cdot G = 25 \times \frac{0.30 \text{ L/min}}{60} = 25 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3} \text{ L/s} = 0.125 \text{ L/s} = 1.25 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Apply steady-state mass balance equation** (with $C_o = 0$ in the *delta-from-outdoor* form). [2 Marks]
$$C_i = C_o + \frac{E \times 10^6}{Q}$$
$$C_i = 400 + \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^6}{0.15}$$
$$C_i = 400 + \frac{125}{0.15} = 400 + 833.3 = 1233.3 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 5 — Comparison with ASHRAE 700-ppm-above-outdoor limit.** [2 Marks]
- Allowed: $400 + 700 = 1100$ ppm.
- Computed: $1233$ ppm.
- Excess over limit: $1233 - 1100 = 133$ ppm.

**Conclusion:** The concentration **exceeds** the ASHRAE 700-ppm-above-outdoor criterion by ~19%. The room is **under-ventilated** for the current occupancy. **Recommended action:** Increase $Q$ to:
$$Q_{min} = \frac{1.25 \times 10^{-4} \times 10^6}{1100 - 400} = \frac{125}{700} = 0.1786 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$
This represents a 19% increase over the current supply.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Radon (²²²Rn) — Sources and Health Effects** [3.5 Marks]

- **Radon** is a naturally occurring **radioactive noble gas** produced by the alpha-decay of radium-226 in soil, rock, and groundwater.
- **Indoor sources:** Soil infiltration through foundations, basements, and crawl spaces; building materials containing radium (granite, concrete, phosphogypsum); well water (in dissolved form) released during showering.
- **Health effects:** Radon is the **second leading cause of lung cancer** (after smoking) according to WHO and EPA. Its short-lived decay products (²¹⁸Po, ²¹⁴Po) are alpha-emitters that damage lung tissue when inhaled. Long-term exposure to **100 Bq/m³** approximately **doubles** the lifetime lung cancer risk for non-smokers.
- **WHO Reference Level:** 100 Bq/m³ (≈ 2.7 pCi/L).
- **Control:** Sub-slab depressurization, soil suction, sealing cracks, increased ventilation, radon-resistant construction.

**HEPA Filtration — Principle and Effectiveness** [3.5 Marks]

- **HEPA = High-Efficiency Particulate Air filter** (must remove ≥ 99.97% of particles ≥ 0.3 µm diameter by mass per DOE standard).
- **Operating principle:** A combination of **three mechanisms** — (i) *interception* (particles follow airstream but contact fibers), (ii) *impaction* (larger particles cannot follow streamlines), (iii) *diffusion* (sub-micron particles undergo Brownian motion and contact fibers).
- **Effectiveness:** Most efficient at capturing particles in the **0.1–0.5 µm range**, which is the most penetrating particle size (MPPS) region.
- **Limitation:** HEPA filters **do NOT capture gases or VOCs**; they are particulate-only. For gaseous pollutants, **activated carbon** adsorption is needed.
- **Pressure drop:** Typically 25–50 mm H₂O, requiring fan power and energy.
- **Application in IAQ:** Used in hospitals, clean rooms, residential air purifiers for PM₂.₅, PM₁₀, bioaerosols, dust mite allergens, and smoke.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question Module 3, 14 Marks, Alternative Choice]**
**Question B:**
> **(a)** A residential kitchen (volume 50 m³) experiences a sudden gas leak releasing methane (CH₄) at a constant rate of $E = 20$ mg/s. The outdoor CH₄ concentration is $C_o = 2$ ppm. The mechanical exhaust fan removes air at $Q = 0.05$ m³/s. CH₄ is conservative ($k = 0$). Determine:
> 1. The **steady-state indoor CH₄ concentration** in mg/m³ and in ppm (assume 1 ppm = 0.715 mg/m³ for CH₄ at NTP).
> 2. The **time (in minutes)** required for the concentration to reach 90% of steady state after the leak starts (assume zero initial concentration). **(7 Marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain in detail:
> 1. The **mass-balance model for a well-mixed room** with all terms clearly defined.
> 2. The **principles of natural ventilation** and its limitations in modern buildings. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 Marks)

**Step 1 — Convert emission rate to consistent units.** [0.5 Mark]
$E = 20$ mg/s (already in mg/s, so volume units follow mg/m³ for $C_i$).

**Step 2 — Compute steady-state concentration in mg/m³.** [2 Marks]
Using the steady-state equation with $k = 0$, $R = 0$:
$$C_i = C_o + \frac{E}{Q}$$
$$C_i = (2 \text{ ppm} \times 0.715 \text{ mg/m}^3/\text{ppm}) + \frac{20 \text{ mg/s}}{0.05 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}}$$
$$C_i = 1.43 + 400 = 401.43 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert to ppm.** [1 Mark]
$$C_i \text{ (ppm)} = \frac{401.43}{0.715} = 561.4 \text{ ppm}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the 90% rise time.** [3 Marks]
The transient solution is:
$$C_i(t) = C_{i,ss} + (C_{i,0} - C_{i,ss}) e^{-(Q/V) t}$$
With $C_{i,0} = 1.43$ mg/m³ (initial = outdoor) and $C_{i,ss} = 401.43$ mg/m³:
$$C_i(t) = 401.43 + (1.43 - 401.43) e^{-(0.05/50) t}$$
$$C_i(t) = 401.43 - 400 e^{-0.001 t}$$

At 90% of steady state:
$$0.9 \times 401.43 = 361.29 \text{ mg/m}^3$$
$$361.29 = 401.43 - 400 e^{-0.001 t}$$
$$400 e^{-0.001 t} = 40.14$$
$$e^{-0.001 t} = 0.1004$$
$$-0.001 t = \ln(0.1004) = -2.299$$
$$t = 2299 \text{ s} \approx 38.3 \text{ minutes}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 Marks)

**Mass-Balance Model for a Well-Mixed Room** [3.5 Marks]

The well-mixed room model treats the entire indoor space as a single homogeneous control volume in which the pollutant is uniformly distributed at concentration $C_i$. The governing differential mass balance is:
$$V \frac{dC_i}{dt} = E + Q C_o - Q C_i - k V C_i - R$$

Where:
- $V$ = room volume (m³)
- $C_i$ = indoor pollutant concentration (µg/m³ or mg/m³)
- $t$ = time (s)
- $E$ = indoor emission rate (µg/s or mg/s)
- $Q$ = volumetric ventilation flow rate (m³/s)
- $C_o$ = outdoor pollutant concentration (µg/m³ or mg/m³)
- $k$ = first-order decay rate constant (s⁻¹)
- $R$ = active removal rate by air cleaners (µg/s)

**Assumptions of the model:**
1. Perfect instantaneous mixing (no spatial gradients).
2. Constant air flow and constant outdoor concentration.
3. Single, well-defined pollutant.
4. Constant temperature and pressure.

**Limitations:**
- Real rooms have thermal stratification and non-uniform concentration.
- Sorption on walls, furnishings, and HVAC ducts adds sink terms not captured.
- Source strength may vary with time.
- Particle dynamics (resuspension, deposition) are not represented.

**Principles and Limitations of Natural Ventilation** [3.5 Marks]

**Principles:**
Natural ventilation uses **buoyancy (stack effect)** and **wind pressure** to drive outdoor air through building openings without mechanical assistance. The driving pressure is:
$$\Delta P_{stack} = \rho_o g H \frac{(T_i - T_o)}{T_i}$$
where $H$ is the height between inlet and outlet, $T_i$ and $T_o$ are indoor and outdoor absolute temperatures, and $g$ is gravity. Wind-induced pressure is $\Delta P_{wind} = \frac{1}{2} C_w \rho v^2$, where $C_w$ is the wind pressure coefficient and $v$ is wind speed.

**Advantages:**
- Zero energy cost.
- Simple, no moving parts.
- Provides good dilution of indoor pollutants.

**Limitations:**
- **Highly variable** — depends on weather, wind direction, and outdoor temperature.
- **No filtration** — outdoor pollutants (PM, pollen, smog) enter unfiltered.
- **No control** — cannot maintain setpoint ventilation rates.
- **Unreliable in modern tight buildings** — infiltration through gaps is too low.
- **Security and acoustic concerns** — windows cannot be left open in urban areas.
- **Inadequate for hot-humid climates** — introduces moisture and cooling load.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 3 — IAQ Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (loses 1–2 marks frequently):**
>    - CFM ↔ m³/s: $1 \text{ CFM} = 1.699 \text{ m}^3/\text{h} = 4.719 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$.
>    - ppm ↔ mg/m³: requires molecular weight and STP assumption. For CO₂: 1 ppm ≈ 1.96 mg/m³; for CH₄: 1 ppm ≈ 0.715 mg/m³.
>    - Students often forget to convert minutes → seconds or hours → seconds when applying decay formulas. **Always check units first.**
>
> 2. **Confusing CO₂ Generation Rate Units:**
>    - Per-person CO₂ generation is typically given as 0.3–0.5 L/min (volumetric) or 5.17 mg/s (mass). Mixing these is the most common mistake.
>
> 3. **Incorrect Application of Steady-State Assumption:**
>    - Students often use the steady-state equation when the problem asks for transient time. Verify whether $t$ is involved before applying the steady-state form.
>
> 4. **SBS vs. BRI Confusion:**
>    - SBS: non-specific symptoms, *clear on leaving building*, *no identifiable cause*.
>    - BRI: specific identifiable illness, may *persist* after leaving, e.g., Legionnaires', asbestosis, hypersensitivity pneumonitis. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
>
> 5. **Ignoring the Decay Term $k$:**
>    - For conservative pollutants (CO₂, CO, radon), $k = 0$.
>    - For reactive pollutants (O₃, some VOCs), $k > 0$. Read the problem carefully.
>
> 6. **Forgetting to Add Outdoor Concentration:**
>    - In the form $C_i = C_o + \frac{E}{Q}$, students often write $C_i = \frac{E}{Q}$ and forget the $C_o$ term, losing 1 mark.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 — Module 3: Indoor Air Quality**

**Definitions & Classifications (must know verbatim):**
- ☐ IAQ formal definition (ASHRAE + WHO).
- ☐ SBS definition + 4 symptoms + 4 causes.
- ☐ BRI definition + 2 examples (Legionella, asbestosis).
- ☐ MCS definition (single chemical trigger).
- ☐ VOC definition + 3 sources.
- ☐ Radon — radioactive noble gas, lung cancer risk.
- ☐ Formaldehyde — main source is urea-formaldehyde resins in plywood.

**Core Equation (must derive from first principles):**
- ☐ Well-mixed room mass balance: $V \frac{dC_i}{dt} = E + Q C_o - Q C_i - k V C_i - R$.
- ☐ Steady-state solution: $C_i = \dfrac{E + Q C_o - R}{Q + k V}$.
- ☐ Transient solution: $C_i(t) = C_{i,ss} + (C_{i,0} - C_{i,ss}) e^{-(Q/V + k) t}$.
- ☐ Minimum ventilation rate: $Q_{min} = \dfrac{E}{C_{limit} - C_o}$ (for $k = 0$, $R = 0$, $C_o = 0$).
- ☐ ACH formula: $ACH = \dfrac{Q}{V}$ (in same time units).

**Numerical Skills (practice until automatic):**
- ☐ Convert CFM ↔ m³/s ↔ m³/h.
- ☐ Convert ppm ↔ mg/m³ using molecular weight.
- ☐ Calculate ACH from Q and V.
- ☐ Find steady-state $C_i$ given $E$, $Q$, $C_o$.
- ☐ Find required $Q$ for a target $C_{limit}$.
- ☐ Find rise time or fall time for transient cases.
- ☐ Solve for emission rate $E$ given measured $C_i$ (used in IAQ audits).

**Standards & Limits (memorize these specific numbers):**
- ☐ ASHRAE 62.1 minimum outdoor air for office: 5 CFM/person + 0.06 CFM/ft².
- ☐ CO₂ comfort: 700 ppm above outdoor.
- ☐ WHO PM₂.₅ annual: 15 µg/m³.
- ☐ WHO CO 8-hour: 9 ppm.
- ☐ WHO Formaldehyde 30-min: 100 µg/m³.
- ☐ WHO Radon: 100 Bq/m³.
- ☐ Sick-building threshold: ACH < 0.5 h⁻¹.
- ☐ HEPA efficiency: ≥ 99.97% at 0.3 µm.

**Control Strategy Hierarchy (always state in order):**
- ☐ Source Control → Ventilation → Air Cleaning → Administrative Controls.

**High-Yield Mnemonics:**
- ☐ **"SeMO-VAC"** — Sources, Mixing, Occupancy, Ventilation, Air-cleaning, Control (the 6 IAQ design levers).
- ☐ **"SPOIL"** for IAQ health effects — Sick Building, Pulmonary, Ocular, Irritation, Lethargy.
- ☐ **"3 S of Radon"** — Soil, Sub-slab, Sump (main entry routes).

**Diagram Recall (for 14-mark answers, always draw):**
- ☐ Well-mixed room schematic with all inflow, outflow, source, sink, and removal arrows.
- ☐ Control strategy hierarchy pyramid.
- ☐ SBS vs. BRI decision tree.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Module 3 problems on IAQ almost always require *one* of the following four operations: (1) compute $C_i$ from given $E$, $Q$, $V$; (2) find $Q_{min}$ for a target limit; (3) compute transient time; (4) discuss control measures. Master the three primary equations above and the unit conversions, and you will secure full marks in the IAQ module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Acoustics, Specification of Sound, Sound Power, Sound Intensity and Sound Pressure Levels
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Basics of Acoustics, Specification of Sound, Sound Power, Sound Intensity and Sound Pressure Levels

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Acoustics — The Science of Sound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> **Acoustics** is the interdisciplinary branch of physics and engineering that deals with the study of all mechanical waves in gases, liquids, and solids, including **vibration, sound, ultrasound, and infrasound**. In environmental engineering, acoustics focuses specifically on unwanted sound — **noise** — and its generation, propagation, measurement, and control.

Acoustics combines principles from fluid mechanics (for sound propagation in air/water), thermodynamics (for compressibility), and signal processing (for frequency analysis).

## 1.2 What is Sound?

> [!NOTE]
> **Sound** is a **mechanical longitudinal wave** that propagates through an elastic medium (solid, liquid, or gas) by means of alternating **compressions and rarefactions** of the medium's particles, transmitting **acoustic energy** from a source to a receiver.

Three essential ingredients are required for sound generation and perception:
1. **A source** (vibrating body / disturbance)
2. **A medium** (any material with mass and elasticity — sound does NOT travel through vacuum)
3. **A receiver** (ear, microphone, or detector)

### Speed of Sound in Air

$$c \;=\; \sqrt{\gamma \, R \, T}$$

where:
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats of air (≈ **1.4** for diatomic gases)
- $R$ = universal gas constant / molecular weight of air (≈ **287 J/(kg·K)**)
- $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin

At standard room temperature ($T = 293\,\text{K}$, i.e., 20°C): **$c \approx 343\,\text{m/s}$**.

For engineering estimations, KTU examiners usually accept: **$c \approx 340\text{–}344\,\text{m/s}$** at 20°C.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Ripples in a Pond

> [!TIP]
> Imagine tossing a pebble into a still pond. The water surface does NOT move outward as a mass; instead, a **disturbance pattern** (concentric ripples) travels outward, while each water particle merely **oscillates** up-and-down at its own position. The *energy* moves outward, but the *medium particles* do not.
>
> Sound behaves identically in air: when a speaker cone pushes forward, it **compresses** adjacent air molecules. These molecules push their neighbours, and so on — propagating a **pressure wave** outward. The air molecules themselves just vibrate back and forth around their equilibrium positions.

This analogy also explains why sound needs a medium — there are no water molecules in a vacuum pond to carry the ripple.

## 1.4 Sound Specification — The Three Fundamental Quantities

A sound field in any environment is completely specified (in the linear acoustic sense) by three physically distinct quantities:

| Quantity | Symbol | SI Unit | Measures | Measured by |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sound Power** | $W$ or $P_{\text{ac}}$ | **Watt (W)** | Total acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a source | Cannot be measured directly; computed |
| **Sound Intensity** | $I$ | **W/m²** | Acoustic energy flowing per unit area per unit time (directional) | Intensity probe (two microphones) |
| **Sound Pressure** | $p$ | **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²** | Local pressure fluctuation about ambient atmospheric pressure | Microphone / Sound Level Meter (SLM) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Sound Power** is a property of the **SOURCE** — independent of the room or distance.
> - **Sound Pressure** is a property of the **FIELD/RECEIVER** — depends on distance, room geometry, and environment.
> - **Sound Intensity** is the *bridge* between them — it is the *flow* of power through a unit area.

## 1.5 Frequency, Wavelength, and the Audible Spectrum

$$c \;=\; f \, \lambda$$

- $f$ = frequency in Hertz (Hz) — vibrations per second
- $\lambda$ = wavelength in metres (m)
- $c$ = speed of sound (≈ 343 m/s in air)

> [!NOTE]
> **Audible Range for Humans:** **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz)**
> - Infrasound: $f < 20$ Hz (felt, not heard — earthquakes, building vibrations)
> - Audible band: 20 Hz – 20 kHz
> - Ultrasound: $f > 20$ kHz (bats, medical imaging)

For environmental noise (KTU Module 4 focus), the **A-weighted** frequency range of interest is roughly **10 Hz – 20 kHz**.

## 1.6 Why Decibels? The Logarithmic Hearing Response

Human hearing responds to a **massive range of sound pressures** — from the threshold of hearing ($\approx 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$) to the threshold of pain ($\approx 200\,\text{Pa}$), a ratio of **1,000,000:1 (10⁶)**. Because handling such a huge linear range is impractical, the **decibel (dB)** scale is used. The decibel compresses this enormous ratio into a manageable numerical range of approximately **0 to 140 dB**.

> [!WARNING]
> The decibel is **dimensionless** but **NOT a unit of sound** — it is a **logarithmic ratio** of a measured quantity to a fixed reference quantity. Always state *which* level (SPL, SIL, PWL) is meant.

## 1.7 Standard Reference Values (KTU Board Standard)

| Quantity | Symbol | Reference Value | Why this value? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reference sound pressure | $p_0$ | **$20\,\mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa** | Threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz |
| Reference sound intensity | $I_0$ | **$10^{-12}$ W/m²** | Intensity of plane wave corresponding to $p_0$ in air |
| Reference sound power | $W_0$ | **$10^{-12}$ W** | Conventional standard; matches $I_0$ at 1 m² |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL) — $L_p$

Sound pressure is the most commonly measured acoustic quantity because microphones respond directly to pressure fluctuations. The **Sound Pressure Level** is defined as:

$$L_p \;=\; 20 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{dB}$$

where:
- $p_{\text{rms}}$ = root-mean-square sound pressure (Pa)
- $p_0$ = reference pressure $= 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa
- The factor **20** (not 10) appears because pressure is an *amplitude* (squared in power).

### Why $p_{\text{rms}}$ and not peak pressure?
A sinusoidal pressure wave $p(t) = p_{\text{peak}} \sin(\omega t)$ has an RMS value $p_{\text{rms}} = p_{\text{peak}} / \sqrt{2}$. The RMS value best represents the **energy content** of the wave, which is what the human ear actually responds to.

## 2.2 Sound Intensity Level (SIL) — $L_I$

$$L_I \;=\; 10 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_0}\right) \quad \text{dB}$$

where:
- $I$ = sound intensity (W/m²)
- $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m²

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **10** is used because intensity is a *power-type* quantity (proportional to amplitude squared).

## 2.3 Sound Power Level (PWL / SWL) — $L_W$

$$L_W \;=\; 10 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \quad \text{dB}$$

where:
- $W$ = sound power of the source (W)
- $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W

## 2.4 The Master Relationship: Intensity ↔ Pressure

For a **plane progressive wave** in a free field (the fundamental case for far-field noise analysis):

$$I \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho \, c}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = density of air (≈ **1.2 kg/m³** at 20°C, 1 atm)
- $c$ = speed of sound (≈ **343 m/s**)
- $\rho c$ = **characteristic acoustic impedance of air** ≈ **$410\,\text{Pa·s/m}$** (Rayl)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Implication for KTU Problems:** At standard conditions, $I_0 = p_0^2 / (\rho c) = (2\times10^{-5})^2 / 410 \approx 9.66 \times 10^{-13}$ W/m². By convention, this is rounded up to exactly $10^{-12}$ W/m², which is why the standard reference value is set there.

## 2.5 Sound Power from Intensity (Source–Field Link)

If a source radiates uniformly through a surface of area $A$ enclosing it (free-field or reverberant approximation):

$$W \;=\; I \cdot A$$

In terms of levels (with $A_0 = 1\,\text{m}^2$ as the reference area):

$$L_W \;=\; L_I \;+\; 10 \, \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{A}{A_0}\right)$$

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $c = f\lambda$ | Wave relation | $c \approx 343$ m/s in air at 20°C |
| 2 | $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ | Sound Pressure Level | $p_0 = 2\times10^{-5}$ Pa |
| 3 | $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | Sound Intensity Level | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² |
| 4 | $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | Sound Power Level | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W |
| 5 | $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2 / (\rho c)$ | Intensity from pressure | Plane-wave field; $\rho c \approx 410$ Pa·s/m |
| 6 | $W = I \cdot A$ | Power from intensity and area | $A$ = area through which sound passes |
| 7 | $L_W = L_I + 10\log_{10}(A)$ | Level form of #6 | $A$ in m² |
| 8 | $L_p \approx L_I$ | In plane/far field | Off by a constant; both use same ref values |
| 9 | $L_{p,\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}\sum 10^{L_{p,i}/10}$ | Combine incoherent sources | Energy addition, not arithmetic |
| 10 | $L_{p,\text{double}} = L_{p,1} + 3\,\text{dB}$ | Doubling identical sources | +3 dB = doubling acoustic energy |
| 11 | $L_{p,\text{halve}} = L_{p,1} - 6\,\text{dB}$ | Halving distance (point source) | Point source: –6 dB per distance doubling |
| 12 | $L_{p,\text{halve}} = L_{p,1} - 3\,\text{dB}$ | Halving distance (line source) | Line source: –3 dB per distance doubling |

## 2.7 Decibel Arithmetic Rules (Essential for KTU Numerical Problems)

Because decibels are logarithmic, ordinary arithmetic does NOT apply. Use these KTU-exam-friendly rules:

1. **Doubling the acoustic energy (or doubling the number of identical, uncorrelated sources)** ⟹ **+3 dB** to the total level.
2. **Ten-fold increase in energy (10 identical sources)** ⟹ **+10 dB**.
3. **Halving the acoustic energy** ⟹ **–3 dB**.
4. **Doubling the sound pressure (amplitude)** ⟹ **+6 dB** (since $20\log_{10}2 \approx 6.02$).
5. **Ten-fold increase in pressure** ⟹ **+20 dB**.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | Use of These Quantities |
|---|---|
| **Occupational Health & Safety** | OSHA / KERALA PCB standards specify max SPL = 85–90 dB(A) for 8-hr exposure |
| **HVAC Equipment Selection** | Sound power of AHUs/fans is the only data that scales with installation; SPL is room-dependent |
| **Environmental Noise Mapping** | $L_{eq}$ calculations use SPL averaging in dB |
| **Architectural Acoustics** | Wall/floor partitions rated by transmission loss expressed in dB |
| **Vehicle Noise Certification** | ISO 362 / AIS standards use pass-by SPL at 7.5 m |
| **Industrial Noise Control** | Source PWL used to compute required silencing equipment |

> [!TIP]
> **Why Source PWL matters most in engineering:** When you order a quiet fan, the manufacturer quotes its **sound power level** (e.g., 72 dB re 1 pW). This single number lets you compute the SPL at any distance, in any room, for any installation — provided you account for distance, directivity, and room acoustics.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Pressure Field to Sound Intensity (Plane Wave)

Consider a 1-D longitudinal sound wave travelling in the $+x$ direction:

$$p(x,t) \;=\; p_{\text{peak}} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

where:
- $p_{\text{peak}}$ = peak acoustic pressure amplitude (Pa)
- $k = 2\pi / \lambda$ = wave number (rad/m)
- $\omega = 2\pi f$ = angular frequency (rad/s)

**Step 1 — Particle velocity from Euler's linearised equation:**

For a linear, lossless, adiabatic acoustic field, Euler's equation gives the acoustic particle velocity $u(x,t)$:

$$u(x,t) \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{peak}}}{\rho c} \cos(kx - \omega t)$$

*Explanation:* Pressure gradient accelerates fluid particles. The integration introduces a factor of $1/(\rho c)$ — this is the **specific acoustic impedance** for plane waves.

**Step 2 — Instantaneous intensity:**

Acoustic intensity is defined as the time-averaged product of pressure and particle velocity:

$$I(t) \;=\; p(x,t) \cdot u(x,t)$$

Substituting both expressions:

$$I(t) \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{peak}}^{\,2}}{\rho c} \cos^{2}(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Time averaging over one period:**

Since the time average of $\cos^2$ over a full period is **1/2**:

$$I \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} I(t)\, dt \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{peak}}^{\,2}}{2\rho c}$$

**Step 4 — Express in terms of RMS pressure:**

For a sinusoid, $p_{\text{rms}} = p_{\text{peak}}/\sqrt{2}$, hence $p_{\text{peak}}^{\,2} = 2 p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}$. Substituting:

$$I \;=\; \frac{2 p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{2 \rho c} \;=\; \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho c} \quad \blacksquare$$

This is the celebrated **plane-wave intensity–pressure relation** used in every KTU problem.

## 3.2 Derivation: Sound Power Level from Sound Pressure Level (Free Field, Point Source)

A point source radiates spherically. The total power $W$ passes uniformly through a sphere of radius $r$:

$$W \;=\; I(r) \cdot 4\pi r^{2}$$

Using $I(r) = p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}(r)/(\rho c)$:

$$W \;=\; \frac{4\pi r^{2} \, p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}(r)}{\rho c}$$

Convert both sides to decibels:

$$L_W \;=\; 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right)$$

$$L_p(r) \;=\; 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{p_{\text{rms}}(r)}{p_0}\right)$$

Take the dB form of the power equation:

$$L_W \;=\; L_p(r) \;+\; 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi r^{2}}{\rho c} \cdot \frac{p_0^{2}}{W_0}\right)$$

After substituting the standard reference values and $\rho c \approx 410\,\text{Pa·s/m}$, the constant term evaluates to:

$$L_W \;=\; L_p(r) \;+\; 20\log_{10}(r) \;+\; 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi}{\rho c / p_0^{2}}\right) \;+\; C_0$$

The familiar working form is:

$$\boxed{\,L_W \;=\; L_p(r) \;+\; 20\log_{10}(r) \;+\; 11\,\text{dB}\,}$$

for $r$ in metres, in standard atmospheric air. (The constant 11 dB accounts for the chosen reference values and the spherical geometry; some textbooks quote 10.99 dB.)

## 3.3 Derivation: Inverse-Square Law in Decibels (Point Source)

From $I(r) = W / (4\pi r^2)$:

$$\frac{I_2}{I_1} \;=\; \frac{r_1^{2}}{r_2^{2}}$$

Take dB:

$$\Delta L \;=\; L_{I,2} - L_{I,1} \;=\; 10\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1^{2}}{r_2^{2}}\right) \;=\; 20\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)$$

**Case: $r_2 = 2 r_1$ (distance doubled):**

$$\Delta L \;=\; 20 \log_{10}(0.5) \;=\; -6.02\,\text{dB} \;\approx\; -6\,\text{dB}$$

So doubling distance from a point source **reduces SPL by 6 dB**. Halving distance adds 6 dB.

## 3.4 Worked Example: A 3-Source Industrial Workshop

Three machines emit sound pressure levels of $L_1 = 80$ dB, $L_2 = 80$ dB, $L_3 = 83$ dB at a worker's station. The sources are uncorrelated. Find the **total SPL**.

**Step 1 — Convert each dB to intensity ratio (linear):**

$$10^{L_1/10} = 10^{8.0} = 1.0 \times 10^{8}$$

$$10^{L_2/10} = 1.0 \times 10^{8}$$

$$10^{L_3/10} = 10^{8.3} = 1.995 \times 10^{8} \;\approx\; 2.0 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 2 — Sum linearly (energy addition):**

$$\sum 10^{L_i/10} \;=\; (1.0 + 1.0 + 2.0) \times 10^{8} \;=\; 4.0 \times 10^{8}$$

**Step 3 — Take 10·log of the sum:**

$$L_{\text{total}} \;=\; 10\log_{10}(4.0 \times 10^{8}) \;=\; 10 \log_{10}(4.0) \;+\; 80$$

$$L_{\text{total}} \;=\; 6.02 \;+\; 80 \;\approx\; 86.0\,\text{dB}$$

**Sanity check:** Two 80 dB sources together give 83 dB (since 80+3=83). Adding a third 83 dB source raises it by another 3 dB to **86 dB**. ✓

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Table for KTU Numericals

| Given Quantity | To Find | Use This Path |
|---|---|---|
| $p_{\text{rms}}$ | $L_p$ | $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/2\times10^{-5})$ |
| $L_p$ | $p_{\text{rms}}$ | $p_{\text{rms}} = p_0 \cdot 10^{L_p/20}$ |
| $p_{\text{rms}}$ | $I$ | $I = p_{\text{rms}}^2/(\rho c)$ |
| $I$ | $L_I$ | $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/10^{-12})$ |
| $W$ | $L_W$ | $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/10^{-12})$ |
| $L_p(r)$ | $L_W$ (point source) | $L_W = L_p(r) + 20\log_{10}(r) + 11$ |
| $L_p(r_1)$ | $L_p(r_2)$ (point) | $L_p(r_2) = L_p(r_1) - 20\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ |
| $L_p(r_1)$ | $L_p(r_2)$ (line) | $L_p(r_2) = L_p(r_1) - 10\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ |
| Multiple $L_{p,i}$ | $L_{p,\text{total}}$ | $L_{\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}\sum 10^{L_{p,i}/10}$ |
| $L_1$ doubled | New $L$ | $L_1 + 3$ dB |
| $L_1$ × 10 sources | New $L$ | $L_1 + 10$ dB |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Acoustic Quantities — Relationships Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram shows how the three fundamental acoustic quantities are related through the decibel and physical relations.

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    swDef -->|divide by 4 pi r squared| intensityField["Sound Intensity I in W per m2 at distance r"]

    intensityField -->|multiply by rho c| pressureField["Sound Pressure p rms in Pa at field point"]

    pressureField -->|microphone| mic1["SLM / Microphone reads"]
    intensityField -->|intensity probe| probe1["Two microphone probe reads"]

    swDef -->|apply log 10 / W0| lW["Sound Power Level LW in dB"]
    intensityField -->|apply log 10 / I0| lI["Sound Intensity Level LI in dB"]
    pressureField -->|apply 20 log10 / p0| lP["Sound Pressure Level Lp in dB"]

    lW --> compareA["Lw independent of room or distance"]
    lP --> compareB["Lp depends on r, room, geometry"]
    lI --> compareC["LI directional, bridge quantity"]

    classDef source fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,color:#1f2937,stroke-width:2px
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**Define Sound Power, Sound Intensity, and Sound Pressure. State their units and standard reference values used in acoustics.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Sound Power (W):** The total acoustic energy radiated per unit time by a source, in all directions. It is a property of the source only. **Unit: Watt (W).** Standard reference: $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W.
>
> **Sound Intensity (I):** The time-averaged rate of acoustic energy flow per unit area perpendicular to the direction of propagation. **Unit: W/m².** Standard reference: $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
>
> **Sound Pressure (p):** The local instantaneous pressure fluctuation about the ambient atmospheric pressure caused by a sound wave. **Unit: Pascal (Pa = N/m²).** Standard reference: $p_0 = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa (= 20 µPa).

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 2024 Scheme]`

**What is the decibel? Why is the logarithmic decibel scale used in acoustics instead of linear pressure or intensity values?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **decibel (dB)** is a logarithmic, dimensionless unit used to express the ratio of a measured sound quantity to a fixed reference value. It is defined as $L = 10 \log_{10}(Q/Q_0)$ for power-type quantities (intensity, power) and $L = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ for pressure-type quantities.
>
> The decibel scale is used because:
> 1. **Huge dynamic range** of human hearing (20 µPa to 200 Pa, a 1,000,000 : 1 ratio) becomes 0 to 140 dB.
> 2. The human ear responds **logarithmically** to stimulus, so dB matches perception.
> 3. It allows convenient **multiplication of effects (distances, areas, sources)** to become simple addition.
> 4. It compresses data for **easier communication and recording** on standard instruments.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** Derive the relationship between sound intensity and sound pressure for a plane progressive wave in a free field. State clearly the role of the characteristic acoustic impedance of air. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A workshop contains two identical machines, each radiating a sound power of 0.5 W. Treating the machines as a single equivalent point source located at the workshop centre, compute:
   (i) The total Sound Power Level $L_W$ in dB re 1 pW.  
   (ii) The Sound Pressure Level at a point 4 m from the equivalent source.  
   (iii) The new Sound Pressure Level if the distance is doubled to 8 m. Comment on the result. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

Consider a plane longitudinal wave $p(x,t) = p_{\text{peak}}\cos(kx - \omega t)$ propagating in the $+x$ direction.

**Step 1 — Particle velocity (from linearised Euler's equation):**

$$u(x,t) = \frac{p(x,t)}{\rho c} = \frac{p_{\text{peak}}}{\rho c}\cos(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous intensity:**

$$I(t) = p(x,t) \cdot u(x,t) = \frac{p_{\text{peak}}^{\,2}}{\rho c}\cos^{2}(kx - \omega t)$$

**Step 3 — Time average over one period $T$:**
