$$I = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T I(t)\,dt = \frac{p_{\text{peak}}^{\,2}}{\rho c}\cdot\frac{1}{2} = \frac{p_{\text{peak}}^{\,2}}{2\rho c}$$

**Step 4 — Express using RMS pressure** ($p_{\text{rms}} = p_{\text{peak}}/\sqrt{2}$):

$$\boxed{\,I = \frac{p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}}{\rho c}\,}$$

The constant $\rho c$ is the **characteristic acoustic impedance** of the medium. For air at 20°C, $\rho \approx 1.2$ kg/m³ and $c \approx 343$ m/s, giving $\rho c \approx 410\,\text{Pa·s/m}$ (Rayl). It represents the medium's resistance to the propagation of sound; the higher the impedance, the lower the intensity for the same pressure amplitude.

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Total Sound Power Level:**

Two machines, each 0.5 W ⟹ total power

$$W_{\text{total}} = 2 \times 0.5 = 1.0\,\text{W}$$

$$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1.0}{10^{-12}}\right) = 10 \times 12 = 120\,\text{dB re 1 pW}$$

**Part (ii) — SPL at 4 m (point source):**

Using $L_p = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$ (free-field point source, standard air):

$$L_p(4) = 120 - 20\log_{10}(4) - 11 = 120 - 12.04 - 11 \approx 97.0\,\text{dB}$$

**Part (iii) — SPL at 8 m (distance doubled):**

For a point source, doubling distance reduces SPL by 6 dB:

$$L_p(8) = L_p(4) - 6 = 97.0 - 6.0 = 91.0\,\text{dB}$$

**Comment:** Doubling the distance from a point source in a free field reduces the sound pressure level by **6 dB** (the inverse-square law in dB form). This reflects the fact that the same acoustic power is spread over a sphere four times larger in surface area, so intensity — and hence $p^2$ — falls by a factor of 4, equivalent to a 6 dB drop.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 / Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]`

**(a)** With the help of neat diagrams, explain the difference between Sound Power Level ($L_W$), Sound Intensity Level ($L_I$), and Sound Pressure Level ($L_p$). State their defining equations and standard reference values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A factory floor has four machines producing sound pressure levels of 78 dB, 78 dB, 81 dB, and 84 dB at a worker's ear. Assuming the sources are uncorrelated:
   (i) Calculate the **combined SPL** at the worker's position.
   (ii) If one of the 78 dB machines is switched off, by how many dB does the level drop?
   (iii) An acoustic enclosure reduces the SPL of the loudest machine (84 dB) by 15 dB. By how much does the combined SPL drop? **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks):**

| Quantity | Definition | Equation | Reference | Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Sound Power Level $L_W$** | Logarithmic measure of the total acoustic power radiated by a source | $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W | Property of the **source** — independent of room/distance |
| **Sound Intensity Level $L_I$** | Logarithmic measure of sound intensity at a field point in a specified direction | $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m² | Property of the **field** — directional, vectorial |
| **Sound Pressure Level $L_p$** | Logarithmic measure of RMS sound pressure at a field point | $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$ | $p_0 = 2\times10^{-5}$ Pa | Property of the **field** — scalar, easily measured |

**Diagram (textual):**

```
   [SOURCE]
      |--- Sound Power W (W)         [SOURCE quantity]
      |
      v
   [FIELD] ---> Intensity I (W/m^2)   [FIELD quantity, vector]
      |
      v
   [RECEIVER] ---> Pressure p (Pa)    [FIELD quantity, scalar]
```

The relationships are: $W = I \cdot A$ and $I = p^2_{\text{rms}}/(\rho c)$. For a plane wave in the far field, $L_p \approx L_I$ (the offset is built into the standard reference values).

---

**Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks):**

**Part (i) — Combined SPL from four sources:**

Convert each level to linear intensity ratio:

$$10^{78/10} = 6.31 \times 10^{7}$$
$$10^{78/10} = 6.31 \times 10^{7}$$
$$10^{81/10} = 1.26 \times 10^{8}$$
$$10^{84/10} = 2.51 \times 10^{8}$$

Sum:

$$\Sigma = (6.31 + 6.31 + 12.6 + 25.1) \times 10^{7} = 50.32 \times 10^{7}$$

$$L_{\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}(50.32 \times 10^{7}) = 10(7 + \log_{10} 50.32) = 10(7 + 1.702) = 87.0\,\text{dB}$$

**Part (ii) — Effect of switching off one 78 dB machine:**

Subtracting one 78 dB contribution:

$$\Sigma' = (6.31 + 12.6 + 25.1) \times 10^{7} = 44.01 \times 10^{7}$$

$$L' = 10\log_{10}(44.01 \times 10^{7}) = 10(7 + 1.643) = 86.4\,\text{dB}$$

Drop = $87.0 - 86.4 = 0.6\,\text{dB}$ (negligible — as expected, removing the weakest source barely affects the dominant ones).

**Part (iii) — Effect of 15 dB enclosure on the 84 dB source:**

New level of loudest machine: $84 - 15 = 69$ dB.

$$10^{69/10} = 7.94 \times 10^{6}$$

New sum:

$$\Sigma'' = (6.31 + 6.31 + 12.6 + 7.94) \times 10^{7} = 33.16 \times 10^{7}$$

$$L'' = 10\log_{10}(33.16 \times 10^{7}) = 10(7 + 1.521) = 85.2\,\text{dB}$$

Drop = $87.0 - 85.2 = 1.8\,\text{dB}$. This is a small overall reduction because the remaining 78 dB and 81 dB sources still contribute significantly — **controlling the loudest source alone is not enough**.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Adding decibels arithmetically** (e.g., $80 + 80 = 160$). WRONG. The correct sum of two 80 dB sources is 83 dB.
> 2. **Using the wrong factor (20 vs 10)** in the SPL formula. Pressure uses **20**; power/intensity uses **10**.
> 3. **Forgetting to convert references** when mixing $p$ and $I$ — the standard refs ($p_0 = 20\,\mu$Pa, $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m²) are chosen so that in a plane wave, $L_p$ and $L_I$ are numerically equal. Don't mix and match.
> 4. **Using $p_{\text{peak}}$ instead of $p_{\text{rms}}$** in the SPL formula. Always use RMS.
> 5. **Point source vs line source** confusion: doubling distance gives –6 dB (point) or –3 dB (line).
> 6. **Quoting dB without stating reference** (e.g., "85 dB" instead of "85 dB re 20 µPa"). KTU examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist:**

- **Sound** = mechanical longitudinal wave; needs a medium; speed in air ≈ 343 m/s at 20°C.
- **Three core quantities:** Sound Power $W$ (W, source), Sound Intensity $I$ (W/m², field-directional), Sound Pressure $p$ (Pa, field-scalar).
- **Reference values:** $p_0 = 2\times10^{-5}$ Pa; $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m²; $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W.
- **Decibel formulas:**
    * $L_p = 20\log_{10}(p_{\text{rms}}/p_0)$
    * $L_I = 10\log_{10}(I/I_0)$
    * $L_W = 10\log_{10}(W/W_0)$
- **Master relation (plane wave):** $I = p_{\text{rms}}^{\,2}/(\rho c)$ where $\rho c \approx 410$ Rayl in air.
- **Power–intensity link:** $W = I \cdot A$ ⟹ $L_W = L_I + 10\log_{10}(A)$ with $A$ in m².
- **Inverse-square law (point source):** Doubling distance → SPL drops by **6 dB** ($L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 20\log_{10}(r/r_0)$).
- **Line source (cylindrical spreading):** Doubling distance → SPL drops by **3 dB** ($L_p(r) = L_p(r_0) - 10\log_{10}(r/r_0)$).
- **Energy addition rule:** Doubling identical sources → +3 dB; ten times sources → +10 dB.
- **Combined SPL** of multiple sources: convert to linear, sum, convert back: $L_{\text{total}} = 10\log_{10}\sum 10^{L_i/10}$.
- **Decibel is logarithmic — you cannot add dB values directly.**
- **Source PWL is invariant** with distance/room — it is the *only* truly portable acoustic quantity.
- **Audible range:** 20 Hz – 20 kHz. Pressure range: 20 µPa – 200 Pa. Level range: 0 – 140 dB.
- **A-weighting** (dB(A)) mimics the ear's frequency response; used universally in environmental and occupational noise standards.
- **Practical ranges for context:**
    * Quiet bedroom: 25–35 dB(A)
    * Office: 45–55 dB(A)
    * City traffic (5 m): 75–85 dB(A)
    * Pneumatic chipper at 1 m: 100–110 dB(A)
    * Jet engine at 25 m: 140–150 dB(A)
- **Perception rules of thumb:**
    * +10 dB ≈ "twice as loud" to the ear
    * +3 dB ≈ just-perceptible change
    * –10 dB ≈ "half as loud"

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Plane, Point and Line Sources, Multiple Sources
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Plane, Point and Line Sources, Multiple Sources

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Acoustic Source Geometries

In environmental noise engineering, every noise source in the real world (a factory chimney, a busy highway, a turbojet engine, a construction site) can be mathematically idealized into one of three fundamental geometries: a **Point Source**, a **Line Source**, or a **Plane Source**. This geometric classification is not arbitrary — it dictates the *rate* at which sound energy decays as it travels away from the source, and therefore the rate at which the Sound Pressure Level (SPL) drops with distance.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (Module 4):** Students must be able to identify the type of source, predict the SPL variation with distance, and apply the inverse-square (point) or inverse-linear (line) decay laws to solve practical noise propagation problems.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions

### A. Point Source
A **point source** is a localized noise emitter whose dimensions are negligibly small compared to the distance to the receiver. Sound radiates **spherically** (or hemispherically if on a reflecting ground) and the sound **intensity** falls off as the **inverse square of the distance** (geometric spreading loss).

### B. Line Source
A **line source** is an elongated emitter (e.g., a stream of vehicles, a railway track, a long conveyor belt) whose length is large compared to the receiver distance but whose width is negligible. Sound radiates **cylindrically**, and the sound **intensity** falls off as the **inverse of the distance** (only one dimension of spreading).

### C. Plane Source
A **plane source** is an extended planar emitter (e.g., a large factory wall, a busy intersection considered at close range). In the far field, sound radiates as a **plane wave** with **no geometric spreading loss** — the SPL remains essentially constant with distance.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Bulb vs. The Tube vs. The Wall Analogy**
> - **Point Source → A bare electric bulb in a dark room:** Light (sound) spreads in *all directions*; as you step back, the brightness (loudness) drops *very rapidly* (the further you go, the faster it fades).
> - **Line Source → A long fluorescent tube on the ceiling:** Light spreads in *one plane only*; brightness fades more slowly because the energy is "stretched" along a line, not a sphere.
> - **Plane Source → A giant glowing LED billboard:** Very close to the surface, the light is uniform everywhere; you don't notice any drop in brightness until you are right next to it.

## 1.4 Fundamental Acoustic Quantities & Reference Constants

| Quantity | Symbol | Reference Value (Standard) |
|---|---|---|
| Reference Sound Pressure | $P_{ref}$ | **20 μPa (= $2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa)** |
| Reference Sound Intensity | $I_{ref}$ | **$10^{-12}$ W/m²** |
| Reference Sound Power | $W_{ref}$ | **$10^{-12}$ W (= 1 pW)** |
| Standard Sound Speed in Air | $c$ | **343 m/s** (at 20 °C) |
| Air Density | $\rho_0$ | **1.21 kg/m³** (at 20 °C) |
| Characteristic Impedance of Air | $\rho_0 c$ | **≈ 415 N·s/m³ (Rayl)** |

> [!NOTE]
> All decibel scales in noise pollution are referenced to these **standard ISO/RFC values**. The factor of 20 in the pressure equation (and 10 in the power equation) comes from squaring the pressure in the intensity relation: $I \propto P^2$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sound Pressure Level (SPL) and Sound Power Level (PWL)

The decibel (dB) is a **logarithmic ratio**, not an absolute unit. The two most-used levels in noise control are:

$$L_P = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

$$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W}{W_{ref}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

where:
- $L_P$ = Sound Pressure Level in **dB (re 20 μPa)**
- $L_W$ = Sound Power Level in **dB (re $10^{-12}$ W)**
- $P$ = RMS sound pressure (Pa)
- $W$ = Sound power (W)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the factor 20 instead of 10 for pressure?**
> Because acoustic intensity $I \propto P^2$, taking $10 \log_{10}(P^2) = 20 \log_{10}(P)$.

## 2.2 The Inverse-Square Law for a Point Source

A point source radiates sound power $W$ uniformly in all directions. The intensity at distance $r$ on a spherical wavefront is:

$$I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

Comparing the SPL at two distances $r_1$ and $r_2$:

$$L_{P_2} = L_{P_1} - 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Doubling distance from a point source → SPL drops by exactly 6 dB.** (A 6 dB drop is perceived as a clear halving of loudness.)

For a source on a hard reflecting ground, sound radiates into a **hemisphere** only:

$$I = \frac{W}{2\pi r^{2}}$$

So the SPL is **3 dB higher** at the same distance compared to free-field (full-sphere) radiation.

## 2.3 The Inverse-Linear Law for a Line Source

A long incoherent line source (vehicles on a highway, a continuous railway line) radiates as a cylindrical wavefront. The intensity falls off linearly with distance:

$$I = \frac{W/L}{2\pi r}$$

where $W/L$ is the power per unit length. Comparing two distances:

$$L_{P_2} = L_{P_1} - 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Doubling distance from a line source → SPL drops by exactly 3 dB.**

## 2.4 The Plane Source (Coherent Extended Source)

For a large, coherent plane source (e.g., the wall of a vibrating machine enclosure), the wavefront remains planar in the near field, and:

$$L_{P_2} \approx L_{P_1} \quad \text{(no spreading loss)}$$

Beyond a transition distance, the source begins to behave like a point source and the inverse-square law takes over.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Source Type | Geometric Spread | SPL Difference Equation | Drop per Doubling of Distance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Point Source (free field)** | Spherical | $\Delta L = -20\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | **6 dB** |
| 2 | **Point Source (hemispherical / on ground)** | Half-sphere | $\Delta L = -20\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | **6 dB** (+3 dB offset vs. free field) |
| 3 | **Line Source (incoherent)** | Cylindrical | $\Delta L = -10\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | **3 dB** |
| 4 | **Plane Source (far field is point-like)** | Mixed | $\Delta L = 0$ (near field) → $-20\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ (far field) | **0 dB → 6 dB** |
| 5 | **Multiple n Identical Sources** | — | $L_T = L_1 + 10\log_{10}(n)$ | — |
| 6 | **Two Different Sources** | — | $L_T = 10\log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10}+10^{L_2/10}\right)$ | — |
| 7 | **PWL to SPL (point, free)** | — | $L_P = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 11$ (since $10\log_{10}4\pi \approx 11$) | — |
| 8 | **PWL to SPL (point, hemi)** | — | $L_P = L_W - 20\log_{10}(r) - 8$ (since $10\log_{10}2\pi \approx 8$) | — |
| 9 | **PWL to SPL (line)** | — | $L_P = L_W/L - 10\log_{10}(r) - 8$ | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "11" and "8" dB constants** are derived from $10\log_{10}(4\pi) \approx 10.99$ and $10\log_{10}(2\pi) \approx 7.98$. These appear in every KTU numerical — memorize them.

## 2.6 Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **Highway Noise Prediction:** A stream of vehicles is treated as a **line source**; doubling the setback distance only buys 3 dB, so noise barriers become essential.
- **Industrial Stack / DG Set Sizing:** Treated as a **point source**; doubling setback gives 6 dB reduction, the standard regulatory metric.
- **Multiple Machine Plants:** Several machines (e.g., 4 pumps + 2 compressors) are summed using the **logarithmic addition rule**, not arithmetic.
- **Acoustic Enclosure Design:** Walls are modeled as **plane sources**; transmission loss (TL) ratings become critical.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: SPL vs. Distance for a Point Source

**Step 1 — Power is conserved on every expanding wavefront.**
A point source emits total acoustic power $W$ uniformly. The intensity at distance $r$ on a spherical surface of area $4\pi r^2$ is:

$$I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 2 — Express intensity in decibels (Sound Intensity Level).**
$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{4\pi r^{2} I_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Use the identity $I = P^2/(\rho_0 c)$ to convert to pressure level.**
$$L_P = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P^{2}}{\rho_0 c I_{ref}}\right)$$

Since $I_{ref} = P_{ref}^{2}/(\rho_0 c)$, this simplifies exactly to:

$$L_P = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right) \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Take the difference between two distances $r_1$ and $r_2$ on the same source.**

$$L_{P_1} - L_{P_2} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{4\pi r_2^{2}}{4\pi r_1^{2}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

Rearranging:

$$L_{P_2} = L_{P_1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \text{ dB}$$

This is the **Inverse-Square Law** for point sources.

## 3.2 Derivation: SPL vs. Distance for an Incoherent Line Source

**Step 1 — A line source of length $L$ emits power per unit length $W' = W/L$.**
**Step 2 — Cylindrical spreading gives the intensity at radius $r$:**

$$I = \frac{W'}{2\pi r}$$

**Step 3 — Form the difference between two distances:**

$$L_{P_1} - L_{P_2} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2\pi r_2}{2\pi r_1}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{L_{P_2} = L_{P_1} - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \text{ dB}}$$

This is the **Inverse-Linear Law** for line sources.

## 3.3 Derivation: Logarithmic Addition of Multiple Sources

**Step 1 — Sound powers add linearly (uncorrelated sources):**
$$W_T = \sum_{i=1}^{n} W_i$$

**Step 2 — Convert each $W_i$ to its level, then back to power for summation:**
$$W_i = W_{ref} \cdot 10^{L_i/10}$$

**Step 3 — Total level:**

$$L_T = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\sum W_i}{W_{ref}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} 10^{L_i/10}\right)$$

**Special case — $n$ identical sources of level $L_1$ each:**

$$L_T = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(n \cdot 10^{L_1/10}\right) = L_1 + 10 \log_{10}(n)$$

This gives the famous KTU quick-reference additions:

| Number of Identical Sources $n$ | Correction $\Delta L$ to add to $L_1$ |
|---|---|
| 2 | **+3 dB** |
| 3 | **+4.8 dB** |
| 4 | **+6 dB** |
| 5 | **+7 dB** |
| 10 | **+10 dB** |
| 100 | **+20 dB** |

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Examples

### Example 1: Point Source Distance Attenuation `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Problem:** The SPL measured at 5 m from a small compressor (point source, on hard ground) is **82 dB(A)**. Find the SPL at **(a)** 20 m and **(b)** 80 m. Also find the SPL at 5 m if the same compressor were suspended in free field (no ground reflection).

**Solution (a) — At 20 m:**
Using inverse-square law:

$$L_{P_2} = L_{P_1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{20}{5}\right) = 82 - 20 \log_{10}(4)$$

$$= 82 - 20(0.602) = 82 - 12.04 = 69.96 \approx 70 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Solution (b) — At 80 m (16× the original distance):**
$$L_{P_2} = 82 - 20 \log_{10}(16) = 82 - 20(1.204) = 82 - 24.08 = 57.92 \text{ dB(A)}$$

Equivalently, distance increased by 4 doublings ($5\to10\to20\to40\to80$), so $82 - 4(6) = 58$ dB(A). ✓

**Solution (c) — Free-field case at 5 m:**
A reflecting ground adds **+3 dB** to the hemispherical case. So in free field:

$$L_{P,\text{free}} = 82 - 3 = 79 \text{ dB(A)}$$

### Example 2: Line Source — Highway Noise `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Problem:** A busy highway (modeled as an infinite incoherent line source) produces **75 dB(A)** at a residential building 50 m away. What is the SPL at **(a)** 100 m, and **(b)** 25 m?

**Solution (a) — At 100 m (2× distance):**
$$L_{P_2} = 75 - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{100}{50}\right) = 75 - 10(0.301) = 75 - 3.01 \approx 72 \text{ dB(A)}$$

(Note: only 3 dB drop for doubling — a line source is much harder to attenuate with distance.)

**Solution (b) — At 25 m (½ distance):**
$$L_{P_2} = 75 - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{25}{50}\right) = 75 - 10(-0.301) = 75 + 3.01 \approx 78 \text{ dB(A)}$$

### Example 3: Logarithmic Addition of Multiple Sources `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Problem:** An industrial plant has three operating machines with sound pressure levels (at a reference position) of: **88 dB**, **85 dB**, and **82 dB**. Find the combined SPL. How does the result change if the 82 dB machine is switched off?

**Solution — Full operation:**

$$L_T = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{88/10} + 10^{85/10} + 10^{82/10}\right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{8.8} + 10^{8.5} + 10^{8.2}\right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(6.31 \times 10^{8} + 3.16 \times 10^{8} + 1.58 \times 10^{8}\right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(11.05 \times 10^{8}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(1.105 \times 10^{9}\right)$$

$$= 10(9.043) = 90.43 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Solution — With 82 dB machine OFF:**

$$L_T = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{8.8} + 10^{8.5}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(6.31 \times 10^{8} + 3.16 \times 10^{8}\right)$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}\!\left(9.47 \times 10^{8}\right) = 10(8.976) = 89.76 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Interpretation:** Switching off the *quietest* (82 dB) machine only reduces the total by **0.67 dB** — almost imperceptible. The 88 dB machine dominates the acoustic environment.

### Example 4: Multiple Identical Sources `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**Problem:** Six identical pumps, each producing **78 dB(A)** at 1 m, operate together. Find the total SPL at 1 m and at 5 m from the cluster (treated as a point source of total power).

**Solution — At 1 m (six sources summed):**
$$L_T = 78 + 10 \log_{10}(6) = 78 + 7.78 = 85.78 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Solution — At 5 m (point-source distance attenuation):**
$$L_T = 85.78 - 20 \log_{10}(5) = 85.78 - 20(0.699) = 85.78 - 13.98 = 71.8 \text{ dB(A)}$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Geometric Spreading Comparison (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Noise Source Geometry] --> B[Point Source]
    A --> C[Line Source]
    A --> D[Plane Source]

    B --> B1[Examples: DG Set, Compressor, Chimney]
    B --> B2[Spherical Wavefront]
    B --> B3[Inverse-Square Law: I propto 1 over r squared]
    B --> B4[SPL drop: 6 dB per doubling of distance]

    C --> C1[Examples: Highway Traffic, Railway, Conveyor]
    C --> C2[Cylindrical Wavefront]
    C --> C3[Inverse-Linear Law: I propto 1 over r]
    C --> C4[SPL drop: 3 dB per doubling of distance]

    D --> D1[Examples: Factory Wall, Large Enclosure]
    D --> D2[Planar Wavefront near field]
    D --> D3[No spreading loss in near field]
    D --> D4[Transition to point-source behavior in far field]
```

## 4.2 Sound Propagation Decay Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Known SPL at Reference Distance] --> Q{Source Type?}
    Q -->|Point| P[Apply 20 log r2 over r1]
    Q -->|Line| L[Apply 10 log r2 over r1]
    Q -->|Plane| PL[No attenuation in near field]
    P --> R[Compute New SPL at Receiver]
    L --> R
    PL --> R
    R --> M{Is Receiver in Far Field?}
    M -->|Yes| END[Final SPL Value]
    M -->|No| ADJ[Add Atmospheric Absorption A and Ground Factor G]
    ADJ --> END
```

## 4.3 Multiple Source Combination Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Multiple Sources Present] --> N{How many?}
    N -->|Two sources| T1[Compute L_T = 10 log 10 power L1 plus 10 power L2]
    N -->|N identical| T2[L_T = L1 plus 10 log N]
    N -->|Many different| T3[Use Lookup Table for Difference]
    T3 --> D1{Difference between sources}
    D1 -->|0 to 1 dB| A1[Add 3 dB to higher]
    D1 -->|2 to 3 dB| A2[Add 2 dB to higher]
    D1 -->|4 to 9 dB| A3[Add 1 dB to higher]
    D1 -->|Greater than 10 dB| A4[Ignore lower source, take higher]
    A1 --> OUT[Combined SPL]
    A2 --> OUT
    A3 --> OUT
    A4 --> OUT
    T1 --> OUT
    T2 --> OUT
```

## 4.4 Wavefront Geometry Schematic (Textual Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POINT[Point Source]
        P1[Source] -->|r1| W1[Spherical Wavefront r1]
        P1 -->|r2| W2[Spherical Wavefront r2]
    end
    subgraph LINE[Line Source]
        LS[Infinite Line] -->|r1| C1[Cylindrical Wavefront r1]
        LS -->|r2| C2[Cylindrical Wavefront r2]
    end
    subgraph PLANE[Plane Source]
        PS[Plane Emitter] -->|near| PW[Planar Wavefront no decay]
        PS -->|far| PWF[Becomes point-like beyond transition]
    end
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Differentiate between a point source and a line source with one suitable example for each.**

**Model Answer:**
A **point source** is a localized emitter whose dimensions are negligible compared to the receiver distance; sound radiates spherically and SPL drops by **6 dB** for every doubling of distance (inverse-square law, $I \propto 1/r^2$). Example: a DG set or a chimney.

A **line source** is an elongated emitter (length $\gg$ width, length $\gg$ distance to receiver) radiating cylindrically; SPL drops by only **3 dB** per doubling of distance (inverse-linear law, $I \propto 1/r$). Example: a continuous stream of traffic on a highway, a railway line.

| Parameter | Point Source | Line Source |
|---|---|---|
| Geometry | Spherical | Cylindrical |
| Intensity decay | $1/r^{2}$ | $1/r$ |
| SPL drop per doubling | 6 dB | 3 dB |

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**State the logarithmic addition rule for combining sound pressure levels of two uncorrelated sources. What is the combined SPL when two identical 80 dB sources operate together?**

**Model Answer:**
The logarithmic addition rule for two uncorrelated sources is:

$$L_T = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(10^{L_1/10} + 10^{L_2/10}\right) \text{ dB}$$

When two **identical** sources of level $L_1 = 80$ dB operate together:

$$L_T = 80 + 10 \log_{10}(2) = 80 + 3.01 \approx 83 \text{ dB(A)}$$

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Derive the inverse-square law for sound pressure level variation with distance from a point source. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Definition.** A point source emits total acoustic power $W$ uniformly in all directions, forming a spherical wavefront of radius $r$.

**Step 2 — Intensity at distance $r$:**
$$I = \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Express in decibels. Sound Intensity Level:**
$$L_I = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{I}{I_{ref}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{4\pi r^{2} I_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Convert to SPL using $I = P^{2}/(\rho_0 c)$ and $I_{ref} = P_{ref}^{2}/(\rho_0 c)$:**
$$L_P = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P}{P_{ref}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Combine (since $L_P = L_I$ in a plane/progressive wave):**
$$L_P = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{4\pi r^{2} I_{ref}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{W}{4\pi I_{ref}}\right) - 20 \log_{10}(r)$$

**Step 6 — Take SPL difference between two distances $r_1$ and $r_2$ (same source, hence same $W$):**
$$L_{P_1} - L_{P_2} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

$$\boxed{L_{P_2} = L_{P_1} - 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right) \text{ dB}}$$

This is the **inverse-square law**. Doubling distance gives $20\log_{10}(2) = 6.02 \approx 6$ dB drop.

---

**(b)** A factory chimney (treated as a point source on hard ground) produces a sound power level of **110 dB re 1 pW**. Calculate the SPL at distances of **(i) 10 m, (ii) 50 m, and (iii) 200 m**. Comment on the results. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Use the hemispherical (on-ground) PWL-to-SPL formula:**
$$L_P = L_W - 20 \log_{10}(r) - 8 \text{ dB (since } 10\log_{10}(2\pi) \approx 7.98\text{)}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate at $r = 10$ m:**
$$L_P = 110 - 20 \log_{10}(10) - 8 = 110 - 20(1) - 8 = 110 - 20 - 8 = 82 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 3 — Calculate at $r = 50$ m:**
$$L_P = 110 - 20 \log_{10}(50) - 8 = 110 - 20(1.699) - 8 = 110 - 33.98 - 8 = 68.02 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate at $r = 200$ m:**
$$L_P = 110 - 20 \log_{10}(200) - 8 = 110 - 20(2.301) - 8 = 110 - 46.02 - 8 = 55.98 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 5 — Comment.**
- Distances: 10 m → 50 m → 200 m are ratio 1 : 5 : 20.
- For each 5× increase, the SPL drops by $20 \log_{10}(5) = 13.98 \approx 14$ dB.
- Verify: $82 - 14 = 68$ ✓ ; $68 - 20\log_{10}(4) = 68 - 12 = 56$ ✓.
- The Indian ambient noise standard (CPCB) for industrial areas is 75 dB(A) day / 70 dB(A) night, so the **safe residential setback** is at least 50 m, but the **50 m value is still above the night-time industrial limit**, indicating the need for an acoustic silencer.

---

### QUESTION B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** A long, straight, busy highway is modeled as an infinite incoherent line source. The equivalent sound level measured at a point **40 m from the road centerline is 78 dB(A)**. Estimate the SPL at **(i) 80 m and (ii) 20 m** from the road. What setback is required to achieve the CPCB daytime residential standard of **55 dB(A)**? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Apply the line-source decay equation $L_{P_2} = L_{P_1} - 10\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$:**

**At 80 m (2× distance):**
$$L_P = 78 - 10 \log_{10}(80/40) = 78 - 10 \log_{10}(2) = 78 - 3.01 = 74.99 \approx 75 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**At 20 m (½ distance):**
$$L_P = 78 - 10 \log_{10}(20/40) = 78 - 10 \log_{10}(0.5) = 78 - 10(-0.301) = 78 + 3.01 = 81.01 \approx 81 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 2 — Setback calculation for 55 dB(A) target:**
$$55 = 78 - 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r}{40}\right)$$

$$10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r}{40}\right) = 78 - 55 = 23$$

$$\log_{10}\!\left(\frac{r}{40}\right) = 2.3$$

$$\frac{r}{40} = 10^{2.3} = 199.53$$

$$r = 40 \times 199.53 = 7981 \text{ m} \approx 8 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering interpretation.** A line source is extremely difficult to attenuate with distance alone (only 3 dB per doubling). A setback of **8 km is impractical**, which is why noise barriers, green belts, and rubberized asphalt are mandated along urban highways.

---

**(b)** A workshop contains **four machines** producing individual SPLs of **85, 83, 80, and 76 dB(A)** at the operator's position. Calculate: **(i)** the combined SPL when all four operate, and **(ii)** the combined SPL when the quietest (76 dB) machine is switched off. Comment on the dominant-source concept. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert each SPL to relative power:**

| Machine | $L_i$ (dB) | $10^{L_i/10}$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 85 | $3.162 \times 10^{8}$ |
| 2 | 83 | $1.995 \times 10^{8}$ |
| 3 | 80 | $1.000 \times 10^{8}$ |
| 4 | 76 | $3.981 \times 10^{7}$ |

**Step 2 — Sum of powers (all four operating):**
$$\sum W_i = (3.162 + 1.995 + 1.000 + 0.3981) \times 10^{8} = 6.555 \times 10^{8}$$

**Combined SPL:**
$$L_T = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(6.555 \times 10^{8}\right) = 10(8.8166) = 88.17 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 3 — With the 76 dB machine OFF:**
$$\sum W_i = (3.162 + 1.995 + 1.000) \times 10^{8} = 6.157 \times 10^{8}$$

$$L_T = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(6.157 \times 10^{8}\right) = 10(8.7894) = 87.89 \text{ dB(A)}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on Dominant Source Concept.**
- Switching off the **76 dB machine** reduces the total by only $88.17 - 87.89 = 0.28$ dB — **inaudible** to the human ear (the just-noticeable difference is 1 dB).
- The **85 dB machine alone** contributes $10\log_{10}(3.162 \times 10^{8}/6.555 \times 10^{8}) = 10\log_{10}(0.482) = -3.17$ dB relative to the total, but it is responsible for the largest single share of acoustic energy.
- **Engineering takeaway:** Noise control efforts should target the **loudest source first**; quieting the loudest machine by 6 dB will reduce the total by ~3 dB, whereas silencing the quietest source has negligible impact.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls (Module 4):**
> 1. **Mixing up the 6 dB and 3 dB rules:** Students often apply $-20\log$ for line sources, losing **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Arithmetic addition of decibels:** A common error is to add dB values directly (e.g., $80 + 85 = 165$ dB), which is physically meaningless. The correct operation is **logarithmic addition of powers**.
> 3. **Forgetting the 11 dB / 8 dB constant** in PWL-to-SPL conversions: this costs 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Confusing the reference pressure (20 μPa) with reference intensity ($10^{-12}$ W/m²)** in the SPL definition.
> 5. **Wrong number of identical sources rule:** Two identical sources = **+3 dB**, not +6 dB (the +6 dB is for 4 sources).
> 6. **Ignoring the hemispherical +3 dB offset** when the source is on a reflecting ground.
> 7. **Unit-mismatch in line-source problems:** $W$ is power per unit length (W/m); the final SPL uses $10\log$ (not $20\log$) because the spreading is one-dimensional.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4 — Plane, Point, Line & Multiple Sources)**
> - **Point Source** → Spherical spreading → Inverse-square law → **−6 dB per doubling** → $L_{P_2} = L_{P_1} - 20\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$.
> - **Line Source** → Cylindrical spreading → Inverse-linear law → **−3 dB per doubling** → $L_{P_2} = L_{P_1} - 10\log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$.
> - **Plane Source** → No decay in the near field; transitions to point-source behavior in the far field.
> - **Hemispherical (on ground) vs. Free field:** +3 dB offset when the source is on a reflecting surface.
> - **Standard PWL-to-SPL constants:** Subtract **11 dB** (free field, $4\pi r^{2}$) or **8 dB** (hemispherical, $2\pi r^{2}$) and $20\log_{10}(r)$.
> - **Reference values:** $P_{ref} = 20\,\mu\text{Pa}$, $W_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W, $I_{ref} = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
> - **Multiple sources combine by power, not by dB:** $L_T = 10\log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_i/10})$.
> - **Identical-source shortcut:** $n$ identical sources $\Rightarrow$ add $10\log_{10}(n)$ to one source's level.
> - **Dominant-source rule:** A source that is ≥ 10 dB louder than the rest essentially determines the total level — silencing the weakest is acoustically useless.
> - **CPCB ambient noise standards to memorize:** Industrial 75/70, Commercial 65/55, Residential 55/45, Silence Zone 50/40 (Day/Night dB(A)).
> - **KTU Exam Mantra:** Always state the source type, identify the propagation geometry, write the appropriate decay equation, and apply logarithmic (never arithmetic) addition for multiple sources.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Outdoor and Indoor Noise Propagation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Outdoor and Indoor Noise Propagation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Outdoor Noise Propagation — Formal Definition

**Outdoor noise propagation** is the study of how acoustic energy travels from a sound source through the open atmosphere to a receiver. Unlike indoor sound, outdoor propagation is dominated by **geometric spreading**, **atmospheric absorption**, **ground reflections**, **meteorological refraction**, and **physical obstructions** (barriers, buildings, terrain). The standard metric used to express outdoor noise is the **A-weighted Equivalent Continuous Sound Level (L\textsubscript{eq(A)})** measured in **dB(A)**, with the reference pressure **p\textsubscript{0} = 20 μPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight
> Outdoor noise propagation is governed by **ISO 9613-1 / ISO 9613-2** standards, which predict the attenuation of sound as a function of distance, ground type, barriers, and atmospheric conditions. Every numerical question on this topic assumes a free-field or hemispherical propagation geometry unless otherwise stated.

### 1.2 Indoor Noise Propagation — Formal Definition

**Indoor noise propagation** describes how sound energy generated inside an enclosed space (or transmitted from outside) behaves within rooms. The two primary phenomena are:

- **Sound Transmission** — sound passing through a partition (wall, door, window, ceiling).
- **Sound Absorption / Room Acoustics** — sound interacting with the room boundaries, furnishings, and occupants.

The governing metrics are **Sound Transmission Class (STC)**, **Transmission Loss (TL)** in dB, **Noise Reduction (NR)** in dB, and **Reverberation Time (T\textsubscript{60})** in seconds.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Pond Ripple Model**
> Imagine dropping a pebble into a still pond. The ripples spread outward in ever-widening circles, losing energy as they travel. Outdoor noise behaves similarly: a point source (e.g., a vehicle horn) radiates sound as an expanding spherical wavefront. The further away you stand, the quieter it gets — this is **geometric attenuation**.
>
> Now, picture the same pebble dropped near a floating leaf. The ripples bend around the leaf, lose a little energy, and continue outward — this bending is **diffraction**, the same phenomenon that lets noise "creep" over a noise barrier.
>
> For indoor acoustics, imagine speaking inside a tiled bathroom versus a bedroom with carpets and curtains. The bathroom is *reverberant* (echoey) because hard surfaces reflect sound, while the bedroom is *acoustically "dead"* because soft materials absorb sound. This contrast defines the entire field of **indoor noise control**.

### 1.4 Reference Quantities (International Standards)

| Quantity | Symbol | Value / Unit | Standard |
|----------|--------|--------------|----------|
| Reference sound pressure | p\textsubscript{0} | **20 μPa** | IEC 61672 |
| Reference sound power | W\textsubscript{0} | **10⁻¹² W** (1 pW) | ISO 3741 |
| Speed of sound in air | c | **343 m/s** (at 20 °C) | ISO 9613-1 |
| Air density (reference) | ρ\textsubscript{0} | **1.21 kg/m³** | ISO 9613-1 |
| Frequency weighting | A, C, Z | dB(A), dB(C), dB(Z) | IEC 61672-1 |

> [!NOTE]
> Why **20 μPa**? This is the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz — the faintest sound a healthy young ear can detect. All sound pressure levels (SPL) are referenced to this value.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Outdoor Noise Propagation — Mechanism Breakdown

Outdoor propagation is the **superposition of multiple physical effects**, each contributing its own attenuation (or sometimes amplification). The general KTU/ISO form is:

$$L_p(r) \;=\; L_w \;-\; A_{\text{div}} \;-\; A_{\text{atm}} \;-\; A_{\text{gr}} \;-\; A_{\text{bar}} \;-\; A_{\text{misc}}$$

Where:
- **L\textsubscript{w}** = Sound Power Level of source (dB re 1 pW)
- **A\textsubscript{div}** = Attenuation due to geometric divergence (spreading)
- **A\textsubscript{atm}** = Attenuation due to atmospheric absorption
- **A\textsubscript{gr}** = Ground effect (reflection / interference)
- **A\textsubscript{bar}}** = Barrier attenuation (insertion loss)
- **A\textsubscript{misc}}** = Miscellaneous (vegetation, industrial sites, housing)

#### Step-by-Step Logical Flow

1. **Geometric Spreading (Divergence)**
   * For a **point source** in free field: energy spreads over the surface of an expanding sphere.
   * Surface area ∝ r², so intensity falls as 1/r².
   * Doubling the distance → intensity quartered → SPL drops by **6 dB**.
   * Doubling the distance for a **line source** (e.g., long highway) → SPL drops by **3 dB**.

2. **Atmospheric Absorption**
   * Air molecules (O₂, N₂, H₂O vapor) convert acoustic energy into heat via **viscous and thermal relaxation processes**.
   * Strongly frequency-dependent: high frequencies (≥ 4 kHz) are absorbed much faster than low frequencies.
   * Expressed as an absorption coefficient **α (dB/km)**, given by ISO 9613-1 tables.

3. **Ground Effect (Ground Reflection)**
   * Sound reflects off the ground, interfering (constructively or destructively) with the direct wave.
   * **Hard ground** (asphalt, water, concrete) → mostly reflective → constructive interference at receiver.
   * **Soft ground** (grass, snow, farmland) → porous → destructive interference at low-to-mid frequencies.
   * Typical values: A\textsubscript{gr} ranges from **−3 dB (amplification)** to **+5 dB (attenuation)**.

4. **Barrier Attenuation (Insertion Loss)**
   * A solid obstacle between source and receiver creates a **shadow zone**.
   * Sound reaches the receiver only by **diffraction over the barrier edge**.
   * The frequency-dependent loss is given by **Maekawa's formula** or the **Fresnel Number** method.

5. **Meteorological Effects**
   * **Wind gradients** (sound faster downwind) and **temperature inversions** refract sound *downward* → increases noise at ground level.
   * **Temperature lapse** (warm air rising) refracts sound *upward* → creates a shadow zone.
   * KTU problems typically ignore meteorology unless explicitly stated.

6. **Vegetation and Miscellaneous**
   * Dense vegetation adds ~5–10 dB attenuation per 100 m for frequencies above 1 kHz.

### 2.2 Indoor Noise Propagation — Mechanism Breakdown

Indoor propagation involves two distinct physical problems:

#### (A) Sound Transmission Through Partitions

1. **Mass Law** — Heavy walls block more sound. Doubling the mass per unit area increases TL by ~5–6 dB.
2. **Coincidence (Critical) Frequency** — Above a critical frequency, walls become poor insulators because bending waves match the speed of sound in air.
3. **STC Rating** — A single-number rating (ASTM E413) used by code authorities to specify partition performance.
4. **Flanking Paths** — Sound bypassing the partition via ducts, doors, structural connections.

#### (B) Room Acoustics (Reverberation)

1. **Reverberation Time (T\textsubscript{60})** — Time for sound to decay by 60 dB after the source stops.
2. **Sabine's Formula** — Relates T\textsubscript{60} to room volume and total absorption.
3. **Noise Reduction (NR)** — Difference between outdoor and indoor SPL, depends on wall TL and room absorption.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are **high-yield** for KTU University Exams. Memorize the symbols, units, and boundary conditions.

#### Outdoor Propagation

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | $L_p \;=\; L_w \;-\; 20\log(r) \;-\; 11$ | Free-field point source (hemispherical) | dB, m |
| 2 | $L_p \;=\; L_w \;-\; 10\log(r) \;-\; 8$ | Free-field line source (cylindrical) | dB, m |
| 3 | $L_{p2} \;=\; L_{p1} \;-\; 20\log\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | Point-source divergence between two points | dB |
| 4 | $L_{p2} \;=\; L_{p1} \;-\; 10\log\!\left(\dfrac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$ | Line-source divergence between two points | dB |
| 5 | $A_{\text{atm}} \;=\; \alpha(r_2 - r_1)$ | Atmospheric absorption (α from ISO 9613-1) | dB |
| 6 | $L_w \;=\; 10\log\!\left(\dfrac{W}{W_0}\right)$ | Sound Power Level, W\textsubscript{0} = 10⁻¹² W | dB |
| 7 | $I \;=\; \dfrac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$ | Intensity from point source (free field) | W/m² |
| 8 | $L_p \;=\; 20\log\!\left(\dfrac{p}{p_0}\right)$ | Sound Pressure Level, p\textsubscript{0} = 20 μPa | dB |
| 9 | $A_{\text{bar}} \;=\; 10\log\!\left(3 \;+\; 20N\right)$ | Maekawa barrier insertion loss (Rayleigh) | dB |
| 10 | $N \;=\; \dfrac{2\delta}{\lambda} \;=\; \dfrac{2\,\delta\, f}{c}$ | Fresnel Number for barrier | dimensionless |
| 11 | $L_{\text{eq}} \;=\; 10\log\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}\!\int_{0}^{T}\!10^{L(t)/10}\,dt\right)$ | Equivalent continuous SPL | dB(A) |
| 12 | $L_{\text{dn}} \;=\; 10\log\!\left(\dfrac{1}{24}\!\left[\,15\!\cdot\!10^{L_d/10} \;+\; 9\!\cdot\!10^{(L_n+10)/10}\right]\right)$ | Day-Night Average Sound Level | dB(A) |

#### Indoor Propagation

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use | Units |
|---|---------|---------------|-------|
| 13 | $TL \;=\; 20\log(mf) \;-\; 47$ | **Mass Law** (m in kg/m², f in Hz) | dB |
| 14 | $f_c \;=\; \dfrac{c^{2}}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{m}{D}}$ | Coincidence frequency (D = bending stiffness) | Hz |
| 15 | $T_{60} \;=\; \dfrac{0.161\,V}{A}$ | **Sabine's reverberation formula** | s |
| 16 | $A \;=\; \sum_i S_i \alpha_i$ | Total room absorption (Sabine) | sabins (m²) |
| 17 | $A \;=\; S\,\bar{\alpha} \;+\; 4\,mV$ | Eyring correction (highly absorptive rooms) | sabins |
| 18 | $\bar{\alpha} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum S_i \alpha_i}{\sum S_i}$ | Average absorption coefficient | dimensionless |
| 19 | $NR \;=\; 10\log\!\left(\dfrac{S\,\bar{\alpha}}{4}\right)$ | Noise Reduction between outdoor and indoor | dB |
| 20 | $SPL_{\text{indoor}} \;\approx\; SPL_{\text{outdoor}} \;-\; TL \;+\; 10\log(S\,\bar{\alpha}/4)$ | Combined transmission + absorption | dB |

> [!NOTE]
> The factor **0.161** in Sabine's formula is **0.161 V/A**, where V is in m³ and A is in sabins. It comes from the experimentally derived constant **0.161 s/m** in SI units (also written as **0.16** in older texts). Use **0.161** for KTU numerical problems.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Highway Noise Modeling** — Predicting noise contours around expressways to design **noise barriers of correct height and length**.
- **Airport Noise Mapping** — Federal Aviation Regulations require **DNL ≤ 65 dB(A)** for residential land-use compatibility.
- **Building Code Compliance** — Apartment walls and floors must meet **STC ≥ 50** (or **IIC ≥ 50**) per the National Building Code of India.
- **Industrial Plant Design** — Locating noisy equipment and designing enclosures to meet **Factories Act** exposure limits (85 dB(A) for 8 hours).
- **Concert Hall Acoustics** — Sabine's formula is still the first tool architects use to size concert halls and auditoria.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Inverse-Square Law for a Point Source

**Starting physical premise:** A point source radiates sound uniformly in all directions (isotropic radiator). At distance **r**, the radiated power **W** is distributed over the surface of a sphere of area **4πr²**.

#### Step 1: Sound Intensity at Distance r

$$I \;=\; \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

This is the definition of intensity as power per unit area on a sphere.

#### Step 2: Relation Between Intensity and Sound Pressure

For a free progressive plane wave in air:

$$I \;=\; \frac{p^{2}}{\rho_0 c}$$

where **ρ\textsubscript{0}c** is the **characteristic acoustic impedance of air** ≈ **412 rayl** (Pa·s/m) at 20 °C.

#### Step 3: Solve for Sound Pressure p(r)

$$p^{2} \;=\; \rho_0 c \cdot I \;=\; \rho_0 c \cdot \frac{W}{4\pi r^{2}}$$

$$p \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_0 c \, W}{4\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{r}$$

#### Step 4: Express SPL at Two Distances r₁ and r₂

$$L_{p1} \;=\; 20\log\!\left(\frac{p_1}{p_0}\right), \quad L_{p2} \;=\; 20\log\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_0}\right)$$

Take the difference:

$$L_{p2} - L_{p1} \;=\; 20\log\!\left(\frac{p_2}{p_1}\right) \;=\; 20\log\!\left(\frac{r_1}{r_2}\right)$$

#### Step 5: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\,L_{p2} \;=\; L_{p1} \;-\; 20\log\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Because p ∝ 1/r, doubling r (r₂/r₁ = 2) → p halves → 20·log(2) = **6.02 dB** drop. Hence the famous **"6 dB per doubling of distance"** rule for point sources.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Sound Power Level from a Hemispherical Source

When a source sits on a reflecting ground plane (e.g., a vehicle on a road), sound radiates into a **hemisphere**, not a full sphere.

#### Step 1: Substitute Hemisphere Area (2πr²)

$$I \;=\; \frac{W}{2\pi r^{2}}$$

#### Step 2: Solve for Sound Pressure

$$p^{2} \;=\; \rho_0 c \cdot \frac{W}{2\pi r^{2}}$$

$$p \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\rho_0 c \, W}{2\pi}} \cdot \frac{1}{r}$$

#### Step 3: Form the SPL Expression

$$L_p \;=\; 20\log\!\left(\frac{p}{p_0}\right) \;=\; 20\log\!\left(\frac{1}{r}\sqrt{\frac{\rho_0 c \, W}{2\pi}}\,\frac{1}{p_0}\right)$$

#### Step 4: Group Terms and Reduce

$$L_p \;=\; 10\log\!\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \;-\; 10\log\!\left(\frac{2\pi r^{2}}{W_0 \rho_0 c / p_0^{2}}\right)$$

Substituting the standard constants ρ\textsubscript{0} = 1.21 kg/m³, c = 343 m/s, W\textsubscript{0} = 10⁻¹² W, p\textsubscript{0} = 20 μPa:

$$L_p \;=\; L_w \;-\; 20\log(r) \;-\; 11$$

#### Step 5: Final Hemispherical Formula

$$\boxed{\,L_p(r) \;=\; L_w \;-\; 20\log(r) \;-\; 11\, \quad \text{(hemispherical, free field, dB re 1 pW)}\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The constant **−11 dB** comes from **10·log(2π·ρ\textsubscript{0}c·W\textsubscript{0}/p\textsubscript{0}²)**. Many textbooks round this to **−10.99** ≈ **−11 dB**. For numerical KTU problems, this constant is usually given.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Maekawa's Barrier Insertion Loss

#### Step 1: Path Difference δ

For a thin rigid barrier between source S and receiver R, the diffracted path over the barrier edge is longer than the direct path by:

$$\delta \;=\; (A + B) \;-\; d$$

where **A** = source-to-barrier-top distance, **B** = barrier-top-to-receiver distance, **d** = straight-line S-to-R distance.

#### Step 2: Fresnel Number

$$N \;=\; \frac{2\delta}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{2\,\delta\, f}{c}$$

#### Step 3: Maekawa Empirical Fit

Based on scale-model experiments, Maekawa (1968) found:

$$A_{\text{bar}} \;=\; 10\log(3 + 20N) \quad \text{for } N > 0$$

For **N < 0** (no diffraction regime): A\textsubscript{bar} ≈ 0 dB. For very large N: A\textsubscript{bar} → 20·log(N) (asymptotic to cylindrical spreading).

#### Step 4: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{\,A_{\text{bar}} \;=\; 10\log\!\left(3 + 20N\right) \;=\; 10\log\!\left(3 + \frac{40\,\delta\, f}{c}\right)\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** N = 1 (δ = λ/2) gives **A = 10·log(23) = 13.6 dB**. N = 10 gives **A = 10·log(203) = 23.1 dB**. Doubling the path difference adds ~6 dB.

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Sabine's Reverberation Formula

#### Step 1: Definition of Reverberation Time T₆₀

T\textsubscript{60} is the time for sound energy density to fall by a factor of 10⁶ (i.e., 60 dB) after the source stops.

#### Step 2: Energy Decay Differential Equation

The mean energy density **E** in a room decays as:

$$\frac{dE}{dt} \;=\; -\frac{c\,A}{4V}\,E$$

where **A** = total absorption (sabins), **V** = room volume (m³), and the factor **c/4** comes from the average path length between reflections in a diffuse field.

#### Step 3: Solve the Differential Equation

$$E(t) \;=\; E_0 \,\exp\!\left(-\frac{c\,A}{4V}\,t\right)$$

#### Step 4: Apply the T₆₀ Definition (E/E₀ = 10⁻⁶)

$$10^{-6} \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{c\,A}{4V}\,T_{60}\right)$$

Take natural log:

$$-6\ln(10) \;=\; -\frac{c\,A}{4V}\,T_{60}$$

#### Step 5: Solve for T₆₀

$$T_{60} \;=\; \frac{24 V \ln(10)}{c\,A}$$

#### Step 6: Substitute Numerical Values

c = 343 m/s, ln(10) ≈ 2.3026, so 24·ln(10)/343 = **0.1611 s/m**:

$$\boxed{\,T_{60} \;=\; \frac{0.161\,V}{A}\, \quad \text{(s, m³, sabins)}\,}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** The constant **0.161 s/m** has units of seconds per metre. Multiply by V/A (m³ / m² = m) to get seconds. If the room is highly absorptive (ᾱ > 0.3), use the **Eyring correction**: T₆₀ = 0.161 V / (−S·ln(1 − ᾱ)).

---

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A point source on a reflecting ground plane has L\textsubscript{w} = 100 dB. Atmospheric absorption coefficient α = 1.5 dB/km. Compute the SPL at (a) 10 m and (b) 500 m.

#### Part (a) — At r = 10 m

$$L_p(10) \;=\; 100 \;-\; 20\log(10) \;-\; 11 \;=\; 100 \;-\; 20 \;-\; 11 \;=\; 69 \text{ dB}$$

#### Part (b) — At r = 500 m

$$L_p(500) \;=\; 100 \;-\; 20\log(500) \;-\; 11 \;-\; 1.5 \times 0.5$$

$$L_p(500) \;=\; 100 \;-\; 53.98 \;-\; 11 \;-\; 0.75 \;=\; 34.27 \text{ dB}$$

> **Conversion Logic:** Atmospheric absorption at 500 m is **α × distance_in_km = 1.5 × 0.5 = 0.75 dB**. We had to convert 490 m of extra distance (from 10 m to 500 m) to 0.49 km, then add the 0.75 dB loss. *(For exam purposes, the simple convention α·(r₂−r₁) uses r in km; many texts add the full α·r to keep it rigorous.)*

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Outdoor Noise Propagation — Attenuation Chain (Block Diagram)

This block diagram shows the **sequential attenuation** experienced by sound traveling from an outdoor source to a receiver, as used in ISO 9613-2 / KTU syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sound Power Level Lw] --> B[Geometric Divergence A div]
    B --> C[Atmospheric Absorption A atm]
    C --> D[Ground Effect A gr]
    D --> E[Barrier Insertion Loss A bar]
    E --> F[Miscellaneous A misc]
    F --> G[Received SPL Lp at receiver]
    G --> H[A weighting and Leq calculation]
    H --> I[Final Lp in dB A]
    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
    style B fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21,color:#000
    style C fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    style D fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    style E fill:#f5cba7,stroke:#a04000,color:#000
    style F fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483,color:#000
    style G fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#196f3d,color:#000
    style H fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d,color:#000
    style I fill:#f7dc6f,stroke:#7d6608,color:#000
```

**Reading the diagram:** Start at the source (L\textsubscript{w}), subtract each attenuation block in turn, then apply A-weighting at the receiver to obtain the final **L\textsubscript{p} in dB(A)**.

---

### 4.2 Spherical vs. Cylindrical Spreading

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PS[Point Source Spherical 6 dB per doubling]
        PS1[Source at origin] --> PS2[Wavefront radius r equals 1 m]
        PS2 --> PS3[Wavefront radius r equals 2 m SPL drops 6 dB]
        PS3 --> PS4[Wavefront radius r equals 4 m SPL drops 12 dB]
    end
    subgraph LS[Line Source Cylindrical 3 dB per doubling]
        LS1[Source along a line] --> LS2[Cylinder radius r equals 1 m]
        LS2 --> LS3[Cylinder radius r equals 2 m SPL drops 3 dB]
        LS3 --> LS4[Cylinder radius r equals 4 m SPL drops 6 dB]
    end
    style PS1 fill:#fdebd0,stroke:#a04000
    style PS2 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21
    style PS3 fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#922b21
    style PS4 fill:#ec7063,stroke:#641e16
    style LS1 fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d
    style LS2 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d
    style LS3 fill:#85c1e9,stroke:#1f618d
    style LS4 fill:#5dade2,stroke:#1a5276
```

**Reading the diagram:** A point source loses **6 dB** per doubling of distance (sphere area ∝ r²). A line source loses only **3 dB** per doubling (cylinder area ∝ r). Use spherical for vehicles, machines, aircraft; use cylindrical for long highways, railways.

---

### 4.3 Indoor Sound Transmission — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SOUND[Outdoor SPL at facade] --> WIN[Window TL value]
    SOUND --> WALL[Wall TL value]
    SOUND --> ROOF[Roof TL value]
    WIN --> FLANK[Flanking paths ducts doors]
    WALL --> FLANK
    ROOF --> FLANK
    FLANK --> TOT[Total effective TL]
    TOT --> ROOM[Indoor SPL inside room]
    ROOMABS[Room absorption S alpha] --> ROOM
    ROOM --> NR[Noise Reduction NR in dB]
    style SOUND fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style WIN fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21
    style WALL fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d
    style ROOF fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d
    style FLANK fill:#f5cba7,stroke:#a04000
    style TOT fill:#e8daef,stroke:#6c3483
    style ROOM fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#196f3d
    style ROOMABS fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d
    style NR fill:#f7dc6f,stroke:#7d6608
```

**Reading the diagram:** Outdoor sound penetrates via three parallel partitions (window, wall, roof), each contributing its own Transmission Loss. Flanking paths (ducts, electrical conduits, structural edges) degrade the combined TL. The indoor SPL also depends on the room's absorption (S·ᾱ). The final **Noise Reduction (NR)** is the difference between outdoor and indoor SPL.

---

### 4.4 Barrier Diffraction — Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Source S] --> A[Direct line from S to R distance d]
    S --> A1[Path S to barrier top length A]
    A1 --> B[Barrier of height h]
    B --> B1[Path barrier top to R length B]
    A1 -. diffracted path .-> B1
    A1 --> DELTA[Path difference delta equals A plus B minus d]
    DELTA --> FN[Fresnel Number N equals 2 delta f over c]
    FN --> IL[Insertion Loss equals 10 log of 3 plus 20 N]
    style S fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608
    style B fill:#ec7063,stroke:#641e16
    style DELTA fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d
    style FN fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#196f3d
    style IL fill:#f7dc6f,stroke:#7d6608
```

**Reading the diagram:** Sound from S must diffract over the top of a barrier of height h to reach R. The extra path length δ depends on the geometry. The Fresnel Number N combines δ with frequency f and speed of sound c. Maekawa's formula then gives the barrier's insertion loss.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*State the inverse-square law for outdoor sound propagation from a point source. What is the SPL drop when the distance is doubled?*

**Model Answer:**

The **inverse-square law** states that the sound intensity **I** from a point source in free field is inversely proportional to the square of the distance **r** from the source:

$$I \;\propto\; \frac{1}{r^{2}}$$

Converting to sound pressure level (SPL), the SPL at distance r₂ relative to r₁ is:

$$L_{p2} \;=\; L_{p1} \;-\; 20\log\!\left(\frac{r_2}{r_1}\right)$$

When r₂ = 2·r₁ (doubling of distance):

$$\Delta L_p \;=\; 20\log(2) \;=\; 6.02 \text{ dB} \;\approx\; 6 \text{ dB}$$

**Therefore, the SPL drops by 6 dB for every doubling of distance from a point source.** [3 Marks]

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the Sound Transmission Class (STC). What is its significance in building acoustics?*

**Model Answer:**

**Sound Transmission Class (STC)** is a single-number rating (defined by ASTM E413) that characterizes the sound-insulating properties of a partition (wall, door, window, floor) over the frequency range of 125 Hz to 4000 Hz.

It is derived by fitting a standardized contour to the measured Transmission Loss (TL) curve of the partition.

**Significance in building acoustics:**

- Provides a single number for code compliance (e.g., Indian National Building Code requires **STC ≥ 40** for inter-residential walls and **STC ≥ 50** for floors between apartments).
- Enables quick comparison between different partition designs.
- Higher STC → better speech privacy and reduced indoor noise.

Typical STC values: hollow-core door ≈ 20, single-pane glass ≈ 26, standard interior wall ≈ 35, insulated double wall ≈ 50. [3 Marks]

---

### Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

**(a) A point source of sound power level L\textsubscript{w} = 105 dB sits on a hard reflecting ground. Compute the SPL at distances of 5 m, 50 m, and 500 m. Take the atmospheric absorption coefficient α = 1.8 dB/km. [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

The hemispherical free-field formula:

$$L_p(r) \;=\; L_w \;-\; 20\log(r) \;-\; 11 \;-\; \alpha r$$

with **r in km** for the absorption term.

**At r = 5 m = 0.005 km:**

$$L_p(5) \;=\; 105 \;-\; 20\log(5) \;-\; 11 \;-\; 1.8 \times 0.005$$

$$L_p(5) \;=\; 105 \;-\; 13.98 \;-\; 11 \;-\; 0.009 \;=\; 80.01 \text{ dB}$$

**At r = 50 m = 0.050 km:**

$$L_p(50) \;=\; 105 \;-\; 20\log(50) \;-\; 11 \;-\; 1.8 \times 0.050$$

$$L_p(50) \;=\; 105 \;-\; 33.98 \;-\; 11 \;-\; 0.090 \;=\; 59.93 \text{ dB}$$

**At r = 500 m = 0.500 km:**

$$L_p(500) \;=\; 105 \;-\; 20\log(500) \;-\; 11 \;-\; 1.8 \times 0.500$$

$$L_p(500) \;=\; 105 \;-\; 53.98 \;-\; 11 \;-\; 0.90 \;=\; 39.12 \text{ dB}$$

**Result:** L\textsubscript{p} = **80.0 dB at 5 m, 59.9 dB at 50 m, 39.1 dB at 500 m**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Important:** Distance went from 5 m to 50 m (×10) → SPL drop = 20·log(10) = 20 dB (matches 80 → 60). Distance 50 m to 500 m (×10 again) → another 20 dB drop. Atmospheric absorption adds a small extra 0.09 dB per decade.

**[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) Explain Sabine's formula for reverberation time. A classroom has volume V = 600 m³ and total absorption A = 60 sabins. Find the reverberation time. If the teacher complains of poor speech intelligibility, what is the recommended range of T₆₀ for a classroom, and how can it be reduced? [7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Sabine's Formula:**

$$T_{60} \;=\; \frac{0.161\,V}{A} \;=\; \frac{0.161 \times 600}{60} \;=\; \frac{96.6}{60} \;=\; 1.61 \text{ s}$$

**Recommended T₆₀ for a classroom** (per ANSI S12.60 and IS 2526): **0.6 – 0.8 s** for optimal speech intelligibility.

Since 1.61 s is much higher than recommended, the room is too reverberant.

**How to reduce T₆₀:**
1. Add **porous absorbers** (acoustic tiles, mineral wool, carpet) to walls and ceiling to increase A.
2. Add **furnishings** with high absorption (curtains, upholstered seats, bookcases).
3. Add **suspended acoustic baffles**.
4. To halve T₆₀ from 1.61 s to 0.8 s, double the absorption from 60 to 120 sabins (add 60 sabins of treatment).

**Verification:** T₆₀ = 0.161 × 600 / 120 = 0.805 s ✓

**[7 Marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Sabine's Formula**
> Many students write **T₆₀ = 0.16 V / A** or **T₆₀ = 0.161 V / A**. Both are accepted, but you **must** include the constant. A common error is writing **T₆₀ = V/A** (dimensionally wrong). Another pitfall: forgetting that **A = ΣSᵢαᵢ** when the room has mixed surfaces — if you are given S and ᾱ, use A = S·ᾱ. For highly absorptive rooms (ᾱ > 0.3), you **must** use **Eyring correction**: T₆₀ = 0.161 V / (−S·ln(1 − ᾱ)). Using Sabine's formula in such rooms is **incorrect** and will cost you 2 marks.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

**(a) A 100 mm thick brick wall has surface density m = 200 kg/m². Calculate the Transmission Loss (TL) at 500 Hz using the Mass Law. If the wall has a coincidence frequency f\textsubscript{c} = 250 Hz, sketch and explain the typical TL vs. frequency curve of the wall, marking the mass-law region, the coincidence dip, and the stiffness-controlled region. [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Mass Law calculation at 500 Hz:**

$$TL \;=\; 20\log(mf) \;-\; 47 \;=\; 20\log(200 \times 500) \;-\; 47$$

$$TL \;=\; 20\log(100{,}000) \;-\; 47 \;=\; 20 \times 5 \;-\; 47 \;=\; 100 \;-\; 47 \;=\; 53 \text{ dB}$$

**TL vs. Frequency curve — Three Regions:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Stiffness controlled below 50 Hz TL drops at 6 dB per octave] --> R2[Mass law region 50 Hz to 250 Hz TL rises at 6 dB per octave]
    R2 --> R3[Coincidence dip at fc equals 250 Hz TL drops sharply by 10 to 20 dB]
    R3 --> R4[Plateau region above 300 Hz TL recovers then rises again]
    style R1 fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#922b21
    style R2 fill:#82e0aa,stroke:#196f3d
    style R3 fill:#ec7063,stroke:#641e16,color:#fff
    style R4 fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1f618d
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Stiffness-controlled region (f < f\textsubscript{s}):** Below the resonant frequency of the wall, the wall behaves like a spring. TL decreases at 6 dB/octave as frequency increases. (Typically f\textsubscript{s} < 50 Hz for masonry.)

2. **Mass-controlled region (f\textsubscript{s} < f < f\textsubscript{c}):** The wall behaves as a rigid mass. TL rises at **6 dB/octave** (i.e., 20·log(mf) − 47). Our calculation at 500 Hz gives 53 dB, which fits this region.

3. **Coincidence region (f ≈ f\textsubscript{c}):** The bending-wave speed in the plate matches the speed of sound in air. The wall radiates efficiently into the room, creating a sharp **dip in TL** of 10–20 dB. This is the weakest point of the partition.

4. **Plateau region (f > 1.4·f\textsubscript{c}):** TL recovers and continues to rise at ~6 dB/octave.

**[7 Marks]**

---

**(b) A 10 m long noise barrier of height h = 4 m is constructed between a road and a residential colony. A vehicle source is at distance 4 m from the barrier on the road side, and a receiver is at 16 m on the residential side, both at ground level. Compute the path difference δ and the Fresnel Number at 500 Hz, then find the Insertion Loss (IL) of the barrier. [7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Geometry:** Source S, top of barrier T, receiver R form a right-triangle configuration.
- Horizontal source-to-barrier = a = 4 m
- Horizontal barrier-to-receiver = b = 16 m
- Barrier height h = 4 m
- c = 343 m/s, f = 500 Hz

**Step 1: Distances from source to top, top to receiver**

$$A \;=\; \sqrt{a^{2} + h^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{4^{2} + 4^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{32} \;=\; 5.657 \text{ m}$$

$$B \;=\; \sqrt{b^{2} + h^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{16^{2} + 4^{2}} \;=\; \sqrt{272} \;=\; 16.492 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2: Direct distance from S to R**

$$d \;=\; a + b \;=\; 4 + 16 \;=\; 20 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Path difference δ**

$$\delta \;=\; A + B - d \;=\; 5.657 + 16.492 - 20 \;=\; 2.149 \text{ m}$$

**Step 4: Fresnel Number N at 500 Hz**

$$N \;=\; \frac{2 \delta f}{c} \;=\; \frac{2 \times 2.149 \times 500}{343} \;=\; \frac{2149}{343} \;=\; 6.265$$

**Step 5: Insertion Loss (Maekawa)**

$$IL \;=\; 10\log(3 + 20N) \;=\; 10\log(3 + 20 \times 6.265)$$

$$IL \;=\; 10\log(3 + 125.3) \;=\; 10\log(128.3) \;=\; 10 \times 2.108 \;=\; 21.08 \text{ dB}$$

**Result:** The barrier provides an **insertion loss of ≈ 21.1 dB** at 500 Hz.

**Comment:** Doubling the barrier height to 8 m would more than double the path difference, raising IL to ≈ 27 dB — a useful design insight.

**[7 Marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Barrier Problem**
> Three common mistakes:
> 1. **Wrong path-difference formula:** Some students use δ = √(A² + B²) − d (incorrect). The correct diffracted path is A + B (sum of two legs), not the hypotenuse of a triangle with legs A and B.
> 2. **Unit confusion:** δ must be in **metres**, f in **Hz**, c in **m/s**. Mixing units gives a wrong Fresnel Number.
> 3. **Maekawa validity:** The formula **10·log(3 + 20N)** is valid for **N > 0** only. If your computation gives N < 0 (e.g., barrier below the line of sight), use **IL = 0 dB**. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for ignoring this.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **6 dB per doubling** is the magic number for **point sources**; **3 dB per doubling** for **line sources** (highways, railways).
- The **hemispherical formula** is **L\textsubscript{p} = L\textsubscript{w} − 20·log(r) − 11** with r in metres — remember the **−11** constant.
- The **inverse-square law** holds only in the **free field**. In the **reverberant near field**, SPL levels off at the **direct-field-to-reverberant-field transition** distance.
- **Atmospheric absorption α** is strongly frequency-dependent: ≈ 0.1 dB/km at 125 Hz, ≈ 1.5 dB/km at 500 Hz, ≈ 5–10 dB/km above 4 kHz.
- **Ground effect** can either **amplify** (hard ground, constructive interference) or **attenuate** (soft ground, destructive interference) — by up to **±3 dB**.
- **Mass Law** doubles wall mass → +5 to +6 dB TL. Coincidence dip can wipe out 10–20 dB.
- **Sabine's T₆₀ = 0.161 V/A** assumes a diffuse field and ᾱ < 0.3. For highly absorptive rooms, switch to **Eyring**: T₆₀ = 0.161 V / [−S·ln(1 − ᾱ)].
- **Noise Reduction (NR)** between outdoor and indoor depends on the wall TL **and** the room's absorption (S·ᾱ). Higher absorption alone does **not** help if the wall is thin.
- For **KTU numericals**, always: (1) identify source type (point/line), (2) pick the right formula, (3) keep units consistent (km vs m, Hz vs kHz), (4) add A-weighting only at the **end**.
- For **barrier problems**, Maekawa's formula is the KTU-favoured shortcut. For full credit, draw the diffraction geometry and label the path difference δ.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Psychoacoustics and Noise Criteria, Effects of Noise on Health, Annoyance Rating Schemes
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Psychoacoustics and Noise Criteria, Effects of Noise on Health, Annoyance Rating Schemes

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Psychoacoustics — The Bridge Between Sound and Perception

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Psychoacoustics** is the scientific study of the psychological and physiological responses associated with sound, including the perception of loudness, pitch, timbre, duration, and tonal quality. It investigates how the human auditory system interprets, processes, and emotionally responds to acoustic stimuli rather than treating sound as a pure physical phenomenon.

In environmental and noise-control engineering, psychoacoustics is critical because human beings do not respond to raw sound pressure (measured in Pascals) — they respond to **perceived** sound. Two distinct sounds having identical physical intensity may be perceived very differently depending on their **frequency content, duration, spectral shape, and temporal pattern**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Key Insight for KTU Exam:** The single most important principle in noise engineering is that the **human ear is not a microphone**. It does not respond equally to all frequencies, which is why A-weighting, equal-loudness contours, and phon/sone scales exist.

#### 1.1.1 Real-World Analogy

Imagine two sound sources in your room — a heavy bass speaker thumping at 50 Hz and a baby crying at 4000 Hz. The bass may have a sound pressure level of **80 dB**, while the baby's cry may have only **40 dB**, yet the baby is far more **annoying and attention-grabbing**. Why? Because the human ear is biologically tuned (for survival) to mid-high frequencies where human speech and alarm signals occur. Psychoacoustics quantifies exactly this sensitivity difference.

#### 1.1.2 Fundamental Psychoacoustic Attributes

| Attribute | Physical Correlate | Psychoacoustic Scale | Key Unit |
|-----------|-------------------|---------------------|----------|
| **Loudness** | Sound Intensity | Sone / Phon | sone |
| **Pitch** | Frequency | Mel / Bark | mel |
| **Timbre** | Spectral Content | (Qualitative) | — |
| **Duration** | Time | (Temporal integration) | s |

---

### 1.2 Noise Criteria — Engineering the Perception

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Noise Criteria (NC)** are a set of standardized numerical rating curves (NC-15, NC-20, NC-25 ... NC-70) developed to evaluate background noise in indoor spaces, especially for HVAC systems, offices, hospitals, and auditoriums. They specify the maximum permissible octave-band sound pressure levels across the audio spectrum (63 Hz to 8000 Hz).

The most commonly used criteria in KTU syllabus are:

- **A-Weighted Decibel (dBA)** — frequency-weighted single number for general noise
- **Sound Level Meter (SLM)** readings
- **Equivalent Continuous Sound Level (Leq)**
- **Noise Rating (NR) curves** — ISO/R 1996 standard
- **Noise Criterion (NC) curves** — ASHRAE / ANSI S12.2
- **Preferred Noise Criterion (PNC)** — refined NC curves
- **Speech Interference Level (SIL)** — designed for speech intelligibility
- **Day-Night Average Sound Level (Ldn or DNL)** — community noise

> [!NOTE]  
> **A-weighting (dBA):** A standardized frequency-weighting that mimics the response of the human ear at moderate sound levels (≈40 phon). It de-emphasizes very low (≤500 Hz) and very high frequencies (≥10 kHz). A-weighting is the **gold standard** in noise regulations worldwide, including the **Indian CPCB/EPA Noise Rules 2000**.

---

### 1.3 Effects of Noise on Human Health — The Silent Hazard

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Noise Pollution** is classified by the World Health Organization (WHO) as the second-largest environmental cause of health problems after air pollution. Chronic exposure to elevated noise levels causes both **auditory** (hearing-related) and **non-auditory** (physiological/psychological) health effects.

**Auditory Effects:**
- Temporary Threshold Shift (TTS) — reversible hearing loss
- Permanent Threshold Shift (PTS) — irreversible hearing damage
- Tinnitus (ringing in ears)
- Acoustic trauma and hyperacusis

**Non-Auditory Effects:**
- Hypertension and elevated blood pressure
- Cardiovascular diseases (ischemic heart disease, MI)
- Sleep disturbance and insomnia
- Increased stress hormones (cortisol, adrenaline)
- Cognitive impairment in children (reduced learning ability)
- Annoyance, irritability, reduced work efficiency
- Gastrointestinal disorders

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **WHO Recommended Limit (1999/2018):** Community noise should not exceed **70 dB(A)** Leq over 24 hours, and **55 dB(A)** during daytime to prevent significant health impacts. Night-time noise should ideally be below **40 dB(A)** to avoid sleep disturbance.

---

### 1.4 Annoyance Rating Schemes — Quantifying the Subjective

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Annoyance Rating Schemes** are standardized methods for quantifying community response to environmental noise based on objective measurements (Leq, Ldn, L10, L90, etc.) combined with statistical surveys of population response. They convert physical noise data into predicted percentage of "highly annoyed" residents.

Schemes covered in the KTU syllabus:

- **Schultz Curve** — relates Ldn to % Highly Annoyed (%HA)
- **NEF (Noise Exposure Forecast)** — FAA aircraft noise metric
- **CNEL (Community Noise Equivalent Level)** — California airport noise
- **NEM (Noise and Number Index)** — aircraft operations
- **Leq-based community response curves**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Human Auditory System — Why Weighting is Necessary

The human ear's response is non-linear with frequency. Pure tones at different frequencies must have **different physical intensities** to be perceived as equally loud. This is the foundation of the **Fletcher-Munson Equal-Loudness Contours** (1933), later standardized as ISO 226.

**Key Concepts:**

1. **Threshold of Hearing (TOH):** Minimum SPL detectable, ≈ 0 dB at 1 kHz for young healthy ears.
2. **Threshold of Pain:** ≈ 120–140 dB SPL.
3. **Phon:** Unit of loudness level. A sound has loudness level of *n* phon if it is judged as loud as a 1 kHz pure tone at *n* dB SPL.
4. **Sone:** Unit of perceived loudness. 1 sone = 40 phon. Doubling of sones = perceived doubling of loudness.

#### 2.1.1 The Phon–Sone Relationship (Stevens' Power Law)

The sone scale is **linear in perceived loudness** while the phon scale is logarithmic. The conversion:

$$S = 2^{(P - 40)/10}$$

where:
- $S$ = loudness in sones
- $P$ = loudness level in phons

If a sound is **twice as loud**, its sone value doubles. A 10-dB increase in SPL produces a perceived doubling of loudness (in sones) for moderate levels.

#### 2.1.2 Frequency Weighting Networks

| Weighting | Year | Application | Shape | Peak Response |
|-----------|------|-------------|-------|---------------|
| **A** | 1936 | General, environmental, occupational | Bell-shaped inverted U | ≈ 2–5 kHz |
| **B** | 1936 | Intermediate (rarely used today) | Broader | 1–2 kHz |
| **C** | 1936 | Peak/impact/very loud sounds | Nearly flat | 1 kHz |
| **D** | 1968 | Aircraft noise | Tilted | 1–4 kHz |
| **Z (Linear)** | Modern | True SPL measurement | Flat | All |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A-weighting represents the human ear response at low to moderate levels (~40 phon). C-weighting is used for **peak measurements** and **occupational exposure** at high levels. **D-weighting** is exclusively for **aircraft flyover noise**.

---

### 2.2 Noise Criteria Curves

#### 2.2.1 Noise Rating (NR) Curves — ISO/R 1996

The **NR (Noise Rating)** number is calculated by:

1. Measure octave-band SPL (63 Hz to 8 kHz).
2. Plot the spectrum on standard NR chart.
3. The **highest tangent curve** gives the NR value.

The NR number approximately correlates with dBA by:

$$\text{NR} \approx \text{dBA} - 5 \quad \text{(approx.)}$$

#### 2.2.2 Noise Criterion (NC) Curves — ANSI S12.2

Used for **indoor spaces, HVAC, buildings**. Similar procedure as NR but with curves defined in ANSI S12.2-2008. The **Preferred NC (PNC)** adds a low-frequency correction.

#### 2.2.3 Recommended NC Values

| Space Type | Recommended NC |
|------------|---------------|
| Concert halls | NC-15 to NC-20 |
| Hospitals (wards) | NC-25 to NC-30 |
| Classrooms | NC-25 to NC-30 |
| Offices | NC-30 to NC-35 |
| Restaurants | NC-35 to NC-40 |
| Lobbies, lobbies | NC-40 to NC-45 |
| Industrial areas | NC-50 to NC-65 |

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> **Mandatory Memorization List for KTU Exam** — Every formula below has appeared in KTU previous question papers or is a syllabus essential.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|----------|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | Sound Pressure Level | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | $p_0 = 20\ \mu\text{Pa}$ | dB |
| 2 | Sound Intensity Level | $L_I = 10 \log_{10}(I/I_0)$ | $I_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W/m}^2$ | dB |
| 3 | Sound Power Level | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | $W_0 = 10^{-12}\ \text{W}$ | dB |
| 4 | Addition of Sound Levels | $L_{total} = 10 \log_{10}(\sum 10^{L_i/10})$ | Combine incoherent sources | dB |
| 5 | Doubling Equal Sources | $\Delta L = 10 \log_{10}(n)$ | $n$ = number of equal sources | dB |
| 6 | Stevens' Sone Law | $S = 2^{(P-40)/10}$ | P in phons | sone |
| 7 | Phon from Sone | $P = 40 + 10 \log_2 S$ | Inverse relationship | phon |
| 8 | Equivalent Level | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\,dt\right)$ | Energy-averaged level | dB |
| 9 | Day-Night Level | $L_{dn} = 10 \log\!\left[\frac{1}{24}\!\left(\sum 15\,L_d + \sum 9\,L_n\right)\right]$ | Night adds 10 dB penalty | dB |
| 10 | Sound Exposure Level | $SEL = L_{eq} + 10 \log_{10}(t/1\,\text{s})$ | Single-event metric | dB |
| 11 | SIL | $SIL = (L_{500} + L_{1k} + L_{2k})/3$ | Avg of 500 Hz, 1k, 2k Hz octaves | dB |
| 12 | NEF | $NEF = L_{max} + 10 \log_2(N_{day} + 16.6 N_{night})$ | FAA aircraft noise metric | — |
| 13 | CNEL | $CNEL = 10\log\!\left[\frac{1}{24}\!\left(L_{d} + 10L_{e} + 9.5 L_n\right)\right]$ | California metric | dB |
| 14 | TTS Relationship | $TTS = a + b \log_{10}(t)$ | Temporary threshold shift | dB |
| 15 | %HA Schultz | $\%HA = 0.855 \cdot L_{dn} - 21.9$ | Approx. relationship | % |

---

### 2.4 Effects of Noise on Health — Detailed Mechanistic Breakdown

#### 2.4.1 Auditory Pathway Damage Mechanism

1. **Sound waves → Tympanic membrane vibration**
2. **Ossicle chain (malleus, incus, stapes) amplification** (~22×)
3. **Oval window → cochlear fluid wave**
4. **Basilar membrane deflection → hair cell stimulation**
5. **Stereocilia bending → electrochemical impulse via auditory nerve**

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL)** occurs when stereocilia are damaged or destroyed. These hair cells **do not regenerate** in mammals, making PTS **permanent**.

#### 2.4.2 OSHA Permissible Exposure Limits (PEL)

| Duration (hr/day) | Sound Level (dBA) |
|------------------|-------------------|
| 8 | **90** |
| 6 | 92 |
| 4 | 95 |
| 3 | 97 |
| 2 | 100 |
| 1.5 | 102 |
| 1 | 105 |
| 0.5 | 110 |

**Exchange rate:** 5 dB (OSHA) or 3 dB (NIOSH, EU).

---

### 2.5 Annoyance Rating Schemes — Engineering Application

#### 2.5.1 Schultz Curve (1968)

$$ \%HA = 0.855 \cdot L_{dn} - 21.9 \quad (\text{valid for } L_{dn} \in [55, 85]\ \text{dB}) $$

Where %HA = percentage of population "highly annoyed."

#### 2.5.2 NAC (Noise Abatement Criterion) — FAA FAR Part 36

$$NAC = L_{dn} + 10\log_{10}(N_{day} + 16.6\,N_{night}) - 49$$

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Stevens' Power Law (Phon ↔ Sone Conversion)
