The fundamental hypothesis of S. S. Stevens (1936): **perceived loudness doubles for every 10 dB increase in SPL at moderate listening levels**.

**Starting Point:**

A 1 kHz tone at **40 dB SPL** is defined as **1 sone** (loudness level = 40 phons).

**Step 1 — Define Reference Points:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{At } L_p = 40\ \text{dB SPL} \quad &\Rightarrow\quad S = 1\ \text{sone} \\
\text{At } L_p = 50\ \text{dB SPL} \quad &\Rightarrow\quad S = 2\ \text{sones} \\
\text{At } L_p = 60\ \text{dB SPL} \quad &\Rightarrow\quad S = 4\ \text{sones} \\
\vdots \quad &\Rightarrow\quad S = 2^n\ \text{sones when } L_p = 40 + 10n\ \text{dB SPL}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Express General Form:**

If $n = (L_p - 40)/10$ where $L_p$ is loudness level in phons, then:

$$S = 2^{n} = 2^{(L_p - 40)/10}$$

**Step 3 — Final Form (Stevens' Power Law for Loudness):**

$$
\boxed{\,S = 2^{(P - 40)/10}\,}
$$

**Step 4 — Inverse (Phon from Sone):**

$$P = 40 + 10 \log_2(S)$$

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- 1 sone ↔ 40 phon
- 2 sones ↔ 50 phon (twice as loud perception)
- 4 sones ↔ 60 phon
- 8 sones ↔ 70 phon
- 16 sones ↔ 80 phon

---

### 3.2 Worked Analytical Example — Leq Calculation

**Problem:** Sound level varies over a 4-hour measurement period as follows. Calculate $L_{eq}$.

| Time Interval | Duration (hr) | Sound Level (dBA) |
|---------------|--------------|------------------|
| 0–1 hr | 1 | 70 |
| 1–2 hr | 1 | 80 |
| 2–4 hr | 2 | 75 |

**Step 1 — Apply the Definition:**

$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\sum_{i=1}^{n} (10^{L_i/10}) \cdot t_i\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the Values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{eq} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{4}\bigl(10^{70/10}\cdot 1 + 10^{80/10}\cdot 1 + 10^{75/10}\cdot 2\bigr)\right] \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{4}\bigl(10^7 + 10^8 + 2\cdot 10^{7.5}\bigr)\right] \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{4}\bigl(1.0\times 10^7 + 1.0\times 10^8 + 6.3249\times 10^7\bigr)\right] \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{4}\bigl(1.7325\times 10^8\bigr)\right] \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[4.3312\times 10^7\right] \\[4pt]
L_{eq} &= 76.37\ \text{dBA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Result:** $L_{eq} \approx 76.4\ \text{dBA}$

> [!NOTE]  
> **Observation:** Note that $L_{eq}$ (76.4 dBA) is dominated by the 80 dBA hour even though it was the shortest duration. This demonstrates the **energetic dominance** principle — higher levels contribute disproportionately.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — Sone/Phon Conversion

**Problem:** A 1 kHz tone produces a measured SPL of 75 dB. Determine its loudness in sones.

**Step 1:** Since 1 kHz at any dB SPL has the same phon level (by definition — phon is anchored at 1 kHz):

$$P = L_p = 75\ \text{phons}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Stevens' Law:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S &= 2^{(75 - 40)/10} = 2^{35/10} = 2^{3.5} \\[4pt]
S &\approx 11.31\ \text{sones}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** This tone is perceived as **11.31 times as loud** as the reference 40 dB tone.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example — Schultz Curve

**Problem:** A neighborhood near a highway experiences $L_{dn} = 70\ \text{dBA}$. Predict the percentage of highly annoyed residents.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%HA &= 0.855 \cdot L_{dn} - 21.9 \\
&= 0.855 \times 70 - 21.9 \\
&= 59.85 - 21.9 \\
&= 37.95\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Engineering Implication:** Over **one-third of residents** would be classified as "highly annoyed" at 70 dBA Ldn, supporting the WHO community recommendation that Ldn should ideally remain below **55 dBA**.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Noise Metrics

| Metric | Type | Primary Use | Weighting | Time Average |
|--------|------|------------|-----------|--------------|
| **SPL (dB)** | Instantaneous | Physical measurement | None (linear) | None |
| **dBA** | Weighted instantaneous | General noise | A | None |
| **Leq** | Energy average | Continuous exposure | A | Yes |
| **Ldn** | Day-night average | Community noise | A | Yes (24 hr) |
| **CNEL** | Day-evening-night | California airport | A | Yes (24 hr) |
| **SEL** | Single-event | Aircraft flyover | A | Normalized to 1 s |
| **SIL** | Speech bands | Office/classroom | A | Octave average |
| **NC** | Octave-band rating | Indoor HVAC | Octave bands | None |
| **NR** | Octave-band rating | Indoor/Industrial | Octave bands | None |
| **NEF** | Number-weighted | Aircraft operations | A | Yes |
| **L10** | Statistical | Traffic noise | A | Yes |
| **L90** | Statistical | Background noise | A | Yes |

---

### 3.6 Detailed Health Effects — Dose-Response

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **KTU Long-Answer Framework:** Whenever asked about health effects, structure your answer in three layers: **(1) Auditory effects**, **(2) Non-auditory physiological**, **(3) Psychological/social**.

| Noise Level (dBA) | Effect |
|-------------------|--------|
| 0–20 | Below normal hearing threshold |
| 20–40 | Very quiet, sleep interference possible |
| 40–60 | Quiet office, mild annoyance begins |
| 60–70 | Conversational interference, stress onset |
| 70–85 | Cardiovascular effects begin, hearing damage (long-term) |
| 85–95 | Hearing damage (8-hr exposure, OSHA limit) |
| 95–110 | Rapid hearing damage, TTS within minutes |
| 110–130 | Pain threshold, immediate damage |
| >130 | Physical pain, instant hearing loss |

---

### 3.7 Annoyance Survey Methodology

The standard ICBEN (International Commission on Biological Effects of Noise) **5-point verbal scale**:

| Score | Verbal Response |
|-------|-----------------|
| 1 | Not at all annoyed |
| 2 | Slightly annoyed |
| 3 | Moderately annoyed |
| 4 | Very annoyed |
| 5 | Extremely annoyed |

%HA = (% of respondents giving 4 or 5)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Psychoacoustic Processing Block Diagram

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how physical sound is processed through the human auditory system and converted into psychoacoustic perception.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[Sound Source Physical Stimulus SPL in dB] --> node2[Outer Ear Pinna Filtering Directional Cue]
    node2 --> node3[Middle Ear Impedance Matching 22x Amplification]
    node3 --> node4[Inner Ear Cochlea Frequency Analysis]
    node4 --> node5[Basilar Membrane Place Theory Tonotopic Mapping]
    node5 --> node6[Hair Cell Transduction Mechanoelectric Conversion]
    node6 --> node7[Auditory Nerve Signal Encoding Rate and Place Coding]
    node7 --> node8[Cochlear Nucleus Initial Integration]
    node8 --> node9[Brainstem Superior Olivary Complex Binaural Processing]
    node9 --> node10[Auditory Cortex Higher Level Perception]
    node10 --> node11[Psychoacoustic Output Loudness in Sone]
    node10 --> node12[Psychoacoustic Output Pitch in Mel]
    node10 --> node13[Psychoacoustic Output Annoyance Rating]
    node10 --> node14[Psychoacoustic Output Timbre Recognition]
```

> [!NOTE]  
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The diagram shows that the SPL measured by an instrument at the source is **transformed** at every stage of auditory processing before reaching perception. This is precisely why **A-weighting alone is insufficient** for predicting community response — additional psychoacoustic metrics (sharpness, roughness, fluctuation strength) are used in advanced ISO 532-2 / 532-3 loudness models.

---

### 4.2 Noise Criteria Selection Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start Identify Application Area] --> nodeB{Indoor Space Type}
    nodeB -->|Residential| nodeC[Use NC or PNC curves Target NC 25 to 35]
    nodeB -->|Office| nodeD[Use NC 30 to 35 plus SIL for speech]
    nodeB -->|Hospital| nodeE[Use NC 25 to 30 Critical care requires lower]
    nodeB -->|Classroom| nodeF[Use NC 25 to 30 plus SIL check 45 dBA]
    nodeB -->|Industrial| nodeG[Use OSHA PEL 90 dBA 8 hr and NR curves]
    nodeB -->|Outdoor Community| nodeH[Use Leq Ldn CNEL plus Schultz curve]
    nodeC --> nodeZ[Plot Octave Band Spectrum Tangent Curve gives NC or NR value]
    nodeD --> nodeZ
    nodeE --> nodeZ
    nodeF --> nodeZ
    nodeG --> nodeZ
    nodeH --> nodeZ
    nodeZ --> nodeF2[Compare with Standard Recommend Control Measures]
```

---

### 4.3 Health Effects Hierarchy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Noise Exposure Source] --> nodeS2[Acute Exposure Short term High Level]
    nodeS1 --> nodeS3[Chronic Exposure Long term Moderate Level]
    nodeS2 --> nodeS4[Auditory Effects Tinnitus TTS Acoustic Trauma]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS5[Auditory Effects PTS NIHL]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS6[Non Auditory Physiological Hypertension CVD GI]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS7[Non Auditory Psychological Sleep Disturbance Annoyance Stress]
    nodeS3 --> nodeS8[Cognitive Effects Reduced Learning Memory Loss in Children]
    nodeS4 --> nodeH1[Medical Treatment Required]
    nodeS5 --> nodeH2[Permanent Disability]
    nodeS6 --> nodeH3[Chronic Disease Burden]
    nodeS7 --> nodeH4[Quality of Life Reduction]
    nodeS8 --> nodeH5[Educational Performance Decline]
```

---

### 4.4 Annoyance Rating Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeQ1[Step 1 Measure A weighted Leq over 24 hour] --> nodeQ2[Step 2 Compute Ldn with 10 dB Night Penalty]
    nodeQ2 --> nodeQ3[Step 3 Conduct ICBEN 5 point Survey]
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ4[Step 4 Compute percent Highly Annoyed]
    nodeQ4 --> nodeQ5[Step 5 Compare with Schultz Curve Predicted Value]
    nodeQ5 --> nodeQ6{Discrepancy Greater than 15 percent}
    nodeQ6 -->|Yes| nodeQ7[Investigate Tonal Impulsive or Intermittent Characteristics]
    nodeQ6 -->|No| nodeQ8[Model Validated Apply for Compliance]
    nodeQ7 --> nodeQ9[Apply Penalty 5 dB for Tonal or Impulsiveness]
    nodeQ9 --> nodeQ8
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Q1. (3 Marks)**  
**Define the term *Phon* and *Sone*. How are they related mathematically?**

**Model Answer:**

> The **Phon** is the unit of *loudness level* on a logarithmic scale. A sound has a loudness level of $n$ phons if a normal listener judges it to be as loud as a **1 kHz pure tone at $n$ dB SPL**.
>
> The **Sone** is the unit of *perceived loudness* on a linear scale. By definition, **1 sone = 40 phons**.
>
> The mathematical relationship (Stevens' Power Law) is:
>
> $$S = 2^{(P - 40)/10}$$
>
> where $S$ = sone, $P$ = phon.
>
> *Inverse form:* $P = 40 + 10 \log_2(S)$.
>
> A doubling of sones corresponds to a 10-phon increase — perceived doubling of loudness.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. (3 Marks)**  
**What is A-weighting? Why is it preferred over linear (unweighted) SPL measurement for environmental noise assessment?**

**Model Answer:**

> **A-weighting** is a standardized frequency-response curve defined in IEC 61672 that adjusts measured SPL across the audio spectrum to approximate the **human ear's sensitivity at moderate listening levels (≈40 phon)**.
>
> The A-weighting curve **de-emphasizes very low frequencies (<500 Hz)** and **very high frequencies (>10 kHz)** while giving most weight to the 1–5 kHz region where human hearing and speech intelligibility peak.
>
> **Why preferred for environmental noise:**
> 1. It correlates better with **human annoyance and hearing damage risk**.
> 2. Most national and international regulations (CPCB, OSHA, WHO, EPA) are framed in dBA.
> 3. C-weighting, used for peak measurements, approximates linear response at high levels.
> 4. Single-number descriptor (dBA) simplifies communication and compliance checking.
>
> The unweighted SPL does not account for frequency perception and may overestimate or underestimate the actual human impact.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 / ESE Module-4 Internal Choice Pattern]**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** *Explain the difference between **equal-loudness contours (Fletcher-Munson)** and **A-weighting**. Discuss their limitations in modern noise assessment. State the **Stevens' Power Law** and derive the sone-phon relationship. **(7 Marks)***

**(b)** *A workshop has the following octave-band SPL readings. Determine the **Noise Rating (NR)** number and the **Noise Criterion (NC)** number. Comment on the suitability for an office environment. **(7 Marks)***

| Octave Band (Hz) | 63 | 125 | 250 | 500 | 1k | 2k | 4k | 8k |
|------------------|-----|-----|-----|-----|----|----|----|----|
| SPL (dB) | 88 | 84 | 80 | 76 | 73 | 70 | 68 | 65 |

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Equal-Loudness Contours vs. A-Weighting:**

> **Equal-Loudness Contours** (Fletcher & Munson, 1933; refined as ISO 226:2003) are sets of curves showing the SPL required at each frequency to be perceived as **equally loud** as a 1 kHz reference tone. They are defined for multiple phon levels (10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100).
>
> **A-weighting** is a single, fixed weighting curve based on the **40-phon equal-loudness contour**, inverted (because attenuation is subtracted).

| Feature | Equal-Loudness Contours | A-Weighting |
|---------|------------------------|-------------|
| **Number of curves** | Many (one per phon level) | Single curve |
| **Based on** | 1 kHz reference at multiple SPLs | Inverted 40-phon contour |
| **Output** | Loudness in phon (level) | Single dBA number |
| **Modern relevance** | Accurate for any loudness | Approximation only |
| **Limitation** | Cannot be read directly on SLM | Fixed shape, ignores spectral cues |

**Limitations:**
- A-weighting assumes the **40-phon contour**, which doesn't apply at very low or very high SPLs.
- It cannot distinguish tonal, impulsive, or intermittent noises — all of which are more annoying than broadband noise at equal dBA.
- Modern standards (ISO 532-1, 532-2, 532-3) use **Zwicker loudness** and **Moore-Glasberg model** for accurate psychoacoustic assessment.

**Stevens' Power Law — Derivation:**

The law is empirical, derived by **S. S. Stevens (1936)** from magnitude estimation experiments. He observed that perceived loudness grows as a **power law** of stimulus intensity:

$$\psi = k \cdot (\phi - \phi_0)^n$$

For 1 kHz tones, $n \approx 0.6$. By convention:

$$S = 2^{(P - 40)/10}$$

where $P$ = phon level, $S$ = sone.

For example:
- $P = 40$ phon → $S = 2^0 = 1$ sone
- $P = 50$ phon → $S = 2^1 = 2$ sones (twice as loud)
- $P = 70$ phon → $S = 2^3 = 8$ sones

---

**Part (b) — NR/NC Determination:**

**Procedure:**
1. Plot the spectrum on the standard NR chart (63 Hz → 8 kHz).
2. Find the highest tangent NR/NC curve.
3. The tangent curve index = the NR/NC value.

**Plotting the data (approx. visual interpretation):**

The spectrum **decreases monotonically** with frequency. The **highest octave-band level is 88 dB at 63 Hz**, so the **tangent** is closest at the low-frequency end.

By reading from the standard NR curves:
- **NR number ≈ 80** (the 63 Hz tangent is on the NR-80 curve).

**NC Number:**
- ANSI S12.2 NC curves are slightly different. The 63 Hz, 88 dB level is on or near **NC-75**.

**Comment on Suitability:**
- **Recommended NC for offices: NC-30 to NC-35.**
- **Calculated NC = 75 → far exceeds acceptable.**
- The workshop is **completely unsuitable** for office use without extensive noise control measures (vibration isolation, enclosure, sound-absorbing treatment).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** *Define **Leq**, **Ldn**, **CNEL**, and **SEL**. Show the mathematical relationship among them. A factory operates 12 hours/day with the following sound level profile. Calculate **Leq,12hr** and **Ldn** assuming nighttime is silent. **(7 Marks)***

| Time | Hours | dBA |
|------|-------|-----|
| 06:00–10:00 | 4 | 85 |
| 10:00–14:00 | 4 | 90 |
| 14:00–18:00 | 4 | 80 |

**(b)** *Discuss in detail the **auditory and non-auditory health effects** of noise pollution. State the **WHO recommended limits** and the **OSHA permissible exposure limits**. What is the **Schultz Curve**, and how is it used in community noise assessment? **(7 Marks)***

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Definitions:**

- **$L_{eq}$ (Equivalent Continuous Sound Level):** The constant SPL which, over the same time interval, delivers the same total acoustic energy as the actual time-varying sound.

$$L_{eq,T} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\,dt\right)$$

- **$L_{dn}$ (Day-Night Average Sound Level):** 24-hour $L_{eq}$ with a **10-dB penalty added to nighttime levels (22:00–07:00)** to reflect increased sleep disturbance.

$$L_{dn} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{24}\!\left(\sum_{\text{day}} 10^{L_d/10} \cdot t_d + \sum_{\text{night}} 10^{(L_n+10)/10} \cdot t_n\right)\right]$$

- **CNEL (Community Noise Equivalent Level):** Similar to $L_{dn}$ but with a **3-dB evening penalty (19:00–22:00)** and **10-dB night penalty (22:00–07:00)**. Used primarily in California (USA).

$$CNEL = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{24}\!\left( L_d + 10 L_e + 9.5 L_n \right)_{\text{energy sums}}\right]$$

- **SEL (Sound Exposure Level):** Total acoustic energy of a single event normalized to **1 second**.

$$SEL = L_{eq} + 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{t}{1\ \text{s}}\right)$$

**Mathematical Relationship:**

$$SEL = L_{eq, T} + 10 \log_{10}(T)$$

where $T$ is the event duration in seconds. $L_{dn}$ and CNEL are special weighted $L_{eq}$ values over 24 hours.

**Numerical Calculation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{eq,12} &= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{12}\bigl(4\cdot 10^{8.5} + 4\cdot 10^{9.0} + 4\cdot 10^{8.0}\bigr)\right] \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{12}\bigl(4\cdot 3.162\times 10^8 + 4\cdot 1.0\times 10^9 + 4\cdot 1.0\times 10^8\bigr)\right] \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{12}\bigl(1.265\times 10^9 + 4.0\times 10^9 + 4.0\times 10^8\bigr)\right] \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{12}\bigl(5.665\times 10^9\bigr)\right] \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}(4.721\times 10^8) \\[4pt]
L_{eq,12} &\approx 86.74\ \text{dBA}
\end{aligned}
$$

**For $L_{dn}$ (assuming 12 hr night = 0 dB → 12 hr day = factory operation):**

$$L_{dn} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{1}{24}\bigl(12\cdot 10^{8.674} + 12\cdot 10^{-10/10}\bigr)\right]$$

Since night is silent, night contribution is negligible.

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_{dn} &\approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left[\frac{12}{24}\cdot 10^{8.674}\right] \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}(0.5 \cdot 4.721\times 10^8) \\[4pt]
&= 10 \log_{10}(2.361 \times 10^8) \\[4pt]
L_{dn} &\approx 83.73\ \text{dBA}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Part (b) — Health Effects & Limits:**

**Auditory Effects:**

1. **Temporary Threshold Shift (TTS):** Reversible hearing loss after short exposure; recovers within hours/days.
2. **Permanent Threshold Shift (PTS):** Irreversible NIHL (Noise-Induced Hearing Loss) after prolonged exposure above 85 dBA.
3. **Tinnitus:** Ringing/buzzing in ears.
4. **Acoustic Trauma:** Sudden damage from impulse noise (>140 dB).

**Non-Auditory Effects:**

| Category | Specific Effects |
|----------|------------------|
| **Cardiovascular** | Hypertension, ischemic heart disease, MI risk ↑ |
| **Endocrine** | Elevated cortisol, adrenaline |
| **Sleep** | Difficulty falling asleep, fragmented sleep, reduced REM |
| **Cognitive (children)** | Impaired reading, memory, attention |
| **Psychological** | Annoyance, irritability, anxiety, depression |
| **Gastrointestinal** | Ulcers, appetite loss |
| **Maternal/Fetal** | Low birth weight, premature birth |

**WHO Recommended Limits (2018):**

| Environment | Recommended $L_{eq}$ (dBA) |
|-------------|---------------------------|
| Outdoor living areas | 55 (daytime) |
| Indoor dwellings | 35 (bedroom, night) |
| School classrooms | 35 (during teaching) |
| Industrial/commercial | 70 (8-hr limit) |

**OSHA PEL:** 90 dBA for 8-hour day with **5-dB exchange rate** (90, 95, 100, 105, 110, 115).

**NIOSH Recommended:** 85 dBA for 8-hour day with **3-dB exchange rate**.

**Schultz Curve:**

Developed by **T. J. Schultz (1978)** based on 161 social surveys worldwide correlating $L_{dn}$ to community annoyance:

$$\%HA = 0.855 \cdot L_{dn} - 21.9$$

**Application in Community Assessment:**

1. Measure $L_{dn}$ over 24 hours.
2. Apply Schultz curve to predict %HA.
3. Conduct ICBEN 5-point verbal survey to validate.
4. If measured %HA exceeds predicted by >15%, investigate tonal/impulsive/intermittent characteristics.
5. Apply 5-dB penalty for impulsive/tonal noise (ISO 1996).

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students commonly lose marks:**
> 1. **Confusing phon and sone:** Phon is a logarithmic *level*; sone is a linear *magnitude*. Reversing them in the formula costs full marks.
> 2. **Forgetting the 10-dB night penalty** in $L_{dn}$ calculations — most common arithmetic error.
> 3. **Using linear SPL addition** instead of logarithmic. Two 80 dB sources ≠ 160 dB; they = 83 dB.
> 4. **Not clarifying dB vs dBA:** Always state whether the value is A-weighted in health-effect answers.
> 5. **Plotting NR/NC incorrectly:** Forgetting to take the *highest tangent*, not the lowest. NC curves are read as maximum tangent.
> 6. **Mixing up Leq with Lmax:** Leq is energy average; Lmax is instantaneous peak.
> 7. **Omitting the 3-dB exchange rate** (NIOSH) vs **5-dB exchange rate** (OSHA) in occupational health questions.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist**

#### **Psychoacoustics**
- ☐ Psychoacoustics = study of psychological response to sound (perception, not just physics).
- ☐ Ear's response is **non-linear in frequency** → need for weighting.
- ☐ **Phon** = logarithmic loudness *level* (anchored at 1 kHz). **Sone** = linear *magnitude*.
- ☐ Reference: **1 sone = 40 phons**.
- ☐ Stevens' Power Law: $S = 2^{(P - 40)/10}$.
- ☐ Fletcher-Munson equal-loudness contours define the basis for A-weighting (40-phon).
- ☐ **A-weighting** for general use; **C-weighting** for peaks; **D-weighting** for aircraft.

#### **Noise Criteria**
- ☐ **NR (Noise Rating)** — ISO 1996 — octave-band tangent method.
- ☐ **NC (Noise Criterion)** — ANSI S12.2 — octave-band tangent method.
- ☐ **PNC (Preferred NC)** — refined NC with low-frequency adjustment.
- ☐ **SIL (Speech Interference Level)** = average of 500, 1k, 2k Hz octave bands.
- ☐ Offices: NC 30–35; Hospitals: NC 25–30; Concert halls: NC 15–20.

#### **Health Effects**
- ☐ Auditory: TTS (reversible), PTS (permanent), tinnitus, NIHL.
- ☐ Non-auditory: hypertension, CVD, sleep disturbance, cognitive decline in children.
- ☐ WHO limit: 70 dBA Leq (24h); 55 dBA daytime; 40 dBA nighttime.
- ☐ OSHA PEL: 90 dBA (8 hr, 5-dB exchange rate).
- ☐ NIOSH: 85 dBA (8 hr, 3-dB exchange rate).

#### **Annoyance Schemes**
- ☐ **Leq** = energy-equivalent continuous level.
- ☐ **Ldn** = 24-hr Leq with **10-dB night penalty**.
- ☐ **CNEL** = Ldn with **3-dB evening + 10-dB night penalty**.
- ☐ **SEL** = single-event level normalized to 1 s.
- ☐ **Schultz Curve:** $\%HA = 0.855 \cdot L_{dn} - 21.9$.
- ☐ **NEF** = FAA aircraft noise metric.
- ☐ **NAC** = FAA FAR Part 36 noise abatement criterion.
- ☐ ICBEN 5-point scale: 1=Not annoyed ... 5=Extremely annoyed; %HA = % of (4+5).

#### **Numerical Traps to Avoid**
- ☐ Doubling equal sources → **+3 dB**, not +6 dB.
- ☐ Halving distance from point source → **+6 dB**.
- ☐ Ten equal sources → **+10 dB**.
- ☐ Ten times intensity → **+10 dB**.
- ☐ Ten times pressure → **+20 dB**.
- ☐ A-weighting decreases low-frequency contribution → low-frequency noise gets dBA penalty.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to the Acoustic Spectrum Beyond Audible Sound

Human hearing is conventionally confined to the frequency band of **20 Hz to 20,000 Hz (20 kHz)**. However, the physical world generates significant acoustic energy both below and above this audible window. The KTU 2024 syllabus categorizes these non-conventional acoustic phenomena under **Special Noise Environments**, which include **Infrasound**, **Ultrasound**, **Impulsive Sound**, and **Sonic Boom**. From an environmental engineering perspective, these are not merely "sounds humans cannot hear" — they are often more dangerous, more penetrating, and more physiologically disruptive than ordinary environmental noise.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PECET756 Module 4):**
> The board examiner frequently tests the ability of students to **classify noise by frequency band**, identify the **physical source** of each special noise, and recognize its **physiological/psychological effect** on human receptors. Memorize the frequency thresholds — they are high-yield.

## 1.2 Formal Definitions (KTU Standardized Terminology)

### A. Infrasound
**Infrasound** is defined as acoustic energy propagating at frequencies **below 20 Hz**, i.e., the sub-audible portion of the acoustic spectrum. The lower practical limit is typically **0.001 Hz (1 mHz)**, although most environmental infrasound lies in the **0.1 Hz to 20 Hz** range.

- **Standard Metric:** Sound Pressure Level (SPL) in **dB** (unweighted, or G-weighted for infrasound specifically).
- **Reference Pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Think of infrasound as the **"bass you feel but don't hear"** at a rock concert — the chest-thump. Infrasound is even lower than that. It is the rumble of a distant earthquake, the hum inside a moving car, the vibration you feel when a heavy truck passes. Because of its extremely long wavelength, it bends around buildings and travels thousands of kilometers with negligible attenuation.

### B. Ultrasound
**Ultrasound** is acoustic energy at frequencies **above 20 kHz**, extending up to several **GHz** in diagnostic/industrial applications. Environmental ultrasound of concern lies typically in the **20 kHz to 100 kHz** band, produced by high-speed machinery, jet engines, and some industrial cleaning devices.

- **Standard Metric:** SPL in dB; in air, attenuation is severe (proportional to $f^2$).
- **Reference Pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Ultrasound is **"the dog whistle effect"** — your dog perks up, but you hear nothing. In industry, ultrasonic cleaners, welders, and high-speed dental drills emit it. Bats and dolphins use it to "see" their world (echolocation). Because air absorbs high frequencies rapidly, ultrasound rarely travels far — but close-up exposure can cause heating, cavitation, and hearing damage.

### C. Impulsive Sound (Impulse Noise)
**Impulsive Sound** is a transient acoustic event characterized by a **rapid rise time** (typically **< 35 milliseconds**), a short **duration** (usually **< 500 ms**), and a **sudden onset** with a peak Sound Pressure Level typically **15 dB or more** above the background. It is non-stationary, broadband, and often contains a high shock component.

- **Standard Metric:** **Impulse Sound Pressure Level (ISPL)** measured with a sound level meter set to **Impulse Time Weighting ("I")** response.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Impulse noise is the **"SLAP"** of sound. Think of a hammer striking a nail, a gunshot, a punch press stamping sheet metal, or a firecracker exploding. Your hearing has no time to adapt (the acoustic reflex takes ~150 ms to engage), so a single impulsive event can cause instantaneous and permanent cochlear damage.

### D. Sonic Boom
A **Sonic Boom** is the impulsive acoustic shock wave generated when an object travels through air at a speed **greater than the local speed of sound** (i.e., **Mach number $M > 1$**). It manifests as a sharp **N-shaped pressure pulse** that propagates as a conical wavefront (the **Mach cone**) from the supersonic object.

- **Standard Metric:** **Overpressure $\Delta p$** in **Pa**, or **Peak Sound Pressure Level** in dB.
- **Reference Pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine dragging a boat's prow through still water at high speed — a V-shaped wake forms behind the boat. Now imagine the same thing happening with a supersonic jet in air. The "wake" is a cone of compressed air called the **Mach cone**, and where this cone intersects the ground, listeners hear a sudden **"BOOM"** — the sonic boom. The Concorde, fighter jets, and the Space Shuttle during reentry all produce sonic booms.

## 1.3 Summary Classification Matrix

| Noise Type | Frequency / Property | Typical Source | Primary Health/Environmental Effect |
|------------|---------------------|----------------|------------------------------------|
| **Infrasound** | $f < 20$ Hz | Earthquakes, volcanoes, heavy machinery, wind turbines | Nausea, disorientation, "infrasound sickness" |
| **Ultrasound** | $f > 20$ kHz | Ultrasonic cleaners, jet engines, industrial welders | Localized heating, hearing damage at close range |
| **Impulsive Sound** | Transient, broad-spectrum | Gunfire, pile drivers, explosions, forging | Instantaneous NIHL (Noise-Induced Hearing Loss) |
| **Sonic Boom** | Shock wave ($M > 1$) | Supersonic aircraft, re-entry vehicles | Startle, structural damage to windows, annoyance |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick — "I U I S":** **I**nfrasound, **U**ltrasound, **I**mpulsive, **S**onic — remember the order is **"Low → High → Transient → Shock."** Examiners love asking you to place them on a frequency/time-domain chart.

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Infrasound — Deep Analysis

### 2.1.1 Physical Characteristics
Infrasound has wavelengths of **$\lambda = c/f$** that can exceed **17 meters** (at 20 Hz) and reach **kilometers** at sub-Hz frequencies. Because the wavelength is much larger than typical building dimensions and obstacles, **diffraction is dominant** — infrasound does not "shadow" behind walls; it flows around them. Atmospheric absorption is also **negligible** at low frequencies, enabling propagation over **thousands of kilometers** with very little loss.

### 2.1.2 Sources of Environmental Infrasound
1. **Natural:** Earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, avalanches, severe weather (tornadoes), ocean waves ("microbaroms" at ~0.2 Hz).
2. **Anthropogenic:** Heavy industrial machinery (compressors, furnaces), large diesel engines, wind turbines (0.5–10 Hz), high-speed trains, HVAC systems.

### 2.1.3 Physiological Effects (Why It Matters)
The human vestibular system and inner ear can detect infrasound at high amplitudes, causing:
- **Nausea, dizziness, and motion sickness** (even when stationary).
- **Visual disturbance** (because the eyeball resonates near ~7 Hz).
- **Sleep disruption** — wind turbine infrasound is a heavily debated topic.
- **"Infrasound sickness"** (headache, fatigue, concentration loss) reported at sustained levels **> 120 dB (G-weighted)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** Because diffraction dominates, **passive enclosure** (e.g., soundproofing walls) is **ineffective** against infrasound. Mitigation requires **active anti-noise systems**, **vibration isolation** of structures, or **damping the source** mechanically.

## 2.2 Ultrasound — Deep Analysis

### 2.2.1 Physical Characteristics
Ultrasound has wavelengths on the order of **centimeters to millimeters** in air. Unlike infrasound, air absorption is **severe** and scales as $\alpha \propto f^2$. Atmospheric attenuation at 20 kHz is roughly **2–3 dB/m**; at 100 kHz it exceeds **40 dB/m**. Therefore, environmental ultrasound is essentially a **near-field, occupational hazard** rather than a community issue.

### 2.2.2 Sources of Environmental Ultrasound
1. **Industrial:** Ultrasonic welders, cleaners, drills, homogenizers.
2. **Aero-acoustic:** Jet engine turbine stages, high-speed reheat exhaust.
3. **Medical:** Diagnostic imaging (not environmental, but related).

### 2.2.3 Effects
At high SPL, ultrasound in air causes **heating of tissue, parametric acoustic arrays** (non-linear generation of audible difference tones), and **localized discomfort**. Occupational exposure limits are set by agencies such as **ACGIH** and **OSHA** (typically 110 dB at 20 kHz, decreasing as frequency rises).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Note:** Ultrasound is the *basis* of non-destructive testing (NDT), medical sonography, and SONAR. From an air-pollution-engineering perspective, it is generally an **occupational noise** concern, not a community noise concern.

## 2.3 Impulsive Sound — Deep Analysis

### 2.3.1 Defining Features
The **ISO Standard 10843** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.95** define an impulse/impulsive noise by these criteria:
1. **Rise Time** $\leq 35$ ms.
2. **Duration** $\leq 500$ ms.
3. **Peak SPL** exceeds background by $\geq 15$ dB.

### 2.3.2 Measurement — Impulse Time Weighting
A standard Sound Level Meter (SLM) set to **"I" (Impulse) time constant** has a much faster attack (35 ms) and slower decay (1.5 s) compared to the "Fast" (125 ms / 1.5 s) and "Slow" (1 s / 1.5 s) weightings. This captures the **true peak** of short transients.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Impulse Weighting Matters:** A genuine peak of 140 dB lasting only 5 ms would be **underestimated by 10–15 dB** under "Fast" or "Slow" weightings, because the meter does not respond fast enough. KTU examiners love to ask: *"Why is the impulse time constant used for gunshots?"* — Answer: because the human ear experiences the **peak instantaneous pressure**, not the time-averaged pressure.

### 2.3.3 Equivalent Energy Metrics
For cumulative damage assessment, the **Equivalent Continuous SPL** ($L_{eq}$) is sometimes used with appropriate integration over the impulsive event window, but for hearing-conservation purposes, the **peak unweighted SPL** ($L_{peak}$) is the controlling metric.

## 2.4 Sonic Boom — Deep Analysis

### 2.4.1 The Mach Cone Geometry
When an object moves at speed $v > c$ (where $c \approx 343$ m/s in air at 20 °C), the disturbance created at any instant cannot outrun the object. Instead, all the spherical wavefronts emitted along the flight path **constructively interfere** along a conical surface — the **Mach cone**.

**Mach Number** is the dimensionless ratio:

$$M = \frac{v}{c}$$

The **half-angle** of the Mach cone (the angle between the flight direction and the cone surface) is:

$$\sin\mu = \frac{c}{v} = \frac{1}{M}$$

### 2.4.2 The N-Wave Pressure Signature
The ground-level pressure signature of a sonic boom is an **N-wave** — a sharp **compression** (overpressure $\Delta p_{+}$) followed by a sharp **rarefaction** (underpressure $\Delta p_{-}$), then return to ambient. The shape resembles a stylized letter **N** on an oscillograph.

### 2.4.3 Key Overpressure Formula (Simplified Whitham/Mach Stem Model)
For a slender supersonic aircraft at altitude $h$ with effective length $L$:

$$\Delta p \;\approx\; \frac{p_0}{\sqrt{2}} \left[ \frac{(\gamma-1)\,M^{2} + 2}{\gamma\,M} \right] \left(\frac{L}{h}\right)^{3/4} \left(M^{2}-1\right)^{1/8}$$

Where:
- $p_0$ = ambient atmospheric pressure ($\approx 101{,}325$ Pa at sea level)
- $\gamma$ = ratio of specific heats for air ($= 1.4$)
- $M$ = Mach number of aircraft
- $L$ = effective aircraft length (m)
- $h$ = flight altitude (m)

A more practical engineering approximation is the **Hayes overpressure formula**:

$$\frac{\Delta p}{p_0} \;\approx\; 0.395 \cdot \frac{M\,\sqrt{M^{2}-1} \cdot L^{3/4}}{h^{3/4}}$$

For a typical fighter aircraft ($L \approx 20$ m) flying at $M = 1.5$ and $h = 1000$ m, this yields $\Delta p \approx 50$–$80$ Pa — corresponding to a peak SPL of approximately **130–135 dB** at ground level.

### 2.4.4 Mitigation
Sonic booms cannot be "directed away" easily. Strategies include:
- **Speed restrictions** (subsonic cruise over land — e.g., FAA Rule above CONUS).
- **Flight path optimization** (over open ocean).
- **Aircraft shaping** (low-boom supersonic designs, e.g., NASA X-59 QueSST).

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wind farm siting (Infrasound):** Engineers must model low-frequency propagation to avoid residential annoyance.
- **Industrial hygiene (Ultrasound):** Audiometric testing and SPL monitoring for ultrasonic welder operators.
- **Hearing conservation (Impulse):** OSHA requires HPD (Hearing Protective Devices) for any peak above 140 dB.
- **Aircraft certification (Sonic boom):** FAA Part 91.817 prohibits civil supersonic flight over U.S. land — a direct regulatory consequence of boom overpressure.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Speed of Sound in an Ideal Gas (Foundation for Mach Number)

**Starting point:** The speed of sound in an ideal gas is derived from the adiabatic compressibility of the medium.

The adiabatic bulk modulus of an ideal gas is:

$$B_s = \gamma \, p$$

where $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ is the ratio of specific heats, and $p$ is the local absolute pressure.

The general acoustic wave speed in a fluid is given by:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{B_s}{\rho}}$$

For an ideal gas, the density is given by the ideal gas law:

$$p = \rho \, R \, T \quad \Rightarrow \quad \rho = \frac{p}{R T}$$

Substituting $B_s = \gamma p$ and $\rho = p/(RT)$:

$$c = \sqrt{\frac{\gamma \, p}{p / (R T)}} = \sqrt{\gamma \, R \, T}$$

**Final result:**

$$c = \sqrt{\gamma \, R \, T}$$

**Engineering Note:** At $T = 293$ K (20 °C), with $\gamma = 1.4$ and $R = 287 \, \text{J/(kg·K)}$ for air:

$$c = \sqrt{1.4 \times 287 \times 293} = \sqrt{117{,}707} \approx 343 \text{ m/s}$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mach Cone Half-Angle

Consider a point source moving at constant supersonic speed $v > c$ along a straight path. At time $t=0$, the source emits a spherical pressure disturbance of radius $c \cdot t_0$. By time $t = t_0 + \Delta t$, the source has moved a distance $v \cdot \Delta t$, while the original wavefront has expanded to radius $c \cdot (t_0 + \Delta t)$.

The geometry of the wavefront envelope forms a right triangle:
- Hypotenuse = distance traveled by sound in time $\Delta t$ = $c \cdot \Delta t$
- Opposite side (along flight path) = distance traveled by source = $v \cdot \Delta t$

The half-angle $\mu$ of the Mach cone is given by:

$$\sin\mu = \frac{\text{opposite (wavefront radius)}}{\text{hypotenuse (source distance)}} = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{v \cdot \Delta t}$$

**Simplification:**

$$\sin\mu = \frac{c}{v} = \frac{1}{M}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{1}{M}\right)}$$

**Numerical example:** For $M = 2$ (twice the speed of sound):
$\mu = \arcsin(0.5) = 30°$ — the Mach cone is narrow and tight to the flight path.
For $M = 1.1$ (just above sonic):
$\mu = \arcsin(0.909) \approx 65.4°$ — the cone is very wide and "drags" nearly perpendicular to the flight path.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sonic Boom Overpressure (N-Wave, Hayes Approximation)

The full derivation from first principles requires solving the **nonlinear Euler equations** with shock discontinuities (Whitham's ray theory / Mach stem analysis). For KTU exam purposes, we present the **engineering approximation** attributed to **Wallace Hayes (1969, NASA)** for a slender body of revolution.

**Step 1 — Start with the slender-body approximation.**
For $M$ close to 1 (transonic), the pressure coefficient $C_p$ on a slender body of length $L$ at axial position $x$ is:

$$C_p(x) = \frac{2}{\sqrt{M^{2}-1}} \cdot \frac{1}{4\pi} \int_0^{L} \frac{S''(x') \, dx'}{(x-x')^{1/2}}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the far-field shock assumption.**
At the ground, the wavefront has steepened into a shock. The overpressure scales with the **rate of cross-sectional area change** $S'(x)$ and propagates according to ray-tube area expansion:

$$\Delta p \propto p_0 \cdot \left(\frac{L}{h}\right)^{3/4} \cdot f(M)$$

**Step 3 — Apply Whitham's result for the Mach number dependence.**
Whitham showed that the strength of the equivalent axisymmetric blast wave follows:

$$f(M) = M \sqrt{M^{2}-1}$$

**Step 4 — Calibrate against flight data (Hayes empirical constant).**
Combining the altitude scaling $(L/h)^{3/4}$ and a calibrated proportionality constant (≈ 0.395 from extensive flight tests):

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\Delta p}{p_0} \;\approx\; 0.395 \, M \sqrt{M^{2}-1} \left(\frac{L}{h}\right)^{3/4}\;}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Peak Sound Pressure Level.**
The peak SPL is defined as:

$$L_{p,\text{peak}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Delta p}{p_{0,\text{ref}}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{\Delta p}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) \quad \text{dB}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Worked Numerical Problem — Sonic Boom at Ground Level

**Given:**
- Aircraft: Fighter jet, $L = 20$ m
- Flight speed: $M = 1.5$
- Altitude: $h = 1000$ m
- Ambient pressure: $p_0 = 101{,}325$ Pa
- Reference pressure: $p_{0,\text{ref}} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the Mach number factor.**

$$M \sqrt{M^{2}-1} = 1.5 \times \sqrt{1.5^{2}-1} = 1.5 \times \sqrt{2.25-1} = 1.5 \times \sqrt{1.25} = 1.5 \times 1.1180 = 1.677$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the geometry factor.**

$$\left(\frac{L}{h}\right)^{3/4} = \left(\frac{20}{1000}\right)^{0.75} = (0.02)^{0.75}$$

Computing $(0.02)^{0.75}$:

$$\ln(0.02) = -3.912, \quad 0.75 \times (-3.912) = -2.934, \quad e^{-2.934} = 0.0532$$

**Step 3 — Compute the normalized overpressure.**

$$\frac{\Delta p}{p_0} = 0.395 \times 1.677 \times 0.0532 = 0.395 \times 0.0892 = 0.0352$$

**Step 4 — Compute the absolute overpressure.**

$$\Delta p = 0.0352 \times 101{,}325 = 3567 \text{ Pa} \approx 3.57 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to Peak SPL.**

$$L_{p,\text{peak}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3567}{20 \times 10^{-6}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(1.7835 \times 10^{8})$$

$$= 20 \times (8 + \log_{10}(1.7835)) = 20 \times (8 + 0.2512) = 20 \times 8.2512 = 165.0 \text{ dB}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner Note:** A value of **165 dB** is **well above the threshold of pain (130 dB)** and **above the structural-damage threshold (~140 dB)** for windows. This is why supersonic flight over populated land is banned in most countries.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Effects

| Parameter | Infrasound | Ultrasound | Impulsive | Sonic Boom |
|-----------|-----------|------------|-----------|------------|
| **Frequency / Type** | $< 20$ Hz | $> 20$ kHz | Transient, broad | Shock wave |
| **Wavelength** | $> 17$ m | $< 1.7$ cm | Mixed | Few m (shock front) |
| **Attenuation in Air** | Very low | Very high ($f^2$) | Geometric spreading | Cylindrical/spherical |
| **Shielding Feasibility** | Very poor (diffraction) | Good (absorption) | Moderate (enclosure) | Impossible (Mach cone) |
| **Primary Effect on Humans** | Vestibular, nausea | Thermal, hearing | Cochlear damage | Startle, structural |
| **Typical Peak SPL** | 90–130 dB | 110–140 dB (close) | 140–180 dB | 130–170 dB |
| **Regulatory Standard** | ISO 7196 (G-weighting) | ACGIH TLV | OSHA 1910.95 (140 dB peak) | FAA Part 91.817 |

## 3.6 Derivation 5: Wavelength for Infrasound and Ultrasound Limits

Using $\lambda = c/f$ with $c = 343$ m/s:

**Lower limit — Infrasound at 20 Hz:**

$$\lambda = \frac{343}{20} = 17.15 \text{ m}$$

**Lower limit — Infrasound at 0.1 Hz:**

$$\lambda = \frac{343}{0.1} = 3430 \text{ m} = 3.43 \text{ km}$$

**Upper limit — Ultrasound at 20 kHz:**

$$\lambda = \frac{343}{20{,}000} = 0.01715 \text{ m} = 1.715 \text{ cm}$$

**Upper limit — Ultrasound at 100 kHz:**

$$\lambda = \frac{343}{100{,}000} = 3.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m} = 3.43 \text{ mm}$$

This demonstrates the **five orders of magnitude** difference in wavelength between infrasound and ultrasound, which directly explains their vastly different propagation behaviors.

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1 — The Complete Acoustic Spectrum

The following flowchart maps the entire acoustic spectrum and the four special noise environments within it, including the principal sources and physiological effects.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Start(["Acoustic Spectrum"]) --> Low["f < 20 Hz: INFRASOUND"]
    Start --> Audible["20 Hz ≤ f ≤ 20 kHz: AUDIBLE SOUND"]
    Start --> High["f > 20 kHz: ULTRASOUND"]

    Low --> LowSrc["Sources: Earthquakes, Volcanoes,<br/>Wind Turbines, Heavy Machinery"]
    Low --> LowEff["Effects: Vestibular disturbance,<br/>Nausea, Disorientation"]
    Low --> LowCtrl["Control: Active anti-noise,<br/>Vibration isolation"]

    Audible --> AudSrc["Sources: Speech, Traffic,<br/>Industrial noise"]
    Audible --> AudEff["Effects: NIHL, Annoyance,<br/>Sleep loss"]
    Audible --> AudCtrl["Control: Enclosures, Barriers,<br/>Silencers, Hearing protection"]

    High --> HighSrc["Sources: Ultrasonic welders,<br/>Jet engines, Industrial cleaners"]
    High --> HighEff["Effects: Thermal heating,<br/>Cavitation, Localized hearing loss"]
    High --> HighCtrl["Control: Absorptive enclosures,<br/>Distance, PPE"]

    Low --- AudBoundary["Boundary: 20 Hz"]
    AudBoundary --- HighBoundary["Boundary: 20 kHz"]
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2 — Sonic Boom Generation Flow

This diagram shows the causal chain from supersonic flight to ground-level perception of a sonic boom.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Supersonic Aircraft<br/>v > c, M > 1"] --> B["Continuous Pressure Disturbances<br/>along flight path"]
    B --> C["Constructive Interference<br/>of Spherical Wavefronts"]
    C --> D["Formation of Mach Cone<br/>sin μ = 1/M"]
    D --> E["Conical Shock Front<br/>N-wave Pressure Pulse"]
    E --> F{"Cone Intersects<br/>Ground?"}
    F -->|Yes| G["Ground Observer Hears<br/>SONIC BOOM"]
    F -->|No| H["Boom Lost in Air<br/>(over open ocean)"]
    G --> I["Peak SPL: 130-170 dB<br/>Duration: ~300 ms"]
    I --> J["Effects: Startle, Window rattle,<br/>Structural damage if Δp > 140 dB"]
```

## 4.3 Diagram 3 — N-Wave Pressure Signature and Mach Cone Geometry

This block-level diagram illustrates the geometric relationship between aircraft motion, Mach cone, and the resulting N-wave as seen by a ground observer.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SG1["Mach Cone Geometry (Side View)"]
        P1["Aircraft position at t = t1"] -.-> Q1["Earlier wavefront radius = c·t1"]
        P2["Aircraft position at t = t2"] -.-> Q2["Later wavefront radius = c·t2"]
        P1 --> P2
        Q1 --> Q2

    end

    subgraph SG2["Ground-Level N-Wave (Pressure vs. Time)"]
        N1["t = 0: Ambient pressure p0"]
        N2["Sharp RISE: Overpressure Δp+"]
        N3["Linear DECAY: Rarefaction Δp-"]
        N4["t = τ: Return to ambient p0"]
        N1 --> N2 --> N3 --> N4
    end

    SG1 -- "Cone intersects ground" --> SG2
```

## 4.4 Diagram 4 — Impulse Noise Detection & Acoustic Reflex Timeline

This timeline diagram explains *why* impulsive noise is uniquely dangerous — the human acoustic reflex engages **after** the impulse has already damaged the cochlea.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T0["t = 0 ms<br/>Impulse Event Onset"] --> T1["t = 0-35 ms<br/>Rapid Pressure Rise"]
    T1 --> T2["t = 35 ms<br/>Peak SPL Reached<br/>(METER: Impulse 'I' weighting)"]
    T2 --> T3["t ~ 150 ms<br/>Acoustic Reflex ENGAGES<br/>(Stapedius muscle contracts)"]
    T3 --> T4["t > 150 ms<br/>Ear Now Partially Protected"]
    T2 --> Damage["Cochlear Damage Already Possible<br/>above 140 dB peak"]
    T0 --> Recovery["Hearing Recovery<br/>hours to permanent loss"]
```

```mermaid
    H3 --> H3b["Ultrasound: Distance, PPE for workers"]
    H3 --> H3c["Impulse: Double ear protection (plugs + muffs)"]
    H3 --> H3d["Sonic Boom: Public awareness, building codes"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**"Define infrasound and ultrasound. Mention two natural and two man-made sources of each."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Infrasound** is acoustic energy at frequencies **below 20 Hz**, which lies below the audible threshold of human hearing. (1 mark)
- **Ultrasound** is acoustic energy at frequencies **above 20 kHz**, above the human audible range. (1 mark)
- **Infrasound sources — Natural:** Earthquakes, volcanic eruptions, ocean waves (microbaroms). **Man-made:** Wind turbines, heavy compressors, diesel engines. (0.5 mark)
- **Ultrasound sources — Natural:** Bat echolocation calls. **Man-made:** Ultrasonic welders, industrial cleaners, jet engine high-frequency components. (0.5 mark)

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**"What is impulsive noise? Why is impulse time weighting used in sound level meters to measure it?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Impulsive noise** is a transient acoustic event with rise time $\leq 35$ ms, duration $\leq 500$ ms, and peak SPL at least **15 dB above background**. Examples: gunshots, hammer blows, explosions. (1.5 marks)
- The **Impulse time weighting ("I")** has a very fast attack time constant (~35 ms) and a slow decay (~1.5 s), allowing the meter to capture the **true peak SPL** of a short transient. (1 mark)
- Under "Fast" or "Slow" weightings, the meter responds too slowly and **underestimates** the true peak by 10–15 dB, which is critical because the human ear experiences the **instantaneous peak pressure** for hearing-damage assessment. (0.5 mark)

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### **Question A — Full 14-Mark Alternative**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice Option A]**

**(a) (7 marks) Derive the expression for the Mach cone half-angle for a supersonic object. A military aircraft flies at Mach 2.0 at an altitude of 5000 m. Calculate the Mach cone half-angle and the lateral spread of the boom on the ground directly below the flight path.**

**(b) (7 marks) Explain the phenomenon of sonic boom with the aid of a neat sketch of the N-wave pressure signature. Discuss the engineering and regulatory measures used to mitigate sonic boom impact on populated areas.**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Derive the Mach cone half-angle (4 marks).**
Consider a point source moving at supersonic speed $v$ along a straight path. The wavefronts emitted at successive times $t_1, t_2, t_3, \ldots$ are spheres of radii $c \cdot t_1, c \cdot t_2, c \cdot t_3, \ldots$ At any instant, all previous wavefronts are tangent to a common conical surface — the Mach cone.

From the geometry, considering the source displacement $v \cdot \Delta t$ and the wavefront radius $c \cdot \Delta t$:

$$\sin\mu = \frac{c \cdot \Delta t}{v \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{c}{v}$$

Since Mach number $M = v/c$:

$$\sin\mu = \frac{1}{M} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mu = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{1}{M}\right) \tag{1}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the half-angle for $M = 2.0$ (1 mark).**

$$\mu = \arcsin\!\left(\frac{1}{2.0}\right) = \arcsin(0.5) = 30°$$

**Step 3 — Compute lateral spread on the ground (2 marks).**
The lateral distance $D$ on the ground from the point directly below the aircraft to the point where the Mach cone intersects the ground is given by:

$$D = h \cdot \tan\mu$$

where $h = 5000$ m is the altitude.

$$D = 5000 \times \tan(30°) = 5000 \times 0.5774 = 2886.75 \text{ m} \approx 2.89 \text{ km}$$

**Conclusion:** At $M = 2.0$ and $h = 5$ km, the Mach cone half-angle is **30°** and the lateral boom footprint extends to about **2.89 km** on either side of the ground track.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition of Sonic Boom (1 mark):**
A sonic boom is the impulsive acoustic shock wave generated when an object travels through air at a speed greater than the local speed of sound ($M > 1$). The wavefront forms a conical surface (Mach cone), and where it intersects the ground, observers perceive a sudden, sharp "boom."

**N-Wave Pressure Signature (2 marks):**
The ground-level pressure signature is a characteristic **N-wave**, consisting of:
- A near-instantaneous **rise** in pressure to a peak overpressure $\Delta p_{+}$ (the leading compression shock),
- A linear **decay** below ambient pressure to a rarefaction minimum $\Delta p_{-}$,
- A return to ambient pressure $p_0$.

The waveform resembles a stylized letter **"N"** on a pressure-vs-time plot. The total duration $\tau$ is typically 100–500 ms. The peak SPL is computed as $L_{p,\text{peak}} = 20 \log_{10}(\Delta p / p_{0,\text{ref}})$.

**Mitigation Measures (4 marks):**

| # | Measure | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | **Speed Restrictions** | Civil supersonic flight over land prohibited (e.g., FAA Part 91.817 in the U.S.); military jets use subsonic cruise when possible. |
| 2 | **Flight Path Planning** | Supersonic cruise conducted over open ocean, away from populated areas. |
| 3 | **Aircraft Shaping** | Low-boom designs (e.g., NASA X-59 QueSST) reduce the strength of the shock by spreading the pressure rise over a longer distance. |
| 4 | **Altitude Management** | Higher cruise altitude reduces ground overpressure (overpressure $\propto h^{-3/4}$). |
| 5 | **Building Codes** | New construction near known supersonic corridors may include reinforced glazing. |
| 6 | **Operational Scheduling** | Supersonic testing scheduled during daytime to reduce sleep disruption from overflights. |

---

### **Question B — Full 14-Mark Alternative (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — Sample Model 2024, Module 4 Internal Choice Option B]**

**(a) (7 marks) Define infrasound and explain why it is difficult to control. Discuss its physiological effects on human beings and provide two engineering control measures.**

**(b) (7 marks) A supersonic aircraft of effective length 25 m cruises at Mach 1.6 at an altitude of 2000 m. Using the Hayes overpressure approximation, calculate the ground-level overpressure and the peak sound pressure level. Comment on the regulatory implications.**

#### Model Solution to Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1 mark):**
Infrasound is acoustic energy at frequencies below 20 Hz (sub-audible band). It is naturally abundant in the environment from geological and meteorological sources.

**Why Infrasound is Difficult to Control (3 marks):**
1. **Extreme wavelengths** — at 10 Hz, $\lambda = c/f = 343/10 = 34.3$ m. This is much larger than typical building dimensions, walls, or enclosures, so **diffraction dominates** and sound "bends around" obstacles.
2. **Low atmospheric absorption** — infrasound attenuation is negligible; it can propagate thousands of kilometers.
3. **Coupling to structures** — infrasound couples efficiently into building structures, causing secondary re-radiation and vibration.
4. **Conventional absorbers are ineffective** — porous absorbers and resonant absorbers are tuned to higher frequencies.

**Physiological Effects (2 marks):**
- **Vestibular system stimulation** causing nausea, dizziness, and disorientation.
- **Visual disturbance** (eye resonance near 7 Hz).
- **"Infrasound sickness"**: headache, fatigue, irritability, sleep disruption.
- At sustained levels > 120 dB (G-weighted), it can impair task performance.

**Engineering Control Measures (1 mark):**
1. **Active anti-noise systems** — speakers generating anti-phase low-frequency signals.
2. **Source modification** — balancing rotating machinery, isolating heavy equipment on vibration mounts.
3. **Building design** — flexible couplings, isolated foundations, double-walled structures.

#### Model Solution to Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- $L = 25$ m
- $M = 1.6$
- $h = 2000$ m
- $p_0 = 101{,}325$ Pa
- $p_{0,\text{ref}} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa

**Step 1 — Compute $M\sqrt{M^2-1}$ (1 mark).**

$$M^{2} = 1.6^{2} = 2.56$$
$$M^{2} - 1 = 1.56$$
$$\sqrt{1.56} = 1.249$$
$$M\sqrt{M^{2}-1} = 1.6 \times 1.249 = 1.998 \approx 2.00$$

**Step 2 — Compute $(L/h)^{3/4}$ (1 mark).**

$$\frac{L}{h} = \frac{25}{2000} = 0.0125$$
$$(0.0125)^{0.75} = e^{0.75 \ln(0.0125)} = e^{0.75 \times (-4.382)} = e^{-3.2865} = 0.03738$$

**Step 3 — Compute $\Delta p / p_0$ (1 mark).**

$$\frac{\Delta p}{p_0} = 0.395 \times 2.00 \times 0.03738 = 0.395 \times 0.07476 = 0.02953$$

**Step 4 — Compute $\Delta p$ (1 mark).**

$$\Delta p = 0.02953 \times 101{,}325 = 2992.3 \text{ Pa} \approx 2.99 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5 — Compute peak SPL (1 mark).**

$$L_{p,\text{peak}} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{2992.3}{2 \times 10^{-5}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}(1.496 \times 10^{8})$$
$$= 20 \times (8 + \log_{10}(1.496)) = 20 \times (8 + 0.1750) = 20 \times 8.1750 = 163.5 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 6 — Regulatory Implications (2 marks):**
A peak SPL of **163.5 dB** substantially exceeds the human pain threshold (130 dB) and approaches levels known to cause minor structural damage (windows, plaster) above ~140 dB. This explains:
- **FAA Part 91.817** (and equivalent ICAO regulations) prohibiting civil supersonic flight over land.
- **Military supersonic overflight restrictions** above populated areas.
- **Need for low-boom supersonic aircraft design** (e.g., NASA X-59 program) to reduce future impact.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the square root:** Many students write $\sqrt{M^2-1}$ as just $M^2-1$ — read the formula carefully.
> 2. **Unit mismatch in SPL:** $\Delta p$ is in Pa, but the **reference** for SPL is $20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$, not 1 Pa. Students frequently lose 1–2 marks by using $p_0 = 1$ Pa or omitting the reference.
> 3. **Altitude vs. ground:** The formula gives ground-level overpressure; the in-flight pressure is much higher.
> 4. **Confusing $L$ and $h$:** $L$ is the **aircraft length**, $h$ is the **flight altitude** — they are not interchangeable.
> 5. **Skipping units in the final answer:** Always state "$\Delta p \approx 3.0$ kPa" and "$L_{p,\text{peak}} \approx 163.5$ dB" with explicit units.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [ ] **Infrasound** = $f < 20$ Hz; long wavelengths ($\lambda > 17$ m); low attenuation; hard to shield.
- [ ] **Ultrasound** = $f > 20$ kHz; short wavelengths ($\lambda < 1.7$ cm); high attenuation in air; occupational hazard.
- [ ] **Impulse noise** = rise time $\leq 35$ ms, duration $\leq 500$ ms; use **Impulse ("I") time weighting** on the SLM.
- [ ] **Sonic boom** = shock wave from $M > 1$ flight; ground signature is an **N-wave**.
- [ ] **Mach Number:** $M = v/c$, where $c = \sqrt{\gamma R T} \approx 343$ m/s in air at 20 °C.
- [ ] **Mach Cone:** $\sin\mu = 1/M$. For $M=2$, $\mu = 30°$. For $M=1.5$, $\mu \approx 41.8°$.
- [ ] **Hayes Overpressure:** $\Delta p / p_0 \approx 0.395 \cdot M \sqrt{M^2-1} \cdot (L/h)^{3/4}$.
- [ ] **Reference Pressure** for SPL in air: $p_{0,\text{ref}} = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ Pa.
- [ ] **Acoustic Reflex Latency:** $\sim 150$ ms — explains why impulse noise is uniquely damaging.
- [ ] **Atmospheric absorption** scales as $\alpha \propto f^2$ — favors low-frequency propagation (infrasound travels far).
- [ ] **Regulatory:** FAA Part 91.817 bans civil supersonic over land; OSHA limits impulse peak to 140 dB.
- [ ] **Mitigation Hierarchy:** Source → Path → Receiver. Active anti-noise is essential for infrasound; aircraft shaping for sonic boom.
- [ ] **SPL formula:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$; remember it is **20** (pressure) not **10** (intensity).
- [ ] **Threshold of pain:** 130 dB; structural damage to windows: ~140 dB; Concorde cruise boom: ~110–130 dB ground-level.
- [ ] **KTU's favorite trick question:** *"Why can't infrasound be blocked by walls?"* — Answer: wavelength >> wall dimensions, so diffraction dominates.
- [ ] **KTU's second favorite:** *"Why is impulse weighting needed for gunshots?"* — Answer: peak is too fast for Fast/Slow meter response; human ear sees the true peak.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** The single most important concept in this module is the **relationship between Mach number, Mach cone angle, and overpressure**. If you master $\sin\mu = 1/M$ and the Hayes formula, you can answer at least **6–8 marks worth** of any Module 4 question.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Noise Standards and Limit Values
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Noise Standards and Limit Values

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Noise Standards and Limit Values** refer to the legally and scientifically prescribed maximum permissible sound pressure levels (expressed in decibels, dB) that are considered acceptable for human exposure in various environments (industrial, commercial, residential, and silence zones) over specified time durations. These standards are established by national and international regulatory bodies to protect public health, prevent noise-induced hearing loss (NIHL), maintain environmental quality, and ensure occupational safety.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PECET756, Module 4):**
> Noise standards define the legally binding threshold values of sound pressure levels (dB(A)) permissible in ambient environments (community noise) and occupational settings (industrial noise), as a function of zone type, exposure duration, and time of day, in order to safeguard human health, hearing, and welfare.

The most widely used standardized unit is the **A-weighted sound pressure level**, denoted **dB(A)**, which approximates the frequency response of the human ear (40 Hz – 4 kHz emphasis).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your ear as a highly sensitive microphone. Just as a microphone has a "tolerance limit" before the audio signal distorts (clips) and becomes permanently damaged, the human ear has a threshold of damage based on two factors: **how loud** the sound is and **how long** you are exposed to it.

- A **firecracker burst** (~140 dB) is like touching a hot pan — instant damage, even for a fraction of a second.
- A **busy traffic roadside** (~80 dB) is like sitting in a mildly hot room — uncomfortable but tolerable for short periods; harmful if you sit in it for 8 hours every day.
- A **whisper** (~30 dB) is like a cool breeze — perfectly safe, no matter the duration.

Noise standards are simply the "occupancy rules" of this sonic environment — they tell you **what level is safe, in which area, and for how long**, so that the cumulative "dose" of noise your ear receives in a day does not exceed the threshold of harm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** A 10 dB increase in noise level is **perceived** as a doubling of loudness by the human ear, but represents a **tenfold increase in acoustic energy** (sound intensity). This logarithmic nature is the foundation of the decibel scale.

## 1.3 Core Metrics Used in Noise Standards

| Metric | Symbol | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | Instantaneous pressure variation in dB |
| A-weighted SPL | $L_A$ or dB(A) | Frequency-weighted SPL matching human hearing |
| Equivalent Continuous Level | $L_{eq}$ | Time-averaged energy of fluctuating noise |
| Sound Exposure Level | $L_{SEL}$ | Total noise energy compressed to 1 second |
| Day-Night Level | $L_{DN}$ or $L_{dn}$ | 24-hour average with 10 dB night penalty |
| Statistical Levels | $L_{10}, L_{90}$ | Levels exceeded 10% / 90% of measurement time |
| Day Time Average | $L_{day}$ | 12-hour daytime average (06:00–22:00 in India) |
| Night Time Average | $L_{night}$ | 8-hour night-time average (22:00–06:00 in India) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Constant Reference:** The standard reference sound pressure is $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{Pa}$, which corresponds to the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Standards Are Necessary — The "Why"

Human hearing damage follows two physical laws:
1. **Acoustic Trauma:** Extremely high SPL (>120 dB) causes immediate mechanical damage to the hair cells of the cochlea, even for very short durations.
2. **Noise-Induced Hearing Loss (NIHL):** Prolonged exposure to moderately elevated levels (≥85 dB(A) over 8 hours) causes cumulative, irreversible metabolic damage to cochlear hair cells.

Additionally, community noise causes:
- Sleep disturbance (cardiovascular stress)
- Cognitive impairment in children
- Reduced work productivity
- Annoyance and mental health impacts

Standards are therefore established based on **dose-response relationships** derived from epidemiological and audiological studies.

## 2.2 The Fundamental Noise Level Equations

### (a) Sound Pressure Level (SPL)
The basic decibel scale definition is logarithmic because human hearing itself responds logarithmically to sound pressure (Weber-Fechner law).

$$L_p = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{p_{rms}}{p_0}\right) \quad \text{(dB)}$$

where $p_{rms}$ is the root-mean-square sound pressure and $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$.

### (b) Sound Power Level (PWL)
$$L_W = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{W}{W_0}\right) \quad \text{(dB)}$$

where $W_0 = 10^{-12} \, \text{W}$ is the reference power.

### (c) Equivalent Continuous Sound Level ($L_{eq}$)
The energy-equivalent steady level that, if continuous over the measurement period, would deliver the same acoustic energy as the actual fluctuating noise.

$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right] \quad \text{(dB(A))}$$

For discrete samples (the practical field case):
$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} 10^{L_i/10}\right]$$

### (d) Day-Night Sound Level ($L_{DN}$)
A 24-hour metric that adds a **10 dB penalty** to all sounds occurring during night hours (22:00 – 07:00 in the US; 22:00 – 06:00 in India) to reflect the greater annoyance/sleep disturbance caused by night noise.

$$L_{DN} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{24}\left(15 \times 10^{L_{day}/10} + 9 \times 10^{(L_{night}+10)/10}\right)\right]$$

### (e) Sound Exposure Level ($L_{SEL}$)
Normalizes any noise event to a reference duration of 1 second.

$$L_{SEL} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{t_0}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right] \quad \text{with } t_0 = 1 \text{ s}$$

### (f) OSHA / ISO Permissible Exposure Time (T)
Based on the **3 dB (ISO) or 5 dB (OSHA) doubling rule**, the permissible exposure time halves for every 3 dB or 5 dB rise above the criterion level.

For OSHA (90 dB(A) criterion, 5 dB exchange rate):
$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-90)/5}} \quad \text{(hours)}$$

For ISO/EU (85 dB(A) criterion, 3 dB exchange rate):
$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L-85)/3}} \quad \text{(hours)}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Formula | Application | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p_{rms}/p_0)$ | Sound Pressure Level | $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ |
| 2 | $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | Sound Power Level | $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W |
| 3 | $L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10} dt\right]$ | Equivalent Continuous SPL | $T$ in seconds |
| 4 | $L_{eq} \approx 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{N}\sum 10^{L_i/10}\right)$ | Discrete-sample $L_{eq}$ | $N$ = number of samples |
| 5 | $L_{DN} = 10\log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{24}\left(15\cdot 10^{L_d/10} + 9\cdot 10^{(L_n+10)/10}\right)\right]$ | Day-Night Level | $L_d, L_n$ in dB(A) |
| 6 | $L_{SEL} = L_{eq} + 10\log_{10}(T/1\,\text{s})$ | Sound Exposure Level | $T$ in seconds |
| 7 | $T_{OSHA} = 8 / 2^{(L-90)/5}$ | OSHA permissible exposure (h) | 5 dB exchange |
| 8 | $T_{ISO} = 8 / 2^{(L-85)/3}$ | ISO/EU permissible exposure (h) | 3 dB exchange |
| 9 | $L_{10} \approx L_{eq} + 3$ dB (for road traffic) | $L_{10}$ traffic approximation | — |
| 10 | $L_{90} + (L_{10}-L_{90})/2 \approx L_{eq}$ | Traffic $L_{eq}$ from $L_N$ | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** $L_{eq}$ is the *single most important parameter* used in Indian (CPCB) and international (WHO, EU) standards because it condenses fluctuating noise into one representative, energy-equivalent value. If you remember only one formula for the KTU exam, remember the $L_{eq}$ expression.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of These Equations

- **Urban Planning:** $L_{DN}$ is the primary metric used by the U.S. EPA and Federal Aviation Administration (FAA) to certify airport noise compatibility.
- **Occupational Safety:** OSHA/NFPA permissible exposure tables (using the 5 dB exchange rule) directly derive from Equation 7, and dictate mandatory hearing-protection programs.
- **Legal Enforcement (India):** The **Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000** specify ambient $L_{eq}$ limits in four zones; any exceedance is punishable under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986**.
- **Acoustic Design of Buildings:** $L_{SEL}$ and $L_{eq}$ guide façade and partition sound transmission class (STC) ratings.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of $L_{eq}$ from First Principles

### Step 1: Start with Sound Intensity Definition
Acoustic intensity is proportional to the square of the sound pressure:
$$I(t) = \frac{p^2(t)}{\rho c}$$
where $\rho$ is air density ($\approx 1.2 \, \text{kg/m}^3$) and $c$ is the speed of sound ($\approx 343$ m/s).

### Step 2: Express Instantaneous SPL
The instantaneous sound pressure level in dB is:
$$L(t) = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{I(t)}{I_0}\right] = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{p^2(t)}{p_0^2}\right]$$

Solving for the squared pressure ratio:
$$\frac{p^2(t)}{p_0^2} = 10^{L(t)/10}$$

### Step 3: Compute the Time-Averaged Energy
The average acoustic energy over a measurement period $T$ is the time-averaged squared pressure ratio:
$$\overline{\left(\frac{p^2}{p_0^2}\right)} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt$$

### Step 4: Define $L_{eq}$ as the Steady Level Giving the Same Energy
By definition, $L_{eq}$ is the constant SPL that, sustained over $T$, would deliver identical energy:
$$10^{L_{eq}/10} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt$$

### Step 5: Take the Logarithm
Taking $10 \log_{10}$ of both sides:
$$\boxed{L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right]}$$

This is the rigorous derivation. The discrete-sample form follows by replacing the integral with a sum and $T$ with $N \cdot \Delta t$.

## 3.2 Worked Example — $L_{eq}$ Calculation (High-Yield for KTU)

**Problem:** The following noise levels were measured at a residential site during a 30-minute survey. Calculate the equivalent continuous sound level $L_{eq}$.

| Interval (min) | 0–5 | 5–10 | 10–15 | 15–20 | 20–25 | 25–30 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $L_i$ (dB(A)) | 62 | 68 | 70 | 65 | 60 | 64 |

**Solution:**

Because each interval is of equal duration, $L_{eq}$ is the energy-average of the six levels:

$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{6}\sum_{i=1}^{6} 10^{L_i/10}\right]$$

Compute each $10^{L_i/10}$:

| $L_i$ | $10^{L_i/10}$ |
|---|---|
| 62 | $10^{6.2} = 1.585 \times 10^{6}$ |
| 68 | $10^{6.8} = 6.310 \times 10^{6}$ |
| 70 | $10^{7.0} = 1.000 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 65 | $10^{6.5} = 3.162 \times 10^{6}$ |
| 60 | $10^{6.0} = 1.000 \times 10^{6}$ |
| 64 | $10^{6.4} = 2.512 \times 10^{6}$ |

**Sum of terms:**
$$\sum 10^{L_i/10} = (1.585 + 6.310 + 10.000 + 3.162 + 1.000 + 2.512) \times 10^{6}$$
$$= 24.569 \times 10^{6}$$

**Energy average:**
$$\frac{1}{6} \times 24.569 \times 10^{6} = 4.0948 \times 10^{6}$$

**Take logarithm:**
$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}(4.0948 \times 10^{6}) = 10 \times (6 + \log_{10}(4.0948))$$
$$= 10 \times (6 + 0.6122) = 10 \times 6.6122 = 66.12 \, \text{dB(A)}$$

$$\boxed{L_{eq} \approx 66.1 \, \text{dB(A)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Although the noise fluctuated between 60 and 70 dB(A), the equivalent continuous level is 66.1 dB(A) — a value dominated by the loudest intervals due to the **logarithmic energy weighting**. The simple arithmetic mean would give only 64.8 dB(A), which underestimates the true energy dose by ~1.3 dB.

## 3.3 Derivation of $L_{DN}$ for the Indian Context

For Indian conditions, daytime = 15 hours (06:00–22:00) and nighttime = 9 hours (22:00–06:00). With a 10 dB night-time penalty:

$$L_{DN} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{24}\left(15 \cdot 10^{L_d/10} + 9 \cdot 10^{(L_n+10)/10}\right)\right]$$

**Numerical example:** If $L_d = 60$ dB(A) and $L_n = 50$ dB(A):

$$L_{DN} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{15 \cdot 10^{6.0} + 9 \cdot 10^{6.0}}{1}\right]/24 \text{ (form)}$$

Compute the numerator terms:
$$15 \cdot 10^{6.0} = 15 \times 10^6$$
$$9 \cdot 10^{(50+10)/10} = 9 \cdot 10^{6.0} = 9 \times 10^6$$

Wait — both day and adjusted-night values yield the same power level because the +10 penalty on the night level exactly compensates for the lower level itself in this specific case. Sum:
$$15 \times 10^6 + 9 \times 10^6 = 24 \times 10^6$$

$$L_{DN} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{24 \times 10^6}{24}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(10^6) = 60 \, \text{dB(A)}$$

If $L_n$ were instead 45 dB(A):
$$9 \cdot 10^{(45+10)/10} = 9 \cdot 10^{5.5} = 9 \times 3.162 \times 10^5 = 2.846 \times 10^6$$
$$L_{DN} = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{15 \times 10^6 + 2.846 \times 10^6}{24}\right) = 10 \log_{10}(7.44 \times 10^5) \approx 58.7 \, \text{dB(A)}$$

## 3.4 OSHA Permissible Exposure Time — Sample Calculation

**Problem:** A factory worker is exposed to 100 dB(A) noise. Using the OSHA criterion (90 dB(A) for 8 h, 5 dB exchange rate), what is the maximum permissible exposure time per day?

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(100-90)/5}} = \frac{8}{2^{2}} = \frac{8}{4} = 2 \, \text{hours}$$

**Problem:** Same scenario using the ISO/EU 3 dB exchange rule (85 dB(A) / 8 h):
$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(100-85)/3}} = \frac{8}{2^{5}} = \frac{8}{32} = 0.25 \, \text{hours} = 15 \, \text{minutes}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Comparison:** The 3 dB rule is more conservative (and physically more accurate) because it equates energy doubling with halving of permissible time. The 5 dB rule is a U.S. legal/regulatory convention that is more lenient.

## 3.5 Noise Standards — India (CPCB, Schedule VII of EPA Rules 1986)

| Zone | Day (06:00–22:00) $L_{eq}$ dB(A) | Night (22:00–06:00) $L_{eq}$ dB(A) |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial | **75** | **70** |
| Commercial | **65** | **55** |
| Residential | **55** | **45** |
| Silence Zone | **50** | **40** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Silence Zone Definition (Rule 3(1)):** An area comprising not less than 100 meters around hospitals, educational institutions, courts, religious places, or any other area declared as such by the competent authority. Use of vehicle horns, loudspeakers, and bursting of firecrackers is prohibited in silence zones.

## 3.6 International Standards — Comparative Matrix

| Authority / Standard | Metric | Limit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **WHO (1999/2018)** | $L_{eq,24h}$ | 70 dB(A) (severe) / 53 dB(A) (annoyance) | Outdoor community |
| **WHO (Night)** | $L_{night}$ | 40 dB(A) | Outdoor at night |
| **OSHA (US)** | TWA $L_A$ | 90 dB(A) / 8 h | Occupational |
| **NIOSH (US)** | TWA | **85 dB(A) / 8 h** | Recommended (more stringent) |
| **EU Directive 2003/10/EC** | $L_{eq,8h}$ | **87 dB(A)** (action) / 85 dB(A) (exposure) | Occupational |
| **U.S. EPA** | $L_{DN}$ outdoor | 55 dB(A) (goal) / 70 dB(A) (threshold) | Airport/transportation noise |
| **ISO 1999:2013** | $L_{EX,8h}$ | Estimates NIHL risk | Audiometric database |
| **FAA / ICAO** | $L_{DN}$ | 65 dB(A) | Airport compatibility |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse **Occupational** (OSHA/NIOSH) standards with **Ambient/Community** (CPCB/WHO) standards. The 90 dB(A) OSHA limit is for 8 hours of *worker exposure*, NOT for general outdoor noise. The Indian residential limit is 55 dB(A), not 90.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

```mermaid
    stepA["START Identify the noise environment type"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Noise Metrics in Standards

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1["INSTANTANEOUS SOUND PRESSURE LEVEL L_p dB"]

    M4["DAY NIGHT LEVEL L_DN dB A"]
    M5["SOUND EXPOSURE LEVEL L_SEL dB A"]
    M6["PERCENTILE LEVELS L_10 L_50 L_90"]

    N1["PHYSICAL QUANTITY PRESSURE"]

    N4["24 HOUR WITH NIGHT PENALTY"]

    M1 --> N1
    M2 --> N1
    M2 --> N2
    M3 --> N2

    M4 --> N4

    M5 --> N5
    M6 --> N3
    M6 --> N6
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** The hierarchy in Section 4.3 shows how instantaneous pressure ($L_p$) → hearing-weighted ($L_A$) → energy-averaged ($L_{eq}$) → 24-hour day-night aggregated ($L_{DN}$) represents a **progressive integration** of acoustic information. Indian CPCB standards use $L_{eq}$; U.S. EPA and FAA use $L_{DN}$; OSHA uses $L_A$ averaged to TWA.
