# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]
**Define A-weighted sound pressure level. Why is dB(A) preferred over dB (linear) in environmental noise standards?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **A-weighted sound pressure level**, denoted dB(A), is the sound pressure level measured after passing the signal through an **A-weighting filter**, which mathematically emulates the frequency response of the human ear at moderate loudness levels (40 phon contour). The filter attenuates very low (<500 Hz) and very high (>10 kHz) frequencies, where human hearing sensitivity is poor.

**Why preferred:**
1. Human hearing is **not equally sensitive** to all frequencies — the ear is most sensitive around 1–4 kHz.
2. The A-weighting closely matches this sensitivity, so dB(A) values correlate directly with **perceived loudness** and **hearing damage risk**.
3. Most international standards (WHO, OSHA, CPCB) prescribe limits in dB(A) to ensure the limit reflects actual auditory impact. (3 marks)

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]
**List the ambient noise standards (in dB(A)) prescribed by the Central Pollution Control Board (CPCB) for industrial, commercial, residential, and silence zones for both day and night.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
As per the **Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000** (Schedule VII of the Environment Protection Act, 1986):

| Zone | Day (06:00–22:00) dB(A) | Night (22:00–06:00) dB(A) |
|---|---|---|
| Industrial | 75 | 70 |
| Commercial | 65 | 55 |
| Residential | 55 | 45 |
| Silence Zone | 50 | 40 |

(3 marks — full table = 3, partial table = proportionate marks)

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> (1) Students frequently confuse **8-hour occupational** limits (90 dB(A) OSHA / 85 dB(A) ISO) with **ambient community** limits (55 dB(A) residential CPCB). This is the **#1 mark-losing error** in this module.
> (2) When asked to calculate $L_{eq}$, students often compute a **simple arithmetic mean** of dB values — this is **wrong**. dB is logarithmic, so you must first convert to intensity ratio, average, then convert back.
> (3) For $L_{DN}$ problems, students forget the **+10 dB night penalty**. Always state this clearly in the solution.
> (4) For OSHA problems, students write "exchange rate" incorrectly as "doubling rate" or "addition rate" — use the exact KTU terminology: **5 dB exchange rate (OSHA)** and **3 dB exchange rate (ISO)**.

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]
**(a)** Define $L_{eq}$ and explain its physical significance. Derive the expression for $L_{eq}$ from sound pressure level principles. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During a 1-hour noise survey in an industrial zone, the following readings were recorded at 10-minute intervals: 72, 76, 78, 74, 80, 73 dB(A). Calculate the $L_{eq}$ and comment on its compliance with CPCB standards. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Definition and Derivation of $L_{eq}$ (7 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Equivalent Continuous Sound Level ($L_{eq}$) is the steady, continuous A-weighted sound pressure level which, if maintained over the same measurement duration $T$, would deliver the same total acoustic energy to the receiver as the actual time-varying noise. It is the **energy-equivalent** of fluctuating noise and is the single most widely used descriptor in environmental noise standards.

**Derivation (5 marks):**
Start with the instantaneous sound pressure level definition (Equation 1):
$$L(t) = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{p^2(t)}{p_0^2}\right]$$

Rearranging, the squared pressure ratio is:
$$\frac{p^2(t)}{p_0^2} = 10^{L(t)/10}$$

The time-averaged energy of the noise is:
$$\overline{E} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt$$

By definition, $L_{eq}$ is the constant level giving the same average energy:
$$10^{L_{eq}/10} = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt$$

Taking $10 \log_{10}$ on both sides:
$$\boxed{L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T 10^{L(t)/10}\, dt\right] \, \text{dB(A)}}$$

For discrete samples of equal duration, this reduces to:
$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} 10^{L_i/10}\right]$$

---

**(b) $L_{eq}$ Calculation and Compliance (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Tabulate and convert each $L_i$ to $10^{L_i/10}$ (3 marks)**

| $L_i$ (dB(A)) | $10^{L_i/10}$ |
|---|---|
| 72 | $10^{7.2} = 1.585 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 76 | $10^{7.6} = 3.981 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 78 | $10^{7.8} = 6.310 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 74 | $10^{7.4} = 2.512 \times 10^{7}$ |
| 80 | $10^{8.0} = 1.000 \times 10^{8}$ |
| 73 | $10^{7.3} = 1.995 \times 10^{7}$ |

**Step 2: Sum the terms (1 mark)**
$$\sum 10^{L_i/10} = (1.585 + 3.981 + 6.310 + 2.512 + 10.000 + 1.995) \times 10^{7}$$
$$= 26.383 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 3: Compute the energy average (1 mark)**
$$\frac{1}{6} \times 26.383 \times 10^{7} = 4.397 \times 10^{7}$$

**Step 4: Take the logarithm (1 mark)**
$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}(4.397 \times 10^{7}) = 10(7 + \log_{10} 4.397) = 10(7 + 0.6429) = 76.43 \, \text{dB(A)}$$

**Step 5: Compliance check (1 mark)**
CPCB industrial day limit = 75 dB(A).
$$L_{eq} = 76.43 \, \text{dB(A)} > 75 \, \text{dB(A)} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{NON-COMPLIANT}$$
Mitigation measures (acoustic enclosures, silencers, mufflers, PPE) are recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – Model Paper]
**(a)** Explain the OSHA and NIOSH occupational noise exposure standards. Compare the 5 dB (OSHA) and 3 dB (ISO/NIOSH) exchange rules. A worker is exposed to 100 dB(A) for 4 hours; calculate the dose received and the corresponding 8-h TWA. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define $L_{DN}$ and $L_{SEL}$. In a residential area, $L_{day} = 58$ dB(A) and $L_{night} = 50$ dB(A). Calculate $L_{DN}$ using the Indian day (15 h) and night (9 h) durations. Is the area compliant with the CPCB standard? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) OSHA vs NIOSH and Dose Calculation (7 marks):**

**OSHA Standard (29 CFR 1910.95) (2 marks):**
- Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) = **90 dB(A)** for 8-hour Time-Weighted Average (TWA).
- Uses **5 dB exchange rate** (doubling rate) — for every 5 dB increase, permissible exposure time halves.
- Hearing conservation program mandatory at **85 dB(A) TWA**.

**NIOSH Recommended Exposure Limit (REL) (2 marks):**
- REL = **85 dB(A)** for 8-hour TWA.
- Uses **3 dB exchange rate** (energy-equivalent, ISO-aligned).
- More conservative; based on energy-doubling principle.

**Comparison Table (1 mark):**

| Aspect | OSHA | NIOSH / ISO |
|---|---|---|
| Criterion Level | 90 dB(A) | 85 dB(A) |
| Exchange Rate | 5 dB | 3 dB |
| Time halving | 5 dB rise | 3 dB rise |
| Conservatism | Lower | Higher (energy-accurate) |

**Dose Calculation (2 marks):**
For a single exposure: $C_i/T_i = 4/2$ (using 100 dB(A) → $T_{OSHA}=2$ h):
$$D_i\% = (C_i / T_i) \times 100 = (4/2) \times 100 = 200\%$$

Therefore, 8-h TWA using OSHA formula $TWA = 16.61 \log_{10}(D/100) + 90$:
$$TWA = 16.61 \log_{10}(2.0) + 90 = 16.61 \times 0.301 + 90 = 5.0 + 90 = 95.0 \, \text{dB(A)}$$

Or directly from the 5 dB rule: $TWA = 90 + 5 \log_2(4) = 90 + 5 \times 2 = 100$ dB(A) for the exposure period; combined with 4 h quiet (assumed 0) gives TWA = 95 dB(A) for 8 h.

> Note: Students may present using either OSHA or NIOSH framework; the key is to show all substitution steps. (1 mark partial deduction if exchange rate is wrong)

---

**(b) $L_{DN}$ and $L_{SEL}$ Definitions, Calculation & Compliance (7 marks):**

**Definitions (2 marks):**
- **$L_{DN}$ (Day-Night Level):** 24-hour energy-equivalent level with a **10 dB penalty** added to all night-time sounds to reflect greater sleep disturbance.
- **$L_{SEL}$ (Sound Exposure Level):** Total acoustic energy of an event normalized to a **reference duration of 1 second**, useful for characterizing transient events (e.g., aircraft flyovers, gunshots).

**Given:** $L_{day} = 58$ dB(A), $L_{night} = 50$ dB(A), Indian durations: day = 15 h, night = 9 h.

**Step 1: Apply the 10 dB night penalty (1 mark)**
$$L_{night, \text{penalized}} = 50 + 10 = 60 \, \text{dB(A)}$$

**Step 2: Convert each to linear intensity (1 mark)**
$$10^{L_{day}/10} = 10^{5.8} = 6.310 \times 10^{5}$$
$$10^{L_{n,\text{pen}}/10} = 10^{6.0} = 1.000 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 3: Apply the time-weighted $L_{DN}$ formula (2 marks)**
$$L_{DN} = 10 \log_{10}\left[\frac{15 \times 6.310 \times 10^5 + 9 \times 1.000 \times 10^6}{24}\right]$$

Numerator:
$$15 \times 6.310 \times 10^5 = 9.465 \times 10^6$$
$$9 \times 1.000 \times 10^6 = 9.000 \times 10^6$$
$$\text{Sum} = 18.465 \times 10^6$$

Denominator: 24

Energy ratio:
$$\frac{18.465 \times 10^6}{24} = 7.694 \times 10^5$$

**Step 4: Compute $L_{DN}$ (1 mark)**
$$L_{DN} = 10 \log_{10}(7.694 \times 10^5) = 10(5 + \log_{10} 7.694) = 10(5 + 0.8864) = 58.86 \, \text{dB(A)}$$

$$\boxed{L_{DN} \approx 58.9 \, \text{dB(A)}}$$

> Note: For residential compliance under CPCB, the day limit is 55 dB(A) and night limit is 45 dB(A). Both are **exceeded**, so the area is **NON-COMPLIANT**. (Mark compliance check: 1 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Summary — Noise Standards & Limit Values**

- **Reference pressure:** $p_0 = 20 \, \mu\text{Pa}$ (always). **Reference intensity:** $I_0 = 10^{-12}$ W/m².
- **A-weighting** emulates 40-phon hearing response; **dB(A)** is the universal reporting unit.
- **$L_{eq}$** is the single most important metric for **Indian (CPCB) and EU standards**.
- **$L_{DN}$** is the primary metric for **U.S. EPA** and **aviation (FAA/ICAO)**; it carries a **+10 dB night penalty**.
- **CPCB Schedule VII (India) limits — must memorize:**
  - Industrial: 75 / 70 dB(A) Day / Night
  - Commercial: 65 / 55 dB(A)
  - Residential: 55 / 45 dB(A)
  - Silence Zone: 50 / 40 dB(A)
  - Day = 06:00–22:00; Night = 22:00–06:00
- **Silence Zone** = 100 m around hospitals, schools, courts, religious places.
- **OSHA** = 90 dB(A) TWA, 5 dB exchange; **NIOSH / ISO** = 85 dB(A) TWA, 3 dB exchange.
- **3 dB rule** = energy doubles with every 3 dB rise. **5 dB rule** = OSHA legal convention.
- **OSHA permissible time:** $T = 8 / 2^{(L-90)/5}$ hours.
- **ISO permissible time:** $T = 8 / 2^{(L-85)/3}$ hours.
- **A 10 dB rise** is perceived as a doubling of loudness, but represents **10× acoustic energy**.
- **A 3 dB rise** represents a doubling of acoustic energy; the 5 dB rule is more lenient than the 3 dB rule.
- **$L_{SEL} = L_{eq} + 10 \log_{10}(T/1\text{s})$** — used for transient event description.
- **$L_{10}$** = level exceeded 10% of the time (typically the "peak traffic noise"). For road traffic, $L_{10} \approx L_{eq} + 3$ dB.
- **$L_{90}$** = level exceeded 90% of the time (the "background" or residual noise).
- **$L_{90} + (L_{10} - L_{90})/2 \approx L_{eq}$** — useful for traffic noise estimation when only $L_N$ values are available.
- **WHO outdoor community limits:** $L_{eq,24h} \leq 53$ dB(A) (annoyance), $\leq 70$ dB(A) (health risk); $L_{night} \leq 40$ dB(A).
- **Noise Pollution (Regulation and Control) Rules, 2000** is enforced under the **Environment (Protection) Act, 1986** in India.
- **Always use energy (logarithmic) averaging** for $L_{eq}$ — never arithmetic mean of dB values.
- **Dosimetry Formula (OSHA):** $D\,\% = 100 \times \sum (C_i / T_i)$; 8-h TWA: $TWA = 16.61 \log_{10}(D/100) + 90$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** In KTU board exams, questions on this module almost always combine a **definition** (3 marks) with a **calculation** (7 marks). Practice $L_{eq}$ from discrete readings, $L_{DN}$ from $L_{day}$/$L_{night}$, and OSHA permissible exposure time. Memorize the CPCB table — it is the single most-tested element of Module 4.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECET756 - AIR AND NOISE POLLUTION CONTROL ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Noise Instrumentation and Monitoring Procedure, Noise Indices, Noise Control Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definitions & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Noise — The KTU Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU / CPCB Terminology):**
> **Noise** is defined as any unwanted, undesirable, or disagreeable sound that interferes with normal activities, communication, hearing, or rest, and which adversely affects the physiological, psychological, and sociological well-being of an individual or community.

In the engineering sense, sound is a mechanical pressure wave travelling through an elastic medium. The human ear responds to these pressure fluctuations, and when the resulting sensation becomes *unpleasant*, *intrusive*, or *harmful*, the sound is reclassified as **noise**.

### Conceptual Analogy — "The Intrusive Guest"

Imagine you are studying in a quiet library. A gentle page-turning sound is **sound**. The same page-turning sound, when amplified to 100 dB, becomes **noise**. Noise is therefore not just about physical intensity — it is about *context, duration, frequency, and receiver perception*. This subjectivity is what makes noise pollution uniquely difficult to characterize and control, compared to air or water pollution.

---

## 1.2 Key Physical Quantities (Foundational Metrics)

> [!NOTE]
> The **Sound Pressure Level (SPL)** is the fundamental measurable quantity. The standard reference pressure is **$p_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$ (0.00002 Pa)**, which is the threshold of human hearing at 1 kHz.

| Quantity | Symbol | Unit | Definition |
|----------|--------|------|------------|
| Sound Pressure | $p$ | Pa (Pascal) | RMS fluctuation about atmospheric pressure |
| Reference Pressure | $p_0$ | 20 µPa | Threshold of hearing (1 kHz) |
| Sound Pressure Level | $L_p$ | dB | $20 \log_{10}(p / p_0)$ |
| Sound Intensity | $I$ | W/m² | Acoustic power per unit area |
| Sound Power | $W$ | W (Watt) | Total acoustic energy radiated per second |
| Frequency | $f$ | Hz | Number of pressure oscillations per second |
| Wavelength | $\lambda$ | m | $c / f$ where $c \approx 343$ m/s in air at 20 °C |

---

## 1.3 Noise Instrumentation — The Tool Chain

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Sound Level Meter (SLM)** is the workhorse instrument of noise engineering. Per **IEC 61672-1**, SLMs are classified into two accuracy classes:
> - **Class 1 (Precision)** — laboratory and field reference use, tolerance $\pm 0.7$ dB.
> - **Class 2 (General Purpose)** — routine field surveys, tolerance $\pm 1.5$ dB.

### Other Critical Instruments
- **Dosimeter** — integrates noise exposure over a worker's shift (personal exposure meter).
- **Octave / Third-Octave Band Analyzer** — decomposes noise into frequency bands.
- **Sound Intensity Probe** — two-microphone probe (pp-probe) for direct intensity measurement.
- **Environmental Noise Monitor (Type 1 unattended)** — weatherproof, continuous-logging station.

---

## 1.4 Noise Indices — The Language of Noise Assessment

> [!NOTE]
> **Noise Indices** are *single-number descriptors* that condense complex time-varying sound into a value useful for regulation, zoning, and impact assessment.

Examples include **$L_{eq}$** (equivalent continuous level), **$L_{10}$, $L_{50}$, $L_{90}$** (statistical levels), **$L_{dn}$** (day-night level), **$L_{CNEL}$** (community noise equivalent level), **$L_{NP}$** (noise pollution level), and **$L_{TNI}$** (traffic noise index).

### Real-World Analogy — "The Weather Summary"

If outdoor sound is like hourly weather, $L_{eq}$ is the *average temperature* of the day, $L_{10}$ is the *hottest hour*, and $L_{90}$ is the *coolest hour*. A single number cannot describe the whole day — so we use several indices to capture the **average**, **peaks**, and **background**.

---

## 1.5 Noise Control Methods — The Engineering Response

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchy of Noise Control (most to least effective):**
> 1. **Source Control** — reduce noise at the point of generation.
> 2. **Path Control** — reduce noise as it travels through the medium.
> 3. **Receiver Control** — protect the recipient (PPE, zoning, hours of operation).

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic — "SPR":** **S**ource, **P**ath, **R**eceiver. This is the golden rule of acoustics engineering. Always attempt Source → Path → Receiver in that order of preference.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Sound Level Meter (SLM) — Block Architecture

A precision SLM consists of **six functional blocks** arranged in series. Understanding each block is essential for KTU derivations and for selecting the correct instrument for a survey.

### Step-by-Step Functional Logic

1. **Microphone (Transducer)**
   - Converts acoustic pressure variations into a proportional electrical voltage.
   - **Capacitor (condenser) microphones** are standard for Class 1 SLMs because of their flat frequency response (typically 20 Hz – 20 kHz) and long-term stability.

2. **Preamplifier**
   - Provides high input impedance so the microphone signal is not loaded. Sits close to the mic to avoid cable-induced losses.

3. **Frequency Weighting Networks**
   - The human ear is not equally sensitive to all frequencies. Weighting networks are analog or digital filters that mimic this response.
   - **A-weighting** — approximates the 40-phon equal-loudness contour. Standard for environmental and occupational noise. Unit: **dB(A)**.
   - **B-weighting** — intermediate contour; largely obsolete.
   - **C-weighting** — approximates the 100-phon contour; used for peak/impact noise.
   - **Z-weighting** — flat (linear) response from 10 Hz to 20 kHz; required by IEC 61672.

4. **RMS Detector & Integrator**
   - Computes the root-mean-square value of the fluctuating pressure signal over a defined averaging time (Fast: 125 ms, Slow: 1 s, Impulse: 35 ms with slow decay).

5. **Logarithmic Amplifier**
   - Converts the linear RMS signal to a logarithmic dB display.

6. **Display / Output**
   - Digital LCD readout, with dB scale typically 0–140 dB. Modern SLMs log data to internal memory for post-processing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why A-weighting dominates:** Although A-weighting was originally designed for low-level sounds (40 dB), it correlates well with hearing damage risk and speech interference at higher levels. Almost every international regulation (ISO, OSHA, EPA, CPCB) uses **dB(A)** as the reporting unit.

---

## 2.2 Noise Monitoring Procedure — Field Protocol

A **CPCB / IS 3029 / IS 4954 compliant** environmental noise survey follows a strict sequence:

### Phase 1 — Pre-Survey Planning
- Identify **monitoring objectives** (compliance, zoning, baseline, complaint).
- Determine **measurement locations** using grid or receptor mapping.
- Select **instrument type** (Class 1 for legal evidence, Class 2 for screening).
- **Calibrate** the SLM with a 94 dB / 1 kHz acoustic calibrator before AND after the survey.

### Phase 2 — Field Measurement
- Position microphone **1.2 – 1.5 m above ground** (free-field conditions), **≥ 3.5 m from any reflective surface** (walls, building façade).
- Use a **wind screen** to filter out wind-induced pseudo-noise (> 5 m/s wind → halt or use windscreen).
- Record **$L_{eq}$, $L_{min}$, $L_{max}$, $L_{10}$, $L_{50}$, $L_{90}$** over a representative duration.
- For traffic: minimum **15-minute** sample at peak hour. For industrial: full shift cycle.

### Phase 3 — Data Reduction & Reporting
- Compute indices (Leq, Ldn, CNEL).
- Apply corrections: **background noise correction**, **reflection correction**, **wind correction**.
- Compare with **ambient air quality standards** (CPCB: industrial 75/70 day/night; commercial 65/55; residential 55/45; silence zone 50/40 dB(A)).

---

## 2.3 Noise Indices — Definitions and Mathematics

### 2.3.1 Equivalent Continuous Sound Level ($L_{eq}$)

The steady, continuous sound level that contains the same acoustic energy as the time-varying sound over a specified period.

$$L_{eq,T} = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{p^2(t)}{p_0^2} \, dt \right]$$

Equivalently, for **discrete sampled data**:

$$L_{eq,T} = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} 10^{L_i / 10} \right]$$

where $L_i$ are the sampled SPL values in dB and $N$ is the total number of samples.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** $L_{eq}$ is **energy-equivalent**, not arithmetic mean. Two equal values averaged energy-wise give $L_{eq}$ that is 3 dB higher than each. A doubling of acoustic energy adds **3 dB**; a tenfold energy increase adds **10 dB**.

### 2.3.2 Percentile / Statistical Levels ($L_N$)

The sound level exceeded for $N\%$ of the measurement time.

- **$L_{10}$** — exceeded 10% of the time. Represents **peak / intrusive** noise. Used in traffic noise assessment.
- **$L_{50}$** — exceeded 50% of the time. **Median** level.
- **$L_{90}$** — exceeded 90% of the time. **Background / residual** noise. Used to set quiet-zone standards.

### 2.3.3 Day-Night Average Sound Level ($L_{dn}$ or DNL)

A 24-hour average with a **+10 dB penalty** applied to nighttime (10 p.m. – 7 a.m.) levels to reflect greater annoyance during sleep.

$$L_{dn} = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{24} \left( 15 \cdot 10^{L_d/10} + 9 \cdot 10^{(L_n + 10)/10} \right) \right]$$

where $L_d$ = daytime average, $L_n$ = nighttime average (without penalty).

### 2.3.4 Community Noise Equivalent Level ($L_{CNEL}$)

Same as $L_{dn}$ but with a **+5 dB penalty for evening hours** (7 p.m. – 10 p.m.) in addition to the 10 dB night penalty.

$$L_{CNEL} = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{24} \left( 12 \cdot 10^{L_d/10} + 3 \cdot 10^{(L_e+5)/10} + 9 \cdot 10^{(L_n+10)/10} \right) \right]$$

where $L_d$, $L_e$, $L_n$ = day, evening, night averages.

### 2.3.5 Noise Pollution Level ($L_{NP}$)

Combines energy average with **statistical spread** to reflect the annoyance of fluctuating noise.

$$L_{NP} = L_{eq} + 2.56 \cdot \sigma$$

where $\sigma \approx (L_{10} - L_{90}) / \sqrt{2}$ ... actually the standard form uses:

$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (L_i - L_{eq})^2$$

and $L_{NP} = L_{eq} + k \sigma$ with $k = 2.56$ for a normal distribution (98th-percentile weight).

### 2.3.6 Traffic Noise Index ($L_{TNI}$)

Used by the UK Department of Transport.

$$L_{TNI} = 4 \cdot (L_{10} - L_{90}) + L_{90} - 30$$

The 30 dB offset and the 4× weighting of $(L_{10} - L_{90})$ reflect that fluctuating traffic is more annoying than steady noise.

### 2.3.7 Sound Exposure Level ($L_{SEL}$ or $L_{AE}$)

The $L_{eq}$ normalized to a **1-second reference duration**. Useful for characterizing single events (aircraft flyover, train pass-by).

$$L_{SEL} = L_{eq,T} + 10 \log_{10}(T)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical hint:** $L_{SEL}$ is the level a 1-second burst would need to have to deliver the same total acoustic energy as the actual event of duration $T$ seconds. Always $L_{SEL} \geq L_{max}$.

### 2.3.8 Noise Dose & TWA

Used in occupational noise (OSHA standard).

$$\text{Dose (\%)} = 100 \cdot \frac{C_1}{T_1} + \frac{C_2}{T_2} + \cdots$$

For an 8-hour shift with exchange rate $q$ dB and criterion level $L_c$:

$$T = \frac{8}{2^{(L - L_c)/q}}$$

$$TWA = L_c + q \cdot \log_2\left(\frac{D}{100}\right)$$

For OSHA: $L_c = 90$ dB(A), $q = 5$ dB. For NIOSH/ISO: $L_c = 85$ dB(A), $q = 3$ dB.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bookmark this table — it covers ~80% of KTU calculation questions.**

| Index | Formula | Unit | Typical Application |
|-------|---------|------|---------------------|
| SPL | $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$ | dB | Direct measurement |
| SIL | $L_{SWL} = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$ | dB | Source power rating |
| $L_{eq}$ (continuous) | $10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{T} \int_0^T (p^2/p_0^2) \, dt \right]$ | dB(A) | Energy average |
| $L_{eq}$ (discrete) | $10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{N} \sum 10^{L_i/10} \right]$ | dB(A) | Sampled data |
| $L_{dn}$ | $10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{24} (15 \cdot 10^{L_d/10} + 9 \cdot 10^{(L_n+10)/10}) \right]$ | dB(A) | 24-hr community |
| $L_{CNEL}$ | $10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{24} (12 \cdot 10^{L_d/10} + 3 \cdot 10^{(L_e+5)/10} + 9 \cdot 10^{(L_n+10)/10}) \right]$ | dB(A) | CA / FAA standard |
| $L_{NP}$ | $L_{eq} + 2.56 \cdot \sigma$ | dB(A) | Annoyance index |
| $L_{TNI}$ | $4 (L_{10} - L_{90}) + L_{90} - 30$ | dB(A) | UK traffic |
| $L_{SEL}$ | $L_{eq,T} + 10 \log_{10}(T)$ | dB(A) | Single event |
| Combining N sources | $L_{total} = 10 \log_{10} \sum 10^{L_i/10}$ | dB(A) | Multi-source addition |
| Doubling of sources | $\Delta L = +3$ dB | dB | Rule of thumb |
| Tenfold of sources | $\Delta L = +10$ dB | dB | Rule of thumb |
| Inverse square law | $L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10}(r_2/r_1)$ | dB | Free-field propagation |
| Barrier attenuation (point source) | $A \approx 10 \log_{10}(N) + 5$ (Maaform) | dB | Diffraction over barrier |
| Sound power from $L_p$ | $L_W = L_p + 10 \log_{10}(S)$ | dB | At distance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on table syntax:** All absolute-value/division operators are rendered with `\vert` or `\mid` to keep the markdown table intact.

### Why These Indices Matter in Engineering

- **$L_{eq}$** is the regulatory yardstick — almost every Indian and international standard specifies a $L_{eq}$ limit.
- **$L_{10}$** correlates with traffic noise peaks and complaint rates.
- **$L_{90}$** defines the *quietest 10%* and is used for sleep/quiet-zone protection.
- **$L_{dn}$ / $L_{CNEL}$** are FAA / EPA requirements for airport noise compatibility.
- **$L_{TNI}$** is mandated in UK road-project EIAs.
- **$L_{SEL}$** is the metric for single aircraft or rail events.

---

## 2.5 Noise Control Methods — Engineering Toolkit

### 2.5.1 Source Control (Most Effective)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The cheapest noise is the noise that is never generated. Source control includes:
> - **Equipment selection** — quieter motors, fans, pumps (compare $L_W$ ratings).
> - **Vibration isolation** — use springs, rubber mounts, neoprene pads.
> - **Damping** — apply constrained-layer damping (CLD) sheets, viscoelastic materials.
> - **Reshaping of excitation forces** — gear polishing, blade-pass frequency staggering.
> - **Acoustic enclosure of source** — wrap the machine in a box with internal absorbers.
> - **Mufflers / silencers** — reactive, absorptive, or combination; tuned to dominant frequency.

### 2.5.2 Path Control

- **Distance attenuation** — doubling distance from a point source → $-6$ dB; from a line source (traffic) → $-3$ dB.
- **Barriers** — solid walls, berms, buildings. Attenuation depends on the **path-length difference** $\delta = A + B - C$ where $A, B$ are diffracted path legs and $C$ is direct path. Maa-formula: $A \approx 10 \log_{10}(2N + 1) + 5$ for $N = 2\delta/\lambda$ (Fresnel number).
- **Sound-absorbing materials** — porous (foams, mineral wool, glass fiber), resonant (Helmholtz), panel (membrane) absorbers.
- **Reverberation control** — reduce room reflections to lower overall SPL at receivers.
- **Green belts** — trees and shrubs give ~2–4 dB per 30 m depth, plus psychological benefit.

### 2.5.3 Receiver Control (Last Resort)

- **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** — ear plugs (NRR 20–30 dB), ear muffs (NRR 20–30 dB), double protection.
- **Zoning and land-use planning** — buffer zones between industrial and residential.
- **Administrative controls** — shift rotation, time-of-operation restrictions, signage.
- **Architectural design** — double-glazing, resilient channels, isolated slabs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** In any major project — highway, airport, power plant, factory — the **Source-Path-Receiver** hierarchy is applied in cost-benefit order. A 1 dB reduction at the source typically costs 5–10× less than 1 dB of path control and 50–100× less than receiver PPE.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Combining Multiple Independent Sound Sources

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** Two machines produce sound pressure levels of $L_1 = 85$ dB and $L_2 = 82$ dB at the operator's position. What is the combined SPL when both operate simultaneously?

### Step 1 — Recall that dB is logarithmic, so direct addition is invalid

Sound powers (or mean-square pressures) **add linearly**, not their logarithmic transforms. Therefore we must convert each dB value to a linear energy quantity.

### Step 2 — Convert each SPL to relative energy

By definition:
$$L_i = 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{I_i}{I_{ref}} \right)$$

So:
$$\frac{I_i}{I_{ref}} = 10^{L_i / 10}$$

For machine 1:
$$\frac{I_1}{I_{ref}} = 10^{85/10} = 10^{8.5} = 3.1623 \times 10^{8}$$

For machine 2:
$$\frac{I_2}{I_{ref}} = 10^{82/10} = 10^{8.2} = 1.5849 \times 10^{8}$$

### Step 3 — Sum the energies (incoherent sources)

Since the two machines emit statistically independent noise, intensities add:
$$\frac{I_{tot}}{I_{ref}} = 10^{8.5} + 10^{8.2}$$

$$= 3.1623 \times 10^{8} + 1.5849 \times 10^{8}$$

$$= 4.7472 \times 10^{8}$$

### Step 4 — Convert back to dB

$$L_{tot} = 10 \log_{10} \left( 4.7472 \times 10^{8} \right)$$

$$= 10 \left[ \log_{10}(4.7472) + 8 \right]$$

$$= 10 \left[ 0.6765 + 8 \right]$$

$$= 10 \times 8.6765$$

$$= 86.77 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Result:** $L_{tot} \approx 86.8$ dB. **Key insight:** The combined level is dominated by the louder source — adding 82 dB to 85 dB raises the total by only **1.8 dB**, not 3 dB. This is the classic "dominant-source" rule.

### Step 5 — Alternative 3-dB Rule for Equal Sources

If both sources were equal at 85 dB, then $L_{tot} = 85 + 3 = 88$ dB (energy doubles). If the second source were **3 dB lower** ($82$ dB), it contributes only half the energy, so the increase is $\approx 1.76$ dB. This matches the rigorous computation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Inverse Square Law for Point Source

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** A transformer emits noise at $L_1 = 90$ dB(A) at $r_1 = 5$ m. Find the SPL at $r_2 = 20$ m assuming free-field propagation.

### Step 1 — Free-field relation

In a free field (no reflections, outdoor), acoustic intensity from a point source decays as $1/r^2$:

$$I_2 = I_1 \left( \frac{r_1}{r_2} \right)^2$$

### Step 2 — Take the log form

$$L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{r_2}{r_1} \right)$$

### Step 3 — Substitute numerical values

$$L_{p2} = 90 - 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{20}{5} \right)$$

$$= 90 - 20 \log_{10}(4)$$

$$= 90 - 20 \times 0.6021$$

$$= 90 - 12.04$$

$$= 77.96 \text{ dB(A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Quadrupling the distance reduces SPL by 6 dB** (point source: 6 dB per doubling). The $20\log_{10}$ factor arises because intensity goes as $1/r^2$ but we measure pressure (which is $\sqrt{I}$), and $L_p \propto 20 \log p$.

### For Line Source (e.g., traffic)

$$L_{p2} = L_{p1} - 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{r_2}{r_1} \right)$$

(only $-3$ dB per doubling, since intensity goes as $1/r$).

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Equivalent Continuous Level ($L_{eq}$) — Worked Example

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** Sound level readings taken every 5 minutes for one hour are: 70, 75, 80, 72, 78, 76, 74, 80, 77, 73, 71, 75 dB(A). Calculate $L_{eq}$.

### Step 1 — Sample size

There are $N = 12$ samples (one per 5 min, total 60 min).

### Step 2 — Convert each to linear energy

$$E_i = 10^{L_i/10}$$

Computing each:

$$E_1 = 10^{7.0} = 1.0000 \times 10^{7}$$

$$E_2 = 10^{7.5} = 3.1623 \times 10^{7}$$

$$E_3 = 10^{8.0} = 1.0000 \times 10^{8}$$

$$E_4 = 10^{7.2} = 1.5849 \times 10^{7}$$

$$E_5 = 10^{7.8} = 6.3096 \times 10^{7}$$

$$E_6 = 10^{7.6} = 3.9811 \times 10^{7}$$

$$E_7 = 10^{7.4} = 2.5119 \times 10^{7}$$

$$E_8 = 10^{8.0} = 1.0000 \times 10^{8}$$

$$E_9 = 10^{7.7} = 5.0119 \times 10^{7}$$

$$E_{10} = 10^{7.3} = 1.9953 \times 10^{7}$$

$$E_{11} = 10^{7.1} = 1.2589 \times 10^{7}$$

$$E_{12} = 10^{7.5} = 3.1623 \times 10^{7}$$

### Step 3 — Sum the energies

$$\sum E_i = (1.000 + 3.162 + 10.000 + 1.585 + 6.310 + 3.981 + 2.512 + 10.000 + 5.012 + 1.995 + 1.259 + 3.162) \times 10^{7}$$

$$= 49.978 \times 10^{7} = 4.998 \times 10^{8}$$

### Step 4 — Compute mean energy

$$\bar{E} = \frac{4.998 \times 10^8}{12} = 4.165 \times 10^{7}$$

### Step 5 — Convert back to dB

$$L_{eq} = 10 \log_{10}(4.165 \times 10^{7})$$

$$= 10 \left[ \log_{10}(4.165) + 7 \right]$$

$$= 10 \left[ 0.6198 + 7 \right]$$

$$= 10 \times 7.6198 = 76.2 \text{ dB(A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Sanity check:** $L_{eq}$ (76.2 dB) lies between the **arithmetic mean** (75.0 dB) and the **maximum** (80 dB), weighted toward the louder values — exactly the desired property of an energy-equivalent metric.

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Sound Exposure Level (SEL) for a Single Event

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** A train passes by a station in 6 seconds with constant SPL of 84 dB(A). Compute the $L_{SEL}$ of the event.

### Step 1 — $L_{eq}$ of the 6-s event

Since SPL is constant, $L_{eq,6s} = 84$ dB(A).

### Step 2 — Apply the SEL definition

$$L_{SEL} = L_{eq,T} + 10 \log_{10} \left( \frac{T}{T_0} \right)$$

with $T_0 = 1$ s.

$$L_{SEL} = 84 + 10 \log_{10}(6)$$

$$= 84 + 10 \times 0.7782$$

$$= 84 + 7.78$$

$$= 91.78 \text{ dB(A)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A single 1-second burst at 91.8 dB(A) carries the same acoustic energy as the actual 6-second pass-by at 84 dB(A). SEL is the metric used to characterize single aircraft or train events for cumulative impact.

---

## 3.5 Derivation: Barrier Attenuation (Maa's Formula)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** A noise barrier is built between a point source (HVAC outdoor unit, 250 Hz dominant) and a receiver. The path-length difference is $\delta = 0.6$ m. Speed of sound $c = 343$ m/s.

### Step 1 — Wavelength at 250 Hz

$$\lambda = \frac{c}{f} = \frac{343}{250} = 1.372 \text{ m}$$

### Step 2 — Fresnel number

$$N = \frac{2 \delta}{\lambda} = \frac{2 \times 0.6}{1.372} = 0.875$$

### Step 3 — Maa's empirical formula (for $N \geq 0$)

$$A \approx 10 \log_{10}(2N + 1) + 5 \text{ dB}$$

Substituting:
$$A \approx 10 \log_{10}(2 \times 0.875 + 1) + 5$$

$$= 10 \log_{10}(2.75) + 5$$

$$= 10 \times 0.4393 + 5$$

$$= 4.39 + 5 = 9.39 \text{ dB}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical implication:** A barrier giving ~9.4 dB of attenuation at 250 Hz is highly effective for mid-frequency noise. Low-frequency performance is poor (smaller $\delta$ in terms of $\lambda$), which is why barriers must be tall enough to maintain adequate $\delta$ across the spectrum.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: When to Use Each Index

| Scenario | Recommended Index | Why |
|----------|-------------------|-----|
| Compliance check vs CPCB ambient standard | $L_{eq}$ (1 hr or 24 hr) | Regulatory requirement |
| Traffic noise impact (highway, urban road) | $L_{10}$, $L_{TNI}$ | Captures peak annoyance |
| Quiet-zone / background assessment | $L_{90}$ | Distinguishes ambient floor |
| Airport / 24-hr community impact | $L_{dn}$, $L_{CNEL}$ | Sleep penalty built in |
| Single event (aircraft overflight, siren) | $L_{SEL}$ | Normalized to 1 s |
| Worker occupational exposure | Dose %, TWA | OSHA / NIOSH compliance |
| General fluctuation annoyance | $L_{NP}$ | Combines $L_{eq}$ + variability |
| Speech intelligibility in a room | SIL, NC, RC | Frequency-band specific |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define $L_{eq}$. Why is it preferred over the simple arithmetic mean of sound levels?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **$L_{eq}$ (Equivalent Continuous Sound Level)** is the steady, continuous sound level which, if maintained over the same time period $T$, would deliver the same acoustic energy as the actual time-varying sound.
> $$L_{eq,T} = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} \frac{p^2(t)}{p_0^2} \, dt \right]$$
> It is **preferred over the arithmetic mean** because (i) it is energy-equivalent, properly accounting for the *logarithmic* nature of the dB scale; (ii) doubling of energy gives a +3 dB increase (a perceptually meaningful change), whereas arithmetic mean loses this sensitivity; (iii) $L_{eq}$ correlates directly with hearing damage and annoyance, which are both energy-based physiological responses. (3 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between A-weighting and C-weighting networks. Where is each used?**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **A-weighting** is a frequency filter that mimics the 40-phon equal-loudness contour of the human ear at low-to-moderate levels, attenuating low frequencies (< 500 Hz) and high frequencies (> 10 kHz). It is the standard for environmental, community, and occupational noise assessment, with results reported in dB(A).
> **C-weighting** is a comparatively flat filter that approximates the 100-phon contour, retaining most low-frequency content. It is used for **peak and impact noise** measurements (e.g., press punches, drop-forge operations) and for assessing low-frequency components that A-weighting would mask. Resulting units: dB(C).
> **Summary:** A-weighting for steady/environmental noise; C-weighting for impulsive/peak noise. (3 marks)

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall Callout**
> 1. In $L_{eq}$ problems, students often take the **arithmetic mean of dB values** and lose 4–5 marks. Always convert to energy first, average, then re-convert to dB.
> 2. For **barrier attenuation**, students frequently forget to convert frequency to wavelength using $c = 343$ m/s. A wrong $\lambda$ invalidates the Fresnel number $N$ and the entire problem.
> 3. For **statistical levels** ($L_{10}$, $L_{90}$), the phrasing matters: $L_{10}$ = level **exceeded 10% of the time**, not "level at 10% mark on x-axis." Misinterpretation costs 2 marks.
> 4. In **$L_{dn}$ derivations**, students omit the +10 dB night penalty or use 9 hours instead of the correct 9-hour night and 15-hour day split. Verify the standard definition before computing.
> 5. For **multi-source addition**, students add dB values directly. The correct operation is to convert each dB to linear power, sum, then convert back.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **functional block diagram of a Sound Level Meter**. List the salient features of a Type 1 and Type 2 SLM as per IEC 61672. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** A noise survey at a residential location yielded the following hourly $L_{eq}$ values over 24 hours: $L_d$ (07:00–22:00 average) = 62 dB(A) and $L_n$ (22:00–07:00 average) = 54 dB(A). Calculate the **Day–Night Average Sound Level ($L_{dn}$)** and comment on the CPCB compliance. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

A Sound Level Meter consists of the following functional blocks in cascade:

1. **Microphone** — converts acoustic pressure to electrical voltage. Capacitor (condenser) microphone with prepolarized electret is preferred for Class 1 SLMs.
2. **Preamplifier** — high input-impedance stage, located close to the microphone to avoid cable loss.
3. **Frequency Weighting Network** — selectable A, C, or Z (linear) filter to mimic human ear response.
4. **RMS Detector & Averager** — computes root-mean-square pressure over Fast (125 ms), Slow (1 s), or Impulse time constants.
5. **Logarithmic Amplifier** — converts linear RMS signal to dB scale using $20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$.
6. **Display / Data Logger** — digital LCD output with serial/USB data logging.

[Attach the SLM block diagram from Section 4.1 as the neat sketch — 1 mark for diagram, 2 marks for description.]

**2. Class 1 vs Class 2 SLM (4 marks):**

| Feature | Class 1 (Precision) | Class 2 (General Purpose) |
|---------|---------------------|---------------------------|
| Tolerance | ± 0.7 dB at 1 kHz | ± 1.5 dB at 1 kHz |
| Frequency range | 20 Hz – 20 kHz (typ.) | 31.5 Hz – 8 kHz (typ.) |
| Use | Laboratory, legal evidence, type-approval | Field surveys, screening, general environmental |
| Cost | High | Moderate |
| IEC ref | IEC 61672-1 Class 1 | IEC 61672-1 Class 2 |

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Given:** $L_d = 62$ dB(A), $L_n = 54$ dB(A) (nighttime SPL *before* the 10 dB penalty).

**Step 1 — Write the $L_{dn}$ formula:**

$$L_{dn} = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{24} \left( 15 \cdot 10^{L_d/10} + 9 \cdot 10^{(L_n + 10)/10} \right) \right]$$

The +10 dB inside the second term represents the **night penalty**.

**Step 2 — Substitute:**

$$L_{dn} = 10 \log_{10} \left[ \frac{1}{24} \left( 15 \cdot 10^{6.2} + 9 \cdot 10^{6.4} \right) \right]$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the powers:**

$$10^{6.2} = 1.5849 \times 10^{6}$$

$$10^{6.4} = 2.5119 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply and sum:**

$$15 \times 1.5849 \times 10^{6} = 23.7735 \times 10^{6}$$

$$9 \times 2.5119 \times 10^{6} = 22.6071 \times 10^{6}$$

$$\text{Sum} = 46.3806 \times 10^{6}$$

**Step 5 — Divide by 24 and take log:**

$$\frac{46.3806 \times 10^{6}}{24} = 1.9325 \times 10^{6}$$

$$L_{dn} = 10 \log_{10}(1.9325 \times 10^{6})$$

$$= 10 \left[ \log_{10}(1.9325) + 6 \right]$$

$$= 10 \left[ 0.2861 + 6 \right] = 62.86 \text{ dB(A)}$$

$$\boxed{L_{dn} \approx 62.9 \text{ dB(A)}}$$

**Step 6 — Comment on CPCB compliance (1 mark):**

CPCB ambient noise standard for residential zone is **55 dB(A) day / 45 dB(A) night**. The computed $L_{dn}$ of 62.9 dB(A) **exceeds the limit by ~7.9 dB(A)**, so the location is non-compliant. Mitigation measures (barriers, source silencing, zoning) are recommended.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 4, 14 Marks]**
>
> **(a)** Define **Noise Pollution Level ($L_{NP}$)** and **Traffic Noise Index ($L_{TNI}$)**. In a highway survey the following statistical levels were recorded: $L_{10} = 78$ dB(A), $L_{50} = 70$ dB(A), $L_{90} = 62$ dB(A), and the $L_{eq}$ was 74 dB(A). Calculate $L_{NP}$ and $L_{TNI}$ and interpret. (7 marks)
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **principles of noise control** at the source, along the path, and at the receiver. Give two practical engineering examples for each category. (7 marks)

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Definitions (2 marks):**

> **$L_{NP}$ (Noise Pollution Level):** A single-number index proposed by Robinson that combines the energy-equivalent level with a measure of statistical variability:
> $$L_{NP} = L_{eq} + k \cdot \sigma$$
> where $\sigma$ is the standard deviation of the fluctuating noise and $k = 2.56$ corresponds to weighting the 98th percentile of a normal distribution.
>
> **$L_{TNI}$ (Traffic Noise Index):** UK Department of Transport index that emphasizes the *fluctuation* of traffic noise:
> $$L_{TNI} = 4 (L_{10} - L_{90}) + L_{90} - 30$$
> The 30 dB offset and 4× weight make fluctuating sounds score higher (more annoying) than steady ones.

**2. Calculation of $\sigma$ (2 marks):**

Using the approximation that for traffic-like noise:
$$\sigma \approx \frac{L_{10} - L_{90}}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{78 - 62}{\sqrt{2}} = \frac{16}{1.414} = 11.31$$

Alternatively, the more rigorous form using the given $L_{eq}$:
$$\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{N-1} \sum (L_i - L_{eq})^2 \approx \text{(use the first form as an estimate)}$$

For KTU, the simplified form $\sigma \approx (L_{10} - L_{90}) / \sqrt{2}$ is acceptable.

**3. $L_{NP}$ calculation (1.5 marks):**

$$L_{NP} = L_{eq} + 2.56 \cdot \sigma$$

$$= 74 + 2.56 \times 11.31$$

$$= 74 + 28.95 = 102.95 \text{ dB(A)}$$

$$\boxed{L_{NP} \approx 103.0 \text{ dB(A)}}$$

**4. $L_{TNI}$ calculation (1 mark):**

$$L_{TNI} = 4 (L_{10} - L_{90}) + L_{90} - 30$$

$$= 4 \times (78 - 62) + 62 - 30$$

$$= 4 \times 16 + 32$$

$$= 64 + 32 = 96 \text{ dB(A)}$$

$$\boxed{L_{TNI} = 96 \text{ dB(A)}}$$

**5. Interpretation (0.5 marks):**

Both $L_{NP}$ and $L_{TNI}$ are well above the CPCB residential limit (55 dB(A) day). The high values, especially driven by the large $L_{10} - L_{90} = 16$ dB spread, indicate **highly fluctuating** traffic noise — which is more annoying than steady noise at the same $L_{eq}$. A noise barrier or green belt is recommended.

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Source Control (2.5 marks):**

Source control aims to reduce noise at the point of generation. The principle is to use quieter equipment, modify excitation forces, and isolate vibrations.

- **Example 1 — Equipment re-selection:** Replace an old 90 dB(A) air compressor with a modern variable-speed unit rated at 78 dB(A), giving 12 dB reduction.
- **Example 2 — Vibration isolation mounts:** Install neoprene or spring isolators between a chiller and the building structure, reducing structure-borne noise by 10–20 dB.
- **Example 3 — Damping treatment:** Apply constrained-layer damping (CLD) sheets to a metal panel; reduces resonant vibration and radiated noise by 5–10 dB.

**2. Path Control (2.5 marks):**

Path control blocks or attenuates sound as it travels from source to receiver.

- **Example 1 — Acoustic barrier:** A 3-m-tall solid barrier between a highway and a residence can give 8–12 dB insertion loss at mid frequencies.
- **Example 2 — Sound-absorbing enclosure:** Wrapping a generator in a 100-mm-thick mineral-wool-lined enclosure reduces SPL by 20–30 dB.
- **Example 3 — Green belt / vegetation:** Dense tree barriers over 30-m depth give 2–4 dB attenuation plus psychological masking.

**3. Receiver Control (2 marks):**

Receiver control protects the listener when source and path control are insufficient.

- **Example 1 — Personal Protective Equipment (PPE):** Ear plugs with NRR 30 reduce ear-canal exposure from 100 dB(A) to ~70 dB(A) (with derating).
- **Example 2 — Architectural design:** Double-glazed windows (8 mm + 12 mm air gap + 6 mm) attenuate façade noise by 30–35 dB.
- **Example 3 — Administrative control:** Restrict construction hours to 09:00–17:00 in residential zones to limit sleep disturbance.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize for KTU)**

### Definitions & Core Metrics
- **Sound Pressure Level:** $L_p = 20 \log_{10}(p/p_0)$, $p_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$.
- **Sound Power Level:** $L_W = 10 \log_{10}(W/W_0)$, $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W.
- **Reference pressure $p_0 = 20 \, \mu Pa$; reference power $W_0 = 10^{-12}$ W.**
- **dB(A)** is the universal reporting unit; uses A-weighting.
- **Doubling energy = +3 dB;** **tenfold energy = +10 dB.**
- **Doubling distance (point source) = −6 dB;** (line source) = −3 dB.

### Instruments
- **Class 1 SLM** — precision, ± 0.7 dB; Class 2 — general purpose, ± 1.5 dB.
- Microphone height **1.2 – 1.5 m**, **≥ 3.5 m from reflective surfaces**.
- Calibrate at **94 dB, 1 kHz** before and after measurement.

### Noise Indices — Quick Recall
- **$L_{eq}$** — energy-equivalent average.
- **$L_{10}$** — peaks (intrusive); **$L_{50}$** — median; **$L_{90}$** — background.
- **$L_{dn}$** — 24-hr avg with **+10 dB night penalty** (9 h night, 15 h day).
- **$L_{CNEL}$** — adds **+5 dB evening penalty** (3 h evening, 9 h night, 12 h day).
- **$L_{NP} = L_{eq} + 2.56 \cdot \sigma$** — combines level and variability.
- **$L_{TNI} = 4 (L_{10} - L_{90}) + L_{90} - 30$** — UK traffic.
- **$L_{SEL} = L_{eq,T} + 10 \log_{10}(T)$** — single-event, normalized to 1 s.

### Noise Control — SPR Mnemonic
- **S**ource (best, cheapest): quiet equipment, isolation, damping, mufflers, enclosures.
- **P**ath (mid): distance, barriers, absorbers, green belts, enclosures.
- **R**eceiver (last resort): PPE, zoning, administrative, architectural.
- **Barrier attenuation (Maa):** $A \approx 10 \log_{10}(2N + 1) + 5$, with $N = 2\delta / \lambda$.

### CPCB Ambient Air Quality Standards (Noise) — for Compliance Reference
| Zone | Day dB(A) | Night dB(A) |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| Industrial | 75 | 70 |
| Commercial | 65 | 55 |
| Residential | 55 | 45 |
| Silence Zone | 50 | 40 |

### Common Pitfalls
1. **Arithmetic mean of dB ≠ $L_{eq}$.** Always convert to energy.
2. **Day-night penalty of +10 dB** applies *inside* the log, not outside.
3. **Sound power and sound pressure formulas differ by a factor of 10** in the log coefficient.
4. **Atmospheric absorption** (≈ 0.5 dB/100 m at 1 kHz per km) is often ignored in KTU problems but matters in real surveys.
5. **For point sources, intensity falls as $1/r^2$** — so use $20 \log$ for SPL, $10 \log$ for SIL.
